cuisine: 洋食（Western-style）; signature ハントンライス (Hunton Rice), hamburg steak, cream soup, pork steak
neighborhood: 片町 (Katamachi)
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [136.651917, 36.562042] for 石川県金沢市片町2-9-15; within Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 40 seats
hours_raw: 11:00-15:20 (L.O. 14:45), 17:00-20:30 (L.O. 19:50). Closed Wednesday (open if Wed is a holiday, with Thursday as substitute holiday). One additional temporary closure per month.
closed_days: Wednesday (open if Wed is a public holiday; Thursday becomes the substitute closed day). Plus one irregular temporary closure each month.
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, no-reservation family-run diner where pointing at the famous Hunton Rice and a few basic Japanese greetings will carry you through; bring cash and be ready to queue.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The kitchen is built around breaded/fried items (marlin and shrimp fries), omelette-over-rice with ketchup, hamburg steak, cream croquettes and flour-based cream/white sauces — all wheat-heavy. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated fryer, and shared frying oil makes this unsafe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The menu centers on seafood (fried marlin/swordfish, shrimp), eggs (the signature dish uses about three eggs per plate), meat (hamburg, pork steak) and dairy-based cream sauces and soups. No vegan options are documented.
caveats: Cash only (PayPay/QR accepted; no credit cards or e-money). No reservations — long lines commonly form out front, especially at lunch. Closed Wednesdays plus one irregular day per month.

bio.background: A brick-fronted Western-style diner (yoshoku) founded in 1957 by Taduzou Otsuka. It is now run by the third generation, Masaki Otsuka, who took over from his father and second-generation owner Shoji Otsuka. The restaurant is widely regarded as the most famous home of Kanazawa's local specialty, Hunton Rice.
bio.specialty: Hunton Rice — a plate of plain ketchup rice topped with silky, partly soft-set egg, two fried marlin (swordfish) cutlets and two fried shrimp, lavished with house tartar sauce and ketchup. The restaurant also has a devoted following for its beef-consomme-and-milk cream soup, pork steak on an iron plate, cream croquettes and omelette hayashi rice.
bio.philosophy: Second-generation owner Shoji Otsuka held that 'diligently making delicious food matters most,' prioritizing quality and consistency over flash.

anecdotes.text: Hunton Rice was born here in the 1960s when the head chef under second-generation owner Shoji recreated a dish he remembered from his Tokyo apprenticeship; the name blends 'Han' (Hungarian) and 'ton' (French for tuna). The original choice to spread the egg over the rice rather than wrap it was unconventional at the time and drew attention.
Today roughly 70-80% of customers order the Hunton Rice, and long queues regularly form outside the shop.
A lesser-known house favorite is the velvety cream soup, built on a sauce veloute base with beef consomme and milk that turns subtly sweet as you eat.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dishes are breaded and deep-fried (marlin and shrimp fries, cream croquettes) in shared oil, with flour-based cream and white sauces throughout the menu — high gluten cross-contamination risk.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen works extensively with eggs, seafood, meat and dairy; the signature dish alone uses about three eggs per plate, so vegan cross-contact is unavoidable.

neighborhood: 近江町市場（青草町）
cuisine: 海鮮・加賀料理（刺身、海鮮丼、治部煮、のどぐろ）
gf_detail: This is a seafood and Kaga-cuisine izakaya built around sashimi, seafood rice bowls, soy-sauce-based dishes, jibuni (which uses wheat-thickened sauce and soy), and tempura. Soy sauce, dashi, and wheat are pervasive, and no gluten-free menu or allergen handling is documented. Not safe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: A seafood-focused restaurant whose entire identity is fresh fish, sashimi, seafood bowls, and Noto beef. No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented, and dashi/fish stock underlies most cooked dishes. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市青草町15-1 returned [136.655823, 36.571144], within the central Kanazawa box (Omicho Market).
seats_text: 47 seats: counter, table, and tatami seating, plus a semi-private room for up to 12.
hours_raw: Weekdays (Mon-Fri): 10:30-21:00 (L.O. 20:30). Saturdays, Sundays & holidays: 09:30-21:00 (L.O. 20:30). Closed Jan 1-4.
closed_days: No regular weekly closing day; closed annually January 1-4.
cultural_comfort_note: A busy, welcoming market izakaya used to tourists and accepting cards and QR payments, so a few words of Japanese and pointing at the seafood bowls will get you a great meal comfortably.
caveats: Heavily soy-, dashi-, and seafood-based; no documented gluten-free, vegan, or allergen accommodation. Lunch can be busy at this market location.

bio.background: Sashimiya sits right inside Kanazawa's Omicho Market, the city's bustling fish market, and is run by a former fish merchant who turned decades of handling the day's catch into a kitchen. The connection to the surrounding stalls is direct: the seafood comes from fishmongers within the same market, so the sashimi reaches the plate while it is still firm and glossy.
bio.specialty: The kitchen is known for generously piled seafood rice bowls (kaisen-don) heaped with tuna, crab, yellowtail, nodoguro/black-throat seaperch, salmon roe, and sea urchin, alongside sashimi platters, nodoguro prepared several ways, tempura of Kaga vegetables, and the local Ishikawa specialty jibuni.
bio.philosophy: The owner's stated aim is to draw out the natural umami of each ingredient through expert preparation rather than heavy seasoning, leaning on the freshness that comes from buying within the market.

anecdotes.text: The owner is a former fishmonger who applies skills honed over many years in the fish trade to select and cut the day's catch.
Its signature seafood bowl is loaded with luxurious toppings like tuna, crab, yellowtail, nodoguro, salmon roe, and sea urchin, all sourced from fishmongers inside Omicho Market.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Menu centers on sashimi, seafood bowls, tempura, and jibuni, all of which rely on soy sauce and dashi; tempura adds wheat batter. No gluten-free options are documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Entirely seafood- and meat-driven (sashimi, seafood bowls, Noto beef), with dashi-based cooked dishes; no vegan or vegetarian items are documented.

cuisine: 純喫茶・喫茶店 (retro Showa-era coffee shop / kissaten)
neighborhood: 下堤町 (Shimotsutsumicho), near Omicho Market / Hokutetsu Kanazawa Station
gf_detail: This is a traditional Showa-era kissaten whose signature dishes are wheat-based: napolitan pasta, pizza toast, ham pilaf, and toast. There is no gluten-free menu and no indication of allergen accommodation. Not safe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: A classic coffee shop serving meat- and dairy-based fare (ham pilaf, pizza toast, napolitan). Beverages include coffee and tea typically taken with milk. No documented vegan options.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市下堤町20 堤町ビル 1F
seats_text: 25 seats (counter seating at the front, table seating in the back)
hours_raw: 10:00 - 18:30, open daily (無休)
closed_days: 無休 (no regular closing day)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, time-capsule local kissaten run in Showa style where little or no English is likely spoken and the menu is in Japanese, so a foreign student will be most comfortable with a guide or a translation app and a respectful, quiet approach. Note: smoking is permitted inside.
caveats: Cash only; smoking allowed indoors; no reservations; menu in Japanese only. Hours and seat count per current listing — verify on the day of visit.

bio.background: Cialda is a long-running retro coffee shop tucked away just steps from Omicho Market in central Kanazawa. Reported in 2024 to be 61 years old, it dates to roughly the early 1960s. Its sign is almost hidden behind a curtain of potted plants, giving it a hidden-away, easy-to-miss charm even for locals who pass nearby.
bio.specialty: Napolitan pasta is the standout dish (served with Tabasco and grated cheese on the side), alongside other kissaten classics like ham pilaf and pizza toast, plus coffee and tea served in delicate floral-patterned cups.

anecdotes.text: The deep interior blends Showa retro with Western vintage: arches and tiles, an amber-toned glow, brick walls in the back room with a striking double-arched wall, shelves of vintage collectibles (Hills Coffee cans, antique pitchers, dog figurines), and even an actual telephone booth inside.

A blogger noted the shop is so screened by abundant potted plants that its signage is nearly invisible from a distance, and that the master, like many old kissaten owners, joked that the cups and saucers gradually stop matching in number over the years.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Signature menu is wheat-heavy (napolitan pasta, pizza toast, ham pilaf, toast); shared kitchen with no documented allergen handling.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are built on ham, cheese, butter and dairy; no documented dairy-free or plant-based preparation.

cuisine: グルテンフリー・ヴィーガン米粉パン専門店（無添加・天然酵母、卵乳不使用）
gf_detail: Dedicated gluten-free rice flour bakery. Bread is made from 100% domestic Koshihikari rice flour with no wheat at all, and the production facility is reported to be free of wheat, eggs, dairy and nuts — meaning products avoid all 8 major Japanese labeling allergens. This makes it one of the few genuinely celiac-suitable bakeries in Kanazawa. Note: it is a takeout bakery/shop, not a sit-down restaurant.
vegan_detail: Egg-free and dairy-free are core to the shop: the rice flour breads use no eggs or dairy, and cakes/cookies use soy-milk meringue in place of egg. Many items are fully plant-based, though not every product is explicitly labeled vegan, so confirm individual items (e.g. cakes) when ordering.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市駅西新町1-4-1 ロイヤルコート駅西105
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市駅西新町1-4-1 returned [136.641159, 36.588394], within the Kanazawa box. About a 10-20 minute walk west of JR Kanazawa Station (west exit).
neighborhood: 駅西新町（金沢駅西口・徒歩約10分）
seats_text: Takeout bakery/shop with no dine-in seating; 2 parking spaces available.
hours_raw: 11:30-16:30（売切次第終了）。木曜・金曜定休。
closed_days: Thursdays and Fridays (plus occasional irregular closures); closes early when sold out.
cultural_comfort_note: A small local rice-flour bakery where you buy bread to take away rather than dine in, so come with a friend or a translation app and be ready to point and pay cash — but it is a reliable, welcoming spot for gluten-free and egg/dairy-free students.
caveats: This is a takeout bakery/shop, not a restaurant with seating. Products sell out and the shop closes early once sold out; calling the day before to reserve specific items is recommended. Cash only.

bio.background: WAGOME is a small specialty bakery in the Ekishi-Shinmachi neighborhood, a short walk west of Kanazawa Station, devoted entirely to gluten-free rice flour baking. It has operated since around 2014 and relocated to its current shop in 2022.
bio.specialty: Naturally leavened gluten-free bread made from 100% domestic Koshihikari rice flour, with a crisp exterior and chewy interior. The lineup extends to allergy-friendly cakes (chiffon, cheesecake, tarts) that use soy-milk meringue instead of egg, plus rice flour cookies, gluten-free pasta and rice flour ramen.
bio.philosophy: A health-conscious, additive-free approach: no chemical additives, natural yeast fermentation, and recipes free of wheat, eggs, dairy and nuts so that families with allergies and dietary restrictions can eat safely.

anecdotes.text: All products are made without any of the 8 major labeled allergens and without additives, using natural yeast and 100% domestic Koshihikari rice flour, in a facility kept free of wheat, eggs, dairy and nuts.
The shop emphasizes additive-free, natural-yeast baking with 100% domestic Koshihikari rice and offers nationwide shipping in addition to in-store sales.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Production facility is reported to be free of wheat, eggs, dairy and nuts, and products avoid all 8 major Japanese labeling allergens, strongly limiting gluten cross-contamination risk.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Egg- and dairy-free products are made in a facility kept free of eggs and dairy; some cakes use soy-milk meringue in place of egg.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Customers can phone the day before to reserve specific items, allowing allergy needs to be communicated in advance.

positives.text: Dedicated gluten-free rice flour bakery using 100% domestic rice flour, no wheat, no additives, and natural yeast.
Recipes are free of wheat, eggs, dairy and nuts, making products suitable for both celiac and egg/dairy-avoiding customers.

lead: キッチンユキ 本店
name_ja: キッチンユキ 本店
cuisine: Yoshoku (Japanese-Western) / Kanazawa curry / Hunton rice
neighborhood: Sachiakira-cho, Hakusan City (Matto area, south of central Kanazawa, near Route 8 / Apita)
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 石川県白山市幸明町331 returned [lng 136.576477, lat 36.51762]. The longitude (136.576) falls just outside the Kanazawa bounding box western edge (136.58–136.72), so coordinates set to null. Note: the 本店 (main branch) is in Hakusan City / Matto, south of central Kanazawa, NOT in the city center — only the branch shops (Meitetsu M'za basement, etc.) are central. Target area description listing it as central Kanazawa is inaccurate for this honten location.
seats_text: Large multi-floor building: parking on 1F, dining on 2F and 3F, roughly 140 seats total (per food-blog coverage).
hours_raw: 11:00-22:30 (L.O. 22:00); official site notes 11:00-15:00 lunch, 15:00-17:00 prep break, 17:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30). Closed Thursdays (basically); closed New Year period and Thursdays in January.
closed_days: Thursdays (and New Year holidays; Thursdays in January)
cultural_comfort_note: A bustling, family-friendly local yoshoku diner where pointing at the picture menu works fine, but English support is minimal so a translation app or guide helps for ingredient questions.
vegan_detail: A meat-and-egg-centric Western diner: signature dishes are katsu (pork cutlet) curry, Hunton rice (egg-topped rice with fried fish), and rich roux-based black curry. No vegan menu and dishes rely on egg, meat and dairy; not suitable for vegans.
gf_detail: Menu is built on wheat-heavy items: thick roux curry (flour-based), breaded katsu, spaghetti, omurice and Hunton rice. No gluten-free options documented and high cross-contamination risk; not celiac-safe.
caveats: The 本店 (main branch) is in Hakusan City / Matto, not central Kanazawa, so it sits outside the Kanazawa city-center geocode box. Hours differ slightly between Tabelog (11:00-22:30) and the official site (lunch 11:00-15:00, break, dinner 17:00-22:00); confirm before visiting. Wheat- and meat-heavy menu unsuitable for celiac or vegan diners.

bio.background: Kitchen Yuki is a beloved Ishikawa institution with over 40 years of history, so well known locally that few residents in the prefecture haven't passed its landmark building on Route 8 near Apita in the Matto area. What began as a neighborhood Western-style diner grew into a multi-floor destination that locals treat as soul food, often queuing at lunchtime.
bio.specialty: The house signature is the 'Golden Kanazawa Black Curry' — a deep, dark, glossy roux praised as one of the richest among Kanazawa-style curries, mild and full-flavored rather than simply hot. Other star dishes include their popular 'Baking rice,' Turkey rice, and the Yuki-style Hunton rice that fuses the Kanazawa classic (egg-draped rice with fried fish) with their own baking rice.
bio.philosophy: Rather than chasing heat, the kitchen aims for a 'rich, mild, deeply flavorful' curry built on their proprietary house sauce recipe, which they also sell as frozen and retort packs and wholesale.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant is described as a local landmark on Route 8 near Matto Apita, so famous among Ishikawa residents that almost everyone has seen it, with lunchtime lines a regular sight.
Regulars favor the half-and-half plate pairing the Kanazawa black curry with egg-and-vegetable spaghetti, a hallmark of the Matto main store.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu centers on flour-thickened curry roux, breaded deep-fried cutlets (katsu), spaghetti and omelette rice, implying shared wheat-heavy prep and frying; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Core dishes rely on pork, egg, fish and dairy; no vegan menu or plant-based preparation is documented.

cuisine: そば（石臼挽き手打ち二八蕎麦・天ぷら）
neighborhood: 金沢市田上さくら（兼六園方面、石川県立図書館近く）
address_ja: 石川県金沢市田上さくら2-47
geocode_note: GSI geocoded to chō-level (田上さくら二丁目); within Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 21 seats (counter and table seating); small, calm space welcoming to solo diners.
hours_raw: Official site: 平日・土日 11:30-14:00 (L.O.); 土日のみ夜営業 17:30-19:30 (L.O.). お蕎麦が無くなり次第終了。定休日：月・火曜日（月曜祝日は営業）。月により不定休あり。
closed_days: Mondays and Tuesdays (open if Monday is a national holiday); plus an irregular monthly closure. Also closes early once soba sells out.
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, café-like soba shop that explicitly welcomes solo women, but it is a small owner-run local place with no English, so a Japanese-speaking guide or a translation app helps with ordering.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The soba is 二八 (80% buckwheat / 20% wheat flour), tempura is wheat-battered, and the dipping broth uses standard soy-sauce-based dashi. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-oriented. Broth/dashi is fish (bonito)-based, the menu features sashimi, toro rice, shrimp tempura and other seafood. No documented vegan dishes.
caveats: Official site and Tabelog differ slightly on closed days (official site lists Mon+Tue closed and accepts cashless payment; older Tabelog listing shows Tue closed and cash-only). Hours and payment here follow the current official site. Closes early when daily soba sells out. No reservations of any kind taken.

bio.origin: Born in Ehime Prefecture, raised in Kanazawa.
bio.background: The owner-chef entered the soba world at age 16 and has spent over three decades honing the craft, also training in Japanese cuisine and confectionery to sharpen both technique and sensibility before opening Kyoka.
bio.specialty: Stone-milled, hand-cut fine 二八 (80/20) soba. Signature offerings include Mori Soba, Tenkake Mori (crisp shrimp and seasonal vegetable tempura served with Noto sea salt), and the weekday Kyoka Gozen set; seasonal homemade side dishes and desserts round out the menu.
bio.philosophy: The chef aims for 'casual like a café, yet authentic soba' — keeping things simple and refined. Buckwheat is bought domestically, vacuum-sealed and cold-stored to lock in the autumn harvest's quality, then stone-milled in-house and freshly ground for aroma; hydration is adjusted daily and boiling times tuned (about 30-35 seconds for cold, ~20 for hot) for the best texture and throat-feel. No reservations are taken so that every guest is treated equally, and soba is made fresh daily until it sells out.

anecdotes.text: The shop is designed as a calm, welcoming space where even a woman dining alone can comfortably enjoy serious soba.
The owner began his soba apprenticeship at 16 and has pursued the craft for more than 31 years, supplementing it with training in Japanese cooking and patisserie.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Soba is 二八 (contains wheat flour) and tempura is wheat-battered, so the kitchen routinely handles wheat; no gluten-free accommodation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dipping/dashi broth is the standard soy-sauce-based type, which contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bonito/fish-based dashi and seafood dishes (sashimi, toro rice, shrimp tempura) are central to the menu; no separate vegan preparation is documented.

cuisine: 喫茶店・洋食（昭和レトロ喫茶、オムライス・ワッフル・パフェ）
neighborhood: 片町（香林坊・片町1丁目交差点付近）
address_ja: 石川県金沢市片町2-7-6 うつのみやクワルトプラザ1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja (片町2-7-6) via GSI; coordinates fall within the central Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 48 seats total: 8 counter seats, 9 four-person tables, 2 two-person tables.
hours_raw: 11:30-20:30 (L.O. 20:00). Closed Wednesdays per cafe sources; one listing also notes Tuesday closure, so confirm before a Tuesday visit. Popular with younger diners — 30+ minute waits are possible.
closed_days: Wednesday (one listing also indicates Tuesday — verify)
cultural_comfort_note: A welcoming, busy old-school Showa-era kissaten where ordering is point-and-eat simple, but staff likely speak little English, so a few basic Japanese phrases or pointing at the picture menu will make the visit smooth.
vegan_detail: A classic yoshoku kissaten built around egg-heavy omurice, cream sauces (mentaiko cream, butter chicken curry, béchamel), parfaits and dairy-topped waffles. No vegan menu and no documented plant-based options; the core dishes rely on eggs, butter, cream and dairy.
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. The menu centers on wheat-based items — waffles, spaghetti, doria, curry roux and breaded/sauced Western dishes — and there is no celiac protocol or gluten-free labeling documented. Even the omurice is typically served in a wheat-flour-thickened sauce. Not suitable for strict celiacs.
caveats: Two slightly different addresses circulate online (片町2-7-6 vs a 香林坊2-7-15 listing that may be a separate "Cafe Plaza Pino"); the Tabelog record and dedicated reviews confirm 片町2-7-6. Closed-day count differs between sources (Wednesday vs Tuesday+Wednesday). No card payment — cash or PayPay only.

bio.background: Pino is a Showa-era kissaten just a minute from the Katamachi 1-chome intersection in central Kanazawa, open for over 40 years with a look that has barely changed since it debuted — stained-glass windows, brick walls and red sofas all original to the space, which was once a UCC coffee franchise location. It is run day to day by a husband-and-wife team.
bio.specialty: Western-style comfort food anchored by a signature omurice served under double sauce (tomato and béchamel), with variations like mentaiko cream and butter-chicken-curry omurice, plus crispy waffles available in around 16-20 topping combinations and roughly 11 styles of parfait. The popular 'Yokubari Set' pairs a main with salad, a drink and a mini parfait or waffle.
bio.philosophy: All sauces and dressings are made in-house, and coffee is brewed individually by siphon rather than batch-prepared, keeping the hand-made, unhurried character of a traditional kissaten.

anecdotes.text: The cafe draws multiple generations — some regulars first came as high schoolers and now bring their grandchildren — and on busy days the owner couple are said to walk roughly 10km inside the shop keeping up with the crowd.
Its enduring Showa-retro atmosphere has made it popular again with younger diners and tourists, with waits of 30 minutes or more possible at peak times.

positives.text: Sauces and dressings are all made in-house, so staff can describe what goes into a dish if you ask, though no formal allergen handling is documented.

lead: 金沢カレー＆ハントンライス だいだい
name_ja: 金沢カレー＆ハントンライス だいだい
cuisine: 金沢カレー・ハントンライス・洋食
neighborhood: 片町（香林坊・片町、金沢中心部）
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodations are documented. The core menu (Kanazawa curry with a wheat-thickened roux, Hanton rice topped with breaded/fried shrimp and a flour-based sauce) is wheat-heavy and not celiac-safe. There is no mention of gluten-free options or allergy handling on the official site or listings.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The signature dishes — Hanton rice (egg omelette over rice with fried shrimp/fish) and Kanazawa-style curry served over pork cutlets — are built around egg, seafood and meat. No vegan or vegetarian options are advertised.
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates for 石川県金沢市片町二丁目8番10号; within the Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 13席（カウンター・テーブル、全席禁煙）
closed_days: 火曜（Tuesday）
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, cash-only local lunch spot where ordering is in Japanese and the menu is mostly photos; a foreign student would do best knowing the dish names (curry, Hanton rice) or pointing at the picture menu.
caveats: Cash only, no parking, 13 seats so it can fill up at lunch. Lunch-only (closes 15:00), closed Tuesdays. No documented gluten-free or vegan options.

bio.background: だいだい opened on April 29, 2023, in central Kanazawa's Katamachi district, just behind Korinbo Tokyu Square near the Seseragi-dori canal walk. It is a small wood-toned dining room built around the city's two best-loved local comfort foods: Kanazawa curry and Hanton rice.
bio.origin: 金沢市片町（金沢中心部）
bio.specialty: The headline dish is the 橙（だいだい）ハントンライス — a Kanazawa-style Hanton rice crowned with two oversized fried shrimp. The kitchen also serves its own coconut-based 'だいだいカレー' and a Kanazawa katsu curry, alongside a yuzu soft drink (柚子乙女).
bio.philosophy: The shop sums up its aim simply: 「人は美味しいと笑顔になる、その笑顔を見たい！」 — people smile when food tastes good, and that smile is what they want to see.

anecdotes.text: The signature 橙ハントンライス is served with two extra-large fried shrimp piled on top, and a coconut-based original curry rounds out the menu.
The restaurant opened on April 29, 2023, behind Korinbo Tokyu Square, dedicated to showcasing Kanazawa's two signature local dishes in one place.

cuisine: 加賀料理・割烹（治部煮・のどぐろ・地物魚介）
neighborhood: 香林坊（せせらぎ通り）
gf_detail: This is a traditional Kaga-cuisine kappo built around tempura, soy-seasoned simmered dishes (jibuni), grilled fish and dashi-based cooking. Wheat (tempura batter, soy sauce) and dashi are central to the menu, and there is no documented gluten-free handling or dedicated equipment. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: A seafood-focused kappo specializing in nodoguro (blackthroat seaperch), sashimi, crab, oysters and the soy/dashi-stewed Kaga dish jibuni. Dishes rely on fish, dashi and meat throughout, with no documented vegan menu or options.
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to chō-level Kōrinbō; within Kanazawa box.
seats_text: Approx. 60 seats total: ground-floor counter (8-9) and a table, plus second- and third-floor private tatami rooms (4, 6, and up to 24 persons).
hours_raw: 16:30-22:00 (dinner only per official site and Tabelog). One tourism listing also notes a 11:30-14:00 lunch service; dinner-only confirmed by official site.
closed_days: Sunday
cultural_comfort_note: This is a traditional Japanese kappo where some Japanese language and familiarity with multi-course Kaga dining will help; reservations by phone and a basic grasp of the menu make for a smoother visit.
caveats: Hours vary slightly by source: the official site and Tabelog list dinner only (16:30-22:00), while one tourism listing mentions an 11:30-14:00 lunch. Anniversary figures also differ (40 vs 50 years) across sources. Confirm lunch availability and reservations directly by phone (076-265-6555).

bio.background: A long-established kappo on Korinbo's Seseragi-dori, an area renowned as one of Kanazawa's culinary streets and within easy reach of the historic samurai district. The restaurant has been serving for several decades (described as a 40- to 50-year establishment), spread over three floors with a ground-floor counter and upstairs private tatami rooms suited to small groups and larger parties.
bio.specialty: Kaga cuisine and seasonal seafood: salt-grilled nodoguro (blackthroat seaperch), the soy-and-dashi simmered Kaga specialty jibuni, assorted sashimi, the house 'Murai special' sweet-shrimp tempura, seasonal natural Noto oysters, crab and pufferfish, and Kaga-vegetable tempura. Signature courses include Kaga kaiseki and a nodoguro course.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen's stated approach values seasonality, carefully chosen local ingredients, and thoughtful presentation, putting heart and technique into each dish; the house motto emphasizes care for the guest and sincerity in cooking ('after, no regrets').

anecdotes.text: Located on Seseragi-dori, a celebrated dining street in central Kanazawa, the place is known as a discreet spot favored by visiting celebrities.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu centers on tempura (wheat batter), soy-seasoned jibuni, and dashi-based dishes; no gluten-free handling is documented, so cross-contamination should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Kaga dishes such as jibuni and accompanying sauces use Japanese soy sauce, which typically contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Cooking is built around fish, shellfish, meat and dashi; no vegan preparation or separation is documented.

cuisine: 手造り豆腐店・豆乳ソフトクリーム／とうふアイス（ヴィーガン向け）
gf_detail: The shop's signature items are soy-based: soy milk soft serve and tofu ice cream, plus tofu, fried tofu (age) and okara. Tofu and soy milk are naturally wheat-free, so the core soy desserts are likely suitable for gluten-avoiders. However, this is a traditional neighborhood tofu shop with no documented allergen handling or celiac protocol, fried items (age) share frying, and cones/toppings are not confirmed gluten-free. A celiac should ask and stick to the plain soy soft serve or tofu rather than fried products.
vegan_detail: A handmade tofu shop whose draw for plant-based eaters is its 豆乳ソフトクリーム (soy milk soft serve) and とうふアイスクリーム (tofu ice cream), both made from soybeans rather than dairy, along with fresh tofu, fried tofu (age) and okara to take away. The soft serve and tofu products are plant-based by nature, though the shop is a tofu maker, not a self-declared vegan business, so individual recipes (e.g. any added ingredients in flavored items) are not certified. The soy milk soft serve is seasonal, typically sold April through October.
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 金沢市野町2-19-13, within the central Kanazawa box (Nishi Chaya district near Nomachi).
neighborhood: にし茶屋街（野町）
seats_text: No indoor dining room; a bench in front of the shop where customers can eat their soft serve or ice cream. Primarily a take-out tofu shop.
hours_raw: 月〜土 10:00-19:00／日曜は豆乳ソフトクリームのみ販売
closed_days: Sundays the shop is largely closed (only soy milk soft serve is sold). Soft serve is seasonal (roughly April–October).
cultural_comfort_note: This is a tiny old neighborhood tofu shop run by an elderly proprietor with essentially no English, so point at the soft serve, have cash ready, and a few words of Japanese will help.
caveats: Not a sit-down restaurant — it is a traditional tofu shop with a bench out front. The headline soy milk soft serve is seasonal (about April–October) and Sundays only the soft serve is sold. Cash only. No documented allergen handling, so confirm directly if you have a strict allergy.

bio.background: A small, long-established handmade tofu shop tucked into the historic Nishi Chaya geisha district near Nomachi. It keeps the unassuming feel of an old neighborhood tofu maker, selling fresh tofu, fried tofu (age), okara and other soy goods, with an elderly proprietor who minds the shop.
bio.specialty: 豆乳ソフトクリーム (soy milk soft serve), a rich, lightly sweetened cone made from the shop's own soybeans, and とうふアイスクリーム (tofu ice cream), a light frozen dessert that locals say even pairs with a drizzle of soy sauce. Alongside the desserts the shop sells handmade tofu, age and okara.
bio.philosophy: Carefully selected soybeans and good water turned into honest, traditional tofu — the soy desserts let visitors taste that tofu character in a cone.
roles: 手造り豆腐店（handmade tofu shop）

anecdotes.text: Visitors describe the soy milk soft serve as having a concentrated soybean flavor with restrained sweetness and a smooth, almost tofu-like texture rather than ordinary dairy ice cream.

One reviewer noted the elderly proprietor sits outside and comes when customers ring a bell, underlining how intimate and family-run the shop is, and that the soft serve is only sold in the warmer months.
The shop's tofu ice cream is described as light in flavor and said to go well with a touch of soy sauce.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tofu ice cream is sometimes eaten with a touch of soy sauce; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, so gluten-avoiders should skip the soy sauce option.

positives.text: The signature items — soy milk soft serve and tofu ice cream — are made from soybeans rather than dairy, making the core offerings plant-based.

lead: 落雁諸江屋 西茶屋菓寮 味和以
name_ja: 落雁諸江屋 西茶屋菓寮 味和以
cuisine: 和菓子・甘味処カフェ（落雁・ぜんざい・かき氷・上生菓子）
neighborhood: にし茶屋街（野町）
geocode_note: GSI geocoded to 野町2-26-1; coordinates within Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 14 seats in the cafe (two 4-person tables, one 6-person table)
hours_raw: 10:00-17:00 (L.O. 16:30); closed Tuesdays (open on public holidays, with Wednesday taken as substitute closure)
closed_days: Tuesdays (open on public holidays, substitute closure on Wednesday)
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed traditional sweets cafe in a historic geisha district where a smile and a little Japanese go a long way; menus are seasonal and mostly in Japanese, so pointing and basic phrases help.
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. Rakugan is traditionally pressed rice-flour/wasanbon sugar candy and is often wheat-free, and zenzai (red bean soup) is rice-and-bean based, but namagashi (上生菓子) and seasonal items can contain wheat, and no allergen information or cross-contact controls are published. Celiac diners must ask about each specific item.
vegan_detail: Traditional Kaga wagashi here (rakugan, zenzai, namagashi) are typically plant-based, made from rice, sugar (wasanbon/wasanbon-tou), and adzuki beans without dairy or egg, so vegan-suitable options likely exist. However, the shop publishes no vegan labeling and kakigori syrups/toppings and some namagashi may vary, so confirm ingredients per item before ordering.

bio.background: Rakugan Moroeya is a Kanazawa wagashi house founded in 1849 (late Edo period), describing itself as having walked the same path as the city for over 170 years. In 2012 it opened the Nishichaya Karyo cafe, named Ajiwai, in the historic Nishi Chaya geisha district of Nomachi, combining a retail shop with a bright, welcoming tea space where travelers can shop and rest.
bio.specialty: Rakugan (pressed wasanbon-sugar candy) is the house specialty, alongside seasonal Japanese sweets, zenzai (sweet red-bean soup), kakigori (shaved ice) served in summer (June-September), and jonamagashi (premium fresh sweets). Signature confections include Hojo-gashi, Kaga Hosho, and Hana-usagi.
bio.philosophy: The house aims to honor the tradition of Kaga meika (famous Kaga sweets) and traditional manufacturing methods while continuing to refine flavors and products beloved in contemporary times.

anecdotes.text: Founded in 1849 near the end of the Edo period, Moroeya says it has shared Kanazawa's path for more than 170 years.
The Nishichaya Karyo cafe opened in 2012 as a combined shop and tea house in the Nishi Chaya district, designed as a bright place to shop and take a break while traveling.

positives.text: Traditional menu of rakugan, zenzai, kakigori and namagashi is largely rice-, bean- and sugar-based, which often suits plant-based diners, though no allergen labeling is published.

caveats: Official archives page (moroeya.co.jp/archives/277) and the menu news page returned 404; menu specifics drawn from official site, tourist association page, and listing. No published allergen or vegan/GF certification, so dietary suitability must be confirmed in person per item.

lead: 地場料理と酒肴 季節会席 金澤 斉や
name_ja: 地場料理と酒肴 季節会席 金澤 斉や
name_en: Jiba Ryori to Shuko Kisetsu Kaiseki Kanazawa Saiya
cuisine: 加賀郷土料理・割烹（加賀治部煮小鍋仕立て）
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature Kaga jibuni is made with wheat-flour-coated meat and Kanazawa fu (sudare-fu / nama-fu, which are wheat gluten), and the kaiseki menu relies on wheat-based soy sauce and dashi throughout. No gluten-free or allergy accommodations are mentioned anywhere. Celiac travelers should treat this as unsafe.
vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian-friendly. This is a seasonal Kaga kaiseki house centered on fish, eel, duck/chicken/pork, and seafood, with dashi-based broths throughout. No vegan or vegetarian menu is offered.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市此花町11-22 フォーナインビル2F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to chome-level for 石川県金沢市此花町11-22; result [136.653763, 36.578831] falls within the central Kanazawa box, near Kanazawa Station east side.
neighborhood: 金沢駅東口・此花町（北鉄金沢駅近く）
seats_text: Official site states 15 seats with private rooms for groups of 4+; Tabelog lists 22 seats. Small intimate venue either way.
hours_raw: Official site: Lunch 11:00-15:00 (last seating 13:00, reservation-only, larger groups), Dinner 18:00-22:00. Tabelog: 12:00-14:00 and 18:00-23:00 (L.O. 21:30). Reservation-only operation. 不定休 (irregular holidays).
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures — call/reserve ahead)
cultural_comfort_note: This is a reservation-only owner-run kaiseki house where some Japanese ability or a Japanese-speaking helper to book and communicate dietary needs will make the visit much smoother for a foreign student.
caveats: Reservation-only (lunch requires larger groups and several days' notice; dinner needs advance booking, ideally a day ahead). Seat count and exact hours differ between the official site and Tabelog. Courses are 7,700-13,200 yen, so it is above a budget price point.

bio.background: Kanazawa Saiya is an owner-run seasonal kaiseki restaurant a short walk from the east side of Kanazawa Station, working with fresh local fish, Kaga vegetables, and seasonal ingredients sourced from around Japan, paired with local sake.
bio.specialty: Its signature is Kaga jibuni served as a small individual hot pot (加賀治部煮小鍋仕立て). While the classic Kanazawa dish uses wheat-flour-coated duck or chicken with sudare-fu and nama-fu (wheat gluten) and root vegetables, this house makes its version with slow-braised pork belly alongside the Kaga fu and root vegetables. Other seasonal dishes include white-soy-glazed eel, flounder tempura, and seasonal rice cooked in clay pots.
bio.philosophy: The owner emphasizes uncompromising, meticulous preparation paired with seasonal ingredients to create hospitality (妥協のない仕込みと旬の食材が織り成すおもてなし).

anecdotes.text: The kaiseki is offered at the more approachable prices typical of an individually owned restaurant, drawing both Kanazawa locals and visitors who return as regulars.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The signature jibuni uses wheat-flour-coated meat and Kanazawa fu (sudare-fu and nama-fu, which are wheat gluten), so wheat is central to the kitchen and shared prep is likely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Seasoning is built on traditional Japanese soy sauce and dashi (including a white-soy eel glaze), which contain wheat; no wheat-free soy alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are dashi-, fish-, and meat-based throughout, so even vegetable items are not prepared vegan.

cuisine: Hand-cut nihachi soba
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The house soba is nihachi (二八), made with 20% wheat flour blended into the buckwheat, and is served with wheat-based soba dashi/tsuyu. There is no gluten-free option and no indication of celiac accommodation. Not safe for those with celiac disease or wheat allergy.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The fixed "osoba-zen" set is built around fish-dashi-based soba broth and seasonal small dishes; the owner is a fisherman who serves his own-caught sashimi when available, and add-on options include herring (nishin) and nameko soba. No documented vegan set.
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned 36.546192, 136.659073 for 石川県金沢市寺町1-14-19, within the Kanazawa box, on the Teramachi 1-chome intersection corner as described.
neighborhood: Teramachi (corner of the Teramachi 1-chome intersection)
seats_text: Counter only, 7 seats
hours_raw: 日・月曜 11:00-14:00 (営業日は日曜・月曜のみ。最新情報はInstagramで要確認)
closed_days: Tuesday through Saturday (open only Sunday and Monday)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 7-seat counter run by a veteran soba craftsman with limited Sunday/Monday-only hours, so a little Japanese and patience help; come ready to order the set course and enjoy the quiet, music-filled space.
caveats: Very limited operations: open only Sundays and Mondays, 11:00-14:00, with just 7 counter seats, so check their Instagram before going and expect it to sell out or close early. The owner also runs a shop in Komatsu and the schedule can be irregular.

bio.background: Sobadokoro WAY opened in autumn 2024 on the corner of the Teramachi 1-chome intersection, inside a renovated building that previously housed a hair salon and a remake shop. The interior was designed by the local architecture and design studio SWAY DESIGN, giving the small space a modern, stylish feel complete with a vacuum-tube amplifier audio system.
bio.specialty: The signature is the 'osoba-zen' (おそば膳), a 1,500 yen set built around freshly hand-cut nihachi soba made from Nagano-grown buckwheat flour, served with a seasonal small dish and rice (and, per one account, a post-meal coffee and small dessert). The soba can be swapped for grated-radish oroshi soba, warm nameko soba, or nishin (herring) soba for an extra 300 yen.
bio.philosophy: The owner wants guests to eat things freshly made with real care and time ('手間ひまかけた出来たてを食べて欲しい').

anecdotes.text: Owner Fujita is a soba craftsman with more than 50 years of experience, hand-cutting each batch of nihachi soba himself.
He also runs a main shop in Komatsu and is a fisherman who serves his own freshly caught sashimi when the previous day's catch allows.

gf_cross_contamination.text: House soba is nihachi (二八), made with 20% wheat flour, so the noodles themselves contain gluten and there is no gluten-free option.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soba is served with a traditional dashi/tsuyu, which in soba shops is wheat-soy-sauce based; no gluten-free tsuyu is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The set centers on fish-dashi broth and seafood (sashimi, herring), so vegan separation is not offered.

cuisine: 昭和レトロ純喫茶（自家製プリン・パフェ・ホットケーキ・コーヒー）
neighborhood: 野町・有松交差点近く（にし茶屋街南方）
geocode_note: GSI address search returned [136.639908, 36.542385] for 石川県金沢市有松2-4-30; within the Kanazawa box.
seats_text: Counter seats and sofa seating; small retro cafe with no published exact seat count.
closed_days: Wednesdays
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional Showa-era kissaten run by an older couple with no English menu and cash-only payment, so a foreign student would be most comfortable bringing a Japanese-speaking friend or a translation app.
gf_detail: A retro coffee shop whose signature items are hotcakes, pudding parfaits and Napolitan-style dishes — all wheat-based or served with wheat sides. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented, and this is not a celiac-safe venue.
vegan_detail: The menu centers on dairy-and-egg desserts (homemade pudding, parfaits with whipped cream, hotcakes) and coffee. No vegan options are documented.
caveats: Cash only (no cards, electronic money, or QR payments). Closed Wednesdays. Not suitable for gluten-free or vegan diets.

bio.origin: Kanazawa, near the Arimatsu intersection just south of the Nishi Chaya geisha district
bio.background: A nostalgic Showa-era pure coffee house that has been welcoming locals for more than 40 years. The cafe is run as a husband-and-wife operation: the master handcrafts the desserts one by one while his wife decorates the plates with carefully cut fruit and whipped cream. The interior has barely changed since it opened, with a stained-glass wall, a chandelier, wine-red sofas, brickwork and framed paintings preserving a gorgeous, slightly bubble-era Western look.
bio.specialty: The signature dish is the homemade pudding (自家製プリン, around 500 yen), a firm, not-too-sweet custard topped with decoratively cut fruit and cream; it also appears as a pudding parfait. Other beloved items include Ogura hotcakes (小倉ホットケーキ) and coffee.
bio.philosophy: Seating and sight lines are arranged so guests never feel watched by other customers, making it an unusually relaxing place to linger over coffee.
roles: master (owner-chef, makes the puddings and desserts)
mama (owner's wife, plates and decorates the dishes)

anecdotes.text: The cafe is locally famous because actress Yoh Yoshida (吉田羊) worked here as a part-time staff member before her acting career.

Soft light filtering through the stained glass makes the menus and desserts a popular photo subject, and the master-and-mama team handcraft each pudding with restrained sweetness so it pairs well with coffee or tea.

cuisine: 江戸前寿司（おまかせカウンター・北陸鮮魚）
neighborhood: 彦三町（近江町市場・北鉄金沢駅近く）
gf_detail: This is a traditional Edomae sushi counter and is not safe for celiacs. Sushi is built on vinegared rice and seafood brushed with nikiri/tsume sauces and served with soy sauce, all of which are wheat-based. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented. Travelers with celiac disease should not rely on this restaurant.
vegan_detail: A seafood-focused omakase sushi restaurant centered entirely on fish sourced daily from Himi and Shinminato ports (yellowtail, abalone, uni, kohada, crab). There is no vegan or vegetarian menu and the entire concept is animal-based. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市彦三町1-8-26 returned [136.662003, 36.572708], within the Kanazawa box.
seats_text: Roughly 8-9 counter seats plus a small private room (sources vary between 8 and 9 counter seats); intimate counter where the chef serves a handful of guests at a time.
hours_raw: 17:00-21:00 (seatings reported around 17:00 and 20:30); closed Sundays plus irregular holidays. Completely reservation-based.
closed_days: 日曜（不定休あり）
cultural_comfort_note: This is a high-end, completely reservation-only omakase sushi counter where the chef serves you directly, so a foreign student should expect a formal Japanese-only dining experience and a substantial budget (roughly 20,000 yen and up).

bio.origin: Born 1973 in Toyama Prefecture; his family runs a fresh-fish business in Oyabe, giving him fish-sourcing in his blood.
bio.background: After starting out as a salaryman, Kibatani found his calling working at a fish shop in Tokyo's Tsukiji market, which sparked a passion for seafood. He trained in Edomae technique at respected sushi houses including Yoshiura in Funabashi and the celebrated Sushi Aoki in Ginza, then spent years as a traveling (catering) sushi chef before opening his own counter, Sushi Kibatani, in Kanazawa's Hikosan-cho district in 2016. The restaurant has since earned a Michelin one-star and become one of Kanazawa's hardest tables to book.
bio.specialty: Edomae sushi built on premium Hokuriku fish landed daily at Himi and Shinminato ports - yellowtail, abalone, horse mackerel, uni and crab. His signature is kohada, cured with meticulous Edomae technique adjusted to the weather and each individual fish. Dashi is shaved fresh from katsuobushi at the counter, and he uses binchotan charcoal for precise grilling; one memorable course pairs Uozu crab with a rich, almost French-style uni sauce over rice.
bio.philosophy: Kibatani speaks with gratitude about his hard years as a traveling chef and treats running his own place as a privilege, devoting himself to letting guests experience the full richness of the Hokuriku sea. He keeps learning even after success - in summer 2022 he closed for about three months to apprentice in uni handling in Hokkaido.

anecdotes.text: Despite running one of Kanazawa's most sought-after sushi counters, Kibatani took a roughly three-month hiatus in summer 2022 to train in uni preparation in Hokkaido.
A signature course pairs Uozu crab with a rich uni sauce over rice, described as enveloping the crab's delicate sweetness in a French-style richness.
His signature kohada is cured with Edomae technique that he adjusts to the day's weather and the character of each fish.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Edomae sushi is built on vinegared rice, soy-based nikiri/tsume sauces, and soy sauce, so gluten is pervasive throughout the meal; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce (wheat-containing) and brushed nikiri/tsume sauces are integral to the sushi served here.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire omakase is seafood-based with fish-derived dashi (freshly shaved katsuobushi at the counter); there are no vegan dishes.

caveats: Address consensus from Tabelog and Hitosara is 金沢市彦三町1-8-26 1F (some travel write-ups loosely describe it as Kazuemachi, the adjacent geisha-district area). Seat count is reported as 8 on Tabelog and ~9 elsewhere; a small private room is also mentioned. Seating times appear fixed (around 17:00 and 20:30) and the restaurant is completely reservation-based with irregular closures, so confirm hours and booking directly via the official site.

lead: Natural Grocery Store midoriya（ミドリヤ）
name_ja: Natural Grocery Store midoriya（ミドリヤ）
cuisine: Vegan & gluten-free natural-foods cafe (rice-flour baked goods, herbal tea, coffee) and natural grocery shop
vegan_detail: The cafe's handmade baked goods (muffins, scones, pound cakes, florentines) and drinks are entirely plant-based, made without eggs, butter or dairy. The attached shop also stocks vegan-certified products. Sweets and house-made items are fully vegan.
gf_detail: Most baked goods are made with rice flour and are gluten-free; the owner makes them daily without wheat. Scones are the documented exception (not gluten-free), so confirm per item. Not a certified celiac-dedicated kitchen, but rice-flour baking is the core focus, so confidence is high for the muffins/pound cakes/florentines.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市安江町11-38 横安江町商店街内
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 安江町11-38, within the Kanazawa box. Location matches the 金澤表参道 (横安江町) shopping street just south of Omichō Market.
neighborhood: 安江町・横安江町（金澤表参道）商店街 — about 3 min south of Omichō Market (近江町市場)
seats_text: Small intimate eat-in cafe space with counter and antique table/chair seating, including window seats; exact seat count not documented. Takeout also available.
closed_days: Tuesdays plus irregular closures
cultural_comfort_note: A warm, small solo-run natural-foods cafe where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the rice-flour sweets will get you a friendly welcome; English service is not guaranteed.
caveats: Hours include irregular days off in addition to Tuesdays, so check the shop's Instagram before visiting. No parking (nearby coin lots). Scones are not gluten-free even though most baked goods are.

bio.background: midoriya is run by Takahashi Tomoko, a former high-school teacher who, after becoming a mother, reconsidered the importance of what we eat and decided to open a natural-foods shop. It first opened in November 2020 on the second floor of her family's longstanding boutique. When the boutique closed, she moved the shop down to the ground floor and reopened in May 2023 with a larger sales floor and a new cafe space.
bio.specialty: House-made vegan, rice-flour (gluten-free) baked goods — muffins, pound cakes, scones and florentines — plus herbal teas, Japanese black tea and coffee.
bio.philosophy: She personally curates every product from a parent's point of view, choosing additive-free, organic and vegan-certified items that are genuinely good for body, mind and the environment. The cafe extends that ethic with reusable-tumbler discounts and sustainable sourcing.

anecdotes.text: The cafe occupies the family's former boutique building (described as roughly 90 years old), and the renovation kept the antique furniture and vintage fabric upholstery from the boutique era, giving the eat-in space a retro charm.
A signature item is a rice-flour muffin made with pesticide-free organic lemon and a vegan custard, served alongside 100%-natural herbal tea.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Baked goods are made fresh in an open kitchen; most are rice-flour gluten-free, but scones are not gluten-free, so wheat is present in the same kitchen. No celiac-grade cross-contamination protocol is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All house-made baked goods and drinks are stated to contain no animal-derived ingredients (no eggs, butter or dairy), so vegan cross-contact risk in the cafe items is low.

positives.text: Sweets are made with rice flour and are vegan, with gluten-free options clearly the focus of the menu.
Egg-free and dairy-free options are available, with explicit vegan and gluten-free framing throughout the menu.

lead: 主計町お料理 いち凛
name_ja: 主計町お料理 いち凛
cuisine: Kanazawa Japanese seasonal cuisine / kaiseki-style courses (kappo)
neighborhood: Kazuemachi (主計町茶屋街), Asano River side
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering documented. This is a traditional Japanese seasonal/kaiseki house built around dashi and soy sauce, with courses centered on seafood and Kaga vegetables. Soy sauce (typically wheat-based) and wheat-containing seasonings are integral to the cooking, so it is not celiac-safe. No dedicated fryer or allergen protocol is published; a celiac traveler would need to inquire directly in advance.
vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented. The kitchen is "particular about fish" and courses are built on seasonal seafood plus fish/kombu dashi, so standard courses are not vegan or vegetarian. The restaurant features Kaga vegetables and local sake, so a plant-forward request might be accommodated for a reserved course, but this is not confirmed and must be arranged with the restaurant ahead of time.
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned [136.663773, 36.572186] for 石川県金沢市主計町2-6; within the Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 26 seats per Tabelog (counter, table seating and a private room available, roughly 2-20 persons). The official site lists 31 seats including a 6-person private room; either way it is a small house with private-room options.
hours_raw: [Tue] 17:00-22:30 (dinner only); [Wed-Fri] 11:30-13:30, 17:00-22:30; [Sat/Sun/holidays] 11:00-14:30, 17:00-22:30; [Mon] closed (open if a public holiday).
closed_days: Mondays (open if Monday is a public holiday); no lunch on Tuesday.
cultural_comfort_note: A polished course-style Japanese restaurant where a reservation and a few words of Japanese (or a translation app) go a long way; staff are used to guests but the experience is formal, so book ahead and flag any dietary needs when you reserve.
caveats: Operated by the local MOMIJI GROUP (not a solo-run independent), so it is categorized OMNI rather than MOM_AND_POP. Note there is an unrelated restaurant of the same name "Ichirin" in the Katamachi area. Dinner courses run roughly 10,000-15,000 yen; lunch is more accessible (a Hanakago lunch and courses from around 1,500-4,000 yen per diner reports). Dinner is limited to 3 hours and cancellation fees apply (30% within three days, 100% same day).

bio.background: Ichirin occupies a renovated Kanazawa machiya on the Asano River in the historic Kazuemachi tea district, a townhouse that was the later-life residence of the American printmaker Clifton Karhu. The interior still displays pieces from his collection, so diners eat surrounded by his artwork while taking in the Edo-period atmosphere of the riverside teahouse quarter.
bio.specialty: Seasonal multi-course Japanese cuisine built on local seafood and Kaga vegetables, paired with local Ishikawa sake; the kitchen is noted for being particular about its fish. Lunch sets such as the Hanakago box make the cuisine approachable at midday.
bio.philosophy: An emphasis on Kanazawa's seasonal local ingredients and regional sake, served in a setting that preserves the heritage of a Kazuemachi townhouse.
roles: part of the local MOMIJI GROUP

anecdotes.text: The restaurant is set in the former home of American woodblock printmaker Clifton Karhu, who spent his final years in this Kazuemachi townhouse; his art is shown throughout the dining space, including a distinctive blue-plaster-walled Japanese room.
It is the riverside Kanazawa restaurant operated by the local MOMIJI GROUP, which also runs other Japanese and Western dining venues in the city, opened in this Kazuemachi location.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Traditional Japanese kaiseki-style kitchen relying on soy sauce and dashi-based seasonings; no gluten-free protocol or dedicated equipment is documented, so cross-contamination with wheat should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Cuisine is built around standard Japanese seasonings including soy sauce, which is typically brewed with wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is advertised.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Courses center on seafood and almost certainly use fish/kombu dashi; no separate vegan preparation line is documented, so animal-derived stocks should be assumed throughout.

positives.text: Reservations are required and arranged by phone or an online form, giving diners a clear channel to communicate dietary restrictions in advance.

cuisine: Family-run kappo serving Kaga local cuisine — duck jibuni, salt-grilled nodoguro, and seasonal fish and vegetables, in a Taisho-era riverside building
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. This is a Kaga kappo built around dashi, soy sauce and seasonal fish. The signature jibuni is served with sudare-fu (wheat gluten) and a soy-and-mirin broth, and dishes use wheat-based soy sauce throughout. No dedicated gluten-free preparation. The kitchen does say it accepts allergy/dietary inquiries by direct phone, so a celiac diner would need to call ahead and discuss in detail — but it should not be considered celiac-safe.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The menu is centered on fish (nodoguro, sashimi) and duck (jibuni), and nearly everything is built on bonito/fish dashi. Vegetables are seasonal but prepared with animal-based broths and soy seasonings. The kitchen accepts allergy/dietary inquiries by phone, but there is no documented vegan offering.
geocode_note: Geocoded from 石川県金沢市並木町1-10 (〒920-0928), confirmed within the Kanazawa box. Located on the Asano River embankment near Ume-no-hashi / Asanogawa-ohashi, across from the Higashi Chaya District. The official site shows a typo "Katamachi" in one render; postal data and Tabelog confirm Namikimachi (並木町).
neighborhood: Namikimachi, by the Asano River near Higashi Chaya / Kazuemachi
seats_text: About 40 seats total: 1F counter (10) plus a few tables and a small tatami room; 2F tatami rooms with river views seating groups (up to ~40-50 for parties). Solo diners welcome at the counter.
hours_raw: 17:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30); some listings give 17:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:00). Closed Sundays and holidays (open for groups of 10+ by reservation).
closed_days: Sundays and public holidays (will open for reservations of 10 or more guests)
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional family-run riverside kappo where some Japanese helps; reservations are taken by phone and the kitchen prefers direct contact, so calling ahead (ideally in Japanese) and being ready to discuss dishes makes for a smoother visit.
caveats: Listed hours vary slightly between sources (closing 22:00 vs 23:00); confirm at booking. Official site shows a typo'd district name ("Katamachi") that conflicts with the verified Namikimachi address.

bio.background: Uotsune is a family-run kappo set in a Taisho-era building on the embankment of the Asano River, just across from the Higashi Chaya District and near the Ume-no-hashi and Asanogawa-ohashi bridges. The weathered old structure is part of the draw: second-floor tatami rooms look out over the river, framing cherry blossoms in spring and colored leaves in autumn.
bio.specialty: Kaga local cuisine led by duck jibuni — duck simmered with sudare-fu wheat gluten and seasonal vegetables in a soy-and-mirin broth — alongside salt-grilled nodoguro (blackthroat seaperch), sashimi, and seasonal fish and vegetables.
bio.philosophy: The house has long run on a simple motto carried down from the previous owner: 'oishikute yasui' — delicious and affordable — pairing seasonal Kaga ingredients with the changing views of the Asano River.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant sits in a building that has kept its Taisho-era character, with upstairs river-view rooms that change with the four seasons of the Asano River.
The kitchen states it does not use automated reservation systems and prefers direct contact, and welcomes inquiries about allergies and dietary needs.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Signature jibuni is made with sudare-fu (wheat gluten) and a soy-sauce-based broth, and the kitchen relies on wheat-based soy sauce and dashi across the menu; not celiac-safe.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Kaga kappo cooking here uses standard wheat-based soy sauce in jibuni and seasonings throughout.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are built on fish/bonito dashi and feature duck and seafood; vegetable items are not prepared vegan.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The official site states the restaurant accepts inquiries about allergies and dietary restrictions and prefers direct phone contact over automated systems.

positives.text: Kitchen is open to allergy/dietary questions when contacted directly by phone before the visit.

lead: 蕎麦処 杏庵（あんあん）
cuisine: Hand-made soba (te-uchi), with duck seiro and creative wa-yo soba dishes
neighborhood: Musashi-machi, near Omicho Market
address_ja: 石川県金沢市武蔵町3-3 レジデンス武蔵 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded to 石川県金沢市武蔵町3-3 via GSI; within Kanazawa box.
seats_text: Small space with counter and table seats; counter seating makes it comfortable for solo diners. Exact seat count not documented.
closed_days: Sundays (regular closing day per the shop's own announcement and local guides; one listing also indicated Tuesday closures, so confirm before a weekday visit)
cultural_comfort_note: A warm, solo-female-owner counter shop where dining alone is easy and welcome; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will go a long way, as English is unlikely.
gf_detail: This is a hand-made soba shop. The noodles are made with Hokkaido buckwheat flour but, as with virtually all Japanese soba, are not certified gluten-free and almost always contain wheat flour binder; broths and dipping sauces are soy-sauce based (wheat-containing). Not suitable for celiacs. No gluten-free accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Menu centers on duck (Zao duck seiro), egg yolk, Parmigiano-Reggiano cheese, and seafood/vegetable tempura, with dashi- and soy-sauce-based broths. No vegan offerings are documented.
caveats: Closed-day reporting differs slightly across listings (Sunday per the shop's own opening announcement and most local guides; one source listed Monday/Tuesday closures). Confirm via Instagram before visiting. Exact seat count not documented.

bio.origin: Opened March 1, 2022 in Kanazawa's Musashi-machi, a short walk from Omicho Market, on the site of a former soba shop (元たんば屋).
bio.background: A casual hand-made soba house run by a welcoming female proprietor. The noodles are hand-cut daily using buckwheat flour from the Isoya region of Hokkaido, prized for its aroma and smooth, easy-to-eat texture, and the kitchen leans on local Ishikawa vegetables and seasonal ingredients.
bio.specialty: The signature dish is tsuke-kamo seiro (duck seiro, ~1,800 yen): freshly hand-cut soba dipped in a warm, mellow, refined broth made with free-range Zao duck. Other standouts include a playful 'cheese soba' piled with Parmigiano-Reggiano and egg yolk (~1,300 yen) and bukkake oroshi soba with seasonal Ishikawa grated daikon (~1,000 yen).
bio.philosophy: The owner wants guests to enjoy the meal all the way through without tiring of it (最後まで飽きずに楽しんでほしい), which shapes the varied, lightly inventive menu.

anecdotes.text: The shop sits on the former site of the soba restaurant Tanbaya, behind the hotel in the Musashi area near Omicho Market.
Run solo by a kind female owner, the casual counter is described as especially comfortable for women dining alone.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Hand-made soba is made and cut in a flour-heavy kitchen; wheat-based binder and shared surfaces make cross-contamination effectively certain.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dipping broths and sauces are soy-sauce based, which standardly contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen handles duck, egg, cheese, and seafood/vegetable tempura; no vegan-segregated preparation is documented.

cuisine: Hand-made soba (buckwheat noodles); signature Kaga-bocha roasted-tea soba
neighborhood: 東山・ひがし茶屋街 (Higashiyama / Higashi Chaya District)
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Soba noodles here are made with sarashina (refined buckwheat) flour blended with wheat and roasted-tea powder, and soba is typically served with a wheat-based soy-sauce dashi tsuyu. No gluten-free menu or precautions are documented. Not safe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. Soba is traditionally served with a katsuo/dashi-based dipping sauce and may include egg or wheat in the noodle binding. A plain tea-soba or vegetable item might be possible but would need to be confirmed in person; assume animal-based dashi by default.
geocode_note: GSI address search returned [136.66658, 36.572369] for 石川県金沢市東山一丁目15番5号; within the Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 27 seats, including an open counter for solo diners
hours_raw: Daytime soba cafe (武右衛門): 11:30-16:00. Evening soba bar (音ゆ): 18:30-25:00, closed Sun & Mon (open Sun if Mon is a holiday).
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular holidays) for the daytime soba cafe; the evening soba bar is closed Sundays and Mondays.
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, modern soba cafe in a renovated townhouse where a friendly greeting and pointing at the photo menu will get you by, though staff may have limited English.
caveats: Hours differ between sources: Tabelog lists daytime service to roughly 19:30 on some days, while the official site frames it as a daytime soba cafe (to 16:00) and a separate late-night soba bar (音ゆ, to 25:00). Confirm same-day, especially for evening visits. Closures are irregular.

bio.background: Buemon occupies a renovated townhouse in a back alley of Kanazawa's Higashi Chaya geisha district. The building was once the private residence of the proprietor of 'Toyu' (東湯), a public bathhouse that the district's geiko once frequented. It has been modernized while keeping the chic character of the chaya district, with a high ceiling and exposed beams, an open counter for solo diners, and a courtyard garden visible through large glass windows.
bio.specialty: Hand-made soba, with the signature 'Kaga-bocha soba' (加賀棒茶そば, around 880 yen) made by kneading powdered Kaga roasted tea into fine sarashina-flour noodles. By day it is a soba cafe; in the evening it runs as a soba bar (音ゆ).
bio.philosophy: A focus on hometown ingredients and Kanazawa's food culture, expressed through the marriage of soba with local Kaga roasted tea.

anecdotes.text: The soba chef is a son of the owner of the 'Toyu' bathhouse; he was involved in launching several soba shops in Tokyo before returning home to open Buemon in the former bathhouse-owner's residence.

The signature Kaga-bocha soba was born from trial and error: the chef gathered roasted teas from across Kanazawa and repeatedly experimented before arriving at this dish, which he calls something you can only taste here.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a soba (buckwheat) shop whose noodles are blended with sarashina flour and roasted-tea powder; wheat is standard in soba dough and in the dipping sauce. No gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soba is served with a traditional soy-sauce-and-dashi tsuyu, which is normally wheat-based; no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Traditional soba tsuyu is based on katsuobushi/animal dashi; no vegan dashi or menu is documented.

lead: 大正浪漫喫茶 金魚庵
name_ja: 大正浪漫喫茶 金魚庵
cuisine: Retro Taisho-era kissaten (Japanese cafe); pour-over coffee, goldfish-bowl cream soda, homemade pudding and classic Western-Japanese sweets
neighborhood: 東山・ひがし茶屋街 / Higashi Chaya District
gf_detail: This is a classic kissaten built around hot cakes, homemade pudding and other flour-, egg- and dairy-based sweets, with no gluten-free menu or allergy program documented. The signature white hot cakes are wheat pancakes. Not suitable for celiac diners; there is no dedicated gluten-free preparation.
vegan_detail: The menu centers on egg- and dairy-rich sweets (custard pudding with strong egg flavor, hot cakes with honey and butter, cream sodas with ice cream). No vegan options are documented. Some drinks such as plain coffee or the jelly/fruit punch may be incidentally plant-based, but you would need to ask; this is not a vegan-friendly destination.
geocode_note: GSI address search returned a precise point for 石川県金沢市東山一丁目10番4号, within the Kanazawa bounding box.
seats_text: 23 seats: five 2-person tables, one 4-person table, two 5-person tables.
hours_raw: 11:00-17:00 (L.O. 16:30); food service until around 14:00. Closed on the 2nd and 4th Wednesdays of the month.
closed_days: 2nd and 4th Wednesdays of the month (even-numbered Wednesdays)
cultural_comfort_note: A welcoming themed cafe popular with tourists where a smile and a few words of Japanese go a long way; staff are used to visitors but English menus or fluent English service are not guaranteed.
caveats: Closed on the 2nd and 4th Wednesdays, so check the calendar before visiting. Reservations are only taken for groups of 4 or more, and the goldfish-bowl soda is a summer-only (roughly June-September) item. Food (savory) service tends to end around mid-afternoon while drinks and sweets continue until closing.

bio.background: Kingyoan opened in September 2022 inside a renovated Kanazawa machiya more than a century old, tucked just off the main lane of the Higashi Chaya District. The whole cafe is built as a 'time machine' back to Japan's Taisho era (1912-1926), when Western culture was first taking root: original red carpets, a working phonograph, period refrigerator, stained glass and antique telephones fill the rooms, and staff serve in kimono with frilly aprons.
bio.origin: The name 金魚庵 (Kingyoan, 'goldfish retreat') is drawn from the goldfish that appears in Kanazawa-born novelist Muro Saisei's fairy-tale work, and goldfish motifs are hidden throughout the interior like a treasure hunt; hanging goldfish lamps mark the vermillion lattice entrance.
bio.specialty: Signature items include cream soda served in custom goldfish-bowl glasses in red, indigo and blue; the summer-only Kingyoan goldfish-bowl soda whose decorations mimic water plants and bubbles; Haikara Punch, a colorful jelly-and-fruit soda with marble-like gel spheres; a dense old-fashioned homemade pudding with strong egg flavor and bitter caramel; and pale 'white' hot cakes recreated from Taisho-era recipes using honey and jam instead of sugar.
bio.philosophy: The cafe recreates Meiji- and Taisho-era recipes and decor to let guests step back in time and experience the romance of early-modern Japan.

anecdotes.text: Cream sodas come in three colors served in goldfish-bowl-shaped glasses, and the summer-limited goldfish soda is designed so that, viewed from above, the bubbles and grass-like decorations look like a real goldfish bowl, with chocolate 'pebbles' at the bottom.
The building is a 100-plus-year-old machiya preserved with its Taisho-era furnishings, and the cafe doubles as a photography studio that can be rented after hours and on closed days.

cuisine: Local izakaya / Kaga cuisine — day-boat Sea-of-Japan seafood, Kaga vegetables, local sake
neighborhood: Kakinokibatake / Katamachi, near the 21st Century Museum (Kōrinbō)
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates within the Kanazawa box for 石川県金沢市柿木畠3-8.
seats_text: 42 seats (17 counter, 25 tatami/zashiki)
hours_raw: 17:30–23:30 (L.O. 23:00); closed Sundays (and the following Monday when Sunday is a national holiday)
closed_days: Sundays (and the Monday after a Sunday national holiday)
cultural_comfort_note: A bustling, hugely popular local izakaya where a few words of Japanese and a reservation go a long way; counter seats let you watch the chefs, but service is fast-paced and not heavily English-oriented.
gf_detail: This is a classic Japanese izakaya built around soy sauce, miso, and bonito/kombu dashi, with sashimi served with wheat-based soy sauce and many fried items (tempura, fried white shrimp). There is no documented gluten-free menu or allergen handling, so it is not safe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: The kitchen is centered on Sea-of-Japan seafood (sashimi platters, nodoguro, crab, yellowtail) with dashi and seafood used throughout. While some Kaga-vegetable dishes exist, they are typically prepared with dashi or other animal products, and there is no documented vegan menu.

bio.background: Founded in 1988 in the Kakinokibatake quarter near Kanazawa's Katamachi district, Itaru grew from a neighborhood spot into what locals call one of Kanazawa's defining izakaya, especially after the Hokuriku Shinkansen reached the city in 2015 brought a wave of visitors alongside its loyal regulars.
bio.specialty: The signature is the kioke-mori otsukuri — a generous sashimi platter heaped in a wooden barrel with bold seasonal cuts of Sea-of-Japan fish. Other standouts include aji no namero (minced horse mackerel), salt-grilled nodoguro (blackthroat seaperch), and shime-saba. Winter brings yopparai-gani (sake-marinated kobako crab) and kan-buri (cold-season yellowtail).
bio.philosophy: The restaurant builds its menu around top local ingredients — day-boat fish from Toyama's Shinminato and Noto, and Kaga vegetables such as Gorojima-kintoki sweet potato, kinjiso red amaranth, Kaga futo-kyuri cucumber, and Noto taro — with chefs cooking in full view of the counter so guests can watch the craft.

anecdotes.text: Itaru pours Tengu-mai sake drawn straight from a brewery mini-tank without bottling, a kura-dashi pour the shop describes as available nowhere else.
The counter is set up as a live-cooking stage where the head chef works alongside apprentices in front of diners.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu relies on soy sauce, miso, and dashi with fried items (tempura, fried white shrimp) sharing the kitchen; no gluten-free accommodation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sashimi and izakaya dishes are served with standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Seafood and dashi are central to nearly every dish; vegetable preparations are typically dashi-based.

caveats: Official site (http://www.itaru.ne.jp/) returned a TLS certificate mismatch on fetch; details corroborated via Tabelog and independent write-ups. No allergen/dietary accommodation is documented either way.

lead: 加登長 横安江町店
name_ja: 加登長 横安江町店
cuisine: そば・うどん (Soba & udon shokudō — old-style Shōwa noodle/set-meal diner)
neighborhood: 横安江町商店街・安江町 (Yokoanjemachi shopping street, between Kanazawa Station and Ōmichō Market)
geocode_note: GSI address search returned [136.655457, 36.574226] for 石川県金沢市安江町13番30号; within Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 47 seats (one 6-person table, nine 4-person tables, two 2-person tables, one counter seat); a small neighborhood diner. (Another listing states 46.)
hours_raw: 11:00–15:00; closed Tuesdays
closed_days: Tuesdays (火曜日)
cultural_comfort_note: A no-frills local lunch spot with no English menu or English-speaking staff expected, so a foreign student is best off going with a guide or someone who reads Japanese.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core menu is wheat udon and soba (buckwheat is typically blended with wheat flour) served in a soy-and-dashi broth, plus fried dishes (tempura, katsu). There is no indication of gluten-free options or any allergen handling, so this is not safe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. Broths are made with fish/bonito dashi, and signature plates include saba (mackerel) miso, tempura rice bowls, ramen, and katsu. No documented vegan options.

bio.background: An old-school Kanazawa soba and udon shokudō tucked into the Yokoanjemachi shopping street, on the walking route between Kanazawa Station and Ōmichō Market. It has the calm, lived-in feel of a Shōwa-era neighborhood diner, serving home-style set meals at lunchtime prices that locals have long relied on.
bio.specialty: Sweet-broth udon and soba alongside hearty teishoku (set meals) — diners single out the saba miso-ni teishoku (mackerel simmered in miso), and the menu also features hayashi katsu rice, tempura rice bowls (ten-toji-don), ramen, and nabe-udon.
bio.philosophy: Warm, home-style cooking in an unhurried, settled atmosphere.

anecdotes.text: Described as a 'warmhearted set-meal restaurant' offering traditional Kanazawa flavors and home-style, comforting food in a calm atmosphere, with the saba miso-ni teishoku singled out for its firm seasoning and never-tiring deliciousness.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat udon and soba share kitchen and likely boiling water; fried items (tempura, katsu) use wheat batter/breading. No allergen separation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy-sauce-and-dashi broths are used throughout; conventional soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths rely on fish/bonito dashi and the menu centers on fish, egg, and meat dishes; no vegan preparation is indicated.

caveats: Cash only; lunch-only (11:00–15:00), closed Tuesdays. No official website found. Seat count reported as 47 on one listing and 46 on another. No English menu or allergen accommodation documented.

cuisine: 海鮮丼・寿司 (Kaisendon / sushi)
neighborhood: 近江町市場 (Omicho Market), Kanazawa
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. This is a sushi and seafood-bowl counter built around vinegared rice served with house sushi soy sauce (blended on a Kanazawa Ohno shoyu base), plus typical accompaniments like tamagoyaki — all wheat-containing. There is no documented gluten-free menu, no dedicated preparation, and no allergy program. Not safe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The entire concept is fresh local seafood — sashimi bowls and nigiri. The signature dish is a 12-topping all-seafood kaisendon. No plant-based options are documented.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市下堤町19 近江町市場 Dエリア
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI from 石川県金沢市下堤町19; first feature coordinates [136.655502, 36.570988], within the Kanazawa box.
seats_text: Approximately 21 seats: about 7 at the counter plus around 14 at tables. Small market eatery.
hours_raw: 11:00～16:00 (approx.); 不定休 (irregular closures), some sources note Wednesday closed.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular); one source lists Wednesday closed. Confirm before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: A small, friendly market counter where pointing at the photo menu and a cheerful greeting will carry you through; little to no English, but the staff are noted as warm and attentive.
caveats: Hours are irregular (不定休) and closed days are inconsistently reported (one source notes Wednesday); seafood-only menu means it is unsuitable for vegans and not celiac-safe due to wheat-based soy sauce and vinegared rice.

bio.background: The owner comes from an established sushi-restaurant background and personally selects the fish, bringing a trained sushi craftsman's eye to a casual market counter in the heart of Omicho Market.
bio.specialty: The signature is the kaisendon (around 2,480 yen) piled with twelve different all-seafood toppings, served over mild, lightly-tart vinegared rice meant to bring out the natural sweetness of the fish. Hand-pressed nigiri of seasonal local fish and the owner's own tamagoyaki round out the menu.
bio.philosophy: A committed local-production, local-consumption ethos: Oku-Noto Koshihikari rice cooked with Hakusan spring water, blended with pure rice vinegar from the old Tsurugi town, and finished with a house sushi soy sauce built on Kanazawa Ohno shoyu — rice, vinegar, water and soy sauce all sourced locally for a balanced bite.
