anecdotes.text: The shop leans into a homey, welcoming feel and has built a loyal following of both tourists and locals who return for the generously heaped seafood bowls.
Every element of the rice is deliberately local — Oku-Noto Koshihikari cooked with Hakusan sacred spring water, Tsurugi-town pure rice vinegar, and a Kanazawa Ohno shoyu-based sushi soy.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: House sushi soy sauce is blended on a Kanazawa Ohno shoyu base; conventional Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

cuisine: Kanazawa oden (金沢おでん), obanzai small plates, and sake
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Oden is simmered in a soy-sauce-and-dashi broth, and many ingredients (fish cakes/hanpen, chikuwa, ganmodoki, fried tofu) contain or are bound with wheat. The signature dishes lean on soy sauce and fish dashi. No gluten-free menu or allowance is documented, and the kitchen is not celiac-safe.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The broth is a fish/seafood dashi, signature items include bai-gai (whelk) simmered whole in the stock and local fish dishes, and most oden ingredients sit in the same animal-based pot. No vegan menu or preparation is documented.
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 石川県金沢市香林坊2-4-21; within the Kanazawa box.
neighborhood: 香林坊・木倉町 (Kōrinbō / Kikuramachi, central Kanazawa)
seats_text: 13 counter seats plus 2 tables (around 4 seats); roughly 17 seats total. Small, counter-focused oden shop.
hours_raw: 17:30-21:30. Closed Sundays; holidays are an irregular closure.
closed_days: Sundays (holidays irregular / 不定休)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, locally loved counter oden shop where some basic Japanese and pointing at the simmering pot will help; an English menu is not documented, so come open to a little adventure.
caveats: Budget is roughly 4,000-4,999 yen per person for dinner. Tiny counter shop, so seating is limited; cards are accepted but electronic money and QR payment are not.

bio.chef_name: Yoshie Koshida (越田良枝) and her son Kōta (皓太)
bio.background: Yoshibou is a long-running Kanazawa oden shop in the Kōrinbō district. It first opened in 1957, and after the previous owner passed away the shop went quiet for a time before the family revived it; today it is carried on by proprietress Yoshie Koshida together with her son Kōta, who together set a warm, welcoming counter atmosphere favored by local businesspeople and people on work assignment in the city.
bio.specialty: Kanazawa-style oden simmered in a clear golden dashi drawn from local ingredients. A signature is bai-gai (whelk) simmered whole in the broth and shucked to order, prized for its briny aroma; the shop is also known for obanzai side dishes and a sake list chosen to match the food.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen measures the salinity of the broth daily and adjusts the seasoning to the weather and temperature, aiming for a stock with lingering, deep flavor. The cooking avoids chemical seasonings in favor of a gentle, ingredient-forward taste, and the shop is a member of the Kanazawa Oden veteran shops association.

anecdotes.text: The shop opened in 1957 and was revived by the founding family's son after the previous owner passed away, returning Yoshibou's oden to its longtime Kōrinbō spot.
Proprietress Yoshie Koshida and her son Kōta run the counter together, creating the warm atmosphere the shop is known for.

The golden dashi is drawn from local ingredients, and bai-gai (whelk) is simmered whole in the broth then shucked just before serving; salinity is measured daily and the seasoning tuned to the day's climate.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Oden is cooked communally in a single soy-sauce-and-dashi pot containing wheat-based items such as fish cakes, chikuwa and fried tofu, so heavy cross-contact with gluten is inherent to the dish.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a soy-sauce-based dashi; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, so the broth and most simmered items are not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The shared simmering broth is a fish/seafood dashi and includes seafood such as whole bai-gai (whelk), so vegan separation is not possible.

positives.text: Cooking avoids chemical seasonings, with broth salinity measured daily and seasoning adjusted to the day's conditions for a gentle, ingredient-forward taste.

lead: ターバンカレー 本店
name_ja: ターバンカレー 本店
cuisine: 金沢カレー (Kanazawa curry)
neighborhood: 広坂・香林坊 (Hirosaka / Kōrinbō)
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Kanazawa curry is built on a wheat-flour roux, and the signature toppings are breaded, deep-fried pork cutlet (rōsu katsu) and croquettes — all wheat-coated. The sauce is also flavored heavily with Worcestershire-style sauce, which typically contains wheat. There is no gluten-free menu and no indication of allergen accommodation. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The curry is a meat-based Kanazawa-style sauce (pork/beef stock) and the menu centers on pork cutlet, sausage, hamburg steak and croquettes. There are no documented vegan or vegetarian dishes; the only vegetable-forward item (a seasonal Kaga renkon / Gorojima sweet-potato tempura curry) still sits on the same non-vegan meat-based curry sauce.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市広坂1-1-48 ウナシンビル 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address 石川県金沢市広坂1-1-48 via GSI; coordinates fall within the central Kanazawa box. Note the shop is in Hirosaka near Kōrinbō and the 21st Century Museum, slightly different from the listed Nomachi area but still central Kanazawa.
seats_text: 16 seats (10 counter seats, plus three two-person tables)
closed_days: Open daily; closed only for year-end/New Year holidays (Dec 30 - Jan 1)
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, fast-paced counter curry shop where you can point at photos on the menu, pay cash, and eat quickly — friendly to a foreign student with just a few words of Japanese, though English support is limited.

bio.background: Turban Curry traces back to 1971 (Shōwa 46), when the original Curry Shop Turban opened in Kanazawa's Katamachi district — a co-venture between former banker Takashi Okada, who fell in love with the curry made by Yoshikazu Tanaka, a Western-style restaurateur. It is one of the founding shops of the now-famous 'Kanazawa curry' style.
bio.specialty: Thick, dark, glossy Kanazawa-style curry ladled over rice, crowned with a crisp breaded pork cutlet (rōsu katsu) and a heap of shredded cabbage, eaten with a fork or spoon off a stainless plate. The famous 'L Set' piles on extra toppings — two sausages and a hamburg steak alongside the katsu. House-made cream croquette curry is another favorite, and seasonal vegetable curries appear from autumn through spring.
bio.philosophy: A rich, Worcestershire-tinged sauce built from a long-simmered roux and more than twenty spices — modest in quantity but intense and punchy, made to be eaten with rice rather than poured over it.

anecdotes.text: Turban is considered a cornerstone of Kanazawa curry's lineage — the founder of the GoGo Curry chain, Hirokazu Miyamori, trained at Turban in his youth before launching his own restaurant.
The signature 'L Set' tops the katsu curry with two sausages and a hamburg steak, the kind of over-the-top plate that locals have loved for decades near the 21st Century Museum.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen deep-fries breaded pork cutlets and croquettes (wheat coating) and uses a wheat-flour roux, so the entire curry line involves gluten; no separation for gluten-sensitive diners is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The curry sauce is heavily flavored with Worcestershire-style sauce, which conventionally contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The base curry sauce is meat-based (pork/beef stock) and the menu is centered on katsu, sausage, hamburg steak and croquettes, so no vegan-safe preparation is available.

caveats: Address is in Hirosaka (near Kōrinbō and the 21st Century Museum), not Nomachi as the target hint stated — but still central Kanazawa. The business has been acquired via M&A by the GoGo Curry Group, so it is no longer an independent operation despite its long heritage. Cash only; no reservations; small 16-seat counter shop that often has a queue.

cuisine: 寿司 (Sushi)
neighborhood: 近江町市場 十間町口 (Omichō Market, Jukkenmachi exit)
address_ja: 石川県金沢市西町藪ノ内通31
geocode_note: GSI address search matched 石川県金沢市西町藪ノ内通31番地 exactly; coordinates within Kanazawa box.
seats_text: Counter seating only, around 10 seats.
hours_raw: Mon-Tue, Thu-Sat: 11:00-13:30 and 17:00-19:00; Sun: 11:00-13:30 (lunch only, no dinner); Wed: closed. (Some listings show slightly later dinner end around 19:30.)
closed_days: Wednesday (Sunday is lunch only)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny traditional counter sushi shop run by an older master and front-of-house hostess; little to no English is likely, so basic Japanese or a translation app and a phone reservation will make your visit smooth.
gf_detail: This is a traditional sushi counter where soy sauce (typically wheat-containing) is central and dishes rely on standard sushi seasonings; there is no indication of gluten-free accommodation. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: A seafood-focused sushi house built around Hokuriku fish (nodoguro, white shrimp, anago). There is no documented vegan offering; even rice and broths likely involve fish-based dashi. Not suitable for vegans.

bio.background: A small, tucked-away counter sushi shop just outside Omichō Market at the Jukkenmachi exit, where the master forms each piece by hand at the counter while the front-of-house hostess attends to guests in a calm, unhurried room.
bio.specialty: Hand-formed nigiri showcasing Hokuriku seafood — seared nodoguro (rosy seabass), white shrimp (shiroebi), sweet shrimp, anago and seasonal local fish. The popular 'Ume' omakase course pairs about a dozen pieces of nigiri with a roll and soup.
bio.philosophy: Sushi made to the diner's pace: the master shapes the pieces as you eat, serving roughly two at a time so each arrives fresh from his hands.
roles: Itamae (master sushi chef) at the counter
Okami-san (hostess) handling the front of house

anecdotes.text: The master hand-forms each piece with precise timing at the counter while the hostess carefully presents the dishes, giving the room a calm, personal feel.
The 'Ume' omakase course of about twelve nigiri plus a roll and soup is praised as outstanding value, with seared nodoguro and white shrimp as highlights.
Sushi is served in pairs, paced to how quickly the diner eats, so each piece is freshly made.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a traditional sushi counter, soy sauce (typically brewed with wheat) is used throughout; no gluten-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is built entirely around fish and shellfish (nodoguro, white shrimp, anago); broths and rice seasoning likely include fish-based ingredients.

caveats: Dinner end time varies slightly across listings (19:00 on Tabelog, ~19:30 elsewhere); confirm by phone. Chef's name and personal background are not documented in available sources. Reservation by phone strongly recommended given the small size.

cuisine: Handmade soba (buckwheat noodles)
neighborhood: Musashi-machi (near Omichō Market)
address_ja: 石川県金沢市武蔵町3-3 レジデンス武蔵1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address 石川県金沢市武蔵町3-3 via GSI; within Kanazawa bounds.
seats_text: Small shop with counter and table seating; no exact count published, but a tiny intimate space comfortable for solo diners.
hours_raw: Wed-Sat 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:30), 17:00-22:00 (food L.O. 21:00); Sun 11:30-15:00. Closed Mon & Tue. (Sources vary slightly on closed days; Tabelog lists Mon/Tue closed, some listings say closed Mondays only.)
closed_days: Mondays and Tuesdays
cultural_comfort_note: A small, friendly female-run soba shop with a casual feel where even solo women feel at ease; likely Japanese-only, so a translation app or pointing at the menu helps.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. Soba here is handmade and Japanese soba is typically blended with wheat flour; the kitchen also serves wheat-based tempura and soy/dashi-based broths (including duck dashi). No gluten-free accommodations are documented. Celiac travelers should avoid.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan-oriented shop. The signature dishes use duck (tsuke-kamo seiro) and fish-based dashi; standard soba tsuyu contains dashi. They emphasize local seasonal vegetables and vegetable tempura, so a vegetable-forward bite may exist, but broth and dashi content must be asked about. No confirmed vegan menu.

bio.background: Anan is a small handmade soba shop that opened on March 1, 2022, tucked down a quiet side street just a few minutes from Kanazawa's bustling Omichō Market in the Musashi-machi district. It is run by an approachable woman owner, and the space has a relaxed, unpretentious feel that makes it easy to drop in even on your own.
bio.specialty: The signature dish is tsuke-kamo seiro (around 1,800 yen): cool, springy hand-cut soba served with a warm dipping broth rich with duck stock made from Zaō-region duck. The buckwheat is milled into noodles fresh in-house, and lunch sets pair the seiro soba with a daily rice dish and small seasonal side plates. There is also a playful house specialty of cheese-topped soba blending Japanese and Western flavors.
bio.philosophy: The owner uses plenty of local vegetables and seasonal ingredients and pays close attention to detail so that diners stay interested and enjoy the meal all the way to the last bite.

anecdotes.text: The shop's soba is made with buckwheat flour from Isoya in Hokkaido and finished with touches like yuzu citrus and Japanese parsley.
A local food blogger highlights the shop's unusual 'cheesy soba' topped generously with Parmigiano cheese as a new-sensation Japanese-Western soba you can only try here.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Handmade soba in a wheat-using kitchen (tempura is on the menu); high risk of gluten cross-contact and soba itself is typically a wheat blend.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soba is served with dashi/soy-based tsuyu and a duck dashi broth, which standardly contain wheat-based soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths use duck stock and fish dashi; vegetable dishes are likely prepared in the same dashi-based kitchen.

positives.text: Uses abundant local vegetables and seasonal ingredients, and offers vegetable tempura, so plant-forward items exist on the menu.

caveats: Closed days differ slightly across sources (Tabelog lists Mon & Tue closed; some local listings say Mondays only). Exact seat count is not published. No documented GF or vegan accommodations; dietary travelers should ask directly.

cuisine: Kaga regional cuisine / izakaya (加賀料理・郷土料理)
neighborhood: Korinbo (香林坊)
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned chō-level coordinates for 金沢市香林坊 (136.654129, 36.563454); within the Kanazawa box.
seats_text: Approximately 40 seats, mostly tatami/zashiki seating with private rooms; the 2nd-floor座敷 seats up to 20 and can be partitioned into private rooms for parties of 8, while the 1st-floor座敷 overlooks an inner garden.
hours_raw: Mon–Sat & day before public holidays: Lunch 11:30–13:30 (food L.O. 13:30); Dinner 17:00–22:00 (food L.O. 21:00). Closed Sundays and public holidays.
closed_days: Sundays and public holidays
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional Kanazawa machiya izakaya where staff likely speak limited English, so a few basic Japanese phrases and pointing at the menu will help a foreign student feel at ease.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The house specialty jibuni (治部煮) is made by dusting duck/chicken in wheat flour and simmering it with Kanazawa fu (wheat gluten) in a wheat-soy-sauce-based broth, and the izakaya menu relies on soy sauce and dashi throughout. There is no indication of gluten-free accommodation, so it is not celiac-safe.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. This is a Kaga-cuisine izakaya built around fresh Hokuriku seafood, duck/chicken jibuni, and bonito/kombu dashi. No vegan menu or documented plant-based accommodation; vegans would have very little to eat.

bio.background: Korinbo Rakuya is a Kanazawa machiya (traditional townhouse) izakaya tucked into the central Korinbo district, where the weathered wooden architecture, tatami zashiki rooms and a serene inner garden give it the feel of an old Kanazawa merchant's home. It is one of the comparatively few local spots where you can order Kaga regional dishes individually rather than only as part of a set course.
bio.specialty: The signature dish is jibuni (治部煮), the classic Kaga stew of wheat-flour-dusted duck simmered with Kanazawa's distinctive sudare-fu (wheat gluten) and seasonal vegetables in a glossy thickened broth, finished with a sharp dab of wasabi. Rakuya is noted for serving jibuni as an affordable single à-la-carte plate, alongside seasonal Hokuriku seafood and Kaga vegetables paired with local sake.

anecdotes.text: The menu centers on Ishikawa seafood, Kaga vegetables and local sake served amid the atmosphere of a Kanazawa machiya townhouse, with a first-floor tatami room looking onto an inner garden.
Diners can enjoy jibuni featuring duck, Kanazawa fu, a distinctive broth and wasabi offered as an inexpensive single dish, a relatively rare way to sample the dish in the city.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The signature jibuni is made with wheat-flour-coated meat and wheat-gluten fu in a soy-sauce broth, so gluten is integral to the kitchen and the menu is not suitable for celiac diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Kaga cuisine such as jibuni is built on wheat-containing soy sauce and dashi; no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is centered on seafood, duck/chicken and bonito/kombu dashi, so plant-only dishes cannot be assumed free of animal-based broth.

caveats: Tabelog lists only dinner hours (17:00–22:00, closed Sun) and a 3.21 rating with 40 seats; the official site and Hotpepper listing confirm an additional lunch service 11:30–13:30 and closure on public holidays as well as Sundays, so the recorded weekday hours combine both. Coordinates are chō-level (金沢市香林坊) from the GSI geocoder, not a rooftop pinpoint. No documented owner/chef name or stated philosophy was found.

cuisine: 町家の甘味喫茶・カフェ（抹茶・パフェ・ぜんざい・かき氷・コーヒー）
gf_detail: This is a traditional sweets cafe whose menu centers on wheat- and dairy-based items: cheesecake, parfaits, Japanese confections (wagashi/上生菓子), and zenzai. There is no documented gluten-free menu, no dedicated preparation, and no allergen handling described. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Plant-based options are not advertised. Plain matcha, hojicha, and brewed coffee/tea are likely vegan as drinks, but the sweets (parfaits, cheesecake, wagashi, zenzai) typically contain dairy, eggs, or refined ingredients. Kakigori syrups may be acceptable but ice/sweets are not verified vegan. A vegan diner would need to ask in person and limit to plain beverages.
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to the building address in Higashiyama 1-chome, well within the central Kanazawa box.
neighborhood: 東山・ひがし茶屋街
seats_text: 40席（うちカウンター4席）。1階はカウンター席、2階はテーブル・ソファ席で、窓からひがし茶屋街の街並みが見える。
hours_raw: 10:00～その日による（閉店時間は日によって変わる）。定休日：水曜（不定休あり）。お一人様につき1品以上のご注文をお願いしている。
closed_days: 水曜（不定休あり）
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, tourist-friendly old-townhouse tea cafe where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the sweets menu will get you a warm welcome, but plan to order at least one item per person as the shop requests.
caveats: Closing time is not fixed — the shop closes "depending on the day," so go earlier in the afternoon to be safe. Kakigori (shaved ice) is a seasonal summer offering. One-item-per-person minimum order. Cash is the safest assumption.

bio.background: A small, independent tea-and-sweets cafe that has quietly operated for over a decade (around 13 years as of the mid-2020s), set inside a renovated traditional townhouse (machiya) right on the main street of Kanazawa's Higashi Chaya geisha district. The owners describe their aim simply: to keep the spirit of an old-fashioned kissaten (Japanese coffeehouse) alive, refining the craft day by day.
bio.origin: 金沢・東山（ひがし茶屋街）
bio.specialty: Stone-ground, cup-by-cup hand-brewed coffee and Viennese coffee; matcha and hojicha parfaits; cheesecake; zenzai (sweet red-bean soup); premium seasonal wagashi (上生菓子) with matcha; and, in summer, around eight kinds of Japanese-style kakigori (shaved ice) served with the ice and syrup separate so guests sweeten to taste.
bio.philosophy: Doing the simple things carefully — grinding and brewing each cup of coffee by hand, and preserving the calm, unhurried feel of a classic neighborhood coffeehouse rather than chasing trends.

anecdotes.text: The shop sums up its own ethos as 'in our 13th year, striving daily to be an old-fashioned coffeehouse.'
On the second floor, window seats look out over the historic Higashi Chaya streetscape, making it a favorite rest stop between sightseeing.
In summer the cafe serves about eight varieties of Japanese kakigori at around 900 yen, with the shaved ice and syrup brought separately so you can adjust the sweetness yourself.

positives.text: Kakigori is served with the ice and syrup separate, so guests can control sweetness and see what they are adding.

cuisine: Machiya (townhouse) café and traditional sweets house — coffee, Vietnamese Dalat coffee, and seasonal wagashi served in a 1913 former teahouse
neighborhood: 主計町茶屋街 (Kazuemachi chaya district), beside the Asano River; a quieter teahouse quarter adjacent to Higashi Chaya, roughly 1.5 km from Omicho Market
geocode_note: GSI address search on 石川県金沢市主計町2-3 returned [136.663544, 36.57233], within the Kanazawa box and consistent with the Kazuemachi riverside location.
seats_text: About 21 seats: 1F counter (3 seats), 2F tatami room with four 2-person tables and one 4-person table.
hours_raw: Listings vary: standard listing shows 10:00–17:00, closed Mon & Tue. A 2023 update reports a change (from Aug 2023) to weekends/holidays only — Sat & Sun 11:00–18:00, holidays 11:00–17:00. Irregular closures noted.
closed_days: Weekdays (Mon–Fri); open weekends and holidays per the most recent reports. Irregular closures occur — confirm before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny owner-run townhouse café where service is in Japanese with little English; lovely but best approached with a phrasebook or a Japanese-speaking friend, and patience for irregular opening days.
caveats: Opening days/hours are inconsistent across sources (standard listing 10–17 closed Mon/Tue vs. a 2023 report of weekends/holidays only); the official site was unreachable at verification, so confirm hours by phone (090-8097-4702) before visiting. No gluten-free or vegan accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu is advertised. The core offering is coffee and seasonal wagashi (Japanese sweets such as yokan, zenzai/sweet red-bean soup, wasanbon-sugar items); many wagashi are plant-based, but ingredients (e.g. dairy in some items, sweets served as a complimentary set you cannot choose) are not specified. A vegan visitor would need to ask in Japanese about each item.
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. The café serves wagashi and toast-style items with no celiac protocols documented; the complimentary sweets are chosen by the house. No dedicated equipment or allergen handling is described, so it is not suitable for someone needing strict gluten avoidance.

bio.background: Tsuchiya occupies a wooden townhouse built in 1913 (Taisho 2) in Kazuemachi, one of Kanazawa's historic teahouse districts, on the banks of the Asano River between the Asanogawa-ohashi and Nakano-bashi bridges. The building is a Kanazawa-registered traditional structure, recognizable by its deep red bengara-pigment walls. It has been converted into a small café that keeps the spirit of the old chaya intact.
bio.specialty: House 'Tsuchiya coffee' (about 500 yen) and Vietnamese Dalat coffee, each accompanied by seasonal wagashi; seasonal specialties include wasanbon iced coffee in summer and zenzai (sweet red-bean soup) in winter, served on Wajima lacquerware.
bio.philosophy: A quiet, gracious tea-time: guests are welcomed with complimentary tea and a seasonal sweet while their order is prepared, and the second-floor tatami room is dressed with seasonal hanging scrolls and alcove crafts reflecting Kanazawa's four seasons.

anecdotes.text: The large second-floor windows frame the cherry-lined Asano River so closely that blossoms feel within reach in spring, while in winter white migratory birds gather on the water below.
Reviewers single out the value: a modestly priced coffee comes with included sweets and a serene riverside view from the old tatami room.

positives.text: Coffee is paired with seasonal Japanese sweets like yokan and zenzai, several of which are traditionally plant-based, though the specific items are chosen by the house and not selectable.

lead: 金沢おでんとおばんざい酒場 さんさん
name_ja: 金沢おでんとおばんざい酒場 さんさん
name_en: Kanazawa Oden to Obanzai Sakaba Sansan
cuisine: 金沢おでん・おばんざい（小さな居酒屋）
neighborhood: 香林坊・片町
gf_detail: A Kanazawa oden and obanzai izakaya. The cuisine is built on dashi (typically fish/kelp/soy-based broth), soy sauce, and wheat-based ingredients — Kanazawa oden famously includes 車麩 (wheel-shaped wheat gluten) and many simmered items soaked in soy-flavored broth. There is no documented gluten-free menu, dedicated preparation, or allergen handling. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: This is a seafood-and-meat-forward izakaya specializing in Kanazawa oden, obanzai, karaage and mapo tofu, with dashi-based broths throughout. Obanzai (seasonal home-style vegetable side dishes) may include some plant-based items, but almost all are prepared with fish dashi and soy seasoning, so they are not reliably vegan. No documented vegan menu or accommodation; a vegan would need to ask and options would be minimal.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市片町1丁目3-9 3階
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市片町1丁目3-9 returned [136.653839, 36.560349], within the Kanazawa box and consistent with the Katamachi/Korinbo nightlife district.
seats_text: 25 seats (10 counter, 15 table); a small, intimate izakaya with counter seating welcoming solo diners.
hours_raw: Tabelog: Mon-Tue, Thu-Sun & holidays 20:00-03:00 (Food L.O. 02:00, Drinks L.O. 02:30); closed Wednesdays. Official site frames it as dinner from 18:00 with a late-night bar continuing afterward.
closed_days: Wednesdays (水曜定休)
cultural_comfort_note: This is a small, late-night local izakaya where you order in Japanese and there is no documented English support, so it is most comfortable if you can manage basic Japanese or come with a Japanese-speaking friend.
caveats: Hours differ between sources: Tabelog lists 20:00-03:00, while the official site describes a dinner service starting around 18:00 that continues into a late-night bar — the late-night character is consistent either way. Recently opened (around late 2024), so details may still shift.

bio.background: さんさん is a small, late-night oden-and-obanzai izakaya tucked on the third floor of a building in Katamachi, the heart of Kanazawa's Korinbo-Katamachi nightlife district. A relatively recent arrival to the scene, it sets out to offer 'local flavors and drinks enjoyed casually at a bar,' with counter seats designed so that solo guests can drop in comfortably.
bio.specialty: Kanazawa oden simmered in a regional broth (including local touches like 車麩 wheel-shaped wheat gluten), a daily-changing platter of obanzai home-style side dishes built around seasonal ingredients, plus crowd-pleasers like karaage and mapo tofu. By the late hours it shifts into bar mode, known for its tea-based shochu highballs alongside sake and cocktails.
bio.philosophy: A relaxed neighborhood spot pairing Kanazawa's home-cooking traditions with easygoing late-night drinking, welcoming everyone from solo diners to groups.
roles: 金沢おでん・おばんざいの居酒屋

anecdotes.text: The kitchen leans into Kanazawa oden's signature local ingredients and rotates its obanzai daily with the season, while the late-night counter is built around tea-infused shochu highballs that are something of a house specialty.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Kanazawa oden is built on soy-and-dashi broth and includes wheat-based items such as 車麩 (wheel-shaped wheat gluten); fried items like karaage are wheat-battered, so shared broth and fryer cross-contact should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The menu centers on oden and obanzai seasoned with soy sauce and dashi, which in Japan are virtually always wheat-containing; no wheat-free seasoning is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths and obanzai are typically made with fish dashi and the menu features karaage, mapo tofu and seafood, so vegetable dishes likely share animal-based broth and preparation surfaces.

cuisine: 湯葉・豆腐の創作懐石（和食）
gf_detail: This is a tofu/yuba kaiseki house built around soy products, dashi, and Japanese soy sauce (which normally contains wheat). There is no documented gluten-free menu, no dedicated fryer, and no stated allergen protocol. Not suitable for celiacs or those needing strict gluten avoidance.
vegan_detail: The official and listing pages note a vegan menu is available (ヴィーガンメニューあり), but the kaiseki courses are otherwise built on dashi and include wagyu and fish options, so a vegan meal must be requested in advance. Reservation is required and you should specify vegan when booking; confirm dashi (often bonito-based) is excluded.
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to lat 36.604511 / lng 136.620285, inside the Kanazawa box. Note: this is the Tomizu (戸水) port/Minato area in northwest Kanazawa, roughly 8 minutes by car from Kanazawa Station and about 2.4km from the nearest rail stop (Mitsuya) — it is NOT in central Kanazawa near the station or Omicho Market as the target brief suggested.
neighborhood: 戸水・みなとエリア（金沢市北西部・金沢港方面）
seats_text: 126 seats total (max party capacity around 48); private rooms, table and sunken (horigotatsu) seating, wheelchair accessible.
closed_days: No fixed weekly closing day (irregular holidays); closed during the year-end/New Year period.
cultural_comfort_note: A larger, somewhat formal kaiseki restaurant where reservations are required and a vegan course must be arranged in advance, so a foreign student would do best with a Japanese-speaking helper or a guide to book and explain dietary needs.
caveats: The target brief described this as near Kanazawa Station / ~1.3km from Omicho Market, but the actual location is in Tomizu in the northwest port area, about 8 minutes by car from the station and not within easy walking distance. It is also a branch of the national 梅の花 (Umenohana) tofu-kaiseki chain, not an independent local shop. Seat count differs between sources (official lists max capacity ~48; the listing shows 126 total seats); courses run roughly 5,000-8,000 yen and up.

bio.origin: A branch of the nationwide Umenohana group, a Japanese restaurant company specializing in tofu and yuba cuisine.
bio.background: Umenohana built its identity around yuba (tofu skin) and tofu as the heart of refined Japanese cooking, pairing these traditional ingredients with seasonal produce to create a modern creative kaiseki.
bio.specialty: Signature dishes include house-made tofu (made here from Hokkaido soybeans), tofu shumai, yudofu (hot pot tofu), deep-fried yuba, and yuba gratin, served as multi-course kaiseki.
bio.philosophy: To open new ground in Japanese cuisine using yuba and tofu as a base, combining them with the flavors of the four seasons.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant makes its own tofu in house from Hokkaido soybeans and frames its courses around yuba and tofu combined with seasonal ingredients to create a new style of creative kaiseki.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is centered on soy sauce, dashi, fried yuba and other items that typically contain or are prepared with wheat; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: A yuba/tofu kaiseki house that relies on Japanese soy sauce and seasoned broths, which normally contain wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Standard courses include wagyu beef and fish and dashi-based seasoning, so a vegan course must be requested in advance and dashi exclusion confirmed.

positives.text: A vegan menu is available on request, and reservations are required, giving the kitchen advance notice to prepare a plant-based course.

cuisine: Showa-retro kissaten / yoshoku (Western-style) cafe — omurice, gratin, parfaits, waffles, siphon coffee
neighborhood: Katamachi (near the Katamachi 1-chome intersection)
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 石川県金沢市片町二丁目7番6号, within the central Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 48 seats (counter 8, nine 4-person tables, two 2-person tables)
hours_raw: 11:30-20:30 (L.O. 20:00); closed Tuesday and Wednesday
closed_days: Tuesday and Wednesday (火曜日・水曜日)
cultural_comfort_note: A laid-back local retro cafe with a long, picture-friendly Western-food menu; staff likely speak limited English, so a translation app or pointing at the menu will make ordering easy for a foreign student.
gf_detail: This is a Western-style yoshoku cafe built around wheat-heavy dishes — gratin, curry, spaghetti, doria, waffles, and sauce-topped omurice — none of which are gluten-free, and there is no documented gluten-free handling. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: The menu centers on egg (omurice, omelet gratin), dairy (cream sauces, parfaits, ice cream, waffles), meat, and seafood (mentaiko, butter chicken). No vegan options are documented.

bio.background: Pino has anchored a corner of Katamachi, Kanazawa's busy entertainment district, for more than 40 years (founded around 1979). It began life as a UCC directly-operated coffee shop, and that heritage still shows: coffee is brewed one cup at a time using a now-rare siphon, producing a brew regulars describe as clean yet mellow. The room has barely changed since the Showa era — stained glass, brick walls, and warm 1970s styling have all been kept largely intact through Showa, Heisei, and Reiwa.
bio.specialty: Beautifully boat-shaped omurice topped with rich sauce (variations include mentaiko cream, butter chicken curry, and double-sauce), plus omelet gratin, parfaits piled with up to seven fruits, and build-your-own waffles with 20+ toppings. The popular 'yokubari' (greedy) set pairs a main with dessert and a drink.
bio.philosophy: The menu grew organically out of saying yes to regulars — the owner notes that 'responding to customers' requests, it just kept growing before I knew it.' Coffee is never batch-brewed; each cup is made to order.

anecdotes.text: Some customers have been coming since their high-school days and now visit with their grandchildren, making it a place that has served three generations of regulars.
Originally a UCC directly-operated coffee shop, Pino still brews each cup individually with a siphon for a clean, mellow flavor.

lead: サロン・ド・テ・カワムラ
name_ja: サロン・ド・テ・カワムラ
cuisine: Japanese sweets café (甘味処) — warabi mochi, kakigori shaved ice, amanatto parfaits
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. The menu centers on Japanese sweets; while items like warabi mochi (bracken starch) and amanatto (candied beans) can be naturally wheat-free, the café also serves wagashi and dessert items that may contain or be cross-contacted with wheat, and no gluten-free protocol is documented. A celiac visitor would need to ask carefully about each item.
vegan_detail: Several plant-based-leaning sweets exist: warabi mochi served with kinako (soybean flour) and kuromitsu (black sugar syrup), amanatto (candied beans), yokan, and bean-based jellies and parfait layers are typically dairy-free. However, parfaits and kakigori include ice cream and condensed milk, and some shiratama/dessert components may use dairy. Vegan items are available but not guaranteed; confirm dairy-free options when ordering.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市野町2丁目24-7 2F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to 野町二丁目24-7; coordinates fall within the Kanazawa box.
neighborhood: にし茶屋街（野町）
seats_text: 22 seats: two 4-person tables and seven 2-person tables
hours_raw: Weekdays (Mon–Sat) 10:00–17:30 (L.O. 17:00); Sundays & holidays 10:00–16:30 (L.O. 16:00). Entry ages 6 and up; one order minimum per person.
closed_days: 1st Tuesday of the month (except January, May, December); winter break 12/22–1/4
cultural_comfort_note: A serene Japanese tea salon where a few words of Japanese and patience help, since handmade desserts take time and English support is limited; note children under 6 are not admitted.
caveats: Children under 6 are not admitted, and a one-order-per-person minimum applies. Handmade items take time to prepare, so allow for a leisurely visit. No reservations are taken.

bio.background: Salon de The Kawamura is the second-floor tea salon above 甘納豆かわむら (Amanatto Kawamura), a specialty shop in Kanazawa's Nishi Chaya geisha district devoted to amanatto — candied azuki and other beans — along with yokan and jelly confections. The salon was created so visitors can sit and savor the same carefully sourced ingredients freshly made on the premises, with large windows looking out over the historic Nishi Chaya streetscape.
bio.specialty: Signature offerings include the Kawamura parfait built around the shop's amanatto with layers of matcha and Kaga bo-cha, 本黒わらび餅 (warabi mochi made with rare domestic black bracken starch, served with kuromitsu from Hatahama black sugar and kinako), and seasonal kakigori such as a hojicha shaved ice with house-made concentrated hojicha syrup, local tofu shiratama, and house red bean paste, plus a matcha version with condensed milk.
bio.philosophy: The salon is conceived as a quiet, serene space (静寂なる空間) emphasizing freshly made sweets from carefully selected ingredients, with the bean as the protagonist of each dish.

anecdotes.text: The salon admits guests aged 6 and older, asks for one order per person, and notes that its handmade sweets require preparation time — a deliberate framing of the experience as an unhurried, grown-up tea break.
The hojicha kakigori hides hojicha ice cream inside the shaved ice and is topped with daidai azuki beans and house-made red bean paste, with shiratama from a neighborhood tofu maker.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Parfaits and kakigori include ice cream and condensed milk, so shared scoops and prep surfaces could contact dairy even on otherwise plant-based bean and warabi items.

positives.text: Warabi mochi is served with kinako (soybean flour) and kuromitsu (black sugar syrup), and amanatto, yokan and bean jellies are typically dairy-free options for plant-based guests.

lead: 金沢和食 みんなのまなぶ
name_ja: 金沢和食 みんなのまなぶ
cuisine: 加賀・金沢の和食（地元食材を使った日本料理。刺身盛り合わせ、のどぐろ、牛すじ煮込みなど）
neighborhood: 片町・香林坊（柿木畠／犀川大橋近く）
address_ja: 石川県金沢市片町2-22-16 ケントレジャックビル1F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI from 石川県金沢市片町2-22-16; result [136.650604, 36.559132] falls within the central Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 19席（カウンター7席、座敷テーブル12席）。一部資料では最大約20名。
closed_days: 日曜日
cultural_comfort_note: This is a warm, no-frills local izakaya-style washoku counter where some basic Japanese will help you order and chat with the chef; an English menu is not advertised, so a translation app is handy.
gf_detail: A traditional Kanazawa washoku spot built around dashi, soy sauce, sake and fried/simmered dishes (e.g. crab cream croquette, beef tendon stew). No gluten-free menu or handling is documented, and wheat-based soy sauce is central to Japanese cooking here — not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: The menu centers on fresh fish (daily-sourced sashimi, nodoguro), seafood, and meat dishes, with dashi-based seasoning throughout. No vegan or vegetarian options are advertised; this is a seafood- and meat-forward Kaga washoku restaurant.
caveats: Dinner-only (opens 17:00), closed Sundays. Official price guidance ranges from ~¥3,500 small sets to ~¥10,000 omakase courses, so the bill varies widely with what you order; reservations recommended for groups of 3+.

bio.background: Chef Manabu Maeda trained for about ten years at the well-regarded kappo restaurant Takeshi, then worked at a fresh-fish shop before going independent and opening his own place in August 2018. The shop's name, 'Minna no Manabu' (everyone's Manabu), reflects its friendly, unpretentious spirit.
bio.specialty: Daily-sourced sashimi platters with six or seven varieties of fish, grilled nodoguro (rosy seabass), and a much-loved beef tendon stew simmered two to three hours in a salt-based broth with daikon and green onion; seasonal Kaga vegetables and winter kobako crab also feature.
bio.philosophy: Showcase fresh local Kanazawa and Kaga ingredients in honest, well-crafted Japanese cooking at accessible prices, served in a relaxed, welcoming atmosphere rather than a formal high-end setting.

anecdotes.text: Maeda spent roughly a decade honing his craft at the popular kappo restaurant Takeshi, then worked at a fishmonger's before opening his own restaurant in August 2018 — experience that shows in the morning-fresh fish sourced daily from a trusted local fish vendor.
The kitchen brings contemporary plating to traditional Japanese techniques and leans on Kaga vegetables, pairing dishes with highballs and locally made Kaga plum wine.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free handling is documented; fried dishes such as crab cream croquette are on the menu, implying shared frying and breading with wheat.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Traditional Kanazawa washoku relying on soy sauce and dashi seasoning; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat and no GF substitute is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dashi (typically fish/bonito based) and fresh seafood are central to the cooking, so animal products pervade the menu.

cuisine: 甘納豆・和菓子専門店（地元で愛される老舗甘味）
neighborhood: にし茶屋街（野町）
gf_detail: A traditional wagashi (Japanese confectionery) maker. Many sweets like daifuku and shitatari-mochi are made from glutinous rice (mochi/gyūhi) and azuki, which can be naturally wheat-free, but yōkan, sweet beans, jellies and shared kitchen equipment mean nothing is verified gluten-free. There is no celiac protocol; ask in person about specific items before ordering. Not a dedicated gluten-free destination.
vegan_detail: The core products are plant-based by tradition: amanatto (sugar-candied beans), yōkan, bean-paste daifuku and fruit jellies are typically made from beans, sugar, agar and rice with no animal products. However, some confections and the 2F tea salon menu may use dairy, eggs or honey, and none of this is labeled vegan. Confirm individual items in person.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市野町2丁目24-7
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市野町2-24-7 returned [136.64711, 36.556637], within the Kanazawa box and matching the にし茶屋街 location.
seats_text: About 22 seats in the 2F tea salon (Salon de The Kawamura); the ground floor is a confectionery shop for purchases and gifts.
hours_raw: Shop: weekdays & Saturday 9:30-18:00; Sundays/holidays 9:00-17:00. 2F tea salon (Salon de The Kawamura): weekdays 10:00-17:00 (closes 17:30), Sundays/holidays 10:00-16:00 (closes 16:30).
closed_days: First Tuesday of the month (except January, May and December); closed year-end/New Year (Dec 31 - Jan 3).
cultural_comfort_note: A welcoming traditional sweets shop and quiet tea salon; staff likely speak limited English, so a translation app or a few Japanese phrases will help you order and ask about ingredients.
caveats: The tea salon does not take reservations and handmade items can need preparation time, so allow for a short wait. Many sweets are sold almost exclusively at this Nishi-Chaya shop, and the signature shitatari-mochi has only a 3-day shelf life.

bio.background: Kawamura began as a wholesale bean and confectionery business in Kanazawa with a history said to stretch back roughly a century. The family opened this retail shop in the historic Nishi-Chaya geisha district in 2001, set in a traditional wooden machiya directly across from the district's old geisha registry office, and renovated it in November 2013.
bio.specialty: Amanatto (sugar-candied beans made without artificial coloring or preservatives), alongside yōkan, seasonal fruit jellies, strawberry daifuku, and the beloved shitatari-mochi, a sweet-bean filling wrapped in gyūhi mochi so tender it seems to drip, sold with just a three-day shelf life.
bio.philosophy: Handcrafted quality using local, seasonal Kanazawa ingredients, served in a calm, serene setting, with sweets made without artificial colors or preservatives even at the cost of a short shelf life.

anecdotes.text: The signature shitatari-mochi is named for being 'so soft it seems to drip,' and is sold with a shelf life of only three days from purchase, prioritizing freshness over longevity.

The shop sits in the Nishi-Chaya geisha quarter in a traditional machiya opposite the old kennibanshō (geisha registry office), and the founder's wife contributed the gentle, calming typeface used on the packaging.

positives.text: Amanatto and other sweets are made without artificial coloring or preservatives, following traditional handcrafted methods.

cuisine: Seafood; freshly fried surimi (satsuma-age) and seafood manju; kaisen-don. Signature "Ageha Gozen" lunch set.
neighborhood: 広坂・柿木畠（香林坊／金沢21世紀美術館近く）
address_ja: 石川県金沢市広坂1-1-28 広坂パレス 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市広坂1-1-28 returned [136.656097, 36.561459], within the Kanazawa box. Building name 広坂パレス (Hirosaka Palace) 1F. Located adjacent to the 21st Century Museum / Korinbo, in the Kakikibatake-Hirosaka area.
seats_text: About 10 counter seats.
hours_raw: Lunch 11:30–until sold out (approx. 15:00); Dinner 18:00–22:00 (reservation only). Closed Mon, Tue, Wed; occasional irregular closures.
closed_days: Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday (plus occasional irregular closures)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 10-seat owner-run counter where lunch sells out and dinner is reservation-only courses; it's cash-only with little English, so come early for lunch, expect to be greeted in Japanese, and have basic phrases or a translation app ready.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The kitchen specializes in surimi (satsuma-age / fish cakes) and seafood manju, and serves sashimi with soy sauce — fried items and fish-paste products commonly contain wheat starch/flour and the soy sauce contains wheat. No gluten-free menu or celiac accommodation is documented. Not safe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. This is a seafood and fish-paste specialist — sashimi, satsuma-age, seafood manju, and fish-based courses are the entire concept. No vegan options are documented.
caveats: Lunch is not reservable and frequently sells out, so arrive early; dinner is reservation-only set courses (roughly 4,000–6,000 yen). Closures can be irregular beyond the regular Mon–Wed. Cash only. No official website found; details drawn from Tabelog and local food media.

bio.background: Before opening Ageha, owner Shigeru Ueda spent some 40 years working at a kamaboko (fish-cake) maker that supplied traditional Japanese restaurants, rising to factory manager. After leaving, he opened this small counter shop to share what most people never taste — surimi and satsuma-age eaten the moment they are made.
bio.origin: Kanazawa (Hirosaka / Kakikibatake area, near the 21st Century Museum)
bio.specialty: The hugely popular lunch 'Ageha Gozen' brings together sashimi, just-fried satsuma-age, and a choice of seasonal seafood manju (sea urchin tofu, clam, pumpkin, sweet shrimp, sablefish, and more), with rice, soup, and small dishes. Dinner is reservation-only chef's courses of fish-paste and seafood dishes, including an occasional 'raw kamaboko' made from fresh fish marinated about a week until it sets.
bio.philosophy: Ueda wants diners to discover that fish cakes and satsuma-age are not the supermarket-processed versions they know — that freshly fried surimi is something exceptional, served light, fluffy, and at its peak the instant it leaves the fryer.

anecdotes.text: Ueda spent about 40 years at a kamaboko manufacturer serving traditional restaurants, finishing as factory manager, before opening this 10-seat counter to let people taste freshly made surimi directly.
The lunch 'Ageha Gozen' is so sought-after that customers regularly line up before opening, and lunch runs until it sells out.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Surimi/satsuma-age (fried fish paste) and seafood manju are the core dishes; fish-paste products and fried items typically contain wheat, and no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sashimi is served as part of the set, normally eaten with soy sauce, which in Japan generally contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire menu is built around fish, seafood, and fish paste; no plant-based dishes are documented.

lead: 西茶屋菓寮 味和以
name_ja: 落雁諸江屋 西茶屋菓寮 味和以
cuisine: Wagashi / Japanese sweets tea room (kanmidokoro) — rakugan, zenzai, kakigori, matcha
neighborhood: にし茶屋街（野町）
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates for 石川県金沢市野町二丁目26番1号, within the Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 14 seats (two 4-seat tables, one 6-seat table)
hours_raw: 10:00-17:00 (winter closes 17:30 per shop; Tabelog lists 10:00-17:00). Closed Tuesdays (open if a public holiday, closed Wednesday instead); year-end/New Year break Dec 24 - Jan 3.
closed_days: Tuesdays (open on public holidays, closed the following Wednesday instead); year-end/New Year (Dec 24 - Jan 3)
cultural_comfort_note: A calm traditional sweets tea room behind a confectionery shop; pointing at the picture menu and a few polite words in Japanese will carry you through comfortably.
gf_detail: Some wagashi here (such as rakugan and azuki-based sweets) can be naturally wheat-free, but nothing is labeled gluten-free and there is no dedicated handling. Matcha and zenzai accompaniments, dango/mochi mixes and shared prep mean cross-contact is possible. Not a celiac-safe destination — confirm each item directly with staff before ordering.
vegan_detail: A traditional kanmidokoro: several items (rakugan, azuki zenzai, syrup-based shaved ice) are plant-based by nature, but nothing is certified vegan and there is no documented vegan program. Ask about specific items if strictly avoiding dairy/egg.

roles: Operated by Rakugan Moroeya, a Kaga confectioner
bio.background: Ajiwai is the tea room (chaya) tucked at the back of Rakugan Moroeya's Nishi Chaya-gai shop. Moroeya is a long-established Kaga wagashi house founded in 1849 (Kaei 2) during the late Edo period, known above all for rakugan — delicate pressed sugar (wasanbon) sweets. At the front the shop sells confectionery; step further inside and a quiet tea room opens onto an inner courtyard garden that shows off each season.
bio.specialty: The signature is the Noto Dainagon zenzai (sweet azuki bean soup) made with prized Noto Dainagon red beans: a small charcoal brazier is brought to your table so you grill the mochi yourself before dipping it into the soup. In summer the kakigori shaved ice — including a matcha Uji Kintoki and a wasanbon kintoki — is the draw, alongside matcha-and-sweets sets.
bio.philosophy: Moroeya describes carrying Kaga's traditional confectionery heritage into the present, sticking to old-fashioned methods while making flavors and products still loved today (昔ながらの製法にこだわりながらも現代にも愛される味覚や商品作りに努力を続けてまいりました).

anecdotes.text: The standout winter dish is a self-grilled experience: a charcoal brazier is carried to your table and you toast the mochi yourself before dropping it into the Noto Dainagon zenzai.
The tea room sits at the back of the shop and looks onto an inner courtyard garden that beautifully reflects the changing seasons, enjoyed with matcha and sweets.

positives.text: Several traditional sweets here are naturally plant-based and wheat-free in concept (rakugan pressed sugar sweets, azuki zenzai, syrup/fruit shaved ice), giving flexible diners some choices.

caveats: Hours simplified to a regular weekly schedule; the shop notes a winter close at 17:30 versus Tabelog's 10:00-17:00, and the Tuesday closure shifts to Wednesday when Tuesday is a public holiday. No allergy/cross-contact information is documented, so GF and vegan claims must be confirmed in person.

cuisine: 寿司 (Edomae-style nigiri sushi using local Ishikawa/Kaga/Noto seafood)
neighborhood: 並木町 (Namikimachi) — near the Asano River and Umebashi Bridge, just by the Higashi Chaya District
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. This is a traditional sushi counter where soy sauce (wheat-based) is used throughout, and dishes like sushi rice seasoning, dashi, and accompaniments commonly contain wheat or soy. No gluten-free accommodation is documented and there is no celiac-safe protocol. Not suitable for someone with celiac disease.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. This is a seafood-focused sushi restaurant centered on local fish, crab, and yellowtail, with omakase courses built around raw and prepared seafood. No vegan options are documented.
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 並木町2-16, within the Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 11 seats (small counter-focused family-run shop)
closed_days: Thursdays (木曜日)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, traditional three-generation neighborhood sushi counter where some spoken Japanese and familiarity with omakase sushi etiquette will help; the family is known for warm hospitality, so a polite greeting goes a long way.
caveats: Evening hours are reported slightly differently across sources (one lists 18:00-22:00, others 17:30-22:00); confirm when reserving. Reservation strongly recommended given the small counter. Dinner omakase runs roughly 10,000-20,000 yen.

bio.origin: Kanazawa, Namikimachi — at the foot of Umebashi Bridge along the Asano River, near the Higashi Chaya District
bio.background: A neighborhood sushi shop founded more than 60 years ago (around 65 years) and run by the same family across three generations. The name 八郎 (Hachiro, literally 'eighth son') comes from the founding grandfather, who was the eighth of twelve siblings. Today the third-generation chef shapes the sushi, having trained at the well-regarded Taihei Sushi in Nonoichi; his father, the second generation, still works the counter beside him, and his mother handles the service. It keeps the easygoing, good-natured feel of an old-fashioned Showa-era sushi shop in a neighborhood that has otherwise modernized.
bio.specialty: Omakase nigiri and sushi-and-sashimi assortments built on local Ishikawa, Kaga and Noto seafood — seasonal fish, crab, and yellowtail — paired with local sake.
bio.philosophy: Showcasing fresh local (jimono) ingredients through skilled, unpretentious craftsmanship while welcoming guests with warm, family hospitality.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name comes from the first-generation founder (the chef's grandfather), who was the eighth of twelve siblings, hence 'Hachiro' — eighth son.
Three generations work together: the third-generation son shapes the sushi (after training at Taihei Sushi), the second-generation father stands at the counter, and the mother handles service.
Praised by a local writer as 'a treasure of Kanazawa' and one of the most quintessentially Kanazawa sushi shops.
The restaurant appeared in the film 'Inochi no Teishajo' featuring Sayuri Yoshinaga.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Traditional sushi counter where wheat-containing soy sauce is central to the meal; no gluten-free handling is documented, so cross-contact with gluten should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce (typically brewed with wheat) is used for nigiri and sashimi; no wheat-free tamari is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Seafood-centric omakase; dishes are prepared on shared surfaces with fish, so no vegan-safe preparation is available.

cuisine: 寿司 (Sushi)
neighborhood: 博労町 (Hakuro-cho), central Kanazawa
gf_detail: This is a traditional sushi restaurant where dishes are served with regular Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat. Nigiri, rolls, miso soup, and homemade sweet glazes (such as on the anago/sea eel) are not gluten-free, and there is no indication of gluten-free accommodation or a celiac-safe kitchen. Not suitable for those who must avoid gluten.
vegan_detail: The menu is built entirely around fresh seafood sourced daily from Omicho Market — nigiri, sashimi, grilled fish, and seafood sets. There are no documented vegan or vegetarian dishes; dashi-based soup and fish are central to nearly everything served.
geocode_note: Geocoded from address 石川県金沢市博労町79 via GSI; result [136.658539, 36.56982] falls within the central Kanazawa box.
seats_text: Approximately 8 counter seats plus about 4 tables (around 12+ seats total); private room/table seating available, family-friendly.
hours_raw: 11:30-13:30, 17:30-22:00 (last order 21:15). Closed Wednesdays and Sunday evenings (lunch-only Sundays per one source; Tabelog lists Sunday as closed). Lunch sets such as the Ichihan are available weekday lunches.
closed_days: Wednesdays; Sunday dinner (sources differ on whether Sunday lunch is open — call ahead).
cultural_comfort_note: The walls are covered with notes and photos from international visitors, an English menu is available, and the family welcomes foreign guests, so a foreign student can dine comfortably here.
caveats: Sunday opening hours vary between sources — confirm by phone (076-231-5964) before visiting. The affordable Ichihan lunch set is weekday lunch only.

bio.origin: Kanazawa, with founding-family roots in the Kansai region
bio.background: A family-run sushi shop in central Kanazawa's Hakuro-cho, founded around 70 years ago by the current owner's grandfather and now run by the fourth generation of the Yoshikawa family. It sits a short walk from Omicho Market, where the chef buys fish fresh each morning rather than in bulk, keeping the catch consistently fresh.
bio.specialty: Local Kanazawa fish platters and the value-priced Ichihan lunch set (nine pieces of nigiri plus a thin roll and miso soup for around 1,100 yen, weekday lunch only). A quiet specialty is the anago (sea eel), finished with a sweet homemade glaze. Reflecting the family's Kansai roots, the menu also includes battera and grilled-mackerel sushi, plus a choi-nomi tasting set with sashimi, grilled fish, pickles and soup.
bio.philosophy: Buying fish daily from Omicho Market to ensure freshness, and offering approachable, affordable sushi that keeps both locals and travelers coming back across four generations.

anecdotes.text: The interior walls are lined with messages and photographs left by international customers over the years, reflecting the shop's long-standing welcome to visitors from abroad.
The family's Kansai roots show up on the menu through dishes like battera (pressed mackerel sushi) and grilled-mackerel sushi.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a traditional sushi restaurant, dishes are served with standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat; this is the default and there is no documented gluten-free or wheat-free soy sauce option.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen centers on seafood from Omicho Market and dashi-based soup, so cross-contact with fish is unavoidable; no separate vegan preparation is documented.

cuisine: グルテンフリー・無添加・天然酵母の国産米粉パン専門店（小麦・卵・乳・ナッツ不使用、ヴィーガン対応あり）
gf_detail: Dedicated 100% rice flour (国産コシヒカリ米粉) bakery with no wheat, eggs, dairy, or nuts used anywhere in the workshop. Bread is leavened with house-made apple yeast instead of using gluten or thickeners, and the shop deliberately avoids the seven mandatory allergen ingredients. As an entirely wheat-free production environment, it is one of the safest options in Kanazawa for celiac and gluten-sensitive travelers. No explicit certified-celiac/PPM testing was documented, but the no-wheat-on-premises policy is stated clearly.
vegan_detail: Many items are egg- and dairy-free by design, and the shop explicitly offers vegan cakes (soy-milk based) and a range of rice flour breads and muffins. Not everything is guaranteed vegan (some pizzas use additive-free cheese), so vegans should confirm the specific item, but a substantial vegan selection is available.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市駅西新町1-4-1
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 石川県金沢市駅西新町一丁目4番1号, within the Kanazawa box.
neighborhood: 駅西新町（JR金沢駅西口から徒歩約10分）
seats_text: Retail bakery on the ground floor of an apartment building; takeout only, no eat-in seating documented.
hours_raw: 11:30-16:30（※売切次第終了）。木曜・金曜定休。
closed_days: Thursday and Friday (plus occasional irregular closures); also closes early when sold out
cultural_comfort_note: A small, friendly neighborhood takeout bakery where a smile and a few basic Japanese words go a long way, though the all-allergen-free concept makes it welcoming and easy to point at what you want.
caveats: Takeout-only bakery (no seating). Hours can end early when items sell out; closed Thursdays and Fridays. Vegan status varies by item (some products use additive-free cheese), so confirm the specific item.

bio.background: Founder Junko Kobayashi worked in Ishikawa restaurants and bakeries and later as a TV and radio reporter before becoming a natural-sweets planner. While presenting at a university hospital event she was struck by how many more people live with food allergies than she had imagined, and that experience inspired her to open a bakery that people with dietary restrictions could enjoy without worry.
bio.specialty: 100% domestic Koshihikari rice flour bread with a crisp crust and chewy (mochi-like) interior; salt bread 'Solte', seven to eight kinds of muffins, rice flour chiffon cakes, cheesecakes and tarts, and soy-milk vegan cakes.
bio.philosophy: Everything is made with 100% domestic rice flour and no wheat gluten, avoiding the seven mandatory allergen ingredients and additives such as thickeners, and leavened with house-made apple yeast so that people with allergies can eat freely. After opening she spent a full year refining the recipes.

anecdotes.text: Kobayashi decided to open the shop after realizing at a university hospital event how many people live with food allergies, opening WAGOME in the Kanazawa station-west area in 2014 after a year of recipe development.
The shop achieves the rice-flour bread's signature 'crispy outside, chewy inside' texture using house-made apple yeast instead of gluten or additives.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No wheat, eggs, dairy, or nuts are used anywhere in the workshop, making it a fully wheat-free production environment.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Many items are egg- and dairy-free by design and dedicated vegan (soy-milk) cakes are offered, but some products such as pizzas use additive-free cheese, so confirm the specific item.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The shop was founded specifically to serve people with food allergies and deliberately avoids the seven mandatory allergen ingredients, so staff are highly allergy-aware, though no individual-order cross-contamination protocol was documented in reviews.

positives.text: 100% domestic Koshihikari rice flour, additive-free, leavened with house-made apple yeast; no wheat gluten used.
Entire workshop is free of wheat, egg, dairy, and nuts, which is rare and reassuring for celiac and allergy travelers.

cuisine: Vegan & gluten-free cafe / natural grocery
gf_detail: Most of the baked goods are made with rice flour and are gluten-free (e.g. the organic-lemon vegan custard rice flour muffin), and the shop markets itself as gluten-free. However it is NOT a dedicated GF facility: the scones are made with wheat flour, and all items are produced in the same open kitchen, so wheat is handled on-site and cross-contamination is possible. Celiac travelers should confirm which specific items are wheat-free before ordering.
vegan_detail: The baked goods are all made without eggs or butter (卵・バター不使用), and the retail products are largely vegan-certified / free of animal-derived ingredients. The cafe menu (rice flour muffins, pound cakes, herbal teas, house-made craft cola, organic banana smoothie, vegan gateau chocolat) is plant-based. Verify individual items, but this is effectively a fully vegan cafe.
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市安江町11-38 returned [136.655289, 36.573399], inside the Kanazawa box. Located in the 横安江町商店街 (Yokoyasue-cho shopping arcade), central Kanazawa near Kanazawa Station.
neighborhood: 安江町／横安江町商店街 (Yasue-cho / Yokoyasue-cho arcade)
seats_text: 10 seats (three 2-person tables and one 4-person table; some counter seating overlooking the street), all non-smoking
hours_raw: 10:00-17:00; closed Tuesdays plus irregular closures
closed_days: Tuesdays, plus irregular closures
cultural_comfort_note: A small, friendly mother-run natural-foods cafe where a basic greeting in Japanese goes a long way; menus and labels are in Japanese, so a translation app helps, but the relaxed counter setting is easy for a foreign student to enjoy.

bio.background: midoriya was founded by Takahashi Tomoko, who first opened it in November 2020 as an additive-free natural food shop on the second floor of her family's former boutique building in the Yokoyasue-cho arcade. The idea grew out of motherhood: after having her child she rethought what she fed her family and decided to seek out and sell only items she had personally, carefully chosen.
bio.specialty: Freshly baked rice-flour sweets made daily in an open kitchen — rice flour muffins (including an organic-lemon vegan custard muffin), scones, caramel-nut and other pound cakes, and florentines — all made without eggs or butter, alongside herbal tea blends, house-made craft cola, and a vegan gateau chocolat.
bio.philosophy: Choosing natural, additive-free foods is good for the body, the mind, and the earth; everyday items deserve genuine quality, and the shop curates everything through a mother's eye so that even children can eat safely.

anecdotes.text: In May 2023 midoriya moved down from the second floor to the first floor and was renovated to add a proper cafe space, furnished with antique pieces re-upholstered in new fabric and counter seats looking out onto the shopping street, deliberately keeping the retro character of the old boutique.
The cafe encourages customers to bring their own tumblers and reusable containers, offering a discount on drinks as part of its low-waste, earth-friendly outlook.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen: scones are made with wheat flour while most other pastries use rice flour, and everything is baked in the same open kitchen, so cross-contact with wheat is possible.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All baked goods are made without eggs or butter and the retail products are largely vegan-certified, so the risk of accidental animal-derived ingredients is low.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The owner curates products with a focus on items children can safely enjoy and labels baked goods as egg/butter/wheat-free where applicable, but no formal allergy protocol is documented; ask about specific items in person.

positives.text: Marketed explicitly as a vegan & gluten-free sweets cafe using organic/pesticide-free ingredients, with many rice-flour, egg- and butter-free options.
Daily-made rice flour muffins and pound cakes are gluten-free and vegan, e.g. the organic-lemon vegan custard rice flour muffin.

caveats: Tabelog lists the venue as an active cafe with no closure/hold notice. It is both a natural grocery store and a small cafe. Hours include irregular closures, so check the Instagram (@midoriya.natural) before visiting. While branded gluten-free, wheat (scones) is handled in the shared open kitchen, so it is not celiac-safe by default.

name_ja: 金沢カレー＆ハントンライス だいだい（橙）
cuisine: Kanazawa curry and Hunton rice Western-style eatery (Yoshoku)
neighborhood: Katamachi (Korinbo)
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The entire menu centers on wheat-based dishes: Kanazawa curry uses a flour-thickened roux served over a breaded (panko) cutlet, and the Hunton rice is topped with deep-fried breaded white fish and breaded shrimp. There is no gluten-free menu, no dedicated fryer, and no documented allergy accommodation. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian friendly. Signature dishes are built around fried fish, shrimp, pork cutlet, egg (omurice base) and meat-based homemade demi-glace and curry roux. No plant-based options are documented.
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市片町2-8-10 returned [136.652527, 36.56242], within the central Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 13 seats (7 counter, 4 table, 2 other)
hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 (L.O. ~14:30-14:45). Closed Tuesdays and the day after public holidays.
closed_days: Tuesdays and the day after public holidays
cultural_comfort_note: A small, casual lunch counter where pointing at the picture menu and a friendly "konnichiwa" will carry you through; bring cash, as cards are not accepted and reservations are not taken.
caveats: Cash only, no reservations, lunch-only (11:00-15:00), and just 13 seats, so expect a possible wait at peak times. Closed Tuesdays and the day after public holidays.

bio.background: Daidai opened on April 29, 2023 in Kanazawa's lively Katamachi district, just behind Korinbo Tokyu Square. The shop's whole concept is to serve the city's two great local specialties under one roof: Kanazawa curry and Hunton rice.
bio.specialty: The flagship 'Daidai Hunton Rice' (around 2,000 yen) is a fluffy omurice crowned with fried white fish, tartar sauce and two oversized shrimp fries; the tartar sauce hides a note of daidai (bitter orange) - the citrus that gives the shop its name - for a refreshing finish. The other pillar is Kanazawa-style cutlet curry (around 1,400 yen), a thick, spiced dark roux over a crisp breaded cutlet and shredded cabbage, served on the classic stainless boat dish.
bio.philosophy: Owner Isao Shimabukuro makes the demi-glace, tartar sauce and curry roux from scratch and refuses to cut corners, saying he simply wants delicious food to make people smile.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's name and its signature tartar sauce both come from daidai (bitter orange), which is folded into the sauce to lift the richness of the fried shrimp and fish.
The retro, wood-toned dining room also serves local Kanazawa craft beer and a house yuzu soda alongside the food.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dishes use flour-thickened curry roux, panko-breaded fried fish, shrimp and pork cutlet, all fried together; no dedicated fryer or gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are built on egg, fish, shrimp, pork and meat-based demi-glace/curry roux; no plant-based preparation is documented.

lead: Cajon to Seed（カホントゥシード）
name_ja: Cajon to Seed（カホントゥシード）
cuisine: Organic healthy café/dining — pesticide-free brown rice, Ishikawa organic vegetables, additive-free seasonings; weekly-changing Asian/multi-cuisine plates
gf_detail: No documented gluten-free program. The kitchen does serve brown rice and many vegetable-forward dishes, but published menus include soy-sauce-style additive-free seasonings, Thai/Asian preparations and muffins, and there is no statement about gluten avoidance, separate fryers, or cross-contamination control. A celiac diner should not assume safety and would need to ask in detail; this is not a gluten-free destination.
vegan_detail: Strongly plant-forward: organic salads, seasonal Ishikawa vegetables, pesticide-free brown rice, vegetable soup, smoothies, fresh-pressed juices and hand-dripped coffee. However the kitchen is omnivore — the signature weekly Seed Lunch and Asian plates routinely include seafood (shrimp, tuna), chicken and egg/fish components, so it is not fully vegan. Vegan diners should ask which of that week's dishes are plant-only; the salad/brown-rice/vegetable-soup base is the safest bet.
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市藤江北 returned [136.624084, 36.588661], within the Kanazawa box (chō-level point for Fujie-kita).
neighborhood: 藤江北（駅西・県庁周辺、金沢市）
seats_text: About 56 seats; roughly one-third of the space is a shop and two-thirds is open, high-ceilinged café/dining with table and counter seating plus a dog-friendly terrace. Can be reserved for parties (up to ~80 seated / 120 standing).
hours_raw: 11:00–21:00 (lunch L.O. 14:00, dinner L.O. 20:00). Closed Mondays and the 2nd Sunday of each month.
closed_days: Mondays and the 2nd Sunday of each month
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, plant-forward organic café in the quieter Ekinishi/Fujie area west of the station — easy and welcoming for a foreign student, though it sits in a residential pocket with little English, so a translation app and a willingness to ask which dishes are meat- or fish-free will help most.
caveats: No Tabelog page was located; the official reservation/listing presence is the operator's own site (cajon.jp) and its gorp.jp/gnavi listing. The signature Seed Lunch often sells out about an hour after opening. Payment methods and a strict gluten-free protocol are not documented and should be confirmed directly.

bio.background: Cajon to Seed began life simply as "Cajon," named after the cajón — the wooden box-drum that founding members fell for at a live concert. The instrument became the shop's metaphor: different parts coming together to make one harmony. The current representative, Okada, who hails from Nomi City in Ishikawa, joined as part of that founding team roughly 18 years ago and took the reins as owner about nine years in, during a major renovation that reshaped the place into the airy, plant-filled organic café-dining it is today.
bio.specialty: The weekly-changing Seed Lunch is the heart of the menu — a colorful plate of four seasonal dishes built around pesticide-free brown rice, an organic salad, a vegetable soup and a drink. It regularly sells out within about an hour of opening. Other favorites include the Seed Taco Rice, chicken-basil stir-fry rice, organic smoothies and fresh-pressed juices, single-origin hand-dripped coffee, and house-made muffins.
bio.philosophy: Okada wants to retire the old idea that organic food has to taste dull — the kitchen chases genuinely delicious organic cooking and treats natural, additive-free eating as an everyday way to look after one's health, while respecting that diners make their own choices. The brown rice is grown pesticide-free in the restaurant's own paddies and cooked with cold Kawachi-village water kept out of direct sun; seasonings are additive-free, including products from Nakashō, a 129-year-old Ishikawa maker.

anecdotes.text: The name comes from the cajón drum that inspired the original founders at a live show — chosen to symbolize how different parts create one harmony.
The pesticide-free brown rice is grown on the restaurant's own rice paddies and prepared with cold water from Kawachi Village; refills of rice are offered.
The Seed Lunch is the number-one seller and typically sells out roughly an hour after the doors open.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Published menus describe additive-free seasonings (including soy-sauce-style products from a traditional Ishikawa maker) and Asian/Thai preparations, so wheat-containing sauces are likely present; no gluten-free assurance is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The omnivore kitchen plates seafood (shrimp, tuna), chicken and egg components alongside vegetable dishes on the same changing lunch set, so plant-only diners should confirm which items are free of animal products that week.

positives.text: Strong base of plant-based items — organic salad, pesticide-free brown rice, vegetable soup, smoothies and fresh juices — gives vegetarians and vegans workable options.
Ingredients are emphasized as organic, pesticide-free and additive-free, including brown rice from the restaurant's own paddies.

lead: キッチンユキ 武蔵店
name_ja: キッチンユキ 武蔵店
cuisine: 洋食・金沢ブラックカレー（金沢カレー源流の老舗洋食店）
neighborhood: 武蔵が辻（金沢エムザ／近江町市場すぐ近く）
gf_detail: A classic Japanese yoshoku (Western-style) restaurant whose signature is Kanazawa Black Curry served over rice with breaded, deep-fried cutlets (katsu). Roux-based Japanese curry is thickened with wheat flour, the cutlets are coated in wheat breadcrumbs, and items like hayashi rice and spaghetti are also wheat-based. There is no documented gluten-free menu or allergen handling. This is not a celiac-safe place.
vegan_detail: The menu is built around meat: pork/chicken cutlet curries, hayashi rice and other yoshoku staples, with the curry roux and sauces typically meat-based. No documented vegetarian or vegan options. Not a vegan destination.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市武蔵町15-1 金沢エムザ B1F
geocode_note: Geocoded to 石川県金沢市武蔵町15-1 (Kanazawa M'ZA department store, Musashi crossing). Within Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 26 seats
hours_raw: Mon-Sat 11:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:00); Sun 11:00-19:00 (L.O. 18:30). Located inside Kanazawa M'ZA B1F; follows the department store's calendar.
closed_days: Follows Kanazawa M'ZA (金沢エムザ) department store holidays; no fixed weekly closed day documented.
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, easy department-store-basement curry counter where you order a clearly named dish (point at the popular Kanazawa Black Katsu Curry) and pay by card or QR, so a foreign student can manage without much Japanese, but staff likely speak little English and there is no allergen support.
caveats: Wheat- and meat-heavy yoshoku curry house with no documented gluten-free or vegan options — unsuitable for celiac or vegan diners. Located in the basement of Kanazawa M'ZA, so it follows the store's calendar.

bio.background: Kitchen Yuki is a long-established yoshoku (Japanese Western-style) restaurant with more than 40 years of history, widely regarded as one of the roots of 'Kanazawa Curry' — the regional style of dark, glossy, thick curry served over rice and eaten with a fork or spoon, usually topped with a crisp cutlet. This Musashi branch brought the storied name into the basement food floor of the Kanazawa M'ZA department store at the Musashi crossing, a short walk from Omicho Market.
bio.specialty: Kanazawa Black Curry (金沢ブラックカレー) — a deeply colored, near-black curry prized for its rich, mellow depth rather than sharp heat. The most popular order is the Kanazawa Black Katsu Curry, alongside pork-cutlet curry, chicken-cutlet curry, Camembert cheese curry, and hayashi rice.
bio.philosophy: The house emphasizes a long-simmered, homemade curry sauce aiming for richness and complexity rather than simple spiciness.

anecdotes.text: Kitchen Yuki is described as one of the origins of Kanazawa Curry, with a black curry sauce so dark it is nearly the color of its name.
The revered Kitchen Yuki name opened a branch inside the Kanazawa M'ZA department store, bringing the old-school yoshoku curry to the city center.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Japanese curry roux is thickened with wheat flour and the signature cutlets are coated in wheat breadcrumbs and deep-fried, so the core menu is inherently wheat-based.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The curry sauce and menu are meat-based (pork and chicken cutlets, hayashi rice), with no documented vegetarian or vegan preparation.

lead: キュリオ エスプレッソ ヴィンテージ デザイン（Curio Espresso and Vintage Design）
name_ja: キュリオ エスプレッソ ヴィンテージ デザイン
cuisine: シアトル系カフェ・エスプレッソ／自家製アメリカンサンドイッチ
neighborhood: 安江町（横安江町商店街・金澤表参道入口、近江町市場/武蔵が辻バス停すぐ）
gf_detail: No dedicated or documented gluten-free menu. Core offerings (BBQ pork sandwiches, breakfast sandwiches, carrot cake, pastries) are wheat-based, so the kitchen is not gluten-free. A celiac traveler should not assume any item is safe; ask staff directly (English-speaking) before ordering.
vegan_detail: No documented vegan menu. The cafe centers on espresso drinks (dairy milk) plus meat/egg/cheese sandwiches and dairy/egg baked goods. Plant-milk substitution for espresso drinks is plausible at a Seattle-style cafe but is not documented, so ask. Few if any fully vegan food options.
