geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates within the central Kanazawa box; matches the 安江町1-13 location at the entrance of the Yokoyasuecho shopping street near Omicho Market.
seats_text: 22 seats (small renovated machiya townhouse cafe)
hours_raw: 8:00-15:00; closed Tuesday and Wednesday (some listings show 9:00-18:00; current Tabelog and cafe coverage indicate 8:00-15:00 with Tue/Wed closed)
closed_days: Tuesday and Wednesday
cultural_comfort_note: Run by a Seattle native and his Japanese partner with English-speaking staff and an American-cafe menu, so a foreign student can order entirely in English and feel at home here.
caveats: Cash only. Hours and closed days vary across listings (8:00-15:00 vs 9:00-18:00; Tue-only vs Tue/Wed closed) - check the cafe's Facebook/Instagram before visiting. No parking lot; use nearby paid lots.

bio.specialty: Seattle-style espresso drinks (cinnamon latte, the coffee-jelly Crystal latte), house-made barbecue pork sandwiches, breakfast sandwiches, and spiced carrot cake
bio.philosophy: Bring genuine Seattle specialty-coffee culture and homemade American cafe food to Kanazawa, using beans sourced from Seattle and roasted locally then blended from five varieties for depth.
bio.background: A small independent cafe opened by a Seattle native and his Japanese partner who previously worked in the Seattle coffee scene. It occupies a renovated traditional machiya townhouse at the entrance of the Yokoyasuecho shopping street (Kanazawa Omote-Sando), a short walk from Omicho Market.

anecdotes.text: The shop is decorated with the owners' eclectic vintage collection - antique cameras and sewing machines - and still rings up orders on a working cash register from 1943.
Beans are sourced from Seattle, roasted in Kanazawa, and blended from five varieties to build complexity in the espresso.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff speak English, which makes it easier for a foreign visitor to ask about ingredients and allergens directly.

positives.text: English-speaking owners and staff at an American-style cafe make communicating dietary needs straightforward.

cuisine: 金沢おでん・居酒屋 (Kanazawa oden and izakaya)
neighborhood: 西町・近江町市場の南すぐ (Nishicho, just south of Omicho Market)
address_ja: 石川県金沢市西町藪ノ内通25-1
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 西町藪ノ内通25; within Kanazawa box.
seats_text: L字カウンター14席のみ (L-shaped counter, 14 seats only)
closed_days: 日曜日 (Sunday)
cultural_comfort_note: This is a tiny 14-seat counter spot that primarily serves local regulars and reportedly declines general tourists, so a foreign student should only visit accompanied by a local who knows the place and speaks Japanese.
gf_detail: A traditional Kanazawa oden and izakaya built around bonito/kombu dashi, soy-seasoned simmered items, tempura, and other dishes that rely on wheat-containing soy sauce and batter. There is no documented gluten-free handling, and this is not a celiac-safe environment.
vegan_detail: The menu centers on fish-based dashi and seafood/meat items (bay shellfish, yellowtail, milt, roast pork, beef tendon, squid). Even vegetable oden pieces are simmered in bonito-kombu broth, so there are no documented vegan options.
caveats: Reportedly limits entry to local acquaintances and work-related visitors and declines general tourists; best visited with a local. Phone 076-231-4878. Hours and access drawn from listing pages.

bio.background: ゑびす is a small, family-style oden counter that has stood just south of Omicho Market for over half a century, cherished by generations of local regulars. Ingredients are hand-picked fresh from Omicho Market each day.
bio.specialty: Kanazawa-style oden with generously large pieces simmered in a bonito and Rishiri-kombu dashi: daikon, bay shellfish (bai-gai), shinoda rolls, ginkgo nuts, rolled cabbage, and tender beef tendon. The kitchen also turns out seasonal home-style obanzai such as roast pork, vinegared dishes, and tempura.
bio.philosophy: A broth that has been topped up and carried forward for decades, so the dashi is said to hold the shop's own history; quality ingredients chosen daily from the neighboring market.

anecdotes.text: The oden pieces are notably large, and a dashi of bonito and Rishiri kombu soaks into each ingredient just right; the broth is described as having the shop's long history simmered into it.
Loved by regulars for over 50 years, with daily ingredients carefully selected from Omicho Market.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Kanazawa oden is simmered in a soy-seasoned bonito/kombu dashi, which in this style relies on wheat-containing soy sauce; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All oden pieces share a fish-based bonito and kombu broth, so vegetable items are not free of animal-derived dashi.

lead: カフェ 安江町ジャルダン（Cafe 安江町ジャルダン）
name_ja: カフェ 安江町ジャルダン
cuisine: カフェ（焼きたてスコーン・厚焼きパンケーキ・葛ミルクゼリーなどのスイーツと自家焙煎コーヒー）
neighborhood: 安江町
address_ja: 石川県金沢市安江町18-12 Machiya5 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI on 石川県金沢市安江町18-12; first feature coordinates [136.653809, 36.576195], within the central Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 12 table seats, no private rooms (can be reserved for small private events of up to ~20 people)
hours_raw: 11:00–18:30, closed Tuesdays (per official site)
closed_days: 火曜日（火曜定休）
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, warmly personal owner-run café in a hidden machiya; the owner happily chats with first-time guests, but there's no indication of English service, so a few basic Japanese phrases will help a foreign student feel at ease.
gf_detail: The signature items are thick wheat pancakes and freshly baked scones, both made from wheat flour, so the menu is wheat-centric and not celiac-safe. No gluten-free menu or dedicated handling is documented.
vegan_detail: Core offerings rely on dairy and eggs — pancakes and scones served with rich Hokkaido cream, and a kuzu milk pudding made with milk and cream. No vegan or plant-based options are documented.
caveats: Hours and closed-day reporting differs between sources; this record follows the official site (11:00–18:30, closed Tuesdays). Scones and the kuzu milk pudding are baked/made to order and take roughly 20–30 minutes, so allow extra time. The thick pancakes have at times been off the menu per recent reviews.

bio.background: Yasuecho Jardin opened in 2014, tucked at the end of a narrow corridor inside Machiya5 — a mixed-use complex of design studios and IT offices set in a restored Meiji-era townhouse, just minutes from Kanazawa Station yet feeling like a step into another world. The owner, Rieko Koshi, made the bold choice to open the café in her 50s rather than slow down, pouring her love of food into a small, personal space.
bio.specialty: Freshly baked scones (about 800 yen, baked to order over ~30 minutes and served warm with rich Hokkaido cream and homemade blueberry jam), thick crisp-edged, fluffy pancakes, and a made-to-order kuzu milk pudding using traditional kuzu, kibi sugar, milk and cream topped with kinako and black syrup. Coffee is a self-roasted blend extracted by cloth drip.
bio.philosophy: Koshi serves only what she herself finds genuinely delicious and refuses to compromise on ingredient quality. The name 'Jardin' is French for 'garden,' reflecting the lush green view from the windows; even the lounge chairs are chosen with care (sourced from Osaka's TRUCK) for comfort and longevity, and sweets are paired with drinks to deepen the experience.

anecdotes.text: The owner readily chats with first-time visitors and happily shares dining recommendations, giving the hidden café a warm, neighborly feel.
Guests walk through narrow machiya corridors that create an 'otherworldly' transition before reaching the soft-lit room overlooking a green garden, with quiet jazz playing.

positives.text: Sweets such as scones and the kuzu milk pudding are prepared fresh to order, so the owner is closely involved in each dish and could be asked about ingredients in person.

cuisine: 加賀料理・割烹 (Kaga cuisine / traditional kappo)
neighborhood: 大工町 (Daikumachi), near Katamachi / Korinbo / Omicho
gf_detail: Traditional Kaga kappo built around dashi, soy sauce, and simmered/braised preparations. No gluten-free menu or accommodations are documented, and wheat-based soy sauce is integral to the cuisine (e.g. jibuni, simmered dishes). Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: A seafood-forward Kaga cuisine restaurant; signature dishes include grilled fish, snow crab, and jibuni (a meat/poultry stew). Dashi is fish/kombu based. No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented.
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市大工町2; coordinates fall within the central Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 25 seats total: 8 counter seats plus 5 private rooms accommodating 2 to 16 people.
hours_raw: Monday-Saturday 17:30-22:00; closed Sundays (plus occasional irregular closures).
closed_days: Sundays (and occasional irregular closures)
cultural_comfort_note: This is a refined, reservation-only kappo where most communication is in Japanese and dinner runs around 10,000 yen and up, so it suits a student comfortable ordering in Japanese for a special occasion rather than a casual drop-in.

bio.background: Founded in 1979, Matsuyoshi is a kappo tucked onto Daikumachi, a quiet side street one block back from busy Katamachi and within walking distance of Korinbo and the Omicho market. The restaurant was built by first-generation owner Akira Matsushita, who set famously strict standards for taste and presentation. After his passing, his sons carried it on; the current chef trained at kappo restaurants in Kyoto and Osaka before returning home to continue the family tradition.
bio.specialty: Seasonal Kaga cuisine sourced daily from the market and prepared through slow simmering, aging, and flavor-infusion. Signature dishes include cedar-plank grilled fish, steamed lotus root, jibuni (Kaga's traditional stew), and snow crab in season.
bio.philosophy: An uncompromising commitment to delicate, gentle flavors and refined seasonal presentation, letting Ishikawa's abundant ingredients speak for themselves.

anecdotes.text: First-generation owner Akira Matsushita was known for an exceptionally strict, no-compromise approach to flavor, and his sons trained in Kyoto and Osaka before returning to preserve that reputation among regulars and connoisseurs.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Traditional Kaga cuisine relies on wheat-based soy sauce and dashi in simmered and braised dishes such as jibuni; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is seafood- and meat-forward (grilled fish, snow crab, jibuni stew) with fish/kombu dashi throughout; no vegan offerings are documented.

caveats: Reservation-only, dinner-only fine kappo with a budget around 10,000-15,000 yen. Family-run by brothers, founded 1979. Not a vegan or gluten-free destination. Cards accepted.

cuisine: Charcoal-grilled eel and loach (unagi/dojo kabayaki), crab, and fresh seafood; Omicho Market deli and takeout
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The signature kabayaki sauce is built on Ohno soy sauce (regular Japanese soy sauce contains wheat) plus malt syrup, brushed onto the grilled eel and loach. This is a soy-sauce-and-seafood shop with no gluten-free accommodation documented, so it is not celiac-safe.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The entire shop is built around animal products: charcoal-grilled eel and loach, crab, oysters, sea urchin, scallops, and shrimp. There are no documented plant-based options.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市青草町88番地 近江町いちば館1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from chō-level address (金沢市青草町88) via GSI; result lat 36.5717, lng 136.6560 falls within the central Kanazawa box and matches the Omicho Market location.
neighborhood: 近江町市場（近江町いちば館1F、北鉄金沢駅近く）
seats_text: No table seating — a market stall with takeout and a standing/counter spot where customers eat freshly grilled eel, loach, oysters and shellfish on the spot.
hours_raw: Approx. 9:00–15:30 (shop ~8:30–16:00 for crab; eel grilling from around 9:00–9:30). Eel not served on Wednesdays.
closed_days: No fixed weekly closing day; closed during Obon and New Year, with occasional temporary closures. Eel (unagi) is not grilled on Wednesdays.
cultural_comfort_note: A bustling market stall where you point at what you want and eat grilled eel or loach skewers standing up — easy and welcoming, but expect Japanese-only service, so it helps to know the dish names or come with a guide.
caveats: This is a market stall and takeout shop, not a sit-down restaurant — you eat grilled items at the counter or take them away. Eel is not grilled on Wednesdays. Strongly soy-sauce and seafood based: unsuitable for vegans and not safe for those needing gluten-free.

bio.background: Kawaki Shoten is one of the long-standing crab-and-eel specialists in Kanazawa's Omicho Market, run as a family business (有限会社 川木商店). It sits on the ground floor of the Omicho Ichiba-kan building, where the family grills eel and loach over charcoal right at the stall and sells crab, oysters, sea urchin, scallops and shrimp from the same counter.
bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled unagi (eel) kabayaki and the more unusual, very affordable dojo (loach) kabayaki at around 100-120 yen a skewer, alongside boiled crab and seasonal Kanazawa seafood like nodoguro and rock oysters.
bio.philosophy: The shop is particular about origin, sauce and grilling method: eels with firm flesh are grilled hot and fast over domestic charcoal to seal in the umami, rested for about an hour, then dipped and grilled again so the sauce soaks in — leaving crisp skin and fluffy flesh.

anecdotes.text: The kabayaki sauce has been kept going and replenished for over 50 years since the shop's founding, made with Ohno soy sauce and malt syrup for a rounded umami without an aggressive edge.
Eels are sourced from Aichi Prefecture for their firm flesh and grilled over domestic charcoal at high heat for a short time, then rested and re-grilled with the sauce.
The loach kabayaki is glazed with a slightly sweeter sauce than the eel and sold for about 120 yen a skewer, making it an easy, cheap taste of an old Kanazawa specialty.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Everything is grilled and glazed on a shared charcoal kabayaki setup with a wheat-containing soy-sauce glaze; no gluten-free preparation is offered.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature kabayaki sauce is made with Ohno soy sauce and malt syrup; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, so the eel and loach glaze is not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: This is a crab, eel, loach and shellfish stall with no plant-based menu items, so cross-contact with animal products is unavoidable.

cuisine: 金沢おでん・おばんざい（居酒屋）
neighborhood: 西町・近江町市場近く
address_ja: 石川県金沢市西町藪ノ内通25-1
geocode_note: GSI address-search geocoded 石川県金沢市西町藪ノ内通25-1 to [136.655731, 36.569851], within the Kanazawa box. Sources note one variant address (25-2); the Tabelog/Gnavi listing gives 25-1.
seats_text: L字カウンターのみ全17席（情報源により14席との記載もあり）
closed_days: 日曜日
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, regulars-filled local oden counter where some Japanese helps and a phone call ahead is wise, since seating is limited and the place is sometimes described as favoring familiar faces.
gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The house oden is built on a continuously-replenished bonito-and-kombu dashi typically seasoned with wheat-based soy sauce, and the menu centers on oden, sashimi, and obanzai such as char siu — all high cross-contamination risk. No gluten-free accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. This is a seafood-and-meat oden/izakaya: bonito dashi broth, sashimi, shellfish oden, and char siu are core offerings. Some vegetable obanzai or oden items (daikon, ginkgo) exist but are simmered in fish-based dashi. No vegan menu is documented.
caveats: Counter-only and very small; multiple sources describe it as packed with regulars and at times effectively members/acquaintances-oriented, so call ahead (076-231-4878) to confirm availability. One source lists 14 seats and 17:30-22:00 versus the more common 17-seat / until-22:30 figures; address appears as both 25-1 and 25-2.

bio.background: Ebisu has stood near Omicho Market, Kanazawa's centuries-old kitchen, since 1966 (Showa 41), making it one of the city's long-running oden institutions — well over half a century of service to local regulars.
bio.origin: 西町藪ノ内通, a few minutes from Omicho Market in central Kanazawa.
bio.specialty: Kanazawa-style oden simmered in a bonito-and-Rishiri-kombu dashi that has been continuously replenished since the shop opened, with notably large oden pieces — daikon, shinoda-maki, shellfish, ginkgo, beef tendon — alongside an abundant, seasonally rotating lineup of home-style obanzai dishes and fresh sashimi sourced daily at the market.
bio.philosophy: Daily selection of quality ingredients from neighboring Omicho Market and a generations-deep dashi are the heart of the kitchen; the small counter is meant to feel, as one writer put it, warming to the heart.

anecdotes.text: The oden broth carries the shop's whole history: a bonito and kombu dashi continuously topped up since the 1966 opening, soaking into each oversized oden piece.
An intimate counter-only room that fills daily with knowledgeable local regulars seeking specific seasonal obanzai — grilled pork, vinegared vegetables, tempura — beyond the oden itself.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is oden, sashimi, and obanzai cooked in a shared bonito-kombu dashi with wheat-based soy sauce and served in a tiny counter kitchen; cross-contamination risk is high and no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Kanazawa oden and obanzai are conventionally seasoned with wheat-containing soy sauce; no wheat-free tamari is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Core broth is bonito (fish) dashi and the menu features sashimi, shellfish, and char siu, so even vegetable items are simmered in animal-based stock.

lead: むすび 近江町市場店
name_ja: むすび 近江町市場店
cuisine: おにぎり・おむすび（手造りおむすび・惣菜・コロッケ）
gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. This is a handmade onigiri (rice ball) stand. While plain salt, umeboshi (pickled plum), or kombu rice balls are naturally rice-based, the shop also sells croquettes (wheat breading) and uses soy sauce-based fillings, and there is no allergen separation or dedicated equipment at this small open stall. No gluten-free handling is documented. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: A few onigiri may be plant-based (e.g. plain salt, umeboshi pickled plum, or kombu seaweed), but most fillings are fish, meat, or mayonnaise-based, and rice may be seasoned with dashi (fish/bonito stock). The shop also sells Noto-beef croquettes and other meat items. No vegan menu is documented; vegans should ask which onigiri are free of fish/meat/dashi, and a language barrier should be expected.
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市下堤町37-1 returned [136.655502, 36.570988], within the central Kanazawa box. Located at the entrance of Omicho Market.
neighborhood: 近江町市場・近江町いちば館（北鉄金沢駅）
seats_text: Takeaway stand at the entrance of Omicho Market; no indoor seating, just one small bench out front.
closed_days: Wednesday (per Tabelog); the official site lists irregular closures for this branch.
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny cash-only grab-and-go onigiri stand at the market entrance with little or no English, so point at what you want and have small bills ready; easiest with a guide or translation app if you need to check fillings.
caveats: Listed cuisine mentions 蕎麦 (soba), but neither Tabelog nor the official site shows soba at this location; soba appears to belong to a separately-named shop "むすびそば" near Omicho Market. This is a takeaway omusubi/croquette/soft-serve stand, not a sit-down eatery. Hours differ between Tabelog (Mon-Fri 08:00-16:00, Sat/Sun/holidays 10:00-17:00, closed Wed) and the official site (11:00-17:00, irregular holidays) — confirm on arrival.

bio.background: Musubi is a handmade rice ball (omusubi) shop that has been making onigiri in Kanazawa for over 20 years. Its main store sits inside JR Kanazawa Station, and this smaller branch stands right at the lively entrance of Omicho Market, the city's centuries-old fresh-food market.
bio.specialty: Hand-shaped onigiri made from rice specially chosen for rice balls, alongside croquettes (including a Noto-beef heart-shaped croquette) and gold-leaf soft-serve ice cream that nods to Kanazawa's famous gold leaf.
bio.philosophy: The shop's motto, '真心むすびます' (rolled with heartfelt sincerity), reflects its craft approach: rice is cooked over strong gas heat with soft water and washed by hand, then each ball is shaped gently by a craftsperson's hands alone, never with a mold.

anecdotes.text: At its peak the company has hand-rolled as many as 8,000 onigiri in a single day, all made for the counter rather than pre-ordered.
The Omicho Market branch leans into local Kanazawa treats, offering a gold-leaf soft serve and a Noto-beef croquette shaped like a heart.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat-breaded croquettes are sold and fried at the same small stall as the rice products, with no documented separation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy sauce-based onigiri fillings are common at such shops and standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fish, meat, and mayonnaise fillings are prepared alongside any plain rice balls at the same counter; no separation is documented.

positives.text: Naturally simple options like plain salt, umeboshi (pickled plum), or kombu onigiri may suit some diners, though fillings should be confirmed in person.

cuisine: Unagi (eel) and dojo (loach) kabayaki — grilled-in-tare freshwater fish specialist
neighborhood: Kanaiwa-kita (Kanazawa port area)
geocode_note: Geocoded from GSI to lng 136.596024, lat 36.602924 — within the Kanazawa box. Shop relocated from its former 寺中町 address to 金石北1-1-1; the original Tabelog listing (17010078) is marked 移転 (relocated), and this is the current Tabelog/official address.
seats_text: 20 seats
hours_raw: 10:30〜21:00 (火〜日・祝). 18時30分〜21時は事前のご予約のみ。最盛期（6月下旬〜8月下旬）は18時まで。定休日：月曜日。
closed_days: Mondays
cultural_comfort_note: A small, traditional family-run grill in a quiet port neighborhood with no indication of English support or menus — a foreign student would do best to visit with a Japanese-speaking guide or come prepared with the dish names written down.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature eel and loach are grilled in a soy-based kabayaki tare, which in Japan is virtually always made with wheat-containing soy sauce, and set meals come with rice, sides and likely wheat-based accompaniments. No gluten-free accommodation is documented. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The entire menu is built around freshwater fish (unagi/eel and dojo/loach) plus Kanazawa-port seafood set meals. No vegan options are documented.
caveats: The original target Tabelog listing (17010078, 寺中町) is marked 移転 (relocated); the shop now operates at 金石北1-1-1, reflected here with the current Tabelog listing (17013739) and the official site. Menu prices are not itemized on the pages fetched; budget per Tabelog is roughly 1,000–1,999 yen lunch and 3,000–3,999 yen dinner. Evening hours (18:30–21:00) are by advance reservation only, and during peak eel season (late June–late August) the shop closes at 18:00.

bio.background: Kabayakiya is a long-standing Kanazawa grill devoted to dojo (loach) kabayaki, a local dish that took root in the city in the early Meiji era and was once a common home-cooked food. As loach has grown scarce in the markets, only a handful of shops in Kanazawa still specialize in it, and Kabayakiya has kept the tradition alive while also serving premium domestic eel. The shop recently relocated within Kanazawa to the Kanaiwa port district and has opened a second branch in nearby Nonoichi.
bio.philosophy: To preserve Kanazawa's traditional food culture by hand-grilling each piece of loach and eel one by one, the old-fashioned artisanal way.
bio.specialty: Dojo (loach) kabayaki is the signature; the shop also serves premium domestic eel kabayaki, set meals, bento, and Kanazawa-port seafood teishoku. The loach is purged in fresh water for two to three days, then dressed live, bones and all, before grilling.

anecdotes.text: Loach is prepared with care: the fish are kept swimming in fresh water for two to three days to purge mud, then processed live and grilled bones-and-all. The shop frames loach as a highly nutritious local food, noting it carries far more calcium than eel.

The shop has drawn media attention over the years, including a 2014 TV visit by TOKIO's Taichi Kokubun, 2015 newspaper coverage of its vacuum-sealing method for nationwide shipping, and a 2016 feature on TV Asahi's SmaSTATION as a Kanazawa specialty.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature dishes are grilled in a kabayaki tare (sauce), which in Japan is standardly soy-sauce based and therefore contains wheat; no wheat-free sauce is offered.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is entirely focused on grilling eel and loach and preparing seafood set meals, so any non-fish item would share equipment and surfaces with fish.

cuisine: 手打ち二八蕎麦・おむすび
neighborhood: 近江町市場（かなざわはこまち 2F）
gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. The house soba is 二八 (nihachi) — a buckwheat-and-wheat blend, so the noodles themselves contain wheat. The dashi-based tsuyu broth, tempura batter, and likely soy sauce all add further gluten. There is no documented gluten-free menu or handling, and cross-contamination from shared wheat-flour soba prep is essentially unavoidable. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. The soba broth is a bonito-and-kombu dashi (contains fish), the onigiri fillings include salmon and Noto pork, and tempura is fried in a shared setting. A plain rice onigiri (e.g., umeboshi) might be obtainable, but there is no confirmed plant-based offering — a vegan would need to ask directly and verify the broth/fillings.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市袋町1番1号 かなざわはこまち 2F
geocode_note: Geocoded from 石川県金沢市袋町1番1号 via GSI; coordinates fall within the Kanazawa box.
closed_days: 水曜（Wednesdays）
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, modern, family-friendly counter-and-table shop inside a shopping building — easy to walk into, point at the picture menu, and order soba or an onigiri even with only a few words of Japanese.
caveats: Opened April 2025; no Tabelog page located yet. Closed-day list on the official floor guide is given month-by-month but consistently falls on Wednesdays. Seat count (36) is at the upper edge for a "small" shop but it is an independent, locally-oriented soba/onigiri specialist.

bio.background: むすびそば opened in April 2025 on the 2nd floor of かなざわはこまち, a shopping complex about a one-minute walk from the historic Omicho Market in central Kanazawa. It is a specialist shop built around two simple staples: hand-rolled soba and freshly made rice balls (omusubi).
bio.specialty: Self-described as 自家製二八蕎麦とおむすびのお店 — a house-made nihachi (80% buckwheat / 20% wheat) soba kneaded and cut in-store using domestic buckwheat flour, served in a bonito-and-kombu dashi. Signature plates include the 海老天そば (shrimp tempura soba) and the むすびそば御膳 set of half-portion soba, onigiri, tempura and a small side. The onigiri come in about six varieties made with Ishikawa-grown rice, including Noto pork with shiso-miso, salmon, and umeboshi.
bio.philosophy: The shop's stated aim is 素朴で優しい美味しさ — a plain, gentle deliciousness in its soba and onigiri. The fuller set menu reportedly grew out of customer requests for something more substantial than soba alone.

anecdotes.text: The soba is a nihachi blend made from domestic buckwheat flour and hand-rolled inside the shop each day.
Onigiri are made with Ishikawa-grown rice and cooked fresh daily, in small handheld portions across about six fillings such as Noto pork with shiso-miso.

gf_cross_contamination.text: House soba is nihachi (buckwheat + wheat), kneaded and cut in-shop, so wheat flour is present throughout soba preparation and surfaces.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soba is served in a dashi-based tsuyu; typical Japanese soba tsuyu and soy sauce contain wheat. No wheat-free seasoning is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is a bonito-and-kombu dashi (contains fish), and onigiri fillings include salmon and Noto pork; tempura is fried in a shared setting.

cuisine: そば・うどん・和食 (soba, udon, washoku)
neighborhood: 近江町市場周辺・下近江町 (near Omicho Market)
address_ja: 石川県金沢市下近江町42
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市下近江町42 returned [136.658203, 36.571548], within the central Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 28 seats: table seating (4 tables x 5) plus a raised tatami platform (2 x 4).
closed_days: Wednesday (Tuesday lunch-only)
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed old-style noodle shop where pointing at the menu and a friendly "konnichiwa" will carry you through; English support is limited and payment is cash only.
gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle house: udon and soba (soba is typically blended with wheat flour), plus tempura, tonkatsu, and a dashi/sweet-soy broth that contains wheat. There is no gluten-free menu and the kitchen is wheat-heavy, so it is not safe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: The broth is a Kansai-style fish dashi made from urume sardine, mackerel and bonito, and signature dishes feature duck (kamo jibuni), so the menu is built around animal products. No documented vegan options.
caveats: Hours and closed days from a restaurant listing; broth/ingredient details from food blogs and a tourism feature. No dedicated allergen information published, so any gluten-free or vegan accommodation would need to be confirmed directly with the shop.

bio.background: Kadocho is widely called Kanazawa's oldest udon shop. The first generation, Wada Chohei, opened the original stall in front of the railway station in Komatsu, and as the rail line pushed north the family followed the tracks and resettled in Kanazawa, where the main shop now stands a short walk from the bustling Omicho Market. Across four generations the shop has been a humble neighborhood fixture for well over a century; the third generation, Yoshida Tarokichi, revived the business and founded the Kadocho association that at its peak counted more than thirty branches around the city. The main shop reopened in July 2020 after a renovation that added evening service and sake alongside the long-standing lunchtime trade.
bio.specialty: Soft, springy 'Kanazawa udon' in a sweet Kansai-style broth blended with Kanazawa's sweet soy sauce; the signature plate is the kamo jibuni set (duck and vegetables simmered with sudare-fu wheat gluten in a sweet-savory sauce, served with udon, salad and mixed rice). Other favorites include mizore udon with grated daikon, bonito and yuzu-kosho, tanuki udon, and Kaga-vegetable tempura.
bio.philosophy: Hand-made noodles using domestic wheat and an inherited 'kaeshi' base, kept affordable so that locals and travelers can share the same simple, warming bowl.

anecdotes.text: The shop is the root of what is now called Komatsu udon, having literally migrated up the rail line from Komatsu to Kanazawa with the spread of the railway.
Its mochi-mochi soft Kanazawa udon, served near Omicho Market, has been celebrated as a noodle beloved even by literary figures.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A wheat-noodle kitchen serving udon, soba, tempura and tonkatsu; flour and wheat-based broth are pervasive, making cross-contamination unavoidable for gluten-sensitive diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth combines Kansai dashi with Kanazawa sweet soy sauce; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Base broth is a fish dashi (urume sardine, mackerel, bonito), so even vegetable dishes are prepared in an animal-stock kitchen.

lead: キュリオ エスプレッソ ヴィンテージ デザイン
name_ja: キュリオ エスプレッソ ヴィンテージ デザイン
cuisine: Seattle-style espresso café — specialty coffee, sandwiches, baked goods
neighborhood: 安江町 (Yasue-cho), a few minutes' walk from Omichō Market
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering documented. The menu centers on wheat-based items — sandwiches on house bread, carrot cake, and other baked goods — so this is not a celiac-safe destination. Ask directly if you need GF.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. Signature items contain meat, eggs, and dairy (BBQ pork sandwich, bacon-and-egg breakfast sandwich, carrot cake). Espresso can be made with milk; plant-milk availability is unconfirmed, so a vegan should ask in advance.
geocode_note: Geocoded from address 石川県金沢市安江町1-13 via GSI; coordinates fall within the central Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 22 seats (counter and tables)
hours_raw: 08:00-15:00; closed Tuesday and Wednesday
closed_days: Tuesday, Wednesday
cultural_comfort_note: Run by an English-speaking, Seattle-experienced couple and popular with international visitors, so a foreign student can order comfortably in English.
caveats: Cash only — no cards, e-money, or QR payments. Open mornings to mid-afternoon only and closed Tuesday and Wednesday.

bio.background: Curio Espresso and Vintage Design was opened in April 2014 by a husband-and-wife team who had worked at a café in Seattle, the heartland of American specialty coffee, before bringing that culture home to Kanazawa.
bio.origin: Seattle-style café concept transplanted to a renovated traditional machiya (wooden townhouse) in central Kanazawa.
bio.specialty: Espresso drinks made from beans imported from Seattle and roasted in Kanazawa — five varieties roasted separately and blended for depth. Signature items include a homemade barbecue pork sandwich (tomato base with around ten spices, onions, on natural-yeast bread), a bacon-egg-and-cheese breakfast sandwich, a spiced carrot cake with chunky carrot, and an iced cinnamon latte with the cinnamon dissolved throughout.
bio.philosophy: Recreating Seattle's specialty-coffee experience while preserving the Japanese aesthetic of the old townhouse it occupies.

anecdotes.text: The space is filled with the owners' personal vintage collection, including antique cameras and sewing machines, and the shop still rings up orders on a working cash register made in 1943.
The couple import their coffee beans directly from Seattle and roast them in Kanazawa, roasting five bean types separately before blending.

positives.text: English-speaking owners with Seattle café experience, making it easy to ask about ingredients in English.

cuisine: 甘味処 / 生麩甘味 (namafu fresh wheat-gluten sweets, traditional Japanese sweets cafe)
gf_detail: Not gluten-free at all — in fact the opposite. The entire shop is built around namafu (生麩), which is fresh WHEAT gluten. The signature shira-tama namafu is essentially pure wheat protein. This is one of the least celiac-safe places imaginable and should be avoided entirely by anyone with celiac disease or wheat allergy.
vegan_detail: Many of the namafu sweets are plant-based: the signature shira-tama namafu is served with anko (red bean paste), kinako (roasted soybean powder), and mitarashi (soy-sauce-based sweet sauce), all of which are vegan. However some items are not — e.g. the soft-serve version (しら玉生麩ソフト) uses soy-milk soft serve which may be vegan, but standard soft serve and certain seasonal items may contain dairy. Vegans should confirm individual toppings/soft-serve. Note: nothing here is gluten-free.
geocode_note: GSI geocode of full address returned [136.667938, 36.572395], within the Kanazawa box. Located in the Higashi Chaya district.
neighborhood: 東山 / ひがし茶屋街 (Higashiyama, Higashi Chaya district)
seats_text: 20 seats, all table seating
hours_raw: 10:00-17:00 (L.O. 16:15); may close earlier when crowded. Closed year-end/New Year (年末年始).
closed_days: Open daily; closed only for year-end and New Year holidays (年末年始). May close earlier than 17:00 when busy.
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, tourist-friendly tea-district sweet shop with a ticket/QR wait system and table seating, where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will get you a beautiful plate of traditional sweets with ease.
caveats: This is the opposite of gluten-free: the entire menu is built on fresh wheat gluten (namafu). Strictly avoid if celiac or wheat-allergic. Plant-based eaters have good options but should confirm soft-serve and seasonal items. No reservations; no eating food outside the shop in the district; no parking.

bio.specialty: Shira-tama namafu (しら玉生麩) — chewy, mochi-like fresh wheat-gluten dumplings served with a choice of anko (red bean), kinako (roasted soybean flour), or mitarashi (sweet-savory) sauce; plus seasonal namafu sweets such as chilled namafu zenzai in summer and hot kinako-and-black-sugar versions in winter.
bio.philosophy: A place to rest and savor a single, refined traditional sweet while strolling the historic tea streets — the shop frames itself as a quiet pause in the middle of sightseeing.
bio.background: Fumuro Chaya is the tea-house and sweets outpost of Fumuroya (不室屋), one of Kanazawa's most storied makers of Kaga-fu (wheat gluten). The family traces its origin to Fumuro village; Kyuemon settled in Owari-cho (today's main shop) and his heir Rokuemon began making fu around February of Keio 1 (1865), naming the house 'Fumuroya' after the founding village. For over 150 years the company has carried on Edo-period techniques for traditional Kaga-fu — including its famous sudare-fu — while developing new products, and this Higashi Chaya shop lets visitors taste that craft as fresh-gluten sweets in a restored townhouse with a tatami room, veranda, and Kanazawa gold-leaf decoration.

anecdotes.text: Fumuroya was founded in Keio 1 (1865) when Rokuemon began producing fu, naming the business after the family's ancestral village of Fumuro.

The shop occupies a restored old townhouse in the Higashi Chaya district and presents its signature shira-tama namafu with three toppings (red bean, roasted soybean flour, and mitarashi sauce) in a traditional tatami setting.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The shop's entire concept is namafu — fresh wheat gluten — so wheat is the core ingredient throughout. There is no gluten-free path here; cross-contamination is moot because the food itself is wheat protein.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The mitarashi sauce topping is a soy-sauce-based sweet glaze, which typically contains wheat-derived soy sauce.

positives.text: Several signature options are plant-based: shira-tama namafu with anko (red bean), kinako (soybean flour), or mitarashi are all dairy- and egg-free.

cuisine: Boulangerie & French bistro (boulangerie et bistro) — house-baked bread, vegetable-rich morning sets, French-based bistro lunch
neighborhood: Nagamachi / Seseragi-dori
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市長町1-6-11 returned [136.651016, 36.566364], within the central Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 22 seats
hours_raw: Morning 8:00–11:00 (L.O. 10:30), Lunch & Cafe 12:00–15:30 (some sources note cafe until 18:00; bread retail sales 8:00–18:00). Closed Mondays plus irregular closures announced via SNS.
closed_days: Mondays (plus irregular closures announced on social media)
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, popular cafe-bakery where ordering is simple and pointing at the bread case works; a little Japanese helps but a friendly greeting and gestures will get you through.
gf_detail: This is a wheat-flour boulangerie whose core products are bread, croissants, quiche, croque madame and similar wheat-based dishes. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented, and it is not safe for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: The kitchen is built around bread, eggs, cheese, béchamel and meat (e.g. croque madame with homemade chicken ham and fried egg, quiche). The morning and lunch sets are vegetable-rich but not vegan — they include egg, dairy and often meat. A vegetarian could likely find some plant-forward sides (salad, carrot rape, ratatouille) or plain bread, but no vegan menu is documented; ask in person.
caveats: Hours vary slightly by source (cafe close time listed as 15:30 in some, 18:00 in others; bread retail 8:00–18:00). Irregular closures occur beyond the regular Monday closure — check social media before visiting.

bio.background: Hiramipan was founded in March 2011 in Kanazawa's Nagamachi district, along the willow-lined Seseragi-dori canal walk. The shop occupies a renovated Taisho-era ironworks (鉄工所跡) wrapped in climbing ivy, with vintage and antique furnishings inside that give it a storybook, Studio-Ghibli-like atmosphere. Founder Takahiro Hirame grew the brand over the years and incorporated it as Hiramipan Co., Ltd. in September 2017, later expanding into related ventures including a creperie, gallery and pizza venue.
bio.specialty: House-baked bread (croissants, melon pan, scones, canelés including the local 金澤カヌレ) and vegetable-rich morning sets — the croque madame set with homemade chicken ham, bechamel and cheddar topped with a fried egg is the signature, alongside quiche and avocado toast sets; lunch plates pair French bistro fare with salad, carrot rape and ratatouille.
bio.philosophy: The team explicitly resists being 'just an ordinary bakery,' treating bread and pastry as a medium for personal vision and design — comparable to how an illustrator uses a picture book or a designer uses clothing — and aiming to avoid generic, 'tacky' bread.

anecdotes.text: The shop is set in a renovated Taisho-era ironworks covered in ivy on Seseragi-dori, an aesthetic so distinctive it is often compared to a Studio Ghibli film.
The croque madame is a destination dish — guests come specifically for it at the 8:00 morning service.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A working boulangerie where wheat flour is used throughout for bread, croissants, quiche crust and bechamel; airborne flour and shared surfaces make this unsuitable for celiac diners.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Core dishes use egg, dairy (cheddar, bechamel) and meat (homemade chicken ham); shared kitchen and bakery equipment, no documented vegan separation.

positives.text: Morning and lunch sets are notably vegetable-forward, including salad, carrot rape and ratatouille alongside the mains.

lead: 手打ち蕎麦 ほやさけ
name_ja: 手打ち蕎麦 ほやさけ
cuisine: Handmade soba and udon (Kanazawa-style)
neighborhood: Higashiyama / Higashi Chaya District, central Kanazawa
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市東山1-26-6 returned [136.667618, 36.57275], within the central Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 29 seats: 9 counter, 4 table, 16 on the 2nd-floor tatami room
hours_raw: 11:00-18:00 daily; closes when sold out (売切れ次第終了)
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures); may close early once the day's noodles sell out
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, walk-in-only neighborhood soba house in a traditional machiya; staff likely speak little to no English, so point at the menu, bring cash, and be ready for the simple counter-and-tatami atmosphere.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core menu is soba (buckwheat noodle flour is typically cut with wheat) and wheat udon, served with dashi/soy-based tsuyu that normally contains wheat. No gluten-free preparation or allergen handling is documented; not safe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan destination. Soba and udon are served in bonito/kombu dashi-based broths (typically containing fish stock) with wheat-based soy sauce, and tempura is offered. A plain dish such as cold zaru soba might be possible but the dipping sauce almost certainly contains fish dashi, so vegans must ask in detail and should not assume anything is suitable.

bio.background: A small, owner-run handmade-soba shop tucked into a quaint traditional machiya (wooden townhouse) in Kanazawa's historic Higashi Chaya geisha district, a short walk from Kenrokuen and Kanazawa Castle.
bio.specialty: Hand-cut soba and Kanazawa-style udon; the kitchen is noted for thin, light-textured wheat udon made from komugi, alongside cold soba, mountain-yam (toro) dishes, and tempura.

anecdotes.text: Set in a quiet, somewhat tucked-away corner of the Higashi Chaya District, the shop is recognizable by its old-fashioned machiya facade and serves noodles only until the day's batch sells out.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free measures documented; soba and udon plus wheat-based tsuyu and tempura share the same kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Broths and dipping sauces are dashi/soy-based and not specified as wheat-free; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Noodle broths typically use bonito (fish) dashi; no vegan or plant-based broth is documented.

caveats: Vegan and gluten-free details are inferred from the standard soba/udon menu; no allergen or dietary information is published, so travelers with strict needs should confirm directly. No official website found.

lead: 近江町市場海鮮丼 魚旨
name_ja: 近江町市場海鮮丼 魚旨
cuisine: 海鮮丼・寿司 (seafood rice bowls and sushi)
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The kitchen builds its sushi and seafood bowls around Kanazawa Ohno soy sauce (wheat-based), and the menu centers on rice seasoned with vinegar and served with soy sauce. There is no documented gluten-free menu, gluten-free soy sauce, or allergen handling, so this is not safe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. This is a dedicated seafood-bowl and sushi counter; every signature dish is built on raw fish, seafood, fish roe, and a house tamago (egg). There are no documented plant-based options.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市下堤町19 近江町市場 Dエリア
geocode_note: Geocoded from chō-level address 石川県金沢市下堤町19 via GSI; result (36.570988, 136.655502) falls inside the central Kanazawa box and matches the Omicho Ichiba market location.
neighborhood: 近江町市場 (Omicho Market), Shimotsutsumi-machi / Musashigatsuji area, central Kanazawa
seats_text: About 21 seats: roughly 7 counter seats and around 14 table seats.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular holidays)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, friendly seafood-bowl counter inside the busy Omicho Market where tourists are common, so a smile and a few words of Japanese will carry you comfortably even with limited menu translation.
caveats: Hours are approximate ("around 11:00 to around 16:00") and the shop closes on an irregular schedule, so check before a special trip. A phone number is not publicly listed.

bio.background: A small, owner-run seafood-bowl and sushi shop tucked into the D-area of Kanazawa's Omicho Market, the city's historic 'kitchen.' It draws a loyal mix of locals and visiting regulars who come back for the fresh, seasonal fish.
bio.specialty: Seafood rice bowls (kaisendon) and sushi built on the day's seasonal fish. House favorites include the Uouma-don (around 2,480 yen) and the Omicho Kaisendon (around 2,900 yen) piled with many varieties of fish and finished with gold leaf.
bio.philosophy: A 'local production for local consumption' approach: the rice is Oku-Noto Koshihikari cooked with 'Hakusan sacred water,' the vinegar is pure rice vinegar from the former Tsurugi town, and the seasoning leans on Kanazawa Ohno soy sauce. The shop's stated aim is that the rice, vinegar, water, and soy sauce are all local so the sushi stays in balance.

anecdotes.text: The chef makes the tamago (egg omelet) by hand, and it has become a quiet favorite among customers.
Every core ingredient is sourced locally: Oku-Noto Koshihikari rice, Hakusan spring water, rice vinegar from the former Tsurugi town, and Ohno (Kanazawa) soy sauce.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shop seasons with Kanazawa Ohno soy sauce, a traditional wheat-fermented soy sauce, and sushi/bowls are served with soy sauce, so wheat gluten is present throughout.

vegan_cross_contact.text: This is a dedicated seafood and sushi counter; raw fish, roe, and egg are used across the entire menu, so cross-contact with animal products is unavoidable.

lead: 近江町食処 鮮彩えにし
name_ja: 近江町食処 鮮彩えにし
cuisine: 海鮮丼・刺身・寿司・のどぐろひつまぶし（近江町市場の鮮魚店「大口水産」直営の海鮮処）
gf_detail: This is a seafood restaurant whose dishes rely heavily on soy sauce (which contains wheat) for sashimi, kaisen-don and hitsumabushi, plus the nodoguro hitsumabushi tea-broth course. No gluten-free menu or celiac handling is documented. Not suitable for strict gluten-free or celiac diners without direct confirmation, and even then cross-contact in a busy market kitchen is likely.
vegan_detail: A fresh-fish-shop-run seafood specialist (sashimi bowls, sushi, nodoguro). Core offerings are fish and shellfish, and dashi/soy-based seasonings are used throughout. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented; not a suitable choice for plant-based diners.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市青草町88 近江町いちば館 2F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市青草町88 returned [136.656036, 36.571659], within the central Kanazawa box near Omicho Market (Musashigatsuji).
seats_text: Around 30-38 seats including 8 counter seats and private rooms for 4-6 guests; private events for 6-20 people can be accommodated (sources vary between 30 and 38).
hours_raw: Mon-Fri 11:00-14:00, 17:00-22:00; Sat-Sun 10:00-14:00, 17:00-22:00 (Tabelog). Official site lists lunch 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:30) and dinner 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:00); hours may vary with ingredient availability.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures; no fixed weekly closing day)
cultural_comfort_note: Easy for foreign students: it is a tourist-friendly market restaurant with English and Korean menus, card and e-money payment, and walk-ins welcome, though signature dishes run higher than a casual budget.
caveats: Hours and seat counts differ slightly between the official site and the listing aggregator; signature dishes (kaisen-don ~3,300 yen, nodoguro hitsumabushi ~5,280 yen) push a meal well over a tight budget. Not suitable for vegan, vegetarian, or gluten-free/celiac diners.

bio.background: Sensai Enishi is a seafood eatery on the 2nd floor of the Omicho Ichiba building inside Kanazawa's lively Omicho Market. It is directly run by Okuguchi Suisan, a fresh-fish wholesaler and shop within the same market, so the restaurant draws on the company's daily access to local catch from the Sea of Japan.
bio.origin: Located within 近江町いちば館 in Omicho Market, the centuries-old fish-and-produce market that locals call Kanazawa's kitchen.
bio.specialty: Two signature dishes define the menu: the Kaisen Enishi-don, a bowl piled with around 13 kinds of seafood such as tuna, nodoguro (blackthroat seaperch), uni, salmon roe, yellowtail, crab and sweet shrimp; and the Nodoguro Hitsumabushi, which uses a whole prized nodoguro and is eaten three ways - first with wasabi and soy, then mixed with uni and garnishes, and finally as ochazuke with a special broth made from the nodoguro bones.
bio.philosophy: Reflecting the market's hospitality tradition, the operator emphasizes おまけ (generous complimentary extras) and offering high freshness at good value to both tourists and locals.
roles: Operated by Okuguchi Suisan Group, a fresh-fish vendor in Omicho Market

anecdotes.text: The Kaisen Enishi-don is recommended to be eaten in stages: first enjoy the sashimi toppings dipped in soy sauce, then drizzle wasabi-mixed soy over the rice and seafood for the rest of the bowl.
The nodoguro hitsumabushi uses an entire nodoguro and ends with a tea-over-rice course using broth simmered from the fish's bones.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sashimi bowls, sushi and the hitsumabushi are served and eaten with soy sauce, which standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat; no wheat-free soy alternative is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is a dedicated seafood operation run by a fish wholesaler; all signature dishes are fish or shellfish, so cross-contact with animal products is unavoidable.

positives.text: English and Korean menus are available, which can help diners ask about ingredients.

cuisine: Kanazawa-style curry (gluten-free, sugar-free, flour-free; healthy individual shop)
gf_detail: The shop's entire curry concept is built around using no wheat flour and no sugar — the roux is thickened without flour and all curries are described as gluten-free. The official site and multiple write-ups state the curry is made 砂糖・小麦粉不使用 (no sugar, no wheat flour). This makes it one of the most gluten-aware spots in central Kanazawa. However, it is not a certified dedicated celiac kitchen; toppings, side items (pickles, soup) and cross-contamination handling are not explicitly documented, so highly sensitive celiacs should confirm specific items and shared-surface risk directly with staff.
vegan_detail: Has clear vegan-friendly options rather than being a fully vegan restaurant (chicken and beef curries are on the regular menu). The 豆カレー (bean curry) is made for vegetarian, macrobiotic, vegan and Muslim diners and excludes the five pungent roots (五葷: garlic, scallions, rakkyo, nira, asatsuki). A newer vegan curry, カカオとナッツのごちそうカレー (cacao & nut curry), is dairy-free, gluten-free and plant-based using coconut milk, nuts and okara-konjac. Vegan diners should confirm which curries and toppings are plant-based on the day, as the menu rotates seasonally.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市広坂1丁目2番18号 中村ビル2F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市広坂1丁目2番18号 returned [136.657257, 36.561756], within the Kanazawa box and consistent with the Hirosaka / 21st Century Museum area.
seats_text: 24 seats (6 counter, 18 table) per the listing; some write-ups describe a smaller intimate layout. Small individual shop on the 2nd floor.
hours_raw: Mon 11:00-14:00; Tue 11:00-15:00; Wed-Sat 11:00-19:00; Sun 11:00-16:00 (hours vary by day across sources; official site notes 11:00-19:30, closed Mondays). Lunch-focused; afternoon/evening availability varies.
closed_days: Hours vary by day; sources differ on a fixed closing day (the official site lists Mondays closed while the venue listing shows reduced Monday hours). Confirm current days before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: A welcoming, health-focused curry shop used to explaining its gluten-free and vegetarian options; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will get a foreign student a comfortable, no-reservation lunch.

bio.background: An independent curry specialist in central Kanazawa. The owner spent more than 15 years researching curry, including time overseas (such as Canada), before realizing a long-held dream of opening the shop around 2015. The result is a personal, ingredient-driven take on Kanazawa curry.
bio.specialty: A refined Kanazawa-style curry made with no sugar and no wheat flour, seasoned with 27 spices. The signature 金澤ななほしカレーセット lets diners choose two curries from options such as chicken, bean, and a seasonal curry, served with a nut-milk soup, original Japanese-style pickles, and a dessert of the day. Seasonal curries (e.g. pork with bamboo shoot and bitter melon, or squid in squid-ink base) rotate through the year. The bean curry is built for vegetarian/vegan/macrobiotic/Muslim diners with the five pungent roots omitted, and a cacao-and-nut vegan curry has been added.
bio.philosophy: Food that is delicious and makes the body feel good — gently made, light on the stomach, and accessible to people of every dietary style. The shop frames itself as offering 'the most Kanazawa-like Kanazawa curry in the world' with a spirit of omotenashi (hospitality).

anecdotes.text: The owner researched curry for over 15 years, including overseas study such as in Canada, before opening the shop around 2015 to realize a long-held dream.
The bean curry is made so vegetarian, macrobiotic, vegan and Muslim diners can all eat it, omitting the five pungent vegetables (garlic, scallion, rakkyo, nira, asatsuki).
Describes its curry as containing no sugar and no wheat flour and uses 27 spices, positioning itself as a body-friendly, healthy Kanazawa curry.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The shop is explicitly oriented toward dietary needs, stating it wants to serve diners of every food style, with a dedicated five-pungent-free bean curry for vegetarian/vegan/macrobiotic/Muslim guests.

positives.text: All curries are made with no wheat flour and no sugar, making the core dishes inherently gluten-free rather than relying on a single modified menu item.
Offers a dairy-free, gluten-free, plant-based cacao-and-nut vegan curry alongside the five-pungent-free bean curry.
The curry is described as additive-free and oil-light (no-oil/ノンオイル, 無添加 in the shop's own positioning), aimed at being easy on the stomach.

neighborhood: Hirosaka / Shiinoki Geihinkan, near Kanazawa City Hall (Korinbo & 21st Century Museum area)
caveats: Hours and the fixed closed day differ between the official site and the public listing; treat hours_week as approximate and confirm before visiting. Seat count and "no sugar/no wheat flour" are well documented; the chef's name and detailed biography are not published in the sources reviewed. Cross-contamination and dedicated-fryer practices are not documented, so celiac-sensitive diners should ask directly.

name_ja: 石臼挽きそば 蕎香
cuisine: 手打ちそば（石臼挽き）・天ぷら / Hand-cut stone-ground soba and tempura
gf_detail: This is a buckwheat soba specialist, but the noodles are not pure buckwheat (juwari) per the available info, and the shop relies on wheat-based soba/tsuyu and standard dashi/soy-based dipping sauce. No gluten-free menu, no allergen accommodation documented. Tempura and soba kitchens share equipment. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: A traditional soba shop whose tsuyu (dipping/broth) is made from katsuobushi/dashi, and the menu features tempura, sashimi sets, and egg dishes. No vegan options are documented and the broth is fish-based, so it is not suitable for vegans.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市田上さくら2丁目47番地
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [136.68956, 36.543907] for 田上さくら2丁目47; within the Kanazawa box. Note: this is the Tagami (田上) district in eastern/southeastern Kanazawa, NOT Musashimachi as the target metadata stated.
neighborhood: 田上さくら（金沢市東部・田上地区）
seats_text: Around 21 seats including counter and table seating; a small, bright shop comfortable for solo diners.
hours_raw: Weekdays 11:30-14:00 (L.O.); Sat/Sun/holidays 11:30-14:00 (L.O.) and 17:30-19:30 (L.O.). Closes early when the day's soba sells out.
closed_days: Closed Tuesdays (the official site also notes Mondays, open if Monday is a holiday); closes early when soba sells out, plus occasional irregular closures.
cultural_comfort_note: A small local soba shop with no English menu or allergy handling, so a foreign student would do best visiting with a Japanese-speaking guide or someone who can read the menu.
caveats: The original target metadata (Musashimachi location, female owner, つけ鴨せいろ duck dish) does not match this Tabelog listing. The actual restaurant at this URL is 石臼挽きそば 蕎香 in the Tagami (田上さくら) district, run by a male chef, with a mori/tempura soba menu and no duck dish. There is a separate, newer Musashimachi soba shop (そば処 杏庵) that serves つけ鴨せいろ — likely the source of the mix-up. Closed days and exact hours differ slightly between sources; confirm before visiting.

bio.origin: Born in Ehime Prefecture, raised in Kanazawa
bio.background: The owner-chef has been making soba since age 16, with decades of experience and additional training in Japanese cuisine and confectionery (pastry arts). He opened Kyouka on December 15, 2010.
bio.specialty: Stone-ground (ishiusu-biki) hand-cut buckwheat soba; signature dishes include mori soba, tempura mori (tenmori), and the weekday Kyoka Gozen set, alongside oroshi and tororo soba.
bio.philosophy: He pursues 'simplicity and refinement,' obsessing over how the buckwheat is stored, milled, and hand-made, and aims to serve quality soba in a relaxed, casual, no-reservation setting.

anecdotes.text: The shop has a white-toned, clean and refined interior designed to feel welcoming even to diners coming alone.
The chef grinds whole buckwheat beans on a stone mill in-house and finishes the noodles by hand, closing each day once the soba runs out.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Soba and tempura are prepared in the same kitchen; no allergen separation or dedicated equipment is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Traditional soba tsuyu/dipping sauce is built on soy sauce and dashi, both standard wheat- and fish-containing preparations.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth and dipping sauce are dashi-based (bonito/fish), and the menu includes tempura, sashimi sets and egg dishes, so vegan cross-contact is unavoidable.

lead: わ食場 はす家 本店
name_ja: わ食場 はす家 本店
cuisine: Kaga regional cuisine / seafood izakaya — jibuni, Kaga lotus root, local fish (nodoguro, sashimi)
gf_detail: No gluten-free program. This is a Kaga-cuisine seafood izakaya built around soy sauce, dashi, and fried/simmered dishes; jibuni is thickened with flour and seasoned with soy-based seasonings, and the menu relies heavily on wheat-containing soy sauce. No dedicated gluten-free menu, fryer, or allergen handling is documented. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: The restaurant is centered on fresh seafood from Kanazawa Port (nodoguro, sashimi) and Kaga dishes like duck jibuni; nearly everything involves fish, meat, or fish/bonito dashi. No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented. A vegetarian could perhaps ask about plain Kaga vegetable preparations, but dashi exposure is likely throughout.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市木倉町1-10 ビルC 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address 石川県金沢市木倉町1-10 via GSI; coordinates fall within the central Kanazawa box (Kiguramachi, Katamachi area).
neighborhood: 片町・木倉町 (Katamachi / Kiguramachi, near Korinbo)
seats_text: About 20-21 seats: counter seating plus table seats in the back.
hours_raw: Mon-Thu 17:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:30); Fri-Sun 17:00-24:00 (L.O. 23:30). Closed Wednesday (open by reservation only).
closed_days: Wednesday (open by reservation only)
cultural_comfort_note: A lively local Kaga-cuisine izakaya where a few words of Japanese and pointing at dishes will go a long way; little English is documented, but staff accept cards and cashless payment so ordering is manageable.

bio.background: Hasuya is a Kaga regional-cuisine seafood izakaya in the heart of Kanazawa's Katamachi/Kiguramachi nightlife district, just off Korinbo. The kitchen works on a local-production-for-local-consumption principle, building its menu around seasonal Kaga vegetables and fish landed at Kanazawa Port.
bio.specialty: Signature dishes include nodoguro (rich, fatty throat-fish) preparations, seasonal sashimi platters, and Kaga lotus root cooked many ways. House favorites are hassu-mushi (steamed lotus root with a chewy texture and gentle flavor) and kamo no jibuni, the classic Kaga simmered-duck dish. A set such as the 'Gold Coast Umami Set' bundles nodoguro, local simmered items, and steamed lotus root.
bio.philosophy: Local production for local consumption: showcasing the seasonal vegetables and seafood of Ishikawa in dishes meant to be both beautiful to look at and delicious to eat.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name plays on renkon (lotus root), which the kitchen treats as a signature ingredient across the menu.
There is a sister location, Hasuya Kanazawa Ekimae-ten, near Kanazawa Station, making the Katamachi shop the honten (main store).

gf_cross_contamination.text: Kaga cuisine such as jibuni is thickened with flour and seasoned with wheat-based soy sauce; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The menu is built on Japanese seasonings (soy sauce, dashi); standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. No wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes rely on fish/bonito dashi and the kitchen centers on seafood and meat, so plant-only dishes would likely still contact animal-based dashi.

caveats: Hours/closed-day detail varies slightly between the official site and listings (Wednesday closed but open by reservation). Seat count reported as about 20-21. Owner/chef name is not documented. under30 budget is unsure: typical spend is reported around 4,000-6,000 yen per person, so a light visit could be under 30 USD but a full dinner with drinks likely exceeds it.

cuisine: 喫茶店・サイフォン珈琲・甘味（夜はバー）/ Retro machiya cafe with siphon coffee, herbal tea, original curry & sweets by day; whisky/wine/calvados bar by night
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination and not celiac-safe. The food menu centers on house cheesecake, gateau chocolat, cake, and an original eggplant curry over saffron rice (curry roux/sauce typically contains wheat). There is no documented gluten-free menu, gluten-free preparation, or allergen handling. A celiac traveler should not assume any dish here is safe.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan or vegetarian destination. The signature sweets (cheesecake, gateau chocolat) contain dairy and eggs. The Gauche-style eggplant curry on saffron rice may be vegetable-forward but is not documented as vegan and could contain dairy/meat-based stock. Siphon coffee (Toraja blend) and herbal teas are available and can be enjoyed black/plant-based. Vegans would need to ask directly; no plant-based menu is documented.
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 石川県金沢市東山一丁目16番5号, within the Kanazawa bounding box.
neighborhood: 東山・ひがし茶屋街 (Higashi Chaya District)
seats_text: 14 seats: 2 counter seats, two 2-person tables, two 4-person tables
hours_raw: Mon, Wed-Sat 11:00-01:00 (next day); Sun 11:00-00:00. Tea time 11:00-19:00, Bar time 19:00 onward. Closed Tuesdays.
closed_days: Tuesday (火曜日)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, atmospheric machiya hidden on a back lane where the owner curates a quiet adult mood, so come relaxed and ready to communicate mostly in Japanese; daytime siphon coffee is the easiest entry point for a foreign student.
caveats: Day and night are two different experiences: it is a calm siphon-coffee tea house until 19:00, then a serious late-night whisky/wine/calvados bar. During tea time, payment may be cash only even though cards (VISA/Master/JCB/AMEX/Diners) are accepted at the bar. The lattice-window facade hides the interior, so it is easy to walk past.

bio.background: Tucked into a quiet back lane of Kanazawa's historic Higashi Chaya geisha district, Gauche occupies a charming old machiya (traditional wooden townhouse). Step off the bustling main avenue and the atmosphere shifts dramatically: a latticed-window facade keeps the interior hidden from passersby while those inside enjoy a soft view out. The space leads a double life, opening as a tranquil tea house from late morning and transforming into an intimate adult bar after 19:00.
bio.specialty: By day, siphon-brewed coffee is the star: made with a Toraja blend and brewed cup by cup, served with a refill pot for roughly three cups while you settle by the lattice window. Sweets include a house cheesecake and gateau chocolat, and the kitchen's original 'Gauche-style eggplant curry' layers plenty of sliced eggplant over saffron rice scented with cumin. By night it becomes a connoisseur's bar with roughly 300 whiskies, 150 wines, 80 calvados and 25 sherries, plus calvados highballs and cigars.
bio.philosophy: An intentionally intimate, unhurried 'adult time' aesthetic. The owner curates the mood down to the music, with Edith Piaf on the sound system and shelves of Kenji Miyazawa's books to read by the window, encouraging guests to linger and slow down.
bio.origin: The name 'Gauche' (ゴーシュ) is widely understood to reference Kenji Miyazawa's beloved story 'Gorsch the Cellist' (セロ弾きのゴーシュ); copies of Miyazawa's books are kept on the shelves for guests, reinforcing the literary connection, though the establishment does not explicitly state this in the sources reviewed.

anecdotes.text: Each cup of siphon coffee is brewed individually and comes with a refill pot for about three cups, and guests can read Kenji Miyazawa's books from the shelves while seated by the latticed window.
Unusually for the Higashi Chaya area, it stays open late into the night, with a bar selection of around 300 whiskies, 150 wines and 80 calvados.

positives.text: Siphon-brewed coffee (Toraja blend) and herbal teas are available and can be ordered plain, which are naturally suitable options for plant-based and gluten-avoiding guests.

cuisine: 薬膳・発酵・玄米菜食カフェ (yakuzen medicinal / fermented / brown-rice plant-based home-style café, with raw sweets)
gf_detail: The kitchen states it avoids wheat flour (小麦粉), white sugar, chemical seasonings, additives and dairy, and some coverage describes gluten-free medicinal dishes and raw sweets, so flour-based dishes appear to be avoided. However there is no documented dedicated gluten-free protocol, no statement on cross-contamination, and fermented seasonings (e.g. miso/shoyu) common in yakuzen and fermented cooking typically contain wheat. Celiac travelers must confirm each dish and seasoning in advance; treat as ask, not celiac-safe.
vegan_detail: A home-style medicinal (yakuzen) and fermented-food café that explicitly avoids dairy and emphasizes organic vegetables, herbs, brown rice and fermented foods, with multi-vegetable smoothies and dessert plates. The cooking is strongly plant-centered and dairy-free, so it reads as largely vegan-friendly, but there is no published statement that the daily set menu is 100% animal-free (egg, fish-based dashi, honey are not confirmed excluded). Vegans should state requirements when reserving.
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県能美市松が岡5-70 returns lng 136.531403, lat 36.438244, which is in Nomi City and falls OUTSIDE the Kanazawa bounding box (lat 36.50–36.64, lng 136.58–136.72) on both axes. The Tabelog '四十万' label is only the area-grouping code; the actual premises are roughly 35–45 minutes by car south of Kanazawa Station. Coordinates set to null per the out-of-box rule.
neighborhood: 能美市松が岡 (Matsugaoka, Nomi City) — not central Kanazawa
seats_text: Small home-style café; no published seat count. Seating includes counter, sofa/table, a raised tatami koagari and a terrace.
closed_days: Wednesday and Sunday (some listings say Tuesday or Wednesday plus Sunday); closures are irregular and the set-menu lunch is reservation-only with monthly booking that fills almost immediately.
cultural_comfort_note: A reservation-only, Japanese-speaking home café deep in rural Nomi (not Kanazawa) that is hard to book and best approached with some Japanese and a confirmed advance reservation.
caveats: LOCATION MISMATCH: although tagged as 四十万 on Tabelog, this café is physically in 能美市松が岡 (Nomi City), ~35–45 min by car from Kanazawa Station, and geocodes outside the Kanazawa box — it is NOT a central Kanazawa eatery. It is a tiny home-based café offering only a daily set lunch (~¥2,500–3,000), reservation-only, cash-only, with bookings that sell out within minutes/months. Reading is にのん/Ninon (Instagram @ninon_cafe); the brief's "-bion-" styling is unverified. Vegan and gluten-free status are inferred from the kitchen's stated avoidance of dairy, wheat flour, white sugar and additives but are not formally certified — confirm directly before visiting.

bio.background: 日穏 (read 'Ninon') is a tiny home-style cafe run as an 'おうちごはん' (home-cooking) kitchen in the Matsugaoka hills of Nomi, Ishikawa. It opened in spring 2020 and built a near-cult local following: its single daily set lunch is reservation-only and the monthly booking window is known to fill within minutes, with waits reported to stretch many months.
bio.origin: 能美市松が岡, Ishikawa (rural southern Ishikawa, outside Kanazawa)
bio.specialty: A daily-changing yakuzen (medicinal) set lunch built around organic seasonal vegetables, brown rice and fermented foods — think beet soup, big colorful vegetable salads, and a dessert/raw-sweets plate — served as a ~¥2,500–3,000 course with drink.
bio.philosophy: Cooking for health and beauty 'from the inside out': the kitchen avoids wheat flour, white sugar, chemical seasonings, additives and dairy, leaning on organic produce, herbs and fermentation.

anecdotes.text: The cafe avoids wheat flour, white sugar, chemical seasonings, additives and dairy, focusing on organic ingredients and fermented foods for health-from-within cooking.
It is so popular that the daily set-lunch reservations fill up within minutes during the monthly booking period.

positives.text: Kitchen states it does not use dairy, wheat flour, white sugar, chemical seasonings or additives, building dishes around organic vegetables, brown rice and fermented foods.
Some coverage describes gluten-free medicinal cuisine and raw sweets, suggesting flour-based and refined items are avoided.

lead: 金沢カレー＆ハントンライス だいだい 橙
name_ja: 金沢カレー＆ハントンライス だいだい 橙
cuisine: 金沢カレー・ハントンライス（金沢2大ご当地グルメの洋食店）
neighborhood: 片町（香林坊東急スクエア裏）
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature dishes are Kanazawa curry (thick wheat-flour roux) and Hunton rice (fried white fish and shrimp in wheat batter over omelette rice), all built on wheat. The owner makes the demi-glace, tartare sauce, and curry roux by hand, but these are wheat-based. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented; not safe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The menu centers on egg-based omelette rice, fried white fish, shrimp tempura, breaded cutlets, and meat-based Kanazawa curry. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市片町2-8-10 returned [136.652527, 36.56242], within the Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 13 seats — 7 counter, 4 table, plus 2 (per Tabelog: カウンター7席、テーブル4席、2席). Official site lists 13席.
hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 (料理 L.O. 14:30 / ドリンク L.O. 14:30; Tabelog lists L.O. 14:45). Closed Tuesdays. Note: やむを得ずお休みする可能性がございます (may close unexpectedly).
closed_days: 火曜日（祝後日も休みの場合あり）
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, casual lunch-only counter spot run by the owner — best visited with a guide or a Japanese-speaking friend, as English support and an English menu are not documented; come with cash.

bio.background: Opened on April 29, 2023 in Kanazawa's Katamachi district, just behind Korinbo Tokyu Square, this warm, wood-toned Western-style diner brings together Kanazawa's two great local specialties under one roof: rich Kanazawa curry and Hunton rice.
bio.specialty: The flagship Daidai Hunton Rice (¥2,000) is omelette rice crowned with fried white fish, two jumbo shrimp tempura, and a house tartare sauce given a bright lift by daidai (bitter orange) citrus — the namesake of the shop. The other pillar is a thick, generous Kanazawa-style katsu curry (¥1,400) with a crisp cutlet.
bio.philosophy: Owner Isao Shimabukuro makes the demi-glace, tartare sauce, and curry roux by hand without cutting corners, saying he is happiest when everyone smiles.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name '橙 (daidai)' comes from the bitter-orange citrus folded into the tartare sauce as a secret ingredient, giving the Hunton rice a refreshing aroma that plays off the crispy shrimp.
Owner Isao Shimabukuro: 'I'm happy when everyone smiles by making hand-crafted demi-glace sauce, tartare sauce, and curry roux without cutting corners.'

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dishes rely on wheat-flour curry roux and battered/breaded fried fish, shrimp, and cutlets cooked in shared fryers; high gluten exposure is expected.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is built around egg, fish, shrimp, and meat; vegan-safe preparation is not documented.

caveats: Lunch-only (11:00-15:00), cash only, no reservations (private buyouts accepted), closed Tuesdays and may close unexpectedly. Not suitable for gluten-free or vegan diners. Hours per official site list L.O. 14:30 while Tabelog lists L.O. 14:45.

cuisine: そば懐石・和食 (soba kaiseki / Japanese cuisine with house-made soba)
neighborhood: 東山・ひがし茶屋街 (Higashi Chaya District)
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The restaurant's signature is buckwheat soba, and Japanese soba is almost always cut with wheat flour (nihachi style); the kaiseki courses also use wheat-based tempura, soy sauce, and dashi seasonings. There is no documented gluten-free menu or accommodation. This is not celiac-safe.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The fixed soba-kaiseki courses are built on seasonal seafood (sashimi, uni, conger eel), duck, dashi, and other animal ingredients. No vegan course or documented plant-based accommodation. As completely-reservation-based course-only dining, options are fixed by the kitchen.
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市東山1-23-10 returned [136.667953, 36.572041], within the Kanazawa box.
seats_text: About 10 seats: two 4-person tables and one 2-person table; solo diners accepted. A small townhouse run by a married couple.
hours_raw: 12:00-14:00 (L.O. 13:00), 17:30-20:00 (L.O. 19:30). Closed Sundays and the first Monday of the month. Completely reservation-based (advance booking required).
closed_days: Sundays; also closed the first Monday of each month
cultural_comfort_note: A serene, formal course-only counter and table restaurant run by a married couple where reservations are essential and ordering is fixed; basic Japanese (or a Japanese-speaking helper to book) will make a foreign student's visit far smoother.
caveats: Completely reservation-based, course-only, and on the higher end (lunch from roughly 9,000 yen, dinner from roughly 13,000 yen). Not suitable for gluten-free or vegan diners. Chef's given-name reading varies across sources (most give Tajiri Atsushi).

bio.origin: The chef is from Sendai in northeastern Japan; he trained at a culinary school in Tokyo and honed his craft in Yamagata and Tokyo.
bio.background: He opened Kyomi Kai in August 2015 in Kanazawa, his wife's hometown. The couple runs the small townhouse together.
bio.specialty: Soba kaiseki: refined seasonal Japanese courses with house-made buckwheat woven throughout, including coarse-ground sobagaki (buckwheat dumpling/cake), hand-cut soba noodles to finish, soba sushi, and dishes pairing soba with seafood such as conger eel, crab, and karasumi (bottarga).
bio.philosophy: Quiet, understated elegance: delicate course cooking that threads soba through each stage rather than serving it as a standalone bowl, letting seasonal local ingredients lead.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant occupies a roughly century-old townhouse from the early Taisho era, fronted by Kanazawa's characteristic kimusuko wooden lattice, tucked in a back lane behind the Higashi Chaya geisha district.
A reviewer who balked at the per-person price before visiting came away astonished at the value compared with big-city dining, praising the airy, sake-lees-seasoned coarse-ground sobagaki.
Kyomi Kai earned a Michelin star (Toyama-Ishikawa 2016 and Hokuriku 2021) and was named to the Tabelog Japanese-cuisine WEST Top 100 in 2025.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen centers on buckwheat soba (typically cut with wheat) plus tempura and soy/dashi seasonings, so wheat is pervasive across the fixed courses.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Courses are built on seafood, duck, and dashi; no plant-based course is documented and the menu is fixed by the kitchen.

cuisine: 金沢スパイスカレー（民家改装の個人店、カレーとお酒）
neighborhood: 茨木町（香林坊・片町近くの住宅街、竪町から徒歩圏）
gf_detail: A spice curry shop where the curry base (clam broth with coconut milk, achaar, etc.) and accompaniments are not gluten-free and no gluten-free accommodation is documented. Spice curries here typically contain wheat-based thickeners and seasonings, and there is no published allergen handling. Not suitable for celiacs; if strictly gluten-free, ask in advance, but assume it is not safe.
vegan_detail: The rotating menu centers on seafood (shrimp, clams, scallops, salmon), chicken, egg, dairy (blue cheese), and clam/fish-based broths. No vegan or vegetarian option is documented. Not a vegan destination.
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI from address 石川県金沢市茨木町24-5; result lat 36.558258 / lng 136.657501 falls within the central Kanazawa box.
seats_text: About 10 seats — counter seating plus one shared table, in a small converted house.
hours_raw: Sources differ after the 2025 reopening. Tabelog: Tue–Sat 11:00–14:00 & 18:00–21:00, Sun 11:00–14:00 (until sold out), closed Mon. A curry-guide source lists post-reopening lunch-only hours 11:00–15:00 (L.O. 14:30), closed Mon & Tue. Hours are subject to change; confirm via the shop's Instagram.
