closed_days: Monday (one source also lists Tuesday closed after the 2025 reopening); Sunday until sold out. Hours/closures are irregular — confirm via Instagram.
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, owner-run hideaway in a converted house with little English signage and contact mainly by Instagram, so basic Japanese and an easygoing attitude help a lot.
caveats: Tiny owner-run shop (about 10 seats); the single owner cooks each plate to order, so service can be slow and the shop may sell out early. Cash only, no parking, no takeout, no children's seating, all non-smoking. Located in a maze-like residential area that can be hard to find. Hours and closed days changed after a temporary closure and July 2025 reopening — confirm on Instagram before going.

bio.background: Gecko-do began as a curry shop in March 2020, born from a bar called GECKOS that the owner previously ran in Kanazawa's Shintenchi area. The owner runs the place solo out of a small converted house with a Showa-era exterior and an exotic, foreign-feeling interior tucked into a residential maze near Korinbo/Katamachi. In May 2025 the shop temporarily closed, but devoted regulars rallied to revive it, and it reopened in July 2025 keeping the original style and flavors.
bio.specialty: Spice curry that ignores convention, with a rotating lineup (often two varieties at a time). The standout is the Seafood Coconut Curry — shrimp, clams, scallops and butter-seared salmon over long-grain rice in a clam-broth-and-coconut-milk base, served with onion achaar and a boiled egg. Other rotating curries have included squid-ink seafood, chicken blue cheese, and chickpea-and-chicken. Guests are welcomed with hibiscus tea and offered several vinegars to vary the flavor mid-meal.
bio.philosophy: The owner cooks 'without attachment to the concept or conventional form of curry' — the guiding question is simply what tastes delicious, with each plate prepared individually rather than batch-cooked.

anecdotes.text: After the shop closed in May 2025, fans of Gecko-do came together to revive it, and it reopened on July 11, 2025 — just two months later — preserving the original taste and style.
The owner works alone and prepares each curry plate one at a time rather than in pre-made batches, so meals take extra time to come out.

cuisine: 海鮮・立ち飲み（角打ち）／のどぐろ炙り刺し・刺身・海鮮丼・せんべろセット
gf_detail: This is a fish-market standing-drink counter run by a seafood wholesaler. Core items are sashimi, torched nodoguro (blackthroat seaperch), seafood bowls and a senbero (cheap drink) set, all of which lean on Japanese soy sauce — which is brewed with wheat — and there is no documented gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or allergen handling. Not celiac-safe; a celiac traveler would at minimum need to bring their own GF tamari and confirm in person.
vegan_detail: A seafood specialist whose entire menu is built around raw and grilled fish, oysters, sea urchin and scallops. There are no documented vegan or vegetable-only dishes; this is not a vegan-friendly destination.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市上近江町21-1
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned [136.657532, 36.570911] for 石川県金沢市上近江町21-1; within the Kanazawa box and consistent with the Omicho Market location.
seats_text: 12 seats; primarily a standing/counter market eatery (tachinomi / kakuuchi style)
neighborhood: 近江町市場（上近江町）
closed_days: 水曜日（Wednesday）
cultural_comfort_note: A bustling, no-frills market stand where two older women serve the counter and little to no English is expected, so a smile, pointing at the senbero set, and a few Japanese phrases will carry you a long way.
caveats: Daytime-only spot (roughly 9-10am to 3pm) inside Omicho Market, closed Wednesdays; cash plus PayPay QR accepted but no credit cards; reservations generally not taken. Soy sauce (wheat) is used throughout and there is no vegan or gluten-free provision, so it suits seafood-loving omnivores rather than dietary-restricted diners.

bio.background: Tucked into a corner of Kanazawa's famous Omicho Market, this is the direct-run counter of Daimatsu Suisan, a long-standing local fish merchant. Because the same company sources the fish, the counter can put genuinely fresh seafood in front of you at market prices rather than restaurant markups. Until the summer of 2023 it operated as a robatayaki-style grill called Omicho Shunsai-yaki; that July the grill format was retired and the shop was renamed simply Omicho Shunsai, reframed as a casual standing spot so that, in the company's words, more visitors could taste what Daimatsu Suisan at Omicho has to offer.
bio.specialty: Torched nodoguro (blackthroat seaperch) aburi-sashi, raw oysters, sashimi platters, seafood rice bowls, and a signature senbero set pairing a draft beer with assorted sashimi and a small side for around 1,000 yen.
bio.philosophy: Let a working fish wholesaler's freshness speak for itself at an everyday, approachable price.

anecdotes.text: The popular senbero set comes with a draft beer, three kinds of sashimi and a small side dish for around 1,000 yen, making it an ideal quick market drink, with the torched nodoguro's fat melting just enough to pair beautifully with sake.
Open from around 9am, it's a stand-and-eat spot for oysters, sea urchin and scallops, where two older women run the counter and look after customers.

With the July 2023 change of format the shop dropped its robatayaki grill and renamed itself Omicho Shunsai, centering the menu on the popular senbero set alongside raw oysters and torched nodoguro sashimi.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The menu centers on sashimi, torched nodoguro and seafood bowls, which are eaten with Japanese soy sauce (brewed with wheat); no wheat-free soy alternative is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: An entirely seafood-based market counter (oysters, sea urchin, scallops, sashimi, nodoguro) with no documented plant-based options; cross-contact with fish is unavoidable here.

cuisine: 餃子・町中華
gf_detail: This is a gyoza (dumpling) specialty shop. The signature White Gyoza is defined by its thick, chewy wheat-flour skin, so the core menu is fundamentally wheat-based and not safe for celiacs. Dipping uses regular (wheat-based) soy sauce/vinegar. No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or allergen accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: The gyoza filling is meat-based (pork). This is a meat-dumpling specialist with no documented vegan or vegetable-only options; not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市もりの里1丁目259
geocode_note: Geocoded from GSI to lat 36.550388, lng 136.689133 (within Kanazawa box). NOTE: The target brief listed the area as 野町 (south of Korinbo/Katamachi), but every source confirms the actual address is もりの里1-259, near Kanazawa University in the northeast of the city, on the river — NOT central Kanazawa near 野町.
seats_text: 1F counter (about 27-35 seats) plus 2F table and tatami seating (paid private tatami rooms); roughly 50 seats total. Source pages vary slightly on the exact count.
neighborhood: もりの里（金沢大学近く、犀川沿い）
hours_raw: 1F 11:00〜21:00 / 2F 11:00〜21:00 / テイクアウト 11:00〜21:00（人手不足により時間短縮）。official site lists 11:00〜02:00 but Tabelog notes current shortened hours to 21:00.
closed_days: 水曜日、年末年始
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, no-reservation local gyoza counter where you can point at the famous round dumplings and order with a few words of Japanese, though expect a wait at peak times and little English.

bio.origin: Kanazawa (もりの里, near Kanazawa University, beside the river)
bio.background: A beloved Kanazawa gyoza institution affectionately known to locals simply as Dai-nana ("the 7th"). It belongs to the White Gyoza (ホワイト餃子) technical-chain lineage whose head shop is in Noda, Chiba, but it has become a soul-food landmark of Kanazawa in its own right, drawing long lines even on weekdays.
bio.specialty: Its signature is the White Gyoza: round, plump dumplings with a thick, chewy wheat skin that turns crisp and golden when deep-fried in oil. Around 90% of customers order them. The shop also serves steamed and boiled (water) dumplings and gyoza set meals at specialist-shop prices.
bio.philosophy: The shop frames itself as a specialist that can offer this distinctive product at low prices precisely because it focuses on gyoza alone, urging diners to eat them piping hot, fresh off the griddle.

anecdotes.text: Locals call it simply 'Dai-nana' (the 7th), and roughly 90% of customers order the White Gyoza; the round, thick-skinned dumplings deep-fried to a crisp are its defining dish, often with waits of an hour even on weekdays.
The riverside upper-floor rooms offer seasonal views — cherry blossoms in spring, ayu fishing in summer, autumn foliage, and snow in winter.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Gyoza are fried/cooked in shared oil and equipment; the entire core menu is wheat-flour dumplings, so heavy gluten cross-contact is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard gyoza dipping sauce (soy sauce / vinegar / chili oil) is used; no gluten-free soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dumplings are pork-based and cooked on shared meat equipment; no vegan options documented.

caveats: The target brief's area (野町, south of Korinbo/Katamachi) is incorrect: every source confirms the real address is もりの里1-259, near Kanazawa University in the city's northeast, beside the river — not central Kanazawa. Hours are currently shortened to 11:00-21:00 (Tabelog cites staffing shortage) versus the official site's listed 11:00-02:00, so verify before a late visit. Seat count varies slightly by source (~27-35 on 1F counter plus 2F seating).

lead: 焼肉ホルモン 芝生
name_ja: 焼肉ホルモン 芝生
cuisine: ホルモン・焼肉（Yakiniku / grilled beef offal）
neighborhood: 金沢駅東口／此花町・七ツ屋エリア
gf_detail: This is a yakiniku/horumon (grilled beef offal) restaurant whose signature dishes revolve around house white and black sauces — the black sauce is a sweet-savory miso preparation — and a finishing bowl of wheat noodles. Soy- and miso-based marinades plus wheat noodles make this menu unsuitable for celiacs, and no gluten-free accommodation is documented. The site mentions allergy information is available, so a GF or wheat-sensitive diner should ask directly, but it is not a celiac-safe destination.
vegan_detail: The entire concept is grilled beef and beef offal (horumon, tongue, throat, heart). There are no documented vegan or vegetarian set offerings; the menu is built around meat. Not a vegan-friendly destination.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市此花町8-3 KONOHANA355 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI from 石川県金沢市此花町8-3; result lat 36.577866, lng 136.651138 falls within the Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 19 seats: counter seating for 17, plus 2 tables (4-tops)
hours_raw: Tuesday–Sunday & holidays: 17:00–00:00 (L.O. 23:00); Monday closed
closed_days: Mondays (contact for holiday/national-holiday adjustments)
cultural_comfort_note: The shop provides an English menu and supports reservations from international visitors, so a foreign student can dine here comfortably with minimal Japanese.
caveats: Meat-and-offal focused; not suitable for gluten-free, vegan, or vegetarian diners. Soy/miso sauces and wheat finishing noodles are central to the experience.

bio.background: A compact horumon-yakiniku counter about three minutes' walk from the east exit of Kanazawa Station, just past the Tsuzumi-mon gate. The shop is built around a single idea: pursuing what it calls the ultimate way to eat meat.
bio.specialty: The house Shibafu course is served in three movements — a 'white sauce' stage featuring premium cuts such as upper tongue and throat, a 'black sauce' stage of beef heart and offal dressed in a sweet-savory miso preparation, and a finishing bowl of special thick noodles designed to soak up the accumulated meat juices. Staff grill each piece individually in front of the diner using a tilted-griddle technique to lock in the juices.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen frames good horumon as the mastery of five elements: the freshness of the meat, the sauce, the griddle, the grilling technique, and the finishing noodles.

anecdotes.text: The signature service is a tilted-griddle grilling method: rather than letting diners cook freely, staff grill each cut individually and tilt the plate so the flavorful juices are preserved and served at the optimal moment.
Sourcing prioritizes freshness, so the available cuts on the menu can change with what arrives.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Signature dishes center on house white and black sauces, the black being a sweet-savory miso preparation, and the meal ends with wheat finishing noodles — indicating soy/miso/wheat are integral and not avoidable.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is entirely grilled beef and beef offal cooked on shared griddles; no plant-based offerings documented.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The official site notes that allergy information is available (linked on the site), so diners with allergies can request details.

positives.text: An English menu and international reservation support are offered, easing communication about ingredients for non-Japanese diners.

cuisine: アンティーク調の老舗喫茶店（個人経営）
neighborhood: 長町武家屋敷エリア
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市長町2-3-1 returned [136.649918, 36.563747], within the central Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 10 seats; small, intimate space with a view of an interior garden (中庭).
hours_raw: Mon-Fri 09:00-13:00; Sat/Sun/Holidays 09:00-14:00
closed_days: Not clearly published; hours differ on weekends/holidays. Call ahead to confirm.
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, traditional owner-run kissaten in a 140-year-old house; little to no English is likely, so basic Japanese phrases and a calm, quiet manner will help.
gf_detail: No gluten-free information is documented. As a traditional Japanese tea house serving sweets such as zenzai (anko/red bean) with nama-fu (wheat gluten cake) and souffle (wheat flour), most items are not gluten-free. A celiac traveler should not assume any safe options; ask directly.
vegan_detail: No vegan information is documented. Some menu items (matcha, certain anko-based sweets) may be plant-based, but souffle/cream items contain dairy and eggs, and no claims about vegan preparation exist. Ask staff about specific items.
caveats: Closed days are not clearly published and hours vary between weekdays and weekends/holidays; phone is non-public on the listing. Confirm timing locally. No official website found.

bio.background: Nigokorei is a quiet, antique-styled tea house tucked into Kanazawa's Nagamachi samurai-residence district, set inside a wooden machiya estimated to be more than 140 years old. The space is filled with the atmosphere of old Japan, with seating arranged so guests can look out over a traditional interior garden (中庭).
bio.specialty: Tea-house sweets served on carefully chosen pottery, including chestnut zenzai (栗入りぜんざい) made with nama-fu, a souffle set (スフレセット) with rich whipped cream, and ceremonial matcha.
bio.philosophy: The proprietor places special emphasis on fine ceramics, particularly local Kutani ware, presenting tea and sweets in carefully selected vessels so the tableware is part of the experience.

anecdotes.text: The cafe is known for striking, eye-catching hina dolls displayed inside, adding to its distinctive retro atmosphere.
Visitors describe the chestnut zenzai's chewy nama-fu against rich, sweet anko, and a moist, gently sweet souffle that pairs well with coffee.

cuisine: Wa-café / kissaten (traditional Japanese tea house café) — matcha, wagashi, anmitsu and gold-leaf sweets
neighborhood: ひがし茶屋街 (Higashi Chaya District)
address_ja: 石川県金沢市東山1-13-24 金澤しつらえ 2F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja via GSI; coordinates fall within the central Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 36 seats — seven 4-person tables and four 2-person tables, some overlooking the courtyard/willow; sofa seating noted on official site.
hours_raw: 10:00-17:00 (L.O. 16:30), daily. 1F gallery shop 金澤しつらえ open 9:00-18:00.
closed_days: Open year-round; closed only January 1st (New Year's Day).
cultural_comfort_note: A polished, tourist-friendly café in a famous gold-leaf brand's gallery; an English Tabelog page and card/QR payment exist, so a foreign student can visit comfortably with little Japanese.
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. The menu centers on wagashi, parfaits and matcha sweets that typically involve wheat or shared kitchen prep, and many sweet/sauce components use gluten-containing ingredients. Some items (e.g. anmitsu with agar and azuki) may be naturally wheat-light, but cross-contact is unaddressed — a celiac visitor would need to ask in detail and should not assume safety.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan venue and no vegan menu is advertised. Some signature items are plant-based by nature (the gold-leaf cream anmitsu uses domestic agar, red bean paste and matcha warabimochi), but parfaits and afternoon-tea sets typically include dairy/cream and the kitchen is not vegan-oriented. A vegan would need to ask which items omit dairy/eggs.
caveats: Operated by the gold-leaf brand 箔一 (Hakuichi) as part of its 金澤しつらえ gallery, so it is brand-run rather than a fully independent owner-operated shop. No documented allergy/dietary handling.

bio.background: Saboh Yanagian is a second-floor wa-café inside 金澤しつらえ, a gallery-shop run by the renowned Kanazawa gold-leaf brand Hakuichi, set within a tea-house (chaya) building over 200 years old in the Higashi Chaya District. The space was restored on the floor plan of the former 'Kyū-Moroeya' era, when geisha performed their arts there, and blends Edo-period architectural design with contemporary touches — including a glass connecting corridor finished in gold leaf.
bio.specialty: Cream anmitsu crowned with a single sheet of gold leaf (over domestic agar, red bean paste and matcha warabimochi), seasonal fruit parfaits, matcha served with Kanazawa wagashi, and seasonal afternoon-tea sets.
bio.philosophy: The café aims to offer 'a quiet moment separated from everyday life,' inviting guests to feel as if they are dissolving into a Japanese painting while gazing at the historic streetscape.
roles: Operated by Hakuichi (箔一) gold-leaf brand

anecdotes.text: The café is named after the 'Mikaeri Yanagi' (backward-glance willow), the iconic tree standing right in front of the building and a symbol of the Higashi Chaya geisha district; table seats look out onto the swaying willow.
The building is a restored 200-year-old chaya, and the second-floor connecting corridor was rebuilt in glass and finished with gold leaf, reflecting Hakuichi's gold-leaf craft heritage.

lead: ターバンカレー 総本店
name_ja: ターバンカレー 総本店
cuisine: 金沢カレー (Kanazawa curry)
neighborhood: 香林坊・広坂
address_ja: 石川県金沢市広坂1-1-48 ウナシンビル 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市広坂1-1-48 returned [136.655212, 36.561962], within the Kanazawa box. Near the 21st Century Museum and Ishikawa Shiko Kinen Bunka Koryukan, off Korinbo.
seats_text: Roughly 16-17 seats: about 10 counter seats plus a few small tables. Small, quick-turnover counter shop.
closed_days: Essentially open year-round; closed only around year-end/New Year (approx. 12/30-1/1).
cultural_comfort_note: A no-frills local counter curry shop with a quick, efficient ordering flow and cash-only payment, so come with yen and be ready to order promptly; a little guidance helps but the menu of cutlet/sausage/hamburg curries is easy to point at.
gf_detail: Kanazawa curry is built on a dark, thick roux thickened with wheat flour and topped with breaded pork cutlet, so the food is wheat-heavy and not celiac-safe. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: This is a meat-centric curry shop; signature dishes feature pork cutlet, sausage, hamburg steak, and a roux made with meat stock. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.

bio.background: Turban Curry opened in 1971 (Showa 46) in Kanazawa's Korinbo district and has served its signature Kanazawa curry for over half a century, celebrating its 50th anniversary in August 2021. This Hirosaka storefront, near the 21st Century Museum of Contemporary Art, is the original flagship (sohonten). The shop relocated to its current spot in 2005.
bio.specialty: Classic Kanazawa-style curry: a thick, dark, glossy roux made with more than twenty spices, served over firm rice on a stainless-steel plate. Signature plates include the L Set (pork cutlet, sausage, and hamburg steak together), pork cutlet (roskatsu) curry, and a house-made cream croquette curry, all available as takeout bento too.
bio.philosophy: A streamlined, efficient counter operation focused on consistently turning out its rich, dark, medium-spiced curry rather than frills.

anecdotes.text: The founder of the now-famous Go Go Curry chain is said to have worked at Turban Curry before starting his own brand, placing this shop at the roots of the Kanazawa curry scene.
The shop marked its 50th anniversary in August 2021, half a century of continuous service in Korinbo.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Curry roux is wheat-flour based and the signature toppings are breaded/fried cutlets and croquettes, so wheat is pervasive throughout the kitchen.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is centered on pork cutlet, sausage, hamburg steak and a meat-stock roux; no plant-based items are documented.

caveats: The Tabelog listing and some directory pages label this exact 広坂1-1-48 location as "ターバンカレー 本店," while the brand and storefront use "総本店"; they refer to the same original flagship shop (the target Tabelog URL matches). Hours reported as 11:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30) on the official-style listing; one source noted 11:00-20:30. Seat count approximate (16-17). GF/vegan safety details are inferred from documented menu composition, not from a stated allergen policy.

cuisine: Nodoguro (blackthroat seabream) kamameshi — Kaga/Kanazawa seafood specialty
neighborhood: Omicho Market (Shimozutsumi-machi / Musashi entrance)
gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. The menu centers on nodoguro kamameshi finished as dashi-chazuke (broth poured over rice), and Japanese dashi/seasonings here typically involve soy sauce, which contains wheat. No gluten-free menu, dedicated preparation, or celiac accommodation is documented. Celiac travelers should avoid.
vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian. This is a seafood specialist whose signature dish is nodoguro clay-pot rice topped with ikura and finished with fish dashi; no plant-based options are documented.
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 石川県金沢市下堤町19, within the Kanazawa box. Maps to the Omicho Market area near the Musashi entrance.
seats_text: 28 seats (8 counter, 20 table); second-floor table seating used for groups
closed_days: None listed (open daily)
cultural_comfort_note: A friendly single-dish counter spot inside the bustling Omicho Market where a basic greeting and pointing at the famous nodoguro kamameshi will get you through comfortably, though the staff and menu are primarily Japanese.
caveats: Allergen, cross-contamination, and dietary-accommodation details are not documented on any fetched source; dietary assessments are inferred from the seafood/dashi-based menu and should be confirmed with the restaurant directly.

bio.origin: Opened May 1, 2021 inside Kanazawa's historic Omicho Market, near the Musashi entrance.
bio.background: Tonariya is the nodoguro-kamameshi specialty shop of the local Noukabanzai brand, a Kanazawa group built around Ishikawa regional ingredients. It puts the spotlight on nodoguro (blackthroat seabream, akamutsu) — a fatty, refined white fish so prized it is nicknamed 'the toro of white fish.'
bio.specialty: Nodoguro kamameshi: each clay pot is cooked to order from raw rice, taking about 20 minutes, and is served with a nodoguro broth carefully drawn from the bones. The dish is designed to be eaten in three stages — first plain to taste the fish, then dressed up with ikura and condiments, and finally as dashi-chazuke with broth poured over the rice.
bio.philosophy: The shop describes its signature as 'a beauty so simple that nothing can be hidden' — meaning the pared-back preparation leaves nowhere to mask anything but the quality of the fish and rice.

anecdotes.text: Nodoguro is described as a premium white fish whose fat is so refined it is called 'the toro of white fish,' and the kamameshi is meant to be enjoyed three ways: as-is, with condiments, and as tea-soaked rice.
The nodoguro broth served alongside the rice is carefully made from the fish bones.

name_ja: PETRA BAKE&COFFEE（ペトラ ベイク&コーヒー）
cuisine: コーヒースタンド／カフェ（オーガニック自家製ベイク、ヴィーガン対応あり。長町MORON CAFE系列）
gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. The bakery openly uses wheat, including spelt wheat (スペルト小麦) in items such as the carrot cake. While some items use rice flour (米粉, e.g. banana bread), everything is baked in the same small shop with no dedicated gluten-free facility or stated allergen protocol, so cross-contamination with wheat is likely. Coeliac travelers should avoid the baked goods. Plain espresso/coffee drinks themselves contain no gluten.
vegan_detail: Vegan-friendly options exist but the shop is not fully vegan. The bakery lists "animal-product-free" (動物性不使用) baked goods alongside conventional items so customers can choose what suits them, and soy milk is available for lattes (e.g. iced soy latte). However many baked goods contain dairy/eggs and the carrot cake contains walnuts, so ask which specific items are animal-free on the day. No claim of a fully separate vegan prep area.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市青草町88 近江町いちば館
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [136.656036, 36.571659] for 石川県金沢市青草町88; within the Kanazawa box and consistent with the Omicho Market entrance (Aokusacho/Shimozutsumicho area).
neighborhood: 近江町市場入口（青草町・近江町いちば館、武蔵口側）
seats_text: Takeout-focused coffee stand with a small counter table (about 4 chair seats) plus standing room.
closed_days: 火曜日（年末年始も休み）
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, stylish takeout coffee stand at the market entrance where pointing at the counter pastries and a few words of Japanese will get you through easily; staff likely have limited English but the order is simple.
caveats: Sources disagree slightly on the closed day (Tabelog listing shows year-round/年末年始 only, while the cafe's own opening coverage and local guides state closed Tuesdays); Tuesdays is the more widely reported regular closing. Hours are 8:00-16:00. Phone not clearly published; contact via Instagram. Vegan and rice-flour items vary by day, so confirm in person.

bio.background: Petra Bake & Coffee is a small, design-forward coffee stand that opened on May 21, 2023 right at the entrance of Kanazawa's Omicho Market. It is the second venture from the team behind the well-loved MORON CAFE in the Nagamachi samurai district, bringing the same organic, homemade ethos into a compact grab-and-go space styled like an overseas cafe.
bio.origin: 金沢市・長町のMORON CAFE系列の2号店
bio.specialty: Homemade baked goods built around organic ingredients - a spelt-wheat carrot cake with walnuts, banana bread made with rice flour, plus cookies, muffins and scones - paired with espresso drinks. Signatures include the self-made spiced cafe mocha and an iced soy latte using aromatic espresso and smooth soy milk.
bio.philosophy: The shop deliberately bakes with as much organic flour as possible and offers a mix of rice-flour, spelt-wheat and animal-product-free items so that customers can pick what fits their own lifestyle and diet.

anecdotes.text: The espresso is a light-roast original blend sourced from the GOOD4LIFE 36CAFE coffee stand, chosen for a clean, gently sweet flavor.
It is the second location of Nagamachi's popular MORON CAFE, opened at the Omicho Market entrance to serve shoppers a quick, stylish break.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat, including spelt wheat (used in the carrot cake), is baked on-site alongside rice-flour items in a single small shop, so cross-contamination is likely; no dedicated gluten-free area is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Animal-product-free baked goods are offered on the same counter as dairy/egg items; no separate vegan prep is documented, and the carrot cake contains walnuts.

positives.text: The bakery explicitly offers animal-product-free (vegan) items and soy milk for lattes, so plant-based diners have real choices.
Some items are made with rice flour (米粉), such as the banana bread, giving non-strict diners lighter-wheat options.

cuisine: Seafood donburi and sushi (kaisendon, nodoguro, uni) — a fish-shop-run eatery inside Omicho Market
gf_detail: This is a seafood-bowl and sushi restaurant where dishes are built around soy sauce, wasabi-soy dressings, and dashi-based broths (e.g. the nodoguro hitsumabushi finishes as a dashi tea-over-rice). Japanese soy sauce contains wheat and no gluten-free accommodation is documented, so this is not a safe choice for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: The entire concept is fresh fish and seafood from the affiliated Omicho Market fish vendor — signature dishes are raw seafood donburi (13-plus kinds of fish, uni, ikura) and nodoguro. There are no vegan offerings.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市青草町88 近江町いちば館2F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address 石川県金沢市青草町88 via GSI; coordinates fall within the central Kanazawa box (Omicho Market).
seats_text: Around 30-38 seats with counter seating and private table rooms for 4-6 guests (sources vary, 30-38).
hours_raw: Mon-Fri 11:00-14:00 and 17:00-22:00; Sat-Sun 10:00-14:00 and 17:00-22:00 (official site lists lunch to 15:00 / dinner to 21:00 with last orders 14:30 and 20:00). Lunch and dinner service with a midday break.
closed_days: Irregular / no fixed closing day (不定休)
cultural_comfort_note: A tourist-friendly market restaurant with English and Korean menus and card payment accepted, so a foreign student can order comfortably without Japanese.
caveats: Reported seat counts (30 vs 38) and exact closing times (22:00 vs 21:00) differ slightly between the official site and Tabelog; signature bowl pricing has ranged from 2,900 to 3,300 yen across sources.

bio.background: 鮮彩えにし sits on the second floor of the Omicho Ichiba building inside Kanazawa's lively Omicho Market, the city's centuries-old kitchen. It is run directly by Oguchi Suisan, a fresh-fish vendor in the market, so the restaurant draws its catch straight from its own stall.
bio.specialty: The namesake 海鮮えにし丼 is a generously piled seafood donburi showcasing more than a dozen kinds of local fish and shellfish — tuna, toro, nodoguro (blackthroat seaperch), uni, ikura, yellowtail, scallop, sweet shrimp and more. The のど黒ひつまぶし lets a whole prized nodoguro be enjoyed three ways: first with wasabi soy, then mixed with uni and garnishes, and finally as a tea-over-rice course with a broth made from the fish bones.
bio.philosophy: As a fish-monger-run spot, it leans on market pricing rather than tourist-inflated rates, aiming to serve both visitors and Kanazawa locals who treat Omicho as their everyday kitchen.

anecdotes.text: Nodoguro is such a draw here that the restaurant offers an 追いのどぐろ ('extra nodoguro') option so enthusiasts can pile on more of the prized fish.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are served with soy sauce and wasabi-soy dressings, and the signature nodoguro hitsumabushi is finished as a dashi tea-over-rice; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

cuisine: 加賀料理・郷土料理（会席）／Kaga regional cuisine, kaiseki
neighborhood: 寺町（野町・にし茶屋街、犀川大橋寄り）
gf_detail: This is a traditional Kaga kaiseki ryotei built around wheat-based soy sauce, bonito/kombu dashi, and dishes like jibu-ni (which uses flour-dusted meat/fish) and tai no karamushi. There is no documented gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or celiac accommodation. Not safe for celiacs. A formal multi-course kaiseki cannot reasonably be made gluten-free here without prior arrangement, and no such accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: A 1752-founded ryotei specializing in Kaga cuisine built on fresh seafood (crab porridge, tai no karamushi, sashimi) and dashi-based dishes. Nothing vegan or vegetarian is documented on the official site or other sources. As a fully reservation-based kaiseki house with chefs who prepare bespoke courses, a vegan/vegetarian course might be possible only by arranging well in advance when booking, but it is not offered as standard and is not confirmed.
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates for 石川県金沢市寺町5-1-8; within the Kanazawa box.
seats_text: Approximately 200 seats, all in private rooms (some sources cite up to ~300 across the full banquet capacity). Not a counter or casual seating restaurant.
hours_raw: 11:00-14:00 (L.O. 13:00), 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 19:00); closed Wednesdays and year-end/New Year. Completely reservation-based.
closed_days: 水曜日、年末年始（Wednesdays, year-end/New Year）
cultural_comfort_note: This is a formal, expensive, reservation-only historic ryotei with private-room kaiseki service and Japanese etiquette expectations; a foreign student should come with a host or guide, dress appropriately, and arrange any dietary needs far in advance.
caveats: High-end formal kaiseki (roughly 20,000 yen and up per person); completely reservation-based and not a walk-in or budget option. No gluten-free or vegan menu is documented; any dietary restriction must be discussed when reserving.

bio.background: Tsubajin is Kanazawa's oldest ryotei, founded in 1752 (Horeki 2). Its roots go back further: the Tsuba family served the Maeda clan of Kaga as hereditary tsuba (sword-guard) craftsmen from the late 16th century, and the third-generation Jinbei opened a small teahouse called Tsubaya alongside that trade, which grew into the restaurant known today as Tsubajin. The house has carried its craft of Kaga cuisine for roughly 260 years on its present footing as a restaurant.
bio.origin: Kanazawa (Teramachi, near Nomachi and the Saigawa river), Ishikawa Prefecture, Japan
bio.specialty: Kaga regional cuisine served as kaiseki: signature dishes include jibu-ni (the representative Kanazawa stew), tai no karamushi (sea bream stuffed and steamed), renmushi (steamed lotus dishes), and a celebrated crab rice porridge (kani-gayu) breakfast. Courses use fresh local seafood and Kaga vegetables.
bio.philosophy: The house describes its enduring aim across 350 years as flavors that satisfy and a heart that thinks of the guest, served entirely in private rooms.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant has hosted many leading historical and literary figures, including the statesman Ito Hirobumi and the writer Akutagawa Ryunosuke.
Its name and lineage come from the Tsuba family's former trade as sword-guard craftsmen for the Maeda lords of Kaga before the family turned to cooking.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Traditional Kaga kaiseki built on wheat-based soy sauce and bonito/kombu dashi; signature jibu-ni traditionally uses flour-dusted protein, so gluten is intrinsic to the cuisine. No gluten-free preparation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Kaga cuisine here relies on standard Japanese soy sauce and dashi-based seasonings, which contain wheat; no wheat-free soy alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menus center on seafood (crab porridge, sea bream, sashimi) and dashi; no vegan or vegetarian course is documented, so animal-derived stock and seafood are pervasive.

positives.text: Fully reservation-based with bespoke multi-course service, so dietary requests can in principle be raised when booking well in advance, though no specific accommodation is documented.

cuisine: 純喫茶・昔ながらの喫茶店 (traditional kissaten — coffee, toast and sandwich sets, おしるこ)
neighborhood: 金沢中心部（長町・近江町周辺）
geocode_note: GSI address search returned [136.646652, 36.566628] for 石川県金沢市長町3-6-12; within Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 21 seats: 3 counter, three 4-tops, one 6-top.
hours_raw: 7:30-17:00, closed Wednesdays
closed_days: Wednesdays
cultural_comfort_note: A quiet neighborhood kissaten run in the old-fashioned way with little to no English, cash only — easiest to enjoy if you bring a Japanese-speaking friend or are comfortable ordering by pointing.
gf_detail: A traditional kissaten whose menu centers on toast and sandwich sets (wheat bread) plus おしるこ; nothing indicates gluten-free options or any allergen handling, so it is not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Old-style coffee shop serving buttered toast, sandwiches and milk-based drinks; no vegan menu or plant-milk options are documented.
caveats: Cash only. Closed Wednesdays. Small 21-seat shop in a residential part of Nagamachi; opens early (7:30) and closes at 17:00. Coffee/dairy/wheat focused — not a dietary-restriction destination.

bio.background: A long-running, old-fashioned kissaten tucked into the residential Nagamachi area near Kanazawa's samurai-house district and within reach of Omicho Market. It opens early at 7:30 in the morning, giving the neighborhood a quiet, unhurried place to start the day.
bio.specialty: Coffee is the heart of the shop, with around ten kinds of beans the owner selects and offers, and toast and sandwich sets are the signature plates. House-made おしるこ (sweet red-bean soup) and a homemade honey-lemon drink round out the menu.
bio.philosophy: A calm, classic kissaten atmosphere that welcomes both families and solo visitors.

anecdotes.text: Regarded among Kanazawa's beloved retro kissaten, the master is particular about coffee — pairing carefully chosen beans with roast — and the sandwiches are singled out as especially good.
Known for toast sets generously topped with vegetables and fruit, plus homemade おしるこ and a complimentary honey-lemon service.

cuisine: 金沢おでん・割烹（居酒屋）
neighborhood: 木倉町（片町／香林坊近く）
gf_detail: This is a traditional oden and kappo izakaya built around dashi-simmered broth, eel with sauce glaze, and Kaga local dishes. The oden broth, the kabayaki-style eel sauce, and the simmered dishes all rely on wheat-containing soy sauce, and there is no dedicated gluten-free preparation. It is not a safe choice for celiacs. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: The kitchen is centered on seafood, eel, crab, and fish-based dashi (the oden broth is made from herring/iwashi dashi). Nearly every dish involves animal stock or seafood, and there is no documented vegan or vegetarian menu. Not suitable for vegans or strict vegetarians.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市木倉町1-5 中泉ビル 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to 石川県金沢市木倉町1番5号; coordinates fall within the central Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 40 seats total: 14 counter seats and 26 tatami (zashiki) seats; can accommodate groups up to about 20.
hours_raw: 16:30-21:30 (Tabelog lists close 21:30; one local source lists last hours to 22:00). Closed Tuesdays (open on the eve of holidays).
closed_days: 火曜日 (Tuesday); open on the eve of public holidays
cultural_comfort_note: A cozy, old-school Showa-era counter izakaya where a little Japanese and an easygoing attitude go a long way; menus and conversation are mostly in Japanese, so a translation app helps for a foreign student.
caveats: Smoking is permitted throughout the restaurant. Hours vary slightly between sources (close 21:30 to 22:00); confirm seasonal eel and crab availability when visiting.

bio.background: Founded in December 1957 (Showa 32) in Kanazawa's Kikuramachi quarter near Katamachi and Korinbo, Ozeki is a long-loved neighborhood kappo-izakaya that still carries an authentic Showa-era atmosphere with its long oden counter and warm, homey feel.
bio.specialty: Kanazawa-style oden simmered in a refined, deep dashi broth (including seasonal items like crab face in November-December), and live-ordered freshwater eel prepared to the customer's choice of white-grilled, sauce-glazed, or combined. Kaga local dishes such as jibuni also feature.
bio.philosophy: A welcoming, unpretentious neighborhood place where regulars gather at the counter; the proprietress is noted for creating a warm, homey atmosphere.

anecdotes.text: The proprietor Heikichi Hirayasu prepares eel that is ordered live by the fish, combining Kanto-style back-cutting with Kansai-style grill-only cooking for a fragrant, crispy exterior and sweet fat.
On Mondays nearly all oden items are offered at a special low price, drawing crowds of regulars.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Oden, simmered dishes, and sauce-glazed eel are built on wheat-based soy sauce and fish dashi; there is no separate gluten-free preparation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Eel is offered sauce-glazed (kabayaki style) and dishes use standard soy-based seasonings, which contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The oden broth and most dishes are made with fish/seafood dashi, and the kitchen centers on eel, crab, and seafood; vegan separation is not possible.

cuisine: 金沢おでん (Kanazawa-style oden)
neighborhood: 石引 (Ishibiki) — east of Kenrokuen / Hondamachi
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市石引2-7-11 returned [136.671967, 36.554035]; within the central Kanazawa box.
seats_text: A single U-shaped (コの字型) counter wrapping the simmering oden pot; small, intimate space. Exact seat count not documented.
hours_raw: 17:00–22:00 (some listings note up to 22:30). Closed Mondays (and Tuesdays per Tabelog); first-come, first-served.
closed_days: Monday (Tabelog also lists Tuesday closed; another listing notes Monday only — confirm before visiting)
cultural_comfort_note: A welcoming, populist old-school counter where even solo diners are made comfortable, but it is a traditional Japanese-only local spot, so a little Japanese (or pointing at the pot) will go a long way.
caveats: Wheat- and seafood-based dashi throughout; not suitable for celiac or vegan diners. Hours and closed days vary between sources — confirm Tuesday closure. Cash only; no reservations, so expect to wait at peak times.
gf_detail: A traditional oden house built on a continuously replenished dried-sardine dashi seasoned in Japanese style; soy sauce (wheat-based) and miso are central, and items like tsumire and dote-yaki are not gluten-free. No gluten-free accommodation is documented. Not celiac-safe.
vegan_detail: The entire menu rests on a fish (niboshi/sardine) dashi, and signature dishes include sardine tsumire, pork-belly dote-yaki, octopus and bay snails. There are a few plant-based oden items (daikon, taro, konnyaku, grilled tofu) but they are all simmered in the fish broth, so the place is not suitable for vegans.

bio.background: Founded in 1935 (Showa 10), Wakaba is one of the great old-school Kanazawa oden houses, tucked away in the residential Ishibiki neighborhood east of Kenrokuen rather than in the tourist quarters. It is run today by third-generation owner Yoshikawa Masashi, working alongside his father, the second-generation proprietor.
bio.specialty: Hand-made iwashi (sardine) tsumire — firm on the outside, remarkably fluffy inside, blended with onion and ginger and drinking up the broth; dote-yaki, simmered pork belly crowned with sweet white miso and a heap of minced green onion; plus Kanazawa classics like daikon, taro, octopus and bay snails. Everything bathes in a clear, lightly seasoned dashi built on a niboshi (dried sardine) base that has been continuously topped up since the shop opened.
bio.philosophy: Rather than chasing the modern 'Kanazawa oden' boom, the family deliberately keeps everything the same — their stated ethos is that they have never changed and never will, and that constancy is exactly why regulars keep coming back across generations.

anecdotes.text: The second-generation owner is the one who created the now-signature sardine tsumire, a recipe carried on by his son.
Prep starts around 9:10 in the morning so the pot is perfectly ready by opening, and the same broth has been replenished rather than replaced for decades.
The late novelist Ikenami Shotaro, who spent time in Kanazawa, was a regular and later wrote about his visits here.

Diners sit at a single U-shaped counter around the bubbling pot, choosing items by sight; the atmosphere is unpretentious and mixes elderly regulars with younger groups, and solo guests are made to feel at ease.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All oden, including otherwise plant-based items like daikon and tofu, is simmered together in a shared fish-and-soy dashi, so cross-contact with wheat and fish is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth and seasoning are traditional Japanese soy- and miso-based, which means wheat-containing soy sauce; no wheat-free alternative is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The base dashi is made from dried sardines (niboshi), and every item shares that fish broth, so no dish is free of animal products.

cuisine: 手打ちそば (hand-made soba)
neighborhood: 東山・ひがし茶屋街
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates [136.66658, 36.572369] for 石川県金沢市東山1-15-5, within the Kanazawa box. This sits in the Higashi Chaya district as expected.
seats_text: 27席 (includes counter seating for solo diners)
hours_raw: Lunch (武右衛門): 11:30-16:00 (L.O. 15:30); per Tabelog Mon & Fri-Sun 11:30-19:30, Tue-Thu 11:30-15:30. Evening soba bar "音ゆ" operates 18:30-25:00, closed Sun & Mon (open if Mon is a holiday).
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular holidays; open on national holidays)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, calm machiya soba shop in the teahouse district where a little Japanese and a relaxed attitude go a long way; counter seating makes it comfortable for a solo diner, though staff may have limited English.
gf_detail: This is a soba (buckwheat) specialist, and the signature 加賀棒茶そば is made with Sarashina-type flour blended with roasted-tea powder — soba noodles here contain wheat and are not gluten-free, and the broth is a katsuo (bonito) dashi base typically finished with wheat-containing soy sauce. There is no indication of dedicated gluten-free preparation. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: The dashi broth is bonito/fish-based and signature dishes include duck (鴨せいろ, 鴨治部煮) and Jibu-ni style soba, plus yam/dashi toppings; no documented vegan menu. Not a vegan destination.

bio.background: Chasoba Buemon occupies a renovated machiya (traditional wooden townhouse) tucked into Kanazawa's historic Higashi Chaya teahouse district. The building was originally the private residence of a bathhouse owner whose establishment once served the district's geisha; the renovation keeps the original wooden beams and pillars while opening the interior with large glass windows that frame a traditional courtyard garden.
bio.specialty: The signature dish is 加賀棒茶そば (Kaga hojicha soba) — buckwheat noodles kneaded with powdered roasted Kaga bocha tea. The owner is said to have gathered roasted tea from across Kanazawa and tested batch after batch to perfect it, using a refined Sarashina-style flour so the soba's own flavor steps back and lets the tea's aroma and faint sweetness come forward. Diners are encouraged to taste it first with sea salt to catch the fragrance. Other dishes include 鴨せいろ (duck seiro), 鴨治部煮せいろ echoing Kanazawa's local jibu-ni, hanamaki with local seaweed, and a 白雪とろろ (snow-yam) soba plated to evoke winter snow.
bio.philosophy: A quiet pursuit of a distinctly Kanazawa soba — letting a local roasted tea, rather than the noodle alone, define the bowl, served in a setting that pairs preserved machiya craftsmanship with vintage vacuum-tube audio and jazz.
roles: lunch soba cafe / evening soba bar

anecdotes.text: The son of the household apprenticed at several Tokyo soba restaurants before returning to Kanazawa, and personally prepares each bowl using Oku-Noto buckwheat flour and a bonito-based broth.
The interior features rare vintage vacuum-tube amplifiers and jazz, with a counter that welcomes solo diners; by night the space becomes the soba bar 音ゆ open until 2 AM.
The noodles deliberately use Sarashina flour to mute the usual soba flavor so the roasted-tea aroma and subtle sweetness dominate; it is recommended to taste with salt first.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Soba/buckwheat noodle shop; the house specialty noodles are made with Sarashina-type wheat-blended flour, so flour and gluten are present throughout the kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Broth is described as a katsuo/bonito-based dashi (tsuyu), which in a traditional soba shop is conventionally made with wheat-containing soy sauce; no gluten-free tsuyu is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is fish/bonito-based and signature dishes feature duck; no separate vegan preparation is documented.

caveats: Hours vary by source: the official site lists lunch 11:30-16:00 (L.O. 15:30) with irregular closures, while Tabelog lists Mon & Fri-Sun until 19:30 and Tue-Thu until 15:30; the weekly array follows Tabelog but the safest assumption is a lunch-focused service that may close mid-afternoon. The separate evening soba bar 音ゆ (18:30-25:00, closed Sun/Mon) shares the space. No allergy/celiac accommodation is documented; treat as not GF and not vegan.

cuisine: Bakery — artisan hard-crust breads and baked goods (small family/solo-run bakery)
gf_detail: This is a wheat-flour bakery; bread is the entire product line. No gluten-free items, dedicated equipment, or allergy accommodations are documented. Not suitable for celiac or gluten-avoidant diners.
vegan_detail: A few of the signature items lean plant-forward — hard breads such as the raisin/currant loaf and Dainagon (red bean) stick may be made without dairy or egg — but the bakery also sells dairy/egg-rich goods (lemon cake, scones, cookies) and publishes no vegan labeling. Vegans would need to ask about each item; nothing is confirmed.
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 尾山町13番2号, within the Kanazawa box near Oyama Shrine.
neighborhood: 尾山町 (Oyama-cho) — beside Oyama Shrine, within walking distance of Kenrokuen and Omicho Market
seats_text: About 8 seats in the Sunday-only second-floor café (three 2-person tables plus two single seats); the bakery itself is primarily takeaway.
hours_raw: Thu–Fri 7:00–sold out; Sat–Sun 9:00–sold out; Sunday café 11:00–17:00 (L.O. 16:00). Closed Mon/Tue/Wed. (An older source listed 10:00 open and closed Mon/Tue, so opening times may vary — confirm via Instagram.)
closed_days: Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny neighborhood bakery run single-handedly with no English signage — point at what you want, pay cash, and go early before the popular loaves sell out.
caveats: Closing time is "when sold out" — popular breads often disappear by mid-morning, so arrive early. The second-floor café is Sunday-only. Cash is safest (PayPay reportedly accepted, but no cards). Hours have shifted between sources; the bakery posts current info on Instagram.

bio.background: COYA. is a small bakery in the Oyama-cho neighborhood beside Oyama Shrine, run single-handedly by baker Ayako Tajiri. Its plain white-washed facade blends into the retro streetscape around the shrine. A 2019 renovation added a Sunday-only café space on the second floor, where coffee from a Toyama roaster is served alongside café-exclusive breads.
bio.specialty: Hard-crust breads are the heart of the shop: a raisin-and-currant loaf, a Dainagon sweet-red-bean stick, and a chocolate baguette (choco-france). The house pain de mie comes in three styles — plain, sesame, and walnut — and draws daily regulars. The most talked-about item is the lemon cake, a French-style pound cake folded with lemon juice.
bio.philosophy: Tajiri puts quality over quantity, baking only as much as she can make well each day and closing once it sells out. In her words she wants to keep making 'unpretentious bread you won't tire of even eating it every day,' letting the natural sweetness of the ingredients come through.

anecdotes.text: The owner refined her craft at Hirami Pan, a celebrated Kanazawa bakery, before opening her own small shop.
A 2019 renovation introduced a Sunday-only second-floor café pouring coffee from a Toyama roaster, popular with both locals and tourists.
Decorative red peppers hang at the storefront as a charm to ward off misfortune.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A wheat-flour bakery where bread is the entire product line, so airborne flour and shared surfaces make cross-contamination unavoidable; not celiac-safe.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dairy- and egg-rich items (lemon cake, scones, cookies) are made in the same small kitchen as the breads, so shared equipment is likely; no vegan separation is documented.

cuisine: 手打ち蕎麦（女性店主のカウンター中心の小さな個人店、つけ鴨せいろが看板）
neighborhood: 武蔵町（近江町市場から徒歩約3分、北鉄金沢駅近く）
address_ja: 石川県金沢市武蔵町3-3 レジデンス武蔵 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市武蔵町3-3 returned [136.654144, 36.570278], within the central Kanazawa box. Matches the near-Omicho-Market / Hokutetsu Kanazawa Station location.
seats_text: Small shop with counter and a few table seats; counter-centric and welcoming to solo diners. Exact seat count not documented.
hours_raw: Wed-Sat 11:30-15:00, 17:00-21:00; Sun 11:30-15:00; closed Mon & Tue (per Tabelog). Some sources list closed Mondays only with evening service to 22:00.
closed_days: Monday and Tuesday
cultural_comfort_note: A small, casual counter soba shop run by a friendly woman who warmly welcomes solo diners; ordering is in Japanese, so a translation app or a few phrases will make ordering smoother for a foreign student.
gf_detail: This is a traditional handmade soba shop; Japanese soba almost always blends wheat flour into the buckwheat, and the dipping broths use soy sauce (wheat) and dashi. There is no documented gluten-free or dedicated celiac-safe preparation, so it is not safe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: The signature dishes feature duck broth (kamo) and dashi, and the soba broths are fish/meat based; there is no documented vegan menu. Seasonal vegetable-topped soba (e.g., grated-radish oroshi soba) exists but the tsuyu/broth contains animal-derived dashi, so a vegan would need to ask and likely find limited options.
caveats: Closed days differ slightly across sources: Tabelog lists Monday and Tuesday closed with Sunday lunch-only, while a local blog lists only Mondays closed with dinner to 22:00. Seat count is not published. Payment is cash plus QR-code payment; cards/e-money not accepted.

bio.background: Sobadokoro Anan opened in March 2022 on a quiet lane just off the main street near Omicho Market in Kanazawa's Musashi-machi district. It is run single-handedly by a warm, approachable female proprietor, and the renovated space has a calm, refined yet casual feel that makes it easy to drop into alone.
bio.specialty: The signature dish is the Tsuke Kamo Seiro (around 1,800 yen): soba hand-cut fresh each morning served with a warm dipping broth rich with duck from Zao. The buckwheat is milled from Hokkaido Isoya-grown soba flour, prized for aroma and a smooth throat-feel. The menu also includes playful creations such as a cheese soba topped with Parmigiano-Reggiano and egg yolk, and seasonal grated-radish (oroshi) soba.
bio.philosophy: Handmade soba made fresh daily with local vegetables and seasonal ingredients, served in carefully chosen ceramics including matcha-bowl dipping cups and colorful Kutani ware for condiments.

anecdotes.text: The owner serves the dipping sauce in a matcha tea bowl and presents condiments on colorful Kutani ware, a small touch of Kanazawa craft that locals single out.
Beyond classic soba, she offers inventive plates like a cheese soba crowned with Parmigiano-Reggiano and egg yolk, giving the casual shop a creative streak.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A handmade soba kitchen where wheat flour is routinely used in noodle making and soy-sauce-based broths; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dipping broths are based on dashi and soy sauce (which contains wheat), including the duck tsuke broth.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature broths use duck and fish/meat dashi; the kitchen is built around animal-derived stocks.

positives.text: Seasonal vegetable preparations such as grated-radish oroshi soba are offered, though the accompanying broth is not vegan.

lead: シンタテベイクストア
name_ja: シンタテベイクストア
cuisine: Bakery
neighborhood: 新竪町 (Shintatemachi)
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to 新竪町三丁目102; within Kanazawa box. Located in the Shintatemachi shopping street, walking distance from Katamachi and Kenrokuen.
seats_text: Primarily a takeout bakery; a small eat-in area (reported as roughly 2 table seats and a few standing spots) was added after opening. Most customers buy bread to go.
hours_raw: 9:00-16:00 (closes earlier when sold out). Closed Monday and Tuesday.
closed_days: Monday, Tuesday (and may close early when sold out)
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed neighborhood bakery where you simply point at the bread you want and pay (cash or QR codes like PayPay accepted), so almost no Japanese is needed.
gf_detail: This is a wheat bakery — its entire identity is bread made from home-milled organic wheat and whole wheat flour. There is no gluten-free offering and it is not safe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: No documented vegan labeling. Breads use natural yeast and organic wheat; some items (canelé, cherry pie, pastries) clearly contain dairy/eggs. Simple lean breads may be plant-based but this is not confirmed, so vegans should ask about specific loaves.
caveats: Closed days have been reported inconsistently across sources (some older listings say Sun/Mon/Tue); the official shopping-street page and Tabelog both list Monday and Tuesday as closed, so the record uses Wed-Sun open. May sell out and close before 16:00.

bio.background: Shintate Bake Store opened on April 12, 2023, in the Shintatemachi shopping street as the sister shop of the much-loved Kanazawa bakery Sakanoue Bakery (坂の上ベーカリー), run by the Wakai couple. In September 2023 the original Sakanoue Bakery closed and its bread workshop moved here, making Shintate Bake Store the continuation of that bakery's craft.
bio.specialty: More than 20 kinds of bread and pastries are laid out daily from around 9 a.m., centered on breads made from home-milled organic whole wheat. Signature items include the Fukumitsuya canelé made with rice shochu from the local Fukumitsuya sake brewery, and the playful 'SS Cherry Pie' (a Twin Peaks reference, where SS stands for Shintate Street).
bio.philosophy: The theme is 'healthy and delicious' (健やかで美味しく) — bread and sweets made with ingredients gentle on children, adults, and the environment, using pesticide-free organic wheat that is milled in-house and natural yeast. The owners deliberately do not serve coffee in the shop, encouraging customers to wander the shopping street and pair their bread with a drink from a neighboring cafe.

anecdotes.text: The shop intentionally avoids selling coffee so that customers will explore the Shintatemachi shopping street and combine the bread with offerings from nearby coffee shops.
It is the successor to Sakanoue Bakery: when that shop closed in September 2023, its bread workshop was relocated into Shintate Bake Store.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a flour-based bakery using home-milled organic wheat and whole wheat as its core ingredient; airborne flour and shared surfaces make it unsafe for celiacs.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Many items contain dairy and eggs (e.g. canelé, cherry pie); no separate vegan production is documented.

positives.text: Ingredients emphasize pesticide-free organic wheat and natural yeast with a 'healthy and delicious' philosophy, so the bread is made from carefully chosen, transparent ingredients.

cuisine: Artisan bakery (French-style hard breads)
address_ja: 石川県金沢市東蚊爪町チ18
neighborhood: 東蚊爪町（蚊爪駅周辺・金沢市北部）
geocode_note: Chō-level geocode of 東蚊爪町 (street block チ18 not resolvable at point level). Within the Kanazawa box. NOTE: this is in northern Kanazawa near Kagatsume Station — it is NOT in the central Omicho Market area as the target brief stated; that part of the brief is inaccurate.
seats_text: Primarily takeout; a small tatami eat-in corner overlooking the garden is available. Exact seat count not documented.
hours_raw: 10:00-17:00. Closed Mondays, the 3rd Tuesday, and the 4th Sunday of each month.
closed_days: Mondays; also the 3rd Tuesday and 4th Sunday of each month
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny neighborhood bakery tucked down a residential lane far from the tourist center, with no English and no website, so a first-time foreign visitor will want a map pin and a little Japanese (or a local friend) to find it and order.
gf_detail: This is a wheat-flour bakery specializing in hard-crust French-style breads (made with French flour and Guérande salt). There are no gluten-free products and no precautions for celiac diners; the entire premises is a flour-heavy environment.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan destination. Many signature items contain dairy/egg (e.g., custard cream pan) or meat and cheese (the Petit Pré with hamburger or spicy chicken). A plain hard loaf such as the Tradi (French flour and Guérande salt) may be free of animal products, but ingredients like butter or milk in the dough are not documented, so a strict vegan should ask before buying.
caveats: Hours are irregular (extra closures on the 3rd Tuesday and 4th Sunday monthly). Hard breads can sell out; takeout-focused. The brief's claim that it is near Omicho Market is incorrect — it is in northern Kanazawa near Kagatsume Station.

bio.background: Ouka is a hidden-gem bakery set inside a beautifully restored traditional house more than 150 years old, down a quiet residential lane in northern Kanazawa. The owner-baker trained in France and brought those techniques home, baking around 30 kinds of bread a day and putting out only the loaves that meet their own standards for color, crust, and texture.
bio.specialty: The shop is best known for its cream pan (about 194 yen), filled with a rich homemade custard made with French sugar and beloved since opening. Other signatures include the Tradi (about 324 yen), a lean loaf made with French flour and Guerande salt that showcases the natural sweetness and aroma of wheat, and the Petit Pre (about 421 yen, introduced December 2019), the house bread filled with demi-glace hamburger or spicy chicken and topped with melted cheese.
bio.philosophy: A quality-first approach: only breads that pass the baker's eye for appearance and texture make it to the display case.

anecdotes.text: The bakery occupies a renovated 150-plus-year-old kominka, and customers can sit on a tatami eat-in corner enjoying bread while looking out at the garden, preserving the old house's atmosphere.
Tucked off the main street in a residential area, it is easy to miss and is treasured as a hidden retreat that draws not only locals but repeat visitors from outside the prefecture.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat-flour bakery making French hard breads; flour is pervasive throughout and there are no gluten-free products or stated celiac precautions.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature items include dairy/egg custard and meat-and-cheese filled breads, baked in a shared kitchen; no vegan-specific handling is documented.

lead: 味処 大工町 よし村
name_ja: 味処 大工町 よし村
cuisine: Kaga cuisine / kappo (Kaga ryori - jibu-ni, hasu-mushi/lotus steaming, Sea of Japan fresh fish, Kaga vegetables)
neighborhood: Daikumachi, Katamachi/Korinbo area (near the Saigawa river)
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市大工町22 returned [136.652664, 36.558487], within the Kanazawa box.
seats_text: Around 16 lacquered counter seats on the first floor plus private tatami rooms upstairs; total capacity up to roughly 50 for banquets (sources vary: counter cited as 15-16 seats).
hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-14:00 (reservation required), Dinner 17:00-22:30. Closed Sundays (and the following Monday when Sunday-Monday fall on consecutive holidays).
closed_days: Sundays (and the consecutive Monday when applicable)
cultural_comfort_note: This is a traditional family-run kappo where you sit at the counter and chat with the chef; reservations and some basic Japanese (or a translation app) will make the experience much smoother for a foreign student.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. This is a traditional Kaga kappo built on soy sauce and dashi, and the signature jibu-ni is thickened and includes fresh wheat gluten (sudare-fu/fu). No gluten-free menu or dedicated handling is documented; not safe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The menu is centered on Sea of Japan seafood (sashimi, sweet shrimp, nodoguro, snow crab) and meat (duck in jibu-ni), with dashi-based seasoning throughout. No vegan options are documented.
caveats: Seat count varies by source (counter cited as 15-16; total banquet capacity up to ~50). Lunch is by reservation only. Dietary accommodation for GF/vegan is not documented.

bio.background: Founded in 1977 in Kanazawa's Daikumachi quarter, a small street historically lined with kappo restaurants near the Saigawa river. The kitchen is now led by the second-generation proprietor, Yoshimura Ryoichiro, who cooks at the counter alongside a head chef in the classic kappo style.
bio.specialty: Kaga cuisine and Sea of Japan seafood. Signatures include jibu-ni (duck and Kaga vegetables with fresh wheat gluten in a thick sauce), Kaga lotus-root dishes such as hasu-mushi (steamed lotus) and lotus-root soup, sashimi of squid, sea urchin, sweet shrimp and konbu-cured fish, and seasonal specialties like hamo with matsutake and snow crab in winter.
bio.philosophy: A 'quality over quantity' ethos: the chef goes to market daily to hand-pick fresh fish, Kaga vegetables and local ingredients, keeping more than 70 seasonal dishes available so guests can eat omakase-style at the counter.

anecdotes.text: A reviewer budgeting around 10,000 yen reported a course that ran about 11,000 yen yet far exceeded expectations in portion size and ingredient quality, singling out an exceptional abundance of matsutake with hamo and a creamy Kaga lotus-root soup.
The signature first-floor seating is a vermilion lacquered counter, where the appeal is watching the chef work and chatting casually while seasonal Kaga dishes are served.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Traditional Kaga kappo cooking relies heavily on soy sauce and dashi, and the house signature jibu-ni includes fresh wheat gluten (fu) and a flour-thickened sauce, so gluten is pervasive in the kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce (wheat-containing) is used throughout Kaga cuisine and its signature dishes here; no wheat-free soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is centered on seafood and meat with dashi-based seasoning; no separate vegan preparation is documented.

lead: 金沢おでん ゑびす
cuisine: 金沢おでん・居酒屋（Kanazawa oden / izakaya）
neighborhood: 西町・武蔵方面（近江町市場近く）
address_ja: 石川県金沢市西町藪ノ内通25-1
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [136.655731, 36.569851] for 石川県金沢市西町藪ノ内通25-1; within Kanazawa box.
seats_text: L字カウンター17席のみ (L-shaped counter, 17 seats only)
hours_raw: 17:30-22:00（食べログ）／ 17:00-23:00（金沢観光推進委員会の紹介）。日曜・祝日休み。
closed_days: 日曜・祝日 (Sundays and public holidays)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, regulars-heavy local oden counter with no English menu and limited seating, so a calling-ahead approach and basic Japanese (or a hotel/taxi referral) will make a visit far smoother for a foreign student.
gf_detail: A traditional Kanazawa oden house: the simmering broth is a continuously-replenished bonito-and-kombu dashi seasoned with soy sauce, and items like beef tendon, meatballs and homemade side dishes are typical wheat-and-soy-containing fare. There is no documented gluten-free accommodation, and the soy-based oden broth is not celiac-safe.
vegan_detail: The kitchen centers on fish-dashi oden plus sashimi, beef tendon, beef tongue and other meat/seafood dishes. The base broth itself is made from bonito (katsuo), so even vegetable oden items are cooked in fish stock; there is no vegan offering documented.
caveats: Hours differ between sources (food listing shows 17:30-22:00; the Kanazawa tourism writeup gives 17:00-23:00) — confirm by phone. The shop is overwhelmingly a locals' place: a tourism writeup stresses the proprietress's welcoming, no-discrimination hospitality and notes visitors arrive via taxi/hotel referral, but at least one online reviewer reported the shop turning tourists away and operating largely on a regulars/phone-ahead basis. Calling ahead (076-231-4878) is strongly advisable.

bio.background: An old-guard Kanazawa oden house founded in 1966 (Showa 41) on Yabunouchi-dori in Nishi-machi, a short walk from the Musashi/Omicho Market area. It is a tiny, family-run counter establishment, run by the proprietress alongside her daughter.
bio.specialty: Kanazawa oden simmered in a bonito-and-kombu dashi that has been continuously replenished since the shop opened in 1966. Beyond oden, the counter is known for hearty homemade plates such as slow-cooked beef tendon (牛すじ煮込み), salt-grilled beef tongue (牛タン塩焼), roast pork and meatballs, plus fresh local sashimi, all in generous portions at reasonable prices.
bio.philosophy: The proprietress is documented as practicing wakehedatenai motenashi — hospitality without discrimination — saying she never wants to betray the trust of customers who come expecting good food.

anecdotes.text: The oden broth is a bonito-and-kombu dashi that has been topped up and carried forward continuously since the shop's founding in 1966.

The shop is run by the proprietress together with her daughter and is so beloved by locals that it is busy daily despite rarely appearing on tourism or review sites; visiting travelers typically arrive via taxi or hotel introductions.
Oden pieces run roughly 80 to 400 yen, with a typical per-person spend around 3,000 yen (about 5,000 yen if ordering crab).

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Kanazawa oden is simmered in a soy-sauce-seasoned bonito/kombu dashi; the broth and most items are wheat- and soy-containing by nature and no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The base simmering broth is made with bonito (fish), so even vegetable items are cooked in animal stock; the menu also centers on meat and seafood.

positives.text: Homemade, generously portioned dishes prepared fresh by a small family team, and a documented welcoming, no-discrimination hospitality from the proprietress.

lead: のど黒めし本舗 いたる
name_ja: のど黒めし本舗 いたる
cuisine: Nodoguro (blackthroat seaperch) rice bowls and Kaga seafood — a specialty shop from the Kanazawa "Itaru" group
neighborhood: 香林坊・片町（香林坊交差点近く）
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [136.652985, 36.561588] for 石川県金沢市片町2-7-5; within the Kanazawa box (片町/香林坊 area, near the Korinbo crossing).
seats_text: About 45 spaced-out seats with counter and table seating; all non-smoking, tablet ordering.
hours_raw: Lunch 12:00-14:30, Dinner 17:30-20:30 (L.O. 20:00). Note: some listings show lunch-focused hours of 11:30-16:30/17:00 (L.O. 16:30). Hours vary by source/season — confirm by phone.
closed_days: Sundays (if a Monday is a public holiday, the shop opens Sunday and closes the Monday instead).
cultural_comfort_note: A popular, tourist-friendly seafood-bowl shop with tablet ordering and card/QR payments; a little Japanese helps but you can manage with basics and pointing at the famous nodoguro bowl.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The food centers on grilled fish over seasoned rice eaten with condiments and a finishing dashi broth, which typically rely on wheat-containing soy sauce and dashi seasonings. No gluten-free menu or celiac handling is documented; not suitable for strict celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. This is a fish specialty shop built around nodoguro (blackthroat seaperch), yellowtail and other Kaga seafood, with fish-bone dashi broth. There are no documented vegan dishes.
caveats: Hours differ across sources (lunch-only vs. lunch+dinner); confirm before visiting. Same phone (076-233-1147) and the 片町2-7-5 building (adjacent to the Itaru Korinbo branch) are consistent across the official site, Tabelog, and the relocation news, confirming identity. No dietary-accommodation information was found.

bio.background: A specialty shop devoted almost entirely to one dish — nodoguro-meshi, a bowl of premium blackthroat seaperch. It belongs to the locally beloved Kanazawa group '酒と人情料理 いたる' (Sake and Heartfelt Cuisine Itaru), and the original nodoguro-meshi shop opened in 2015 to coincide with the Hokuriku Shinkansen reaching Kanazawa. In February 2021 it relocated from Kakinokibatake to the Korinbo area, into a building originally constructed in 1964 (the year of the Tokyo Olympics) and lovingly renovated.
bio.origin: Kanazawa, Ishikawa — a homegrown offshoot of the city's Itaru izakaya group.
bio.specialty: Nodoguro-meshi (3,300 yen): generously piled, aromatically grilled nodoguro over seasoned rice, eaten in three stages — first plain, then with condiments, and finally as ochazuke with a rich, cloudy broth simmered from nodoguro bones. Other dishes include a sesame yellowtail bowl (ゴマぶり丼), and handmade sweets such as umeboshi-filled 梅つつみ.
bio.philosophy: Owner Ishiguro Itaru wanted the relocated shop to feel like a welcoming hatago (traditional travelers' inn), evoking an inn his family once ran. He filled the interior with heirlooms, including Inami wood carvings and paintings left by his late father.

anecdotes.text: When the shop moved to Korinbo in 2021, the owner designed it as a nostalgic 'hatago'-style retreat and decorated it with family heirlooms — Inami wood carvings and his late father's paintings.
The signature nodoguro-meshi is meant to be savored in three escalating stages, finishing with a cloudy soup extracted from the nodoguro's own bones poured over the rice.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are seasoned-rice fish bowls finished with condiments and a fish-bone dashi broth; these preparations conventionally use wheat-containing soy sauce and dashi seasonings, so the menu is not safe for strict gluten avoidance.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Entirely seafood-based (nodoguro, yellowtail, Kaga fish) with fish-bone broth; no vegan options are documented.

cuisine: グルテンフリー米粉パン・天然酵母ベーカリー（米粉パン、米粉ケーキ・シフォン・チーズケーキ・タルト、グルテンフリー麺。小麦・卵・乳・ナッツ不使用、無添加）
gf_detail: Dedicated gluten-free bakery. All breads use 100% domestic Koshihikari rice flour with no wheat gluten; the shop states it avoids all seven mandatory-label allergens (which includes wheat) as well as thickeners and other additives, and the workshop does not handle wheat. This is one of the rare central-Kanazawa spots a celiac diner can treat as genuinely safe, though no third-party celiac certification is documented.
vegan_detail: Egg- and dairy-free baking is central to the shop and the official site lists vegan items, but some products use additive-free cheese or may contain honey, so confirm per item.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市駅西新町1丁目4-1 ロイヤルコート駅西105
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 石川県金沢市駅西新町一丁目4番1号; within the Kanazawa box.
neighborhood: 駅西新町（金沢駅西、JR金沢駅から徒歩約10分）
seats_text: Small takeout-focused neighborhood bakery on the ground floor of a residential building; counter/retail service rather than a sit-down dining room. Seat count not documented.
hours_raw: 11:30-16:30（売り切れ次第閉店、不定休あり）。木曜・金曜定休。
closed_days: 木曜・金曜（Thursday & Friday）。売り切れ次第閉店、その他不定休あり。
cultural_comfort_note: A small owner-run neighborhood bakery where a little Japanese and pointing will go a long way; allergen labeling is detailed but mostly in Japanese, so come prepared to ask about a specific item.
caveats: Hours are irregular: the shop closes once items sell out and takes additional ad-hoc days off beyond the regular Thursday/Friday closures, so go early. While the workshop is wheat/egg/dairy/nut-free, individual sweets may include honey or additive-free cheese, so vegan diners should confirm the specific product.

bio.background: Kobayashi trained at a pastry school and worked at restaurants and bakeries across Ishikawa, and also spent time as a TV and radio reporter; in 2012 she published a recipe book on fruit-enzyme extracts and works as a natural-sweets planner.
bio.specialty: 100% domestic Koshihikari rice-flour breads leavened with her own homemade apple yeast — a crusty-outside, chewy-inside loaf — plus salt bread, muffin-style rolls, gluten-free chiffon and cheesecakes, tarts, madeleines, and gluten-free pasta and ramen for mail order.
bio.philosophy: Make additive-free, allergen-conscious baking that people with food allergies can eat with confidence, using natural yeast and no wheat gluten, eggs, dairy, nuts, thickeners or additives.

anecdotes.text: Kobayashi started baking rice-flour bread after a friend asked her to, then spent a full year perfecting the recipe through extensive trial and error.
She resolved to open the shop after an event at a university hospital revealed how many people live with food allergies, deciding to support that underserved community.
She says she hopes customers experience the 'crispy outside, chewy inside' texture that is unique to rice-flour bread.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The bakery is built around rice flour and states its workshop does not contain wheat (and avoids all seven mandatory-label allergens), which strongly limits gluten cross-contact, though no formal celiac certification or explicit shared-equipment statement is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Some products use additive-free cheese (e.g. genoise and anchovy pizzas), so cheese is present in the kitchen; vegan diners should confirm individual items.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The owner opened the shop specifically to serve people with food allergies and avoids the seven mandatory allergens, so staff are oriented around allergy-conscious baking.

positives.text: All breads use 100% domestic Koshihikari rice flour with no wheat gluten, homemade apple yeast, and no thickeners or additives.
Products avoid the seven mandatory-label allergens (wheat, egg, milk and others), and the official site lists dedicated gluten-free and vegan baked goods including chiffon and cheesecakes.

lead: コーヒー処 ノバール
name_ja: コーヒー処 ノバール
cuisine: 純喫茶・レトロ喫茶
gf_detail: A classic Showa-era kissaten whose menu centers on pudding, parfaits, cakes, napolitan pasta, and sandwiches, all wheat- and dairy-based. No documented gluten-free offering and no allergy accommodation. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: The signature items (custard pudding with fruit and whipped cream, pudding parfaits, cakes) rely heavily on eggs and dairy, and the cafe also serves napolitan and sandwiches. No vegan or plant-based options are documented.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市有松2丁目4-30
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 石川県金沢市有松二丁目4番30号; within the Kanazawa bounding box.
neighborhood: 有松（香林坊・片町の南、有松交差点付近）
seats_text: Small independent cafe with table seating throughout in a nostalgic Showa interior; exact seat count not published.
hours_raw: 9:00-19:00; closed Wednesdays. Hours and holidays may change, so check before visiting.
closed_days: Wednesdays
cultural_comfort_note: A warm, family-run retro coffee house where the master and mama greet regulars; little to no English, so a few words of Japanese or pointing at the menu will make your visit smooth.
caveats: Cash only and no reservations. The cafe is in the Arimatsu area south of Korinbo/Katamachi, an easy bus ride or walk from the central shopping district. Hours and the weekly holiday can change, so confirm before a special trip.

bio.background: A long-loved retro coffee house near the Arimatsu intersection, Nobaru has been operating for more than 40 years. It is run by a husband-and-wife team, the master and the mama, and its brick exterior and Showa-era interior, with stained-glass partitions casting soft light, have remained almost unchanged since it opened.
bio.specialty: The signature is the homemade firm-set custard pudding: the master makes the pudding by hand and the mama decoratively cuts fruit and adds whipped cream on top. It is gently sweetened so it pairs well with coffee or tea.
