The pudding parfait (around 750 yen) is praised for its generous volume, and the cafe also serves classic kissaten fare like napolitan, sandwiches, cakes and breakfast sets, with most items under 1,000 yen.
bio.philosophy: The seating is laid out so that sight lines between customers are calculated to keep others unobtrusive, creating a relaxed space where guests can linger comfortably.
roles: mama (front of house, fruit and cream decoration)

anecdotes.text: Actress Yoshida Yo is said to have worked part-time at this cafe when she lived in Kanazawa in her younger years, which has added to its local fame.
The interior is nearly unchanged from when it opened, with stained-glass partitions that cast soft, reflective light, making it a popular photography spot.

lead: 特別名勝兼六園 堤亭
name_ja: 特別名勝兼六園 堤亭
cuisine: 加賀料理・治部煮・甘味処（茶店）
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The signature dishes are jibu-ni (a stew thickened with wheat flour and seasoned with wheat-based soy sauce), plus udon and soba, all containing wheat. Sweets like dango and ankoro-mochi use rice flour but are served with soy-based or sweet sauces. No gluten-free menu, labeling, or accommodation is documented. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: This is a Kaga-cuisine teahouse centered on jibu-ni (duck/meat stew) and seafood (sweet-shrimp rice bowl), so the savory menu is not vegan. However, as a 甘味処 (sweets house) it offers matcha tea, plum juice, soft-serve, and dango/ankoro-mochi sweets — some of which may be plant-based but are not verified vegan (sauces and dashi unconfirmed). No declared vegan menu. A vegan visitor could likely have matcha and certain sweets only; confirm ingredients with staff.
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市兼六町1-15 returned [136.661621, 36.563438], within the central Kanazawa box. Matches the Kenrokuen tea-shop street location near the Katsurazaka gate.
neighborhood: 兼六園（茶店通り・桂坂口付近）
seats_text: Second-floor tatami seating; the Kenrokuen tourism association lists 64 tatami seats, while the official site describes roughly 140 seats across its second-floor banquet rooms. Veranda/outdoor seating is also available for sweets and matcha.
hours_raw: 9:00–16:30 daily (lunch service to ~14:30). Seasonal: 9:00–17:00 (Mar 1–Oct 15), 9:00–16:00 (Oct 16–end of Feb). Dinner by reservation only, extended during illumination and cherry-blossom seasons. Tabelog lists 08:15–17:00.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular holidays). Hours and days vary by season; open daily in general but closures are not on a fixed schedule.
cultural_comfort_note: A welcoming tourist-oriented teahouse inside Kenrokuen where you can point at picture menus and order matcha or jibu-ni with just a few words of Japanese; a friendly greeting goes a long way.
caveats: Located inside the Kenrokuen tea-shop area via a gravel path up Sumiyakizaka; entry to Kenrokuen Garden itself may require an admission fee depending on the entrance used. Hours change by season and closures are irregular — check ahead. Savory dishes are wheat- and seafood/meat-heavy; not suitable for celiac or strict vegan diners beyond sweets and matcha.

bio.background: Tsutsumitei was founded in 1888 (Meiji 21) and occupies a building more than a century old on the historic tea-shop street of Kenrokuen, one of Japan's three great landscape gardens. With its calm, russet-walled rooms and second-floor tatami chambers overlooking the garden, it has served generations of visitors near the Katsurazaka entrance and Kanazawa Castle's Ishikawa Gate.
bio.origin: Kanazawa, Ishikawa — within the grounds of the Special Place of Scenic Beauty, Kenrokuen Garden.
bio.specialty: Kanazawa's signature Kaga dish jibu-ni (a stew of duck/meat and vegetables), served on its own or as jibu-ni udon and soba; also a sweet-shrimp rice bowl. As a sweets house it is known for matcha, homemade-sauce dango, ankoro-mochi (sweet bean-paste rice cakes), seasonal parfaits, soft-serve, and house-made plum juice.
bio.philosophy: A traditional teahouse experience: enjoy regional Kaga cuisine and Japanese sweets in a historic garden setting, with veranda seating for a quick matcha and dango or upstairs rooms for a full meal.

anecdotes.text: The shop safeguards carved wooden statues of the prosperity deities Daikoku and Ebisu as family heirlooms, made from timber salvaged from the historic Saikawa Bridge during its 1918 reconstruction.
On the first floor the shop sells local souvenirs, crafts, Kaga-yuzen textiles, and regional sake, while the second-floor tatami rooms serve meals with garden views.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Jibu-ni is traditionally thickened with wheat flour and seasoned with wheat-based soy sauce, and the menu prominently features udon and soba; assume wheat is present throughout the savory offerings.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen centers on duck/meat jibu-ni and seafood (sweet-shrimp bowl); dashi and broths likely contain animal products, so even vegetable or sweet items may share preparation surfaces.

positives.text: As a 甘味処, matcha tea, plum juice, and rice-based sweets (dango, ankoro-mochi) are available, giving non-meat diners some options alongside the savory Kaga dishes.

cuisine: Kaga regional Japanese
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The signature dishes are built on wheat: jibu-ni dusts chicken/seafood in wheat flour before simmering in dashi, and the house "jibu soba" uses noodles that contain wheat (standard soba is a buckwheat-wheat blend). Soy-based dashi and seasonings are used throughout. No gluten-free menu or accommodations are documented. Celiac travelers should not consider this safe.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan destination. The cooking centers on chicken, seafood (sea urchin, nodoguro, sushi), Noto beef, and dashi stock. No vegan or vegetarian set menus are documented. A vegan diner could potentially ask about the cafe (sweets, matcha lattes, local teas), but wagashi and dairy lattes are not reliably plant-based and nothing is confirmed. Best to treat as ask-only with very limited prospects.
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市兼六町1-20 returned [136.661621, 36.563438], within the Kanazawa box and matching the Kenrokuen garden location.
neighborhood: Kenrokuen Garden (inside the garden, in front of Japan's oldest fountain)
seats_text: Roughly 60 seats per the garden association (about 40 indoor chairs plus a 20-seat terrace). A separate listing cites 120 seats; the indoor-plus-terrace figure is used here.
hours_raw: Breakfast 9:00–11:00 (reservation required, by 3pm previous day); Lunch 11:00–14:00; Cafe 9:00–16:30 (to 16:00 in winter); Evening "Genan" dining 18:00+ by reservation, light-up season only. Open year-round per one listing; official site notes irregular closures.
closed_days: No fixed closing day (essentially open daily with the garden); official site lists irregular closures.
cultural_comfort_note: An English menu is available and the garden setting draws many overseas visitors, so a foreign student can order comfortably here.
caveats: Lunch sets and breakfast run higher than a casual meal (jibu soba sets around 2,800 yen, jibu-ni sets 3,300 yen and up; evening kaiseki 15,000 yen+), so this is not a budget stop. Breakfast and evening dining require advance reservation; lunch and cafe are generally walk-in. Hours shift with the season and the garden's light-up calendar.

bio.background: Kenrokutei occupies one of the most enviable addresses in Kanazawa: inside Kenrokuen Garden, looking out over what is said to be Japan's oldest fountain. The kitchen is led by a chef who trained at a well-regarded Kyoto establishment, and the place leans on that pedigree while staying rooted in Kaga's own food culture.
bio.specialty: The house specialty is an original "jibu soba" — the classic Kaga dish jibu-ni reimagined over noodles, with crisp roasted chicken and Kaga renkon (lotus root) worked into the soba. The broader menu runs through jibu-ni sets, seafood rice bowls (sea urchin, nodoguro), Noto beef sukiyaki, and hand-rolled sushi, alongside a garden cafe of wagashi, matcha lattes and local teas.
bio.philosophy: The cooking emphasizes handmade, additive-free dishes built on seasonal local vegetables and seafood, served in a calm room with garden views.

anecdotes.text: Beyond daytime dining, the same address transforms in the evening into a private dining room called Genan (幻庵), where guests can take a kaiseki course while looking out over the illuminated garden during light-up season.
The signature jibu soba kneads crisp roasted chicken and Kaga lotus root into the noodles, turning the regional braise jibu-ni into a noodle dish unique to this house.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Jibu-ni is prepared by dusting chicken and other ingredients in wheat flour before simmering, so wheat is integral to the signature cooking and shared prep surfaces.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes rely on dashi and Japanese seasonings (soy-based), and the signature soba contains wheat alongside buckwheat; no wheat-free preparations are documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen centers on chicken, seafood, beef and dashi; vegetable and cafe items are prepared in the same kitchen with no documented vegan separation.

positives.text: An English menu is available, which can help travelers communicate basic needs even though no formal allergen program is documented.

name_ja: ジャルダン ポール・ボキューズ
cuisine: フレンチ（クラシック／イノベーティブ）
neighborhood: 広坂・香林坊（しいのき迎賓館内）
gf_detail: No documented gluten-free program. This is classic French cuisine where flour-thickened sauces, bread, and wheat-based preparations are central, so it is not celiac-safe. Allergy consultations are accepted at the time of reservation, so a guest could ask, but cross-contamination cannot be assumed to be controlled.
vegan_detail: A vegetarian menu is available, but it must be arranged in advance at reservation. There is no documented fully vegan menu, and as a French kitchen built around butter, cream, dairy, and seafood/meat stocks, vegan diners should confirm a fully plant-based course directly when booking rather than assume it.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市広坂2-1-1 しいのき迎賓館2F
geocode_note: Geocoded from GSI to 広坂二丁目1番; within central Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 60 seats in the main dining room plus a private room (sources vary: 1 room up to 36 people, or 2 private rooms).
hours_raw: Lunch 11:30–15:00 (L.O. 13:30); Dinner 17:30–22:00 (L.O. 20:00). Closed Mondays (or the following day if Monday is a holiday).
closed_days: Mondays (or the following day if Monday is a national holiday)
cultural_comfort_note: This is a polished fine-dining French restaurant inside a historic guesthouse that hosts international guests and offers online reservations in English, so a foreign student would be comfortable, though the price point makes it a special-occasion choice.
caveats: Vegetarian menu and allergy needs must be arranged in advance at reservation; this is a high-end French restaurant (lunch from roughly 8,000 yen, dinner 15,000 yen and up), not a casual or budget option.

bio.background: Jardin Paul Bocuse occupies the second floor of Shiinoki Geihinkan, the former Taisho-era Ishikawa Prefectural Government building in central Kanazawa, restored as an elegant guesthouse near Kenrokuen. It opened in 2010 and is one of only two places (alongside Tokyo) carrying the Paul Bocuse name in Japan, inheriting specialties from the celebrated main restaurant in Lyon, France.
bio.specialty: Classic Lyonnaise French specialties handed down from the Paul Bocuse main restaurant, combined with seasonal French dishes built around Ishikawa and Hokuriku seafood and produce.
bio.philosophy: A fusion of Lyonnaise gastronomic tradition with the bounty of Ishikawa, set in a historic building accented with Kanazawa cultural touches such as gold leaf.

anecdotes.text: It is described as one of only two restaurants in the world outside Lyon carrying the Paul Bocuse name in Japan, alongside its Tokyo counterpart.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Allergy consultations are accepted at the time of reservation.

positives.text: A vegetarian menu is available with advance arrangement.

cuisine: Kaga ryori (kaiseki); signature jibu-ni and tai no karamushi
neighborhood: Oyama-cho, near Oyama Shrine (Korinbo / Hokutetsu Kanazawa area)
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates for 金沢市尾山町2-27; within Kanazawa box.
gf_detail: Traditional Kaga kaiseki built on dashi and soy sauce (which contains wheat), with wheat-thickened jibu-ni and various wheat-containing components. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated handling, and nothing about celiac safety is documented. Not suitable for those who must strictly avoid gluten.
vegan_detail: A seafood- and dashi-centric formal Kaga cuisine ryotei; signature dishes are sea-bream and the duck/seafood stew jibu-ni, and stocks are bonito/fish based. No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented. As a fully reservation-based ryotei it may be possible to request a customized course in advance by phone, but this is not advertised and would need to be arranged ahead.
seats_text: Formal ryotei with private tatami rooms; exact seat count not published.
hours_raw: 11:00-15:00, 17:00-21:00 (completely reservation-based; phone reservations 9:00-18:00)
closed_days: Wednesdays (and 3rd Tuesday); New Year holidays. Reservation-based, so hours can vary.
cultural_comfort_note: This is a formal, completely reservation-based heritage ryotei with multi-course kaiseki at high prices, so a foreign student would be most comfortable visiting with a Japanese-speaking guide or host who can book and explain the courses.
caveats: Completely reservation-based; book by phone in advance (9:00-18:00). Lunch courses around 6,000 yen and dinner around 12,000 yen and up, before tax/service. Not vegan and not gluten-free; arrange any dietary needs at the time of booking. Hours/closed days can vary as a reservation-only ryotei.

bio.background: Otomoro is a long-established Kanazawa ryotei founded in 1828 (Tenpo era), whose family long served as cooks to the Maeda lords of the Kaga domain. Over generations it has preserved the refined, ceremonial cooking of the castle town. When Kanazawa Station opened in 1898 the house began producing railway bento, and it continues to run a bento shop and prepared-foods counter alongside its formal dining rooms.
bio.specialty: Traditional Kaga cuisine, above all jibu-ni (a duck/seafood stew thickened and simmered in a savory broth) and tai no karamushi (steamed sea bream), served in seasonal kaiseki courses.
bio.philosophy: To carry forward Kanazawa's culinary heritage by pursuing the elegance, aroma and beauty of traditional Kaga cuisine, using carefully selected seasonal ingredients and meticulous handwork while offering heartfelt hospitality.
roles: Maker of historic Kanazawa railway bento

anecdotes.text: The family served as cooks to the Maeda lords of the Kaga domain, and the house has carried that castle-town cuisine forward since its founding in 1828.
Its tea room Isseian occupies a roughly 250-year-old building that once served as a place of tea instruction for Kaga-domain samurai.
The restaurant began selling station bento when Kanazawa Station opened in 1898, beginning a long railway-food tradition.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Traditional Kaga kaiseki here relies on soy sauce and dashi-based seasoning; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, and the signature jibu-ni is traditionally thickened with wheat flour.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Cooking is centered on fish/bonito dashi and seafood (sea bream, jibu-ni), so plant-only dishes are not part of the documented menu and shared stocks would be used throughout the kitchen.

name_ja: 金沢カレー＆ハントンライス だいだい（橙）
cuisine: 金沢カレー・ハントンライス（洋食）
gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The menu is built around breaded/fried cutlets (katsu curry), fried fish and shrimp on omelette rice (Hunton rice), and roux-based Kanazawa curry — all wheat-heavy. No gluten-free accommodations are documented on the official site or elsewhere.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. Signature dishes center on egg (omelette rice), fried fish, shrimp, pork cutlets and meat-based Kanazawa curry. No plant-based or vegetarian options are documented.
geocode_note: GSI geocoded 石川県金沢市片町2-8-10 to [136.652527, 36.56242], within the central Kanazawa box. Matches the documented location behind Korinbo Tokyu Square near Seseragi-dori.
seats_text: 13 seats total (7 counter, 4 table, 2 other) — small, easy for solo diners
neighborhood: 片町（香林坊東急スクエア裏・せせらぎ通り近く）
hours_raw: 11:00–15:00 (L.O. 14:45 / food L.O. 14:30). Closed Tuesdays and the day following public holidays.
closed_days: Tuesdays (and the day after public holidays)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, casual local diner with no English menu documented; a foreign student would likely be most comfortable going with someone who can read Japanese or with a translation app, and should note it is cash-only.
caveats: Lunch-only operation (11:00–15:00); the official site lists hours to 15:00 while some listings noted an earlier evening service that has since changed. Cash only, no reservations, very small seating, so expect possible waits at peak lunch.

bio.origin: Opened April 29, 2023 in Katamachi, central Kanazawa, just behind Korinbo Tokyu Square near Seseragi-dori.
bio.background: A small independent western-style diner created to let visitors enjoy Kanazawa's two great local specialties in one place: Kanazawa curry (a dark, thick roux served on a stainless steel plate with shredded cabbage and a breaded cutlet) and Hunton rice (an omelette-over-rice dish topped with fried fish or shrimp and tartar sauce, a Kanazawa invention dating to around 1970).
bio.specialty: The signature 橙（だいだい）ハントンライス is the headliner — a deluxe Hunton rice topped with two oversized fried shrimp and house-made tartar sauce. The other pillar is Kanazawa-style katsu curry. A local yuzu cider (柚子乙女) is offered to drink.
bio.philosophy: To showcase Kanazawa's two best-known comfort foods side by side in a warm, retro, wood-toned space that is welcoming even for solo diners.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant pairs the two dishes Kanazawa is most famous for; its flagship Hunton rice is plated with two especially large fried shrimp atop a fluffy omelette with house-made tartar sauce.
Hunton rice itself is a Kanazawa original, said to have been devised around 1970 in connection with the well-known local bakery German Bakery.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Kitchen revolves around deep-fried breaded items (pork cutlets, fried fish, shrimp) and wheat-based curry roux, so heavy gluten cross-contact is inherent.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Core dishes use egg (omelette rice), seafood, and meat; no plant-based items are documented, making vegan cross-contact effectively unavoidable.

cuisine: Edomae / Noto-mae sushi (Hokuriku seafood), counter omakase
gf_detail: This is a traditional Edomae/Noto-mae sushi counter built around soy sauce, wheat-containing seasonings, and vinegared rice — not celiac-safe. Nigiri is brushed/served with soy-based nikiri sauce (contains wheat), and there is no documented gluten-free protocol or dedicated preparation. A determined celiac would need to discuss it directly when booking, but it is not set up for gluten-free dining.
vegan_detail: A dedicated high-end sushi restaurant: the entire omakase is built on fish, shellfish, sea urchin, conger eel, and seafood-based dishes, plus dashi and seafood appetizers. There are no documented vegan or vegetarian options; this is not a suitable destination for plant-based diners.
geocode_note: GSI address search returned [136.662003, 36.572708] for 石川県金沢市彦三町1-8-26, within the Kanazawa box. Location is in Hikosomachi, central Kanazawa, near Omicho Market / Musashigatsuji and the Kazuemachi teahouse district.
neighborhood: Hikosomachi (彦三町), central Kanazawa — near Omicho Market / Musashigatsuji and Kazuemachi
seats_text: Counter only; reported as 8 counter seats (some listings note up to ~9). Single counter facing the chef with a small Japanese garden view.
hours_raw: 17:00–21:00 (dinner only). Closed Sundays; occasional irregular closures.
closed_days: Sundays; occasional irregular closures (不定休)
cultural_comfort_note: This is an intimate, high-end (roughly ¥22,000+ per person), reservation-only omakase counter where the chef serves you directly — comfortable if you are confident with sushi-counter etiquette and some Japanese, but a big splurge and fairly formal for a casual student outing.
caveats: Reservation-only (online), dinner only, and a serious splurge at roughly ¥22,000–¥39,999 per person for the omakase. Eight-seat counter, so seats are limited and booked well ahead. Not a drop-in spot.

bio.origin: Toyama Prefecture (born 1973, from the Oyabe area)
bio.background: Chef Mitsuhiro Kibatani took an unusual route to the sushi counter. He worked at the Tsukiji fish market and ran his family's seafood business before going to culinary school, then trained in Edomae technique in Tokyo — including under the Yoshiura lineage and at Ginza's renowned Sushi Aoki. After about six years running a catering sushi operation in his Toyama hometown, he opened his own shop, Sushi Kibatani, in Kanazawa's Hikosomachi district in April 2016.
bio.specialty: Refined Edomae-style nigiri reimagined with Hokuriku seafood ('Noto-mae'): well-known signatures include his fragrant, fatty anago (conger eel) with a light sweet glaze, aori squid, sweet shrimp with konowata, fugu milt, and uni paired with crab. The meal is an omakase course of appetizers and roughly 15 pieces of nigiri over well-balanced red-vinegar rice.
bio.philosophy: Restraint and ingredient quality over heavy treatment — the nigiri is deliberately light so the diner stays comfortable through the whole course. The chef makes early-morning runs to Hokuriku fishing ports such as Himi and Shinminato to hand-select fish at the auctions, pairing the catch with Noto pesticide-free rice and a red-vinegar shari.

anecdotes.text: Kibatani visits fishing ports around 5 a.m. to personally choose seasonal local fish at the markets and auctions before service.

A reviewer was so impressed after first encountering his sushi in early 2016 that they booked months ahead of the restaurant's April 2016 opening; the chef's conger eel arriving fresh from Tsukiji 'dissolves seamlessly with the well-balanced vinegared rice.'
He trained in Tokyo and at Ginza's Sushi Aoki, and delivers near Tokyo-level quality at roughly 60-70% of metropolitan sushi prices, opening at 17:00 to suit evening shinkansen departures.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As an Edomae/Noto-mae sushi counter, nigiri is finished with soy-based nikiri sauce and the meal relies on standard soy sauce and seasonings, which typically contain wheat; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

cuisine: Kaga local cuisine / washoku (Japanese kappo) — jibuni, nodoguro, crab, local sake
neighborhood: Korinbo/Katamachi (Nagamachi samurai district)
geocode_note: GSI geocoded to 長町一丁目2番22号; coordinates within Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 50 seats total (counter 10, tables, 3 private tatami rooms)
hours_raw: Mon-Sat 17:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30). Closed Sundays (except three-day holiday weekends), year-end/New Year.
closed_days: Sundays (except three-day holiday weekends), year-end/New Year
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional, well-established kappo-style washoku restaurant where a few words of Japanese and a phone reservation will smooth your visit; staff are used to tourists but it is an evening dining destination rather than a casual drop-in.
gf_detail: This is a traditional Japanese restaurant whose cooking relies heavily on soy sauce, dashi, and Kaga local dishes like jibuni (which is typically thickened/seasoned with soy and often dusted with wheat flour). No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented, and there is no dedicated fryer. Not safe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: The menu is built around seafood (nodoguro, crab, oysters, gas shrimp), duck (jibuni), and fish-based dashi. No vegan or vegetarian offering is documented; this is a seafood-and-meat washoku destination, not suitable for vegans.

bio.background: A long-running Kanazawa washoku restaurant, established in 1971, set near the Nagamachi samurai district just off Korinbo. Over more than half a century it has built its reputation on Kaga regional cooking and the seafood of the Sea of Japan.
bio.specialty: Nodoguro (blackthroat seaperch, grilled or as sashimi), Ishikawa snow/Kano crab, natural rock oysters, white gas shrimp sashimi, the house crab-cream croquette, and Kaga local dishes including jibuni (duck simmered in soy-seasoned broth). Seasonal lineup spans firefly squid and gas shrimp in spring, rock oysters and Kaga vegetables in summer, hairy crab and sweet shrimp in autumn, and Kano crab and winter yellowtail in winter.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen keeps preparation as simple as possible to let the natural flavor of carefully sourced ingredients come through (素材本来の味を生かす為、調理はできるだけシンプルにして), aiming to make refined Kanazawa cuisine approachable.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant frames its food around the seasons of the Sea of Japan and the Kaga region, rotating its centerpiece dishes through firefly squid in spring, rock oysters in summer, hairy crab in autumn, and prized Kano crab in winter.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Cooking is centered on Kaga local dishes such as jibuni and a range of simmered and grilled preparations that conventionally use soy sauce and dashi; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is dominated by seafood, crab, duck and fish dashi, so vegan dishes and the avoidance of animal-based stock are not accommodated.

cuisine: Creative washoku izakaya / kappo (seasonal Japanese cuisine, seafood, sake)
neighborhood: Katamachi (Katamachi 2-chome), Kanazawa
gf_detail: This is a Japanese seasonal cuisine izakaya built around sashimi, simmered and grilled seafood, and sake. Dishes rely heavily on dashi and Japanese soy sauce (which contains wheat), and there is no documented gluten-free menu or allergy program. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: The menu centers on fresh fish and seafood (sashimi, anago, uni hand rolls) with daily market catch as the core draw, and dashi-based seasoning throughout. No vegan or vegetarian offering is documented.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市片町2-23-3 プリンスビル 1F 101
geocode_note: Geocoded from chō-level address (片町二丁目23番3号) via GSI; coordinates fall within the central Kanazawa box.
seats_text: Counter seating plus 4-person and 6-person sunken (horigotatsu) tables and a small private room. Listed sources vary between 28 and 32 seats.
hours_raw: Mon–Sat 17:00–00:00; Sun 17:00–22:00. Irregular closures (不定休).
closed_days: No fixed closed day; irregular closures (不定休)
cultural_comfort_note: This is a local counter izakaya where ordering and conversation happen in Japanese, so a little Japanese or a translation app will help a foreign student feel comfortable.

bio.background: Shintani grew up around cooking — his father was a professional chef and his siblings also went into the trade — and he entered the kitchen himself at age 15. He trained in hotels and traditional ryokan and worked his way up to head chef at the popular Katamachi izakaya 'Sho' before going independent and opening Shokudo Tsuki in April 2022. He is a certified sake sommelier (kikisake-shi).
bio.specialty: Daily-changing seasonal Japanese cooking driven by what he hand-picks at the market each morning, with fresh fish at the heart of the menu. Signature plates include seasonal sashimi (季節の鮮魚のお造り), a chilled simmered-anago bowl, grilled yanagi-sawara finished with sudachi, and a raw Hokkaido (Nemuro) uni hand roll that regulars have followed him from his 'Sho' days as a favorite closing dish. He pairs these with local sake sourced from across Japan.
bio.philosophy: The name 食道 ('the path of food') reflects his wish to roam freely across genres rather than be boxed into formal washoku, balancing respect for tradition with playful creativity. He wants guests to drop in casually and spend a relaxed, unfussy evening.

anecdotes.text: The raw uni hand roll using Hokkaido Nemuro uni has been a customer favorite since Shintani's time at the izakaya 'Sho', and longtime guests still order it as their finishing dish.
Shintani came from a cooking family — his father was a professional chef and his siblings also pursued culinary careers — and he started in the kitchen at 15.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The cooking is traditional Japanese seafood cuisine relying on dashi and soy-sauce-based seasoning, which in Japan normally contains wheat; no gluten-free accommodation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is built around fresh fish and seafood with dashi-based seasoning; no vegan or plant-only dishes are documented.

caveats: Seat count differs between sources (Tabelog 28; official site 32). Chef tenure is described as starting at age 15 with ~15 years' experience on his profile page but as "over 10 years" on the official site. No allergy or dietary program is documented anywhere; GF/vegan assessments are inferred from the seafood- and dashi-based menu.

cuisine: Soba / udon / donburi (traditional Kanazawa noodle shop)
neighborhood: Shimo-Ōmichō, beside Ōmichō Market (near Hokutetsu Kanazawa Station)
address_ja: 石川県金沢市下近江町42番地
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市下近江町42番地 returned [136.658203, 36.571548], within the Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 28 seats: 5 tables of 4, plus two raised tatami-style seating areas of 4 each (one source lists ~16).
hours_raw: Mon, Thu–Sun 11:00–21:00 (L.O. 20:00); Tue 11:00–16:00 (L.O. 15:00); Wed closed. (General listings note 11:00–21:00.)
closed_days: Wednesday
cultural_comfort_note: An old-school local noodle counter where staff likely speak little English and there is no English menu, so a foreign student should arrive ready to point at photos or order simple dishes by name.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core menu is wheat udon and buckwheat soba (soba is typically cut with wheat), all served in a wheat-based shoyu/dashi broth; noodles are made daily from wheat flour, salt and water. No gluten-free accommodation is documented and there is no dedicated equipment.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The broth is a katsuo/kombu-style dashi and signature dishes feature herring, duck (jibuni), egg (oyakodon) and fish-cake toppings. No vegan options or plant-based dashi are documented.
caveats: The shop's address is in Shimo-Ōmichō directly beside Ōmichō Market, a short walk from Hokutetsu Kanazawa Station, rather than strictly in Yokoyasuemachi (a separately listed branch, 加登長 横安江町店, also exists). Seat count and detailed hours come from listing pages; the simpler 11:00–21:00 / closed Wednesday appears on the official tourism listing. Cash-only per listing.

bio.background: Kadochō is widely regarded as Kanazawa's oldest popular noodle shop, a casual udon-and-soba house founded in the Meiji era (sources cite 1897, with the lineage traced to 1901) and run continuously thanks to the support of local residents. Its roots reach back to a Kyoto udon craftsman the founder brought to the region; that Kyoto base was then adapted with local light-colored soy sauce and tuned to Kanazawa tastes, helping shape what is now known as Kanazawa-style udon.
bio.origin: Kyoto udon tradition, adapted to Kanazawa
bio.specialty: Soft, delicate udon in a sweet, umami-rich dashi broth, eaten by coating the fine noodles thoroughly in the soup. Signature dishes include nishin (herring) soba, Kaga-style ankake udon, tanuki udon with ribbon-fried tofu and ginger, Kaga-vegetable tempura udon, and duck jibu-ni; donburi such as oyakodon are also offered.
bio.philosophy: Each day the shop makes its noodles and broth from scratch using only wheat flour, salt and water, then adjusts the seasoning to suit each specific dish.

anecdotes.text: The novelist Iwaki Gōki praised Kanazawa udon in his writing as 'udon truly befitting a town tied to the Maeda clan,' helping cement the dish's cultural standing.
The shop calls itself 'Kanazawa's oldest casual udon house' and underwent a renovation in July 2020, featuring Wajima lacquerware and traditional ceramics.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen centers on wheat udon, soba (typically wheat-blended) and tempura, so flour and wheat-based broth are pervasive; no celiac accommodation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are served in a regular shoyu/dashi broth made with local light soy sauce, which contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The dashi is fish/kombu-based and signature items include herring, duck, egg and fish cake, so animal products are integral to the menu.

lead: 大口水産 ヤキヤキコーナー
name_ja: 大口水産 ヤキヤキコーナー
cuisine: Grilled seafood standing counter (scallop, squid, nodoguro skewers, sashimi, sushi)
neighborhood: 近江町市場 (Omichō Market), Musashigatsuji
address_ja: 石川県金沢市上近江町40
geocode_note: Geocoded from 石川県金沢市上近江町40 via GSI; coordinates fall within central Kanazawa / Omichō Market area.
seats_text: Standing/eat-in counter inside the market; seat count not published. Single diners and stand-and-eat customers welcome.
hours_raw: 9:00–15:00 (per Omichō Market official page); some listings show 9:00–17:00. Closed Wednesdays.
closed_days: Wednesdays (plus occasional irregular market closures)
cultural_comfort_note: A bustling open-air market counter where you point at the skewer you want and eat standing up; a few words of Japanese and pointing go a long way, and English is unlikely.
gf_detail: This is a grilled-seafood and sushi counter where items like salt-grilled nodoguro, scallops and squid are likely fine on their own, but no gluten-free handling is documented, soy-based sauces and shared grills are in use, and staff are not set up for celiac-level allergy management. Not safe for strict celiacs.
vegan_detail: A seafood-focused grilled counter run by a fishmonger; the menu is built entirely around fish, squid, scallops, shrimp and eel. There are no vegan options.
caveats: Hours vary by source (9:00–15:00 on the market's own page vs 9:00–17:00 on Tabelog); the eat-in grilled corner typically winds down before the shop's full closing. Seat count is not published. Address appears as both 上近江町40 and 38 across listings.

bio.background: The Yakiyaki Corner is the grilled-seafood standing counter of Ohguchi Suisan, one of the largest and longest-running fishmongers in Kanazawa's 300-year-old Omichō Market, which holds the biggest sales floor in the market. Alongside its fresh-fish cases, the shop runs an open grill where market-fresh seafood is skewered and cooked right in front of customers to eat on the spot.
bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled skewers from about 200 yen each — striped (mongo) squid legs, plump scallops, salt-grilled nodoguro (blackthroat seaperch), round whelk shellfish, eel liver, Noto-region fugu and shrimp — plus fresh sashimi and sushi packs, often washed down with a 350-yen draft beer.
bio.philosophy: The shop sums up its approach as 安くてうまい — cheap and delicious — leaning on ingredients pulled straight from the market's own fish counters.
roles: grilled-seafood counter operated by Ohguchi Suisan

anecdotes.text: Skewers such as scallop, squid and eel liver are grilled to order right in front of you, making it a favorite stop for eating while strolling the market.
A 350-yen draft beer alongside 200-yen skewers makes it an easy, affordable pause in the middle of the market.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Skewers are cooked on a shared open grill alongside marinated and sauced items, so cross-contact with wheat-containing sauces is likely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sashimi and sushi are served, which are typically eaten with standard (wheat-containing) soy sauce; no gluten-free soy sauce is documented.

positives.text: Salt-grilled items such as nodoguro shioyaki and plain salted scallops are grilled simply with salt rather than sauce.

cuisine: 割烹・日本料理（季節の加賀会席）
neighborhood: 香林坊・せせらぎ通り
gf_detail: This is a traditional Kaga kaiseki/kappo kitchen built around dashi, soy sauce, and seasonal seafood — all of which involve wheat-based seasonings (regular soy sauce contains wheat). There is no gluten-free menu and no documented allergen handling. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: A seafood- and Kaga-vegetable-focused seasonal kaiseki restaurant serving fixed omakase courses. Fish, dashi (typically bonito/kombu), and animal products are central to every course; there is no vegan menu and dishes cannot meaningfully be made vegan. Not a vegan destination.
geocode_note: GSI chō-level geocode for 香林坊 returned [136.654129, 36.563454], within the Kanazawa box.
seats_text: About 14 seats — roughly 6 at the counter plus four tables for two, run by the chef himself.
hours_raw: Mon-Sat 18:00-22:00 (dinner only); closed Sundays.
closed_days: Sundays
cultural_comfort_note: A small, quiet owner-run counter where the chef serves set omakase courses — comfortable for a student with some Japanese who can phone ahead to reserve, but there's likely little English and no menu choices to point at.
caveats: Dinner-only, reservation-strongly-advised counter place; courses run roughly 6,000-10,000 yen pre-tax, so plan for a higher spend. Not suitable for gluten-free or vegan diners.

bio.background: The chef behind this small Seseragi-dori counter spent nine years training at the celebrated Asadaya ryotei in Kanazawa, then served nine more years as head chef at Lamp no Yado in Suzu, the famed seaside ryokan on the Noto Peninsula, before opening his own place in May 2014.
bio.specialty: Seasonal Kaga kaiseki built on local seafood and Kaga vegetables, served as fixed omakase courses — refined cooking that plays with temperature, texture, and seasonal pairings.
bio.philosophy: He cooks for contrast and season — temperature, flavor and texture, seasonality, and the cleverness of combinations — and deliberately keeps prices approachable so that this level of craft stays within reach.

anecdotes.text: He runs the whole kitchen single-handedly, turning out a long, intricately composed sequence of dishes alone — a feat regulars marvel at.
Tucked into a single-story wooden house on Seseragi-dori, where a clear canal runs past year-round, the room is just a six-seat counter facing the open kitchen and a handful of small tables.
Despite elaborate plating and unexpected touches, the courses remain modestly priced — a point reviewers single out as unusually generous.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Traditional kaiseki built on dashi and soy-based seasonings; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, and there is no documented gluten-free handling.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Kaga kaiseki cooking relies on soy sauce and dashi throughout the courses; assume wheat-containing soy sauce is used.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Courses are seafood- and dashi-centric with animal products in nearly every dish; no plant-only option is documented.

cuisine: 昭和レトロな喫茶店（カレーオムライス、焼きめし、パフェ、サイフォン珈琲）
neighborhood: 柿木畠（片町・香林坊／金沢21世紀美術館近く）
gf_detail: A Showa-era kissaten whose signature dishes are curry omurice, fried rice, cheese toast and parfaits — wheat-based bread, roux, and flour are central to the menu. No gluten-free accommodation is documented and the kitchen is a single owner-run operation. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: The menu centers on egg-topped curry, omelette-based dishes, butter chicken curry, cheese toast, and dairy-rich parfaits and coffee drinks. No vegan options are documented. Animal products (egg, dairy, meat) are core to nearly every dish.
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市片町1-1-11 returned [136.654678, 36.561325], within the Kanazawa box. Matches the described 柿木畠 location near the Kurazuki canal and 21st Century Museum.
seats_text: Small layout — counter on the left, table seating on the right. Sources vary (Tabelog ~24 seats; an interview describes 6 counter seats plus two large tables), but it is consistently a compact, intimate room.
hours_raw: Tabelog lists Mon–Sat 10:00–21:00, closed Sunday. An owner interview states 11:00–19:00, closed Sundays and Mondays. Hours have varied between sources — confirm by phone (076-221-0946).
closed_days: Sundays (Tabelog); some sources note Sundays and Mondays. Confirm by phone.
cultural_comfort_note: This is an old-school, owner-run Showa kissaten with no English menu, Wi-Fi, or BGM, so a little Japanese and an easygoing attitude will help you settle into its quiet, time-capsule atmosphere.
caveats: Hours and closed days differ between sources (Tabelog vs. an owner interview) — best to call ahead at 076-221-0946. No Wi-Fi, no power outlets, no background music; it is a non-smoking, cash-friendly neighborhood spot.

bio.background: Tucked down a lane in the 柿木畠 (Kakinokibatake) quarter just off Katamachi — reached by crossing a small bridge over the Kurazuki canal, near the 21st Century Museum — Hickory opened in 1988 as 珈琲 ヒッコリー. The owner first took over his father's dry-cleaning shop on this spot before converting it into a coffee house a few years later. In its early years it filled with students, when Kanazawa University still occupied the nearby castle grounds (until 1995).
bio.philosophy: The owner, a former smoker, deliberately made the cafe non-smoking so families would feel welcome. There is no Wi-Fi, no power outlets and no BGM — it is a quiet, unhurried room built for coffee and conversation.
bio.specialty: A Showa-retro kissaten known for siphon-brewed coffee, curry topped with a soft omelette (curry omurice), parfaits, cheese toast and a hazelnut coffee. Its most personal dish is 太洋軒の焼きめし, a nostalgic fried rice the owner revived from the long-closed local restaurant Taiyoken — 'only I can make it,' he says.

anecdotes.text: Opened in 1988 as 珈琲 ヒッコリー; the owner inherited his father's dry-cleaning business on the site before turning it into a cafe, and it thrived with students while Kanazawa University was still on the nearby castle grounds.
The owner revived 太洋軒の焼きめし, a fried-rice recipe from a defunct local restaurant, saying 私しか作られんからね ('only I can make it').
Reached by crossing a small bridge over the Kurazuki canal in the 柿木畠 district; the siphon-brewed coffee is praised as remarkably mellow.

cuisine: 寿司 / Edomae-style sushi and seafood, Ishikawa local catch
neighborhood: Kazuemachi / Owaricho (along the Asano River, near Omicho Market)
gf_detail: This is a traditional sushi house. Sushi is served with wheat-based soy sauce, and nimono/marinades and dashi typically contain soy sauce and other wheat-containing seasonings. No gluten-free menu or celiac protocol is documented. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: A seafood-focused omakase sushi restaurant built around fish, shellfish and Ishikawa coastal catch (e.g. nodoguro). There is no vegan menu and the cuisine is fundamentally animal-based. Not suitable for vegans.
geocode_note: Geocoded from 石川県金沢市主計町2-13 via GSI address search; first feature coordinates [136.664169, 36.571865], within the central Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 7-seat counter on the first floor, plus private rooms (a 4-seat table room and tatami rooms); total capacity is roughly 18-23 across all rooms, but the heart of the place is the small ground-floor counter.
hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-14:00, Dinner 17:30-22:00. Closed Wednesday and Thursday.
closed_days: Wednesday and Thursday
cultural_comfort_note: This is an intimate, reservation-only counter sushi house where the meal is an omakase course at a high price point, so a foreign student should book ahead and be ready for a quiet, Japanese-speaking setting where following the chef's lead is the norm.
caveats: Seat-count and total-capacity figures vary slightly between listings (the counter is consistently described as 7 seats, with private rooms bringing the total to roughly 18-23). The "1F counter only" framing in the brief is the spirit of the place; private tatami/table rooms also exist.

bio.chef_name: Trained successor of Morita Kazuo (the legendary sushi chef known as "Komatsu Yasuke")
bio.background: Sushidokoro Asanogawa opened in the summer of 2016 in Kanazawa's Kazuemachi geisha district, on a quiet lane beside the Asano River near Omicho Market. Its name was bestowed by Morita Kazuo, the revered sushi master who ran the celebrated 'Komatsu Yasuke,' and the shop carries on his techniques. The current head chef, born in 1974, grew up in a restaurant family, studied Japanese cuisine in Kyoto for four years after high school, then returned to Kanazawa to deepen his knowledge of local seafood before inheriting Morita's methods when Komatsu Yasuke closed.
bio.specialty: Counter omakase sushi built on carefully selected fish, with Ishikawa coastal catch such as nodoguro (rosy seabass); a marinated red-tuna (maguro no akami-zuke) preparation is a signature touched by the Komatsu Yasuke lineage.
bio.philosophy: 妥協しない技で寿司に向き合い、心を込めておもてなし致します — to face the sushi with uncompromising technique and serve with wholehearted hospitality.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name was given by Morita Kazuo, the master behind the legendary 'Komatsu Yasuke,' who continued to guide and cooperate with the chef here.
Morita Kazuo, the mentor whose techniques live on at this counter, was recognized in 2019 by Japan's Ministry of Health, Labour and Welfare as a 'Contemporary Master Craftsman' (現代の名工).

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a traditional sushi house, dishes are served with standard (wheat-based) soy sauce and seasonings; no gluten-free soy sauce or celiac accommodation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is entirely seafood-based (raw fish, shellfish, fish dashi), so vegan diners cannot avoid cross-contact; no plant-based menu exists.

lead: 小料理とおでん 燗九郎
name_ja: 小料理とおでん 燗九郎
cuisine: おでん・小料理居酒屋（フレンチ出身店主の和洋折衷おでん）
neighborhood: 木倉町・せせらぎ通り（片町・香林坊）
geocode_note: Geocoded from address 石川県金沢市片町2-5-8 via GSI; coordinates fall within the central Kanazawa box (Katamachi).
seats_text: Small intimate space with counter seating and sunken-kotatsu (horigotatsu) seating areas; exact seat count not documented, but described as small so booking ahead is advised.
hours_raw: 18:00-02:00 (翌2時まで)、ラストオーダー23:30。日曜定休。
closed_days: Sundays
cultural_comfort_note: A small late-night local oden and small-plates spot where a little Japanese helps; warm and welcoming but not set up for English, so come ready to point at the menu and try a few words.
caveats: Dashi-, oden-, and meat/seafood-forward menu (duck broth, dried-fish stocks, smoked salmon, steak, roast beef); there is a per-person cover charge (otoshi) typical of izakaya. Opens evenings only and runs late into the night.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. Oden and the broths rely on Japanese dashi and almost certainly wheat-based soy sauce, and the kitchen is a small shared izakaya space. No gluten-free accommodation or dedicated equipment is documented; not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The food is built on animal stocks (duck-bone broth, bonito/dried-fish and shiitake dashi) and the signatures are fish, scallop, egg, smoked salmon, steak and roast beef. No vegan options are documented.

bio.background: Run by a chef who trained in French cuisine and channels that experience into a new-style 'bistro oden' specialty shop. The place opened in July 2020 just behind the Katamachi Kira building, in the space that previously housed a yakitori shop, and quickly became known around Kanazawa for its French-meets-Japanese take on a humble winter classic.
bio.specialty: Two oden lineups side by side: a Western-leaning set built on a slowly simmered duck-bone broth (tomato, a creamy Western-style egg whose broth regulars like to drink, a rich scallop surimi), and a more traditional Japanese oden in a bonito-kelp-shiitake dashi (including a half-rare sardine dumpling). Rounded out with a French bar menu of smoked salmon, steak and roast beef, paired with both sake and wine.
bio.philosophy: Bringing French technique and ingredients to the everyday comfort of oden, so a simmered-pot standard becomes something new while still feeling at home in Kanazawa.

anecdotes.text: Opened July 21, 2020 in Katamachi as a 'new-sensation bistro oden' shop, taking over a former yakitori location behind Katamachi Kira.

Local coverage credits a French-trained chef for 'revolutionary' French oden such as a duck-dashi base and a sweet-sour tomato oden, alongside Kanazawa-style standards, with both sake and wine pairings.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Oden and small-plate izakaya cooking in Japan standardly uses wheat-containing soy sauce and dashi; no gluten-free preparation is documented for this kitchen.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are animal-based (duck-bone stock; bonito and dried-fish/shiitake dashi) and signature items center on fish, scallop, egg and meat, so vegan dishes and avoidance of animal cross-contact are not provided.

lead: 手打そば 更科藤井
name_ja: 手打そば 更科藤井
cuisine: 手打ち蕎麦（二八・更科・十割など蕎麦各種と一品料理、日本酒）
gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. The house standard is nihachi soba (80% buckwheat, 20% wheat flour), and the signature pale "sarashina" soba uses refined buckwheat but is still made in a wheat-handling kitchen. A 100% buckwheat (juwari) soba appears on the menu, but it is prepared in the same kitchen alongside wheat-bound noodles, so cross-contamination is essentially certain. Dipping sauces (tsuyu) and many side dishes also use wheat-based soy sauce. Celiac travelers should avoid.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan destination. The dipping broth is bonito-dashi based, and the menu leans on duck, scallop, sea urchin and oysters. Plain noodles and vegetable tempura exist but most accompaniments are not vegan; ask before ordering.
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市柿木畠3-3 returned [136.656082, 36.56028], within the Kanazawa box (Kakigibahake, behind the 21st Century Museum of Art near Korinbo / City Hall).
neighborhood: 柿木畠（香林坊・金沢21世紀美術館裏手／市役所近く）
seats_text: Small townhouse-style shop with counter seating and tables; no private rooms. Exact seat count not documented, but a compact, intimate space.
hours_raw: [火～土] 11:30-14:00 / 17:30-20:30　[日・祝] 11:30-19:30　※そばが売り切れ次第終了　定休日：月曜（祝日の場合は翌日休）＋不定休
closed_days: Mondays (open if a national holiday, then closed the following day), plus irregular closures; also closes early whenever the day's soba sells out.
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional owner-run soba shop where a little Japanese and quiet, respectful dining go a long way; English service is not assured, so point at the menu and keep it simple.
caveats: Cash only. Lunch is walk-in only (no reservations); dinner reservations are accepted. Closes early when soba sells out, and has irregular closures on top of the Monday holiday, so go earlier in the service. Not suitable for celiac diners (wheat in noodles, tsuyu and sides).

bio.background: Owner-chef Fujii Makoto trained for more than a decade in Nagano and Tokyo, including time in the Edo soba tradition, before opening his own shop in 1999 - an era when dedicated soba specialists were still rare in Kanazawa. The restaurant moved from its original Katamachi location to the quiet Kakigibahake lane behind the 21st Century Museum of Art in March 2011, settling into a calm, machiya-like townhouse space.
bio.specialty: Hand-cut soba in several styles - the everyday nihachi (80% buckwheat) zaru, a coarse-ground (arabiki) version, a 100% buckwheat juwari, and the elegant pale 'sarashina' (gozen) soba made from highly refined buckwheat, prized for its clean throat-feel and delicate aroma. Alongside the noodles, Fujii serves refined small plates such as roast duck, scallop-filled tamagoyaki, vegetable tempura, oysters and salted sea urchin, all meant to pair with sake or buckwheat shochu.
bio.philosophy: Fujii treats raw material as everything - 'ingredients are the lifeblood of soba' - and travels each year to meet his buckwheat producers in person, sourcing directly rather than settling for what is convenient.

anecdotes.text: Fujii's soba became such a Kanazawa benchmark that, back in the late-night Katamachi days, even rival soba shop owners would come in to eat his noodles, and locals credit him with helping raise the whole city's soba culture.
He spent over ten years apprenticing in Nagano and Tokyo before going independent in 1999, when soba specialists were still uncommon in Kanazawa.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen hand-cuts wheat-bound nihachi soba (80% buckwheat / 20% wheat) as its standard, so any 100% buckwheat (juwari) option is made in the same wheat-handling environment - cross-contamination is essentially unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soba tsuyu and the small side dishes rely on standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat; no wheat-free seasoning is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The dipping broth is built on bonito (katsuo) dashi and the menu centers on duck and seafood, so plant-only diners cannot assume the noodles, broth, or shared kitchen are animal-free.

lead: 居酒屋割烹 源左ェ門 木倉町本店
name_ja: 居酒屋割烹 源左ェ門 木倉町本店
cuisine: 加賀郷土料理・地魚割烹（金沢港直送の鮮魚と加賀野菜）
neighborhood: 木倉町（片町・香林坊）
gf_detail: This is a Kaga regional cuisine and local-fish kappo where dashi, soy sauce, miso (hoba miso), tempura, and sake-based seasonings are central to nearly every dish. There is no documented gluten-free menu or allergen accommodation, and the kitchen relies heavily on wheat-containing soy sauce and miso. Not safe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: The restaurant is built around fresh fish from Kanazawa Port (sashimi, nodoguro, etc.), Noto beef sukiyaki, and dashi-based regional dishes. While Kaga vegetable dishes exist (e.g., Kaga thick cucumber, lotus-root dumplings), they are prepared with fish/bonito dashi and are not vegan. No vegan menu is documented.
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 石川県金沢市木倉町５番３号; within the Kanazawa box.
seats_text: Around 52 seats including counter seating and a private second-floor room that seats roughly 30-38 for banquets (sources vary between 52 and 56 total seats).
hours_raw: Mon/Tue/Thu/Sun & holidays 17:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:30); Fri/Sat & day before holidays 17:00-23:30 (L.O. 23:00); closed Wednesdays. Irregular closures during Golden Week, Obon, and year-end/New Year.
closed_days: Wednesdays (plus irregular closures during Golden Week, Obon, and year-end/New Year)
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional, locally loved evening izakaya-kappo where some Japanese language ability and comfort with regional Japanese food (raw fish, dashi, sake) will help a foreign student feel at ease.
caveats: Dinner-only spot; closed Wednesdays. Heavily seafood- and dashi-based with extensive sake list, so not suitable for vegan, vegetarian, or gluten-free diners. Evening budget runs roughly 5,000-8,000 yen per person.

bio.background: Genzaemon sits in Kiguramachi, a Kanazawa dining quarter with over 300 years of history. The restaurant takes its name from Genzaemon Sano, said to be a Kamakura-era samurai whose descendants carried on a tradition of hospitality. Owner Yohei Sano runs the house in a traditional farmhouse-style (kominka) setting, with counter seats that look out onto Kaga Yuzen textiles.
bio.specialty: Sashimi and dishes built around fresh fish delivered from Kanazawa Port (nodoguro/blackthroat seaperch is a highlight), Kaga regional cooking using Kaga vegetables, Noto beef sukiyaki, and a signature lotus-root dumpling in dashi broth that combines grated lotus root with mountain yam for a fluffy, chewy texture. A hidden specialty is hoba miso yaki (miso grilled on a magnolia leaf) using beef or seasonal items like scallop and firefly squid.
bio.philosophy: Owner Sano describes his consistent approach as responding to things from the customer's point of view, pairing local fish and Kaga vegetables with a deep selection of Hokuriku and nationwide sake.

anecdotes.text: The shop is named after Genzaemon Sano, said to be a Kamakura-era samurai whose descendants served generals, establishing a tradition of hospitality the restaurant continues today.
A signature lotus-root dumpling combines grated lotus root with mountain yam for a fluffy, chewy texture, served in dashi broth.
Counter seats look out onto displays of Kaga Yuzen textiles, and a hidden specialty is hoba miso yaki featuring beef or seasonal seafood like scallop and firefly squid.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Cooking centers on regional Kaga dishes, sashimi with soy sauce, miso-grilled hoba miso, and dashi-based preparations, all typically using wheat-containing soy sauce and miso.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is fish- and meat-forward (Kanazawa Port sashimi, nodoguro, Noto beef sukiyaki) with dashi used throughout, so vegetable dishes are not free of animal products.

neighborhood: 主計町・彦三町（浅野川沿い／近江町市場近く）
cuisine: 加賀懐石・日本料理（月替わりの季節の懐石）
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市彦三町1-9-69 returned [136.662064, 36.573338], within the Kanazawa box. Restaurant sits along the Asano River near the Kazue-machi/Shukeimachi teahouse district and Omicho Market.
seats_text: All private rooms; limited to 4 groups per service. Second floor has a 6-person room and a 4-person room (window seats overlooking the Asano River); first floor has table seating (with a view of a small garden). Approximately 18 seats total across the private rooms.
closed_days: 水曜日（Wednesday）
cultural_comfort_note: This is an intimate, reservation-only kaiseki ryotei where bookings often fill six months out and service is paced personally for each group, so some Japanese ability (or a Japanese-speaking friend to phone-reserve) will make the visit far smoother for a foreign student.
gf_detail: Traditional Kaga kaiseki built on dashi and Japanese soy sauce, with no documented gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or allergen handling. Wheat-containing soy sauce is integral to this style of cuisine, so it should not be considered celiac-safe.
vegan_detail: Course menus are seafood- and fish-dashi-centered (sashimi, scallop/squid salads, white fish and crab in winter, beef hot pot at the Sunday breakfast). No vegan or vegetarian course is documented. As a reservation-only, fixed-course kaiseki, plant-based diners would need to inquire well in advance and should not assume accommodation.
caveats: Tabelog lists the address as 彦三町1-9-69 while some guides describe the location as the 主計町 (Kazue-machi) teahouse district; both refer to the same riverside area along the Asano River. Completely reservation-only, limited to 4 groups per service, with bookings frequently filling up to six months ahead. Sunday morning "Kanazawa asage" breakfast (3 groups) opens for booking on the 1st of each month at 10:00. Dietary/allergen details are not documented; confirm directly when reserving.

bio.background: Chef-owner Nakagawa Seiichi trained at hotels and specialty restaurants in Kanazawa before opening Kifune in 2008 in the Kazue-machi/Shukeimachi teahouse district along the Asano River.
bio.origin: Opened 2008 in the riverside teahouse quarter near Omicho Market, in a quiet building whose second-floor window seats look out over the Asano River and whose first-floor tables face a small inner garden (tsuboniwa).
bio.specialty: Monthly-changing seasonal kaiseki that turns over on the 15th of each month, with winter highlights of crab and white fish, beautifully composed multi-course meals, and a celebrated Sunday-only 'Kanazawa asage' breakfast where rice is cooked tableside in individual clay pots using premium Ishikawa rice.
bio.philosophy: Nakagawa caps service at four groups per seating so that every dish can be prepared and timed perfectly for each table — an expression of his ideal of offering 'the finest hospitality possible today' while keeping the essence of traditional Japanese cuisine approachable across ages and cultures.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant deliberately limits itself to four groups per service because that is the most Nakagawa can fully prepare for and serve with perfectly timed courses.

Its Sunday-morning 'Kanazawa asage' breakfast for only a few groups is so beloved that over 90% of guests return, and reservations open on the 1st of each month at 10:00 yet often fill the slots up to six months ahead.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Traditional Kaga kaiseki and Japanese cuisine of this style is built on dashi and wheat-based Japanese soy sauce; no gluten-free soy sauce or alternative is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Courses are seafood- and meat-inclusive (sashimi, scallop and squid, white fish, crab, beef hot pot), prepared with fish dashi; no vegan preparation is documented.

cuisine: 町家の豆甘味カフェ（黒豆かん・豆しるこ・豆スープなど豆スイーツ専門）
neighborhood: 東山・ひがし茶屋街
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [136.667816, 36.574642] for 石川県金沢市東山2-3-21; within Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 座敷テーブル席のみ（2名×2卓、6名×1卓）。非常に小さな町家で、混雑時は入店できないこともある。
hours_raw: 11:00-18:00（水曜・木曜定休、日曜は不定期営業）
closed_days: 水曜・木曜（日曜は不定期）
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny owner-run machiya café where Japanese will go furthest; the menu is short and bean-focused, so a foreign student should be comfortable but shouldn't expect English service or a printed English menu.
gf_detail: The signature sweets are agar-and-bean based (黒豆かん, 豆しるこ) and many items are naturally wheat-free, but there is no documented gluten-free program, no dedicated equipment, and items like 餅 (mochi) and seasonal dishes vary. Not a celiac-safe destination; a celiac diner would need to ask about each item directly in Japanese.
vegan_detail: The menu is plant-forward and bean-centered: 黒豆かん (black beans, agar, and a syrup reduced from the beans' own cooking liquid), 四色福豆しるこ (heirloom beans), seasonal 豆スープ with simply-dressed vegetables and pickles, ume juice and house-made ginger drinks. Many items appear vegan, but it is not a certified vegan kitchen — mochi, broths, or syrups could vary seasonally, so confirm specific items. No documented use of dashi/animal products in the headline sweets.
caveats: Sunday hours are irregular and the café is very small (~10 seats), so calling ahead is advised. Cash only; no reservations. Two sources differ slightly on the owner's gender/given name; the more detailed account names Kitade Miyuki.

bio.background: Mamezuki was opened in 2016 by Kitade Miyuki and her husband Kitade Takesuke, a first-class architect, who relocated to Kanazawa from Oiso in Kanagawa. Searching for a traditional Japanese way of living, they were drawn to Kanazawa's active machiya-preservation scene and fell for an 80-plus-year-old townhouse in the Higashiyama district, which Takesuke renovated by hand while preserving its courtyard garden, tea room, and alcove.
bio.specialty: Beans are the whole story. The signature 黒豆かん (¥700) is built on black beans cooked deliberately firm for an edamame-like crunch, set with agar and finished with a syrup reduced from the beans' own cooking liquid rather than sugar. Other staples include the seasonal 四色福豆しるこ made with four heirloom bean varieties and locally pounded mochi, a weekly-rotating 豆スープ served with simply dressed vegetables and pickles, and drinks like ume juice and house-made ginger.
bio.philosophy: The couple cook at 'the pace of the beans' — black beans are simmered slowly over roughly three days, never with a pressure cooker — and live by a motto of doing things 'within their means' (身の丈に合っている), honoring local culture and preparing each dish with care. The shop typically keeps around 15 varieties of beans on hand and serves them in ceramics by regional and national artisans.

anecdotes.text: The recipe for the firm, crisp-textured black beans comes from Kitade's childhood: her grandmother in Hokkaido would cook black beans for her, and she set out to recreate that 'ideal bean' of memory by learning to simmer them herself.
Rather than sweetening with black sugar, the syrup over the 黒豆かん is made by reducing the liquid the beans were cooked in, so the flavors stay in harmony with the beans themselves.

positives.text: Headline sweets are naturally plant-based and bean/agar-based: black beans set in agar with a syrup made from the beans' own cooking liquid, and bean shiruko made from heirloom beans.
Seasonal bean soup is served with simply prepared vegetables and pickles, indicating a strongly plant-forward kitchen.

cuisine: 甘味処・和菓子 (traditional Japanese sweets cafe) — kuzukiri, matcha cream zenzai, wagashi with matcha
neighborhood: 広坂・柿木畠 (Hirosaka, near Kenrokuen Garden, the 21st Century Museum, and Korinbo)
gf_detail: This is a traditional Japanese sweets shop. The menu centers on wagashi, mochi, anko (red bean), kuzukiri and toast — items that typically involve wheat-based confections and shared kitchen prep. No gluten-free accommodation or dedicated handling is documented, and a celiac traveler should not assume safety here.
vegan_detail: Some sweets are plant-based by nature (kuzukiri, anko, matcha syrup), but dairy is used in items like matcha cream zenzai, and no vegan labeling or accommodation is documented. A vegan would need to ask which items contain milk, cream or egg. Nothing specific is published.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市広坂1-1-60 能作ビル4F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja via GSI; first feature within the Kanazawa box.
seats_text: Around 20 seats — an 8-seat counter plus a handful of small tables (three 3-tops, one 4-top, one 2-top).
hours_raw: 11:00-18:00 (L.O. 17:30). Closed Tuesdays and Wednesdays (irregular closures in August; open on public holidays).
closed_days: Tuesday and Wednesday (irregular in August)
cultural_comfort_note: A calm sit-down sweets cafe inside a lacquerware shop where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the picture menu will get you a lovely matcha dessert with garden-side views; little English is documented.

bio.background: Urushi no Mi is the tea room on the 4th floor of Nosaku, a Kanazawa lacquerware house founded in 1780 that has carried on Kaga maki-e and Wajima-nuri traditions for more than two centuries. The cafe opened over twenty years ago, conceived as a quiet, elegant salon — the kind you might find tucked inside a luxury boutique — where guests rest with a sweet after browsing the lacquerware below.
bio.specialty: Matcha cream zenzai (matcha cream over mochi dumplings and sweet red beans) and made-to-order matcha kuzukiri are the signature offerings, alongside high-grade Yoshihashi wagashi served with matcha. Dumplings and kuzukiri are prepared fresh to order.
bio.philosophy: The room is built around savoring the seasons of Kanazawa — cherry blossoms, fresh greenery, autumn color and snow seen from the windows over Hirosaka — paired with refined, traditional Japanese sweets in a hushed, unhurried setting.

anecdotes.text: The cafe sits four floors above the Nosaku lacquerware shop, so a visit doubles as a window onto 1780-founded Kaga maki-e and Wajima lacquer craft before you sit down to dessert.

From the 4th-floor windows guests look out over Hirosaka toward Kenrokuen and the surrounding cultural landmarks, with the view changing through cherry blossoms, greenery, autumn leaves and snow across the year.

caveats: Closed-day sources are largely consistent (Tuesday and Wednesday per Tabelog and the official site); one secondary listing mentioned only Wednesday, so confirm before a Tuesday visit. No allergy, gluten-free or vegan handling information is published, so dietary travelers should ask in person.

cuisine: 手打ち蕎麦（二八そば）/ Hand-made soba
neighborhood: 柿木畠（香林坊・金沢21世紀美術館近く）
geocode_note: Geocoded from address 石川県金沢市柿木畠3-3 via GSI; coordinates within central Kanazawa box.
seats_text: Small soba shop with counter and table seating; exact capacity not published.
closed_days: Mondays, plus occasional irregular closures; may close early when soba sells out.
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional owner-run soba counter where ordering is simple if you know a few menu words; little English is expected, so pointing at the menu works fine.
gf_detail: This is a nihachi (二八) soba shop, meaning the noodles are 80% buckwheat and 20% wheat flour, so they contain gluten. Dipping sauces (tsuyu) are typically made with wheat-based soy sauce. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
vegan_detail: A classic soba house; the soba dashi/tsuyu is almost certainly based on bonito (katsuo) and other fish stock, and side dishes include items like salted sea urchin. A plain zaru soba might be ordered, but the dipping broth is not vegan unless confirmed otherwise. Vegans should ask directly.

bio.background: Owner-chef Makoto Fujii trained for over a decade in Nagano and Tokyo before opening his own shop in 1999, at a time when specialty soba restaurants were still a rarity in Kanazawa. The shop originally stood in the Katamachi district and moved to its current Kakinokibatake location in spring 2011, a quiet street just steps from the 21st Century Museum of Art.
bio.specialty: Hand-cut nihachi (80/20) soba, served simply as zaru/seiro to show off the texture and aroma of the buckwheat, alongside carefully chosen small plates and sake.
bio.philosophy: Fujii is uncompromising about ingredients, saying 'the raw materials are the very life of soba' (原料はそばの命ですから). He travels to producing regions each year to buy directly from growers, and is described locally as a 食の求道者 — a seeker on the path of food.

anecdotes.text: Fujii is remembered as a craftsman who allows no compromise, hand-making the soba and pairing it with refined one-plate dishes.
The shop is regarded as having helped define the standard of soba in Kanazawa, with its nihachi soba called a defining dish for the city.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Soba is nihachi (80% buckwheat, 20% wheat) so the noodles themselves contain wheat; shared cooking water and a wheat-heavy environment make this unsafe for celiac diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soba tsuyu (dipping broth) is traditionally made with wheat-based soy sauce; no wheat-free version is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Soba dashi/tsuyu is typically fish-stock based and the menu includes seafood side dishes such as salted sea urchin, so vegan separation is not documented.

caveats: Seat count is not published; capacity inferred as small. Hours and closed days confirmed via Tabelog and the Kakinokibatake merchant association; treat hours as irregular since the shop may close early when soba sells out. Vegan/GF safety details are inferred from the nihachi soba style and were not explicitly documented by the shop.

cuisine: Kissa / yoshoku cafe — Kanazawa specialties (Hunton rice, tea-house katsu sandwich, Kaga/Kanazawa curry) plus coffee and house-made sweets
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The signature dishes are wheat-based — the katsu sandwich uses bread and a breaded cutlet, Hunton rice is topped with a wheat-flour omelette and ketchup/demi sauce, and the menu leans heavily on curry, chiffon/gateau cakes and warabi mochi. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented, and Japanese curry roux and katsu breading both contain wheat. A celiac diner should not consider this a safe option.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-oriented. The menu centers on a pork-cutlet sandwich, omelette-topped Hunton rice and meat curry, and the sweets (gateau chocolat, pudding, chiffon cake, soft serve) are dairy- and egg-based. No vegan or vegetarian dishes are advertised. A plant-based diner would have very limited options here; ask in advance.
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 石川県金沢市野町2-24-4, within the Kanazawa box. Location matches the Nishi Chayagai / Nomachi area near Nomachi Station.
neighborhood: 野町・にし茶屋街 (Nomachi / Nishi Chayagai)
seats_text: 27 seats total: counter 7, tables 12, private tatami room 8.
hours_raw: Cafe 10:00–17:00; Bar 18:00–23:00 by reservation only. Closed Wednesdays.
closed_days: Wednesdays
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed townhouse cafe in the tea-house district where a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu will get you by; daytime cafe service is casual and tourist-friendly, though the reservation-only evening bar may expect more Japanese.

bio.background: Yusura is a cafe set inside a traditional Kanazawa machiya townhouse in the atmospheric Nishi Chayagai geisha district, a short walk from Nomachi Station. The building previously housed a sushi restaurant that operated for around twenty years; it reopened as a cafe in the wave of interest that followed the Hokuriku Shinkansen reaching Kanazawa in 2015. Behind a preserved historic facade, the interior is bright and modern, with counter and table seating, a small tsubo-niwa courtyard garden, and a private tatami room.
bio.specialty: The signature is the 'tea-house katsu sandwich' (茶屋のカツサンド) — fluffy bread around a crisp pork cutlet with a house sauce and butter. The kitchen also serves local Kanazawa comfort dishes including Hunton rice and Kaga/Kanazawa curry, alongside coffee and house-made sweets such as a not-too-sweet gateau chocolat finished with gold leaf, custard pudding, chiffon cake, and gold-leaf soft serve.

anecdotes.text: The cafe has served as a filming location for a national TV drama and is frequented by geisha working in the surrounding tea-house district.
Its walls display numerous celebrity autographs, and it offers a discount for junior- and senior-high-school students dining alone.
Set in a former sushi restaurant of about twenty years, it reopened as a cafe after the 2015 Shinkansen extension to Kanazawa.

caveats: Bar service in the evening (18:00–23:00) is reservation-only; cafe hours are walk-in. No dietary (GF/vegan) information is published, and the wheat-heavy menu makes it unsuitable for celiac or strict plant-based diners.

cuisine: 薬膳・喫茶（季節の薬膳スープランチ、薬膳茶、ブックカフェ）
gf_detail: No documented gluten-free program. The cafe serves yakuzen (medicinal) soups with stone-cooked Ishikawa rice, homemade tsukudani and pickles, and herbal teas. While rice-based dishes may be naturally wheat-light, no celiac-safe handling, dedicated equipment, or gluten-free menu is documented; spice cakes and other baked goods contain wheat. Celiac travelers must ask directly and treat it as not confirmed safe.
vegan_detail: Plant-forward menu built around seasonal medicinal vegetable soups, soy-milk potages, Ishikawa rice, pickles, and herbal teas — many items are vegetable-based and the philosophy is "anything you eat becomes medicine." However, no item is explicitly labeled vegan, and soups may use dashi or animal-derived stock; the cafe does not document a dedicated vegan/vegetarian protocol. Vegan diners should confirm soup base and ingredients on the day.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市野町1-2-43 安藤芳園堂ビル 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市野町1-2-43 returned [136.649246, 36.556675], within the Kanazawa box. Matches the Nomachi Hirokoji intersection location.
seats_text: 16 seats; small independent cafe with antique furniture and a book-cafe atmosphere, well suited to solo visitors.
hours_raw: 10:00-18:00; closed Tuesday and Wednesday
closed_days: 火曜日・水曜日 (Tuesday, Wednesday)
cultural_comfort_note: A calm, quiet book-cafe where a few words of Japanese and a relaxed, respectful manner go a long way; English support is not documented, so point at the menu and a smile will carry you.
neighborhood: 野町・寺町（野町広小路交差点角、寺町寺院群近く）

roles: owner-operated by Ando Pharmacy (安藤薬局)
bio.background: Kusuriya Cafe opened in April 2017 on the ground floor of the historic Ando Hoendo Building at the Nomachi Hirokoji intersection, near the temple district of Teramachi. It is run by Ando Pharmacy (安藤薬局), an apothecary with roots reaching back to the Edo period — hence the name 'Kusuriya' (the medicine shop). The space took over the furniture and chandeliers of a long-running coffeehouse that previously occupied the spot, giving it a nostalgic, European-tinged elegance.
bio.specialty: The signature is the seasonal yakuzen (medicinal) soup lunch (around 1,200 yen): a generous vegetable-rich soup paired with seasonal medicinal tea, stone-cooked Ishikawa rice, and homemade tsukudani and pickles. The monthly-changing menu has featured turnip-and-taro minestrone for warming the body in cold months and winter-melon soups for cooling in summer. Sweets include a yakuzen spice cake with ginger preserves, goji berries and walnuts, and rose-raisin tea.
bio.philosophy: The cafe embraces the idea that 'anything you eat becomes medicine,' offering approachable yakuzen rather than intimidating formal medicinal cuisine — eating seasonally and according to what the body needs.

anecdotes.text: The interior doubles as a book cafe with roughly 500 curated volumes on architecture, travel, literature and coffee, and a quiet 'no-conversation' rear section for contemplation — visitors are said to linger for hours.
The cafe is housed in the Ando Hoendo Building and operated by the Edo-period-rooted Ando Pharmacy, tying its medicinal-food concept to a real apothecary heritage.

positives.text: Menu is built around vegetable-forward seasonal soups, soy-milk potages, rice, pickles and herbal teas, so plant-based and lighter dishes are part of the regular offering.

caveats: Vegan/vegetarian and gluten-free status are not explicitly documented by the cafe; the menu is plant-forward but soup bases and baked goods should be confirmed on the day. Hours and closed days (Tue/Wed) are from listings; the official site mentioned weekend 11:00-16:00 in a staff-recruitment post, so off-season hours may vary — confirm before a special trip.

cuisine: さつまいもスイーツ専門カフェ（焼きいもブリュレ・スイーツ）
neighborhood: 寺町・野町（広小路バス停近く、寺町寺院群方面）
geocode_note: GSI address search returned [136.650742, 36.55547] for 石川県金沢市寺町5-5-10; within the Kanazawa box (Teramachi temple district near Hirokoji).
seats_text: Counter seats only; sources vary between about 4 and 5 seats. Tabelog lists 5 counter seats.
hours_raw: 10:00-18:00, closed Tuesdays (plus occasional sudden/irregular closures noted humorously on the shop sign as "とつぜん")
closed_days: Tuesdays (plus occasional irregular closures)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny owner-run counter café where the chatty master cooks desserts right in front of you, so a foreign student with little Japanese will be most comfortable bringing a Japanese-speaking friend or guide to enjoy the conversation that is part of the experience.
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. While roasted sweet potatoes themselves are naturally gluten-free, the café also serves items such as imo curry and various sweets in a single tiny kitchen with no stated allergen handling, so it should not be considered celiac-safe.
vegan_detail: The base ingredient is sweet potato, and plain stone-baked/roasted sweet potato and the sweet potato chips are likely plant-based, but the signature まるごと焼きいもブリュレ uses vanilla ice cream and custard (dairy/egg), the smoothie and soft cream use milk, and coffee can be had black. There is no documented vegan menu, so plant-based diners can find a few items but should ask. Not formally vegan.
caveats: Hours are subject to occasional sudden/irregular closures noted by the owner. Seat count is reported as roughly 4-5. Sweet potato variety on offer rotates 3-4 types daily.

bio.background: Imoya Cafe Tano is a tiny counter cafe in Kanazawa's Teramachi temple district, opened by Nishikawa Masahiro, who previously worked as a French chef in hotels and restaurants. After earlier restaurants closed, he was invited to take over this small Teramachi space, which he fitted out himself with his own cooking tools and interior.
bio.specialty: Sweet potato everything: the signature まるごと焼きいもブリュレ (a whole roasted sweet potato with vanilla ice cream and custard, finished with caramelized sugar), plus an imo smoothie topped with gold-leaf cream, imo soft cream, sweet potato chips, chilled stone-baked sweet potato, and freshly ground coffee. He rotates several premium sweet potato varieties such as Beni Haruka, Goro-shima Kintoki (a Kaga heirloom vegetable), Silk Sweet and Annou Imo.
