bio.philosophy: The shop name 楽 is read 'tano' rather than 'raku'. It comes from advice a French service manager once gave him that food is not truly nourishing without laughter and enjoyment; he wanted to run the place 'amusingly and joyfully' for both himself and his guests.

anecdotes.text: The chef draws on his French training to develop the desserts through trial and error, plating each one fresh in front of the guest at the counter.
The shop sign humorously lists irregular closures as 'とつぜん' (suddenly), reflecting the relaxed, personality-driven spirit behind the name 'tano'.

positives.text: Desserts are made to order at the counter in front of you, so you can see the ingredients and speak directly with the owner-chef about what goes into each dish.

cuisine: Community machiya cafe / Japanese curry and lunch
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering is documented. The signature "Tanto Curry" is a Japanese-style curry, which is made with a wheat-flour roux, and the cafe also serves coffee/tea and light fare in a casual community setting. There is no indication of gluten-free preparation or allergen handling, so this is not safe for celiacs. Ask directly if you have a medical need.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. The kitchen focuses on local-ingredient home-style lunches like the Tanto Curry (650 yen); Japanese curry typically contains meat and/or dairy and wheat. The cafe is small and home-style, so a vegetable-based plate may be possible on request, but nothing is confirmed in available sources. Vegetarians/vegans should ask in advance.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市野町1丁目3-22
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned [136.648453, 36.553658] for 金沢市野町1丁目3-22; within the Kanazawa box. Located in Nomachi, at the foot of the Teramachi temple district along the Hirokoji bus street.
seats_text: Small machiya (townhouse) cafe with tatami rooms (shoes off) plus some table seating; exact seat count not published.
hours_raw: 10:00～16:00. Closed Sundays, public holidays, and year-end/New Year. (Phone reachable Mon–Fri 10:00–16:00.)
closed_days: Sundays, public holidays, and year-end/New Year
cultural_comfort_note: This is a warm, very local neighborhood community cafe with little to no English, so a foreign student should come with a Japanese-speaking guide or translation app and a relaxed, friendly attitude.
caveats: Address is in Nomachi (野町) at the foot of the Teramachi temple district, not Teramachi proper. No vegetarian/vegan or gluten-free menu is documented despite the target note; Japanese curry here is wheat-based and likely contains animal products, so dietary travelers must ask. Cash assumed (small welfare-run community cafe); confirm on arrival.

bio.background: Zenrin Cafe is a community cafe set in a restored Kanazawa machiya (traditional townhouse) at the foot of the Teramachi temple district, run by the social-welfare corporation Daiichi Zenrin-kan. Beyond serving visitors, it operates as a workplace that provides continued-employment support for people with disabilities, in partnership with the NPO Fureai Kobo Tanto and the general incorporated association Life PALM.
bio.philosophy: A place where neighbors and travelers mingle casually over a cup of coffee, built on community welfare and local production for local consumption (chisan-chisho) using Kanazawa ingredients.
bio.specialty: The signature lunch is the home-style Tanto Curry (650 yen) made with local Kanazawa ingredients; coffee starts at 300 yen and there is royal milk tea around 350 yen. Guests can relax in tatami rooms (shoes off) during a break between temple visits.

anecdotes.text: The cafe doubles as an off-site employment-support workplace, so visiting for a coffee directly supports work opportunities for people with disabilities.
Travelers can take off their shoes and rest in a quiet tatami room mid-sightseeing, with the budget-friendly Tanto Curry made from local ingredients as the recommended lunch.

name_ja: 和食ダイニング ばん莱
cuisine: Japanese washoku / sushi / set meals (teishoku)
neighborhood: Nomachi / Nishi-chaya district (にし茶屋街)
address_ja: 石川県金沢市野町2丁目25番22号 2F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市野町2丁目25-22 returned [136.647491, 36.556988], within the Kanazawa bounding box.
seats_text: 19 seats total: 7 counter seats, three tables for 4, plus a private room.
closed_days: Not specified; the listing notes hours/holidays may change, so confirm before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: A small local washoku spot where a foreign student should be comfortable pointing at the lunch set menu, though staff likely speak mainly Japanese.
gf_detail: This is a wheat- and dashi-heavy Japanese restaurant — fried horse mackerel (aji fry), sushi, oden, and soy-based seasonings dominate the menu. No gluten-free accommodation is documented, and it is not safe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Menu is centered on seafood (sashimi, sushi, fried aji), Noto pork, eel, roast beef, and dashi-based dishes. No vegan options are documented.
caveats: The blog source titled this place "ばん菜," but the official site and Tabelog list it as 和食ダイニング ばん莱 (Banrai) at the same Nishi-chaya address with the same signature dishes — they are the same establishment. Closed days were not published.

bio.background: Banrai opened on April 18, 2023 in Kanazawa's quieter Nishi-chaya teahouse district, occupying an upper floor of a townhouse building it shares with a sister seafood restaurant, 町家食堂 晴れやか, which opened the same day on the ground floor.
bio.origin: Set in the Nishi-chaya district, about ten minutes by car from Kanazawa Station, in a renovated machiya-style building.
bio.specialty: Hearty, affordable set meals such as the aji fry teishoku (fried horse mackerel) and the Noto pork loin teishoku, alongside sushi, sashimi, oden, and lunch rice bowls (seafood chirashi, premium eel, roast beef).
bio.philosophy: The kitchen emphasizes local, seasonal ingredients (chisan-chisho) and aims to bring out the natural appeal of each ingredient.

anecdotes.text: A visiting blogger praised the Nishi-chaya district as a calmer alternative to Kanazawa's busier teahouse areas and enjoyed both the aji fry set and the Noto pork loin set here, noting the meals came with assorted small sides.
Banrai and its ground-floor seafood sibling 町家食堂 晴れやか opened together on April 18, 2023 in the Nishi-chaya district.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The signature aji fry is breaded and deep-fried (wheat batter), and the menu includes sushi and soy-seasoned dishes, so gluten and cross-contact are pervasive.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: A sushi and washoku restaurant; standard wheat-containing soy sauce is used and no gluten-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen is seafood-, pork-, and dashi-based; no vegan preparation or separation is documented.

lead: 海鮮丼 ゆたか水産
name_ja: 海鮮丼 ゆたか水産
cuisine: 海鮮丼・地魚（Seafood rice bowls / local Hokuriku fish）
neighborhood: 近江町市場（武蔵ヶ辻／近江町市場内）
gf_detail: This is a seafood-bowl specialist with no gluten-free provision. The official menu lists no gluten-free, vegan, or vegetarian options. Bowls are built on sushi rice seasoned with citrus-juice vinegar plus ingredients like kanpyo, and every bowl is served with aosa miso soup (dashi/miso typically contains wheat), and nigiri/seafood would be served with soy sauce. There is no documentation of a dedicated fryer or any allergen-handling protocol. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: A dedicated seafood-bowl restaurant — every signature item is built on raw fish, shellfish, roe, or meat (e.g., the flagship bowl piles on 15 kinds of seafood; other bowls feature nodoguro, crab, or wagyu roast beef). Bowls come with miso soup made with seafood-based dashi. No vegan or vegetarian dishes are documented.
geocode_note: GSI address search for 石川県金沢市十間町31 returned [136.656738, 36.570744], within the central Kanazawa box (near Omicho Market). No retry needed.
seats_text: 25 seats (about 5 counter seats and 20 table/sofa seats); small individually run shop.
hours_raw: 7:00–15:00; closed Wednesdays (open if Wednesday is a public holiday). An early-morning breakfast bowl (朝丼) is available from 7:00.
closed_days: Wednesdays (open when a Wednesday falls on a public holiday)
cultural_comfort_note: A bustling, no-frills market seafood-bowl counter where a smile, a greeting, and pointing at the photo menu will get you a long way, though staff may have limited English during the morning rush.
caveats: Cash only — bring yen, no cards or e-money. Walk-in only for individuals (reservations taken only for groups of 10+, and only for the flagship bowl). Closed Wednesdays. Prices for the signature bowl vary across sources (roughly 3,500–4,000 yen) depending on season/market rates.

bio.background: The shop is run by chef Toyoshi Nishimura, formerly the head chef of the Katamachi restaurant 'Shoujuku' (笑宿), which closed beloved by its regulars. He trained under Rokusaburo Michiba, the celebrated 'Iron Chef' of Japanese cuisine. Opening a seafood-bowl shop inside Omicho Market had long been his dream.
bio.specialty: Lavish seafood rice bowls built on fish bought each morning at the market auction. The flagship 'Yutaka Saikyo-don' heaps on roughly 15 kinds of seafood — nodoguro (blackthroat seaperch), bluefin tuna, sea urchin, salmon roe, crab, yellowtail, sweet shrimp, Noto pufferfish and more. Other bowls include a nodoguro bowl, buri-crab bowl, and a wagyu roast-beef bowl.
bio.philosophy: A fine-dining craftsman applying kappo techniques to the humble rice bowl — the shop's own line is 'what happens when a fishmonger sources the ingredients and a ryotei chef builds the bowl.' Each ingredient is treated with selective grilling and precise knife work; the rice is seasoned with shiitake, kanpyo, sesame and ginger and finished with citrus juice instead of vinegar for a clean aftertaste.

anecdotes.text: The owner-chef trained under Rokusaburo Michiba, Japan's famous 'Iron Chef,' and previously ran the head kitchen at Katamachi's 'Shoujuku' before opening his long-dreamed-of Omicho seafood-bowl shop.
There is a 1,000-yen morning-only 'asa-don' breakfast bowl served from the 7am opening — a generous deal noted by diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Bowls are served with miso soup and seafood/nigiri are eaten with soy sauce; no gluten-free soy sauce or wheat-free dashi is documented, so wheat exposure should be assumed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every dish centers on raw seafood, roe, or meat and bowls come with seafood-dashi miso soup; no vegan items exist on the menu.

lead: 近江町海鮮丼家ひら井 本店
name_ja: 近江町海鮮丼家ひら井 本店
cuisine: 海鮮丼・和食 (seafood rice bowls / Japanese)
neighborhood: 近江町市場 (Omicho Market, Musashigatsuji)
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. This is a seafood-bowl specialist where dishes are served with wheat-based soy sauce, and alternatives include tempura and unagi (eel) bowls that use wheat batter and wheat-containing tare sauce. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated preparation, and dashi/seasonings are wheat-and-soy based. Not safe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. The entire menu is built around raw seafood rice bowls (tuna, salmon roe, sea urchin, crab, sweet shrimp, nodoguro) and fish-based alternatives. Rice is typically vinegared and dishes use fish dashi and soy sauce. No plant-based options are offered.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市上近江町29 近江町市場
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [136.656784, 36.570999] for 石川県金沢市上近江町29, within the central Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 26 seats (20 counter, 8 table); some sources cite ~25. Small shop.
hours_raw: Weekdays 11:00-15:00; weekends/holidays 10:30-15:00. Closed Wednesdays (and New Year's Day).
closed_days: Wednesday (and New Year's Day)
cultural_comfort_note: A friendly market kaisendon counter where pointing at the photo menu works fine; a few words of Japanese help, but it is used to tourists and is welcoming to foreigners.

bio.origin: Omicho Market (近江町市場), Kanazawa's historic central food market
bio.background: Hirai is a seafood rice-bowl specialist set inside Omicho Market, Kanazawa's bustling 300-year-old central market. The honten (main shop) sits among the market's fish stalls, sourcing its catch from the very vendors around it. It now operates additional branches (an Ichiba-kan location and a Kenrokuen-area shop), but the main shop remains the original Omicho counter.
bio.specialty: Generously heaped seafood bowls (kaisendon) featuring fish from the Sea of Japan. Around 20 varieties are offered; signatures include the 近江町海鮮丼 (Omicho seafood bowl), 金沢三味丼 (Kanazawa three-taste bowl), and ウニ・イクラ丼 (sea urchin and salmon-roe bowl), plus a 'special selection' bowl loaded with tuna, crab, sweet shrimp and sea urchin. Smaller bowls and cooked alternatives like tempura or eel bowls are available for those who prefer not to eat raw fish.
bio.philosophy: A 'ganko' (stubborn/uncompromising) approach to portions and freshness — large, abundant bowls built from carefully selected morning catch and arranged with care.

anecdotes.text: The shop describes its bowls as colorful and piled high with the bounty of the Sea of Japan (彩り美しく、日本海の幸をたっぷり盛りつけた様々な海鮮丼).
Located in Omicho Market within a 15-minute walk of Kenrokuen, the 21st Century Museum and the Higashi Chaya district.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Seafood bowls are eaten with standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat; no gluten-free soy sauce is mentioned.

caveats: Hours end-time varies slightly by source (15:00 on Tabelog/tourism site vs 15:30 on the market site); weekend opening is earlier at 10:30. Closed Wednesdays. Seat count cited as 26 (Tabelog) or ~25 (official site).

lead: 海鮮丼ひかりや 近江町市場店
name_ja: 海鮮丼ひかりや 近江町市場店
cuisine: 海鮮丼・魚介（看板「まるひ丼」＝特製ごま醤油だれ→追い出汁でだし茶漬けの二度楽しめる海鮮丼）
neighborhood: 近江町市場（武蔵ヶ辻）
address_ja: 石川県金沢市下近江町26 フルーツむらはた近江町ビル1F
geocode_note: GSI address search on 石川県金沢市下近江町26 returned [136.656876, 36.57172]; within Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 31 seats total (approx. 26 table + 5 counter); small-scale shop with counter seating.
hours_raw: 10:00-16:00 (L.O. 15:30); occasional evening service 17:30-21:00. Closed Mondays (or following Tuesday if Monday is a holiday). Sources vary slightly (9:00/10:00 open; 15:30/16:00 close).
closed_days: Mondays (following Tuesday if Monday is a public holiday); occasional irregular closures
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, tourist-friendly seafood-bowl counter inside the bustling Omicho Market; a few basic Japanese greetings and pointing at the picture menu will get you a warm welcome, though detailed English may be limited.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. The signature まるひ丼 is dressed with a house sesame-soy sauce (ごま醤油だれ) and finished by pouring hot dashi broth to make chazuke; standard Japanese soy sauce and dashi typically contain wheat. No gluten-free menu or accommodations are documented. Celiacs should avoid.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. This is a dedicated seafood donburi specialist — the entire concept is raw fish/shellfish bowls (ikura, snow crab, sweet shrimp, tuna, octopus, scallop) over rice with fish-based dashi. No plant-based options are documented.
caveats: The Tabelog URL provided in the task (17006790) returned a different establishment (魚旨/Uosashi, Omicho Market D-area) when fetched, so identity was confirmed instead via the Omicho Market official directory, HotPepper, Retty, and the restaurant's own food page. Open/close times differ slightly across sources (9:00 vs 10:00 open; 15:30 vs 16:00 close); 10:00-16:00 used from HotPepper. Address given as 下近江町26 (フルーツむらはた近江町ビル1F); the task's "近江町いちば館1F" descriptor was not corroborated by the official directory.

bio.background: 海鮮丼ひかりや is billed as the only original seafood-bowl specialty shop inside Kanazawa's historic Omicho Market, the city kitchen that has fed locals for centuries. It sits on the market's ground floor in the Fruit Murahata Omicho building, a few shops in from the Kaga-Hachiban-machi entrance, with a compact counter-and-table layout that draws solo travelers, market workers, and families alike.
bio.specialty: The house signature is the まるひ丼 (Maruhi-don), offered in three tiers — 梅 (¥1,280), 竹 (¥1,780), and 松 (¥2,380). The premium 松 piles on seven toppings including ikura, snow crab, sweet shrimp, toro bincho tuna, minced maguro, octopus, and baby scallops.
bio.philosophy: The whole bowl is built to be eaten twice: first the seafood is enjoyed over rice with the shop's special sesame-soy sauce (ごま醤油だれ), then hot dashi is poured over the leftover rice and fish to finish it as a clean, savory だし茶漬け (dashi chazuke) — 'one bowl, twice delicious.' Reviewers note the dashi is tuned so the hot broth never brings out any fishiness from the raw seafood.

anecdotes.text: The bowl is designed as a two-act meal — eat it first as a sesame-soy seafood don, then pour the provided hot dashi over the rest to turn it into dashi-chazuke for a second flavor in the same bowl.
A reviewer praised that the hot-dashi finish, which often draws out a fishy smell from raw seafood, produced none here — 'まったく出てこなかったのでとっても美味しかった.'
Described as Omicho Market's only original kaisen-don specialty shop, with the premium 松 まるひ丼 layering seven toppings including ikura, snow crab, sweet shrimp and tuna.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Bowls are seasoned with a house sesame-soy sauce (特製ごま醤油だれ) and finished by pouring hot dashi to make chazuke; standard soy sauce and dashi typically contain wheat, making the dish unsafe for celiacs.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is entirely raw fish and shellfish bowls (ikura, crab, shrimp, tuna, octopus, scallop) over rice with fish-based dashi; no plant-based options documented.

cuisine: 寿司・海鮮丼
neighborhood: 近江町市場（武蔵ヶ辻）
address_ja: 石川県金沢市上近江町36-2
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市上近江町36-2 returned [136.656357, 36.57132], within the Kanazawa box.
seats_text: Seat count not documented; described as a very small market eatery that fills up quickly.
closed_days: 不定休（irregular closures）
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, bustling market counter where you point at the seafood-bowl photos and pay by card or cash; a few words of Japanese or a translation app go a long way, so bring a guide or be ready to gesture.
gf_detail: A seafood donburi and sushi shop built around rice bowls served with wheat-based soy sauce; no gluten-free menu, labeling, or dedicated prep is documented, so this is not safe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: The entire concept is raw fish and seafood rice bowls (sashimi over rice). There are no documented vegan or vegetarian options.
caveats: Hours are stated as roughly 9:00 until evening/early afternoon with irregular closures; confirm before a special trip. Seat count is undocumented.

bio.background: 海鮮丼いちば sits right in the heart of Kanazawa's Omicho Market, the 300-year-old warren of fishmongers and grocers that locals call their kitchen. It is run as part of the Omicho Ichiba Zushi family of shops and is one of the market's tiniest seafood-bowl counters, the kind of place that fills to capacity within minutes of opening.
bio.specialty: Seafood rice bowls heaped with the morning's catch: a seasonal omakase bowl, a 'daimyo' bowl piled with almost everything in the case, and a Hokuriku luxury bowl featuring local treasures like nodoguro (rosy seaperch) and Toyama Bay white shrimp. Aburi (lightly seared) nodoguro and gas-ebi shrimp are signatures.
bio.philosophy: The shop emphasizes seasonal and local ingredients (旬の食材・地物ネタ) bought fresh from the surrounding market stalls each day.

anecdotes.text: Described on the official Omicho Market site as one of the one-or-two most popular tiny restaurants in the whole market, so small that seats vanish fast.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Raw fish over rice is served with standard wheat-based soy sauce; no gluten-free accommodation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a sashimi/donburi shop, standard Japanese soy sauce (which contains wheat) is the default condiment.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every documented dish is seafood-based; raw fish is the core ingredient, so vegan cross-contact is unavoidable.

cuisine: 手打ち蕎麦・信州料理
neighborhood: 泉が丘（犀川以南・野町駅方面） Izumigaoka, south of the Saigawa near Nomachi
gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. While juwari (十割) soba is 100% buckwheat, the kitchen also makes wheat-containing soba (外二/外二八, roughly 20% wheat flour) and Shinshu oyaki dumplings whose dough uses wheat, so there is heavy shared wheat handling and flour in the air. The dipping broth (tsuyu) is made with soy sauce, which standardly contains wheat. No dedicated gluten-free preparation or celiac protocol is documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-oriented. The tsuyu and broths are dashi-based (fish/bonito), and signature dishes include negitoro (raw tuna) bowls and tempura. The walnut-miso (kurumi) soba and nameko-oroshi soba may appear plant-based but are served with fish-dashi tsuyu. No vegan menu or vegan dashi is documented; a vegetarian would need to ask, and even then dashi makes it unlikely.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市泉が丘2-11-32 ドリームA.Y 1F
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [136.645645, 36.539818] for 石川県金沢市泉が丘2-11-32; within the Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 27 seats total: 13 counter seats plus small raised-tatami sections (a 4-person and a 6-person area).
hours_raw: 11:00-14:00, 18:00-20:30. Closed Wednesdays and the 2nd Tuesday of each month.
closed_days: Wednesdays and the 2nd Tuesday of each month
cultural_comfort_note: A small owner-run neighborhood soba shop where ordering in basic Japanese (or by pointing at the menu photos) is the easiest path; staff are unlikely to speak much English but the atmosphere is calm and welcoming.

bio.chef_name: Yasumura Hiroshi (安村弘 / やすむら)
bio.background: The owner trained as a soba maker in Shinshu (Nagano), Japan's most celebrated buckwheat region, and brings a Japanese-cuisine chef's discipline to the craft. He opened the shop in December 2011 in the Izumigaoka neighborhood south of the Saigawa river.
bio.specialty: Two styles of hand-cut soba are offered: a juwari (100% buckwheat) made with locally milled Torigoe-area buckwheat flour, prized for its aroma and clean throat-feel, and a sotoni/soto-nihachi (roughly 80% buckwheat, 20% wheat) made with Hokkaido flour that pairs well with the house tsuyu. The signature is kurumi-soba (walnut soba): fragrant roasted walnuts blended with white miso into a dipping sauce that diners loosen with broth using a small whisk. Other favorites include nameko-oroshi soba topped with large Yoshino-area nameko mushrooms, tempura and ten-don sets, sobagaki, and a multi-course soba tasting menu (around 4,500 yen) ending with house-made soba-tea ice cream.
bio.philosophy: Showcasing the bounty of the Shinshu satoyama (foothill countryside) through careful, from-scratch soba craft.

anecdotes.text: The kurumi-soba is rooted in a regional specialty of Komoro in Nagano: roasted walnuts ground with sweet white miso, dissolved into the dipping broth before the noodles go in.
The shop also serves Shinshu-style oyaki, savory dough dumplings wrapped around fillings such as miso-seasoned nozawana pickled greens.
A four-course soba tasting lets guests experience buckwheat in several forms, finishing with fragrant soba-tea ice cream.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen handles wheat-containing soba (soto-nihachi, ~20% wheat) and wheat-dough oyaki alongside the 100% buckwheat juwari, so shared flour and boiling water make cross-contact very likely even for the juwari noodles.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soba is served with a soy-sauce-based dipping broth (tsuyu); standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are dashi-based and the menu features fish (negitoro) and tempura; no separate vegan stock is documented.

positives.text: A genuine juwari (100% buckwheat) soba is on the menu, milled from local Torigoe buckwheat, for those avoiding wheat in the noodle itself (though shared equipment and the soy-based tsuyu remain concerns).

caveats: Address per Tabelog is 泉が丘 (Izumigaoka), in the 犀川以南 area near Nomachi, slightly different from a strict 野町 address. Chef name romanization (Yasumura Hiroshi) is taken from one secondary blog source and may not be exact. No explicit allergen/celiac handling policy was documented; GF and vegan assessments are inferred from documented ingredients and standard soba-shop practice.

cuisine: Japanese chestnut specialty cafe and sweets (Noto roasted-chestnut Mont Blanc)
neighborhood: Higashi Chaya District / Kannon-machi (near the Asano River, Higashiyama)
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature Noto-chestnut Mont Blanc is built on wheat-based components (sponge cake and pastry cream layers), and there is no documented gluten-free menu or celiac handling. Cross-contamination from flour in a small dessert kitchen is likely. Not safe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The flagship Mont Blanc and related desserts contain dairy throughout (mascarpone, custard/creme patissiere, unsweetened fresh whipped cream) and egg-based components such as meringue and sponge. No vegan dessert option is documented despite the chestnut paste itself being sugar-free.
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates within the Kanazawa box for 石川県金沢市観音町3-1-16.
seats_text: About 10 seats inside a renovated 100-year-old machiya townhouse; takeout also available.
hours_raw: 11:00-17:00 (last order 16:30), open daily with irregular closures (不定休)
closed_days: Irregular closures (不定休) — no fixed weekly closed day
cultural_comfort_note: A calm machiya cafe slightly off the main Higashi Chaya street where pointing to the signature Mont Blanc and a smile will carry you, though detailed English explanations may be limited.
caveats: The signature Mont Blanc is a limited daily run (around 20 servings), is made to order, and contains wheat, dairy, and egg, so it is unsuitable for gluten-free, vegan, or dairy-free diners. Closures are irregular, so check before visiting. Tariff around 2,420 yen for the Mont Blanc with a drink pairing.

bio.background: Waguri Shiratsuyu is a Japanese-chestnut specialty cafe set inside a renovated machiya townhouse more than a century old, on a quiet lane just off the main street of Kanazawa's Higashi Chaya district. The owner, who comes from Noto, opened the shop to champion the region's chestnut growers and the idea of local production for local tables, personally overseeing sourcing and keeping direct relationships with the orchards.
bio.specialty: The signature is the 'Harizuri' premium Mont Blanc made from Noto aged roasted chestnuts. The chestnut paste carries no added sugar, drawing its sweetness entirely from naturally fallen Noto chestnuts that are aged and pressure-roasted; it is piped to order into ultra-thin roughly 1mm strands over layers of mascarpone, sponge, meringue, custard, and unsweetened fresh cream.
bio.philosophy: Local production and local consumption (chisan-chisho) — using the cafe as a way to connect visitors with Noto and to support chestnut farmers who struggle with sustainability.

anecdotes.text: The chestnut grower the shop highlights, Matsuo of Wajima, developed an aging-and-roasting technique after discovering Noto chestnuts online in 2005; the cafe was created in part to bring that work to a wider audience.

Guests can watch and photograph the chestnut paste being extruded through a custom press into delicate roughly 1mm strands, a piece of theater meant to heighten the aroma and texture of each made-to-order Mont Blanc.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Desserts are assembled in a small kitchen using wheat-based sponge and pastry cream, so flour is present and cross-contamination is likely; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature Mont Blanc and related sweets contain dairy (mascarpone, custard, fresh cream) and egg (meringue, sponge); no separate vegan preparation is documented.

positives.text: The chestnut paste itself uses only Noto aged roasted chestnuts with no added sugar, so the chestnut element is naturally simple.

cuisine: 石臼挽き手打ち十割そば（民家を利用した山手の蕎麦処）
neighborhood: 別所町（寺町・犀川以南の山手）
gf_detail: This is a juwari (100% buckwheat) soba shop, so the noodles themselves are made without wheat flour. However, it is NOT a celiac-safe destination. The dipping sauce (tsuyu) is built on bonito and kelp dashi plus soy sauce, which contains wheat, and tempura is served, meaning flour and shared frying are present in the kitchen. There is no indication of dedicated gluten-free handling, separate equipment, or cross-contamination control. Travelers with celiac disease should not assume the juwari noodles are safe here.
vegan_detail: The broth (tsuyu) is made from bonito (katsuo) and kelp dashi, so even plain soba is not vegan. The menu centers on duck soba, tempura with seasonal mountain vegetables, and seafood-adjacent items. There is no documented vegan offering. Wild-vegetable (sansai) and bamboo-shoot dishes exist seasonally but are prepared with fish-based dashi.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市別所町ツ55
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned coordinates for 石川県金沢市別所町ツ55; within the Kanazawa box. Location is in the southern foothills (山手) of the city, consistent with the Bessho-cho address.
seats_text: 40 seats (tatami/table seating in a renovated private house; shoes removed at entry)
hours_raw: 11:30〜15:00 ※売り切れ次第終了。定休日：火曜、第3水曜（祝日の場合は営業）。
closed_days: Tuesdays and the third Wednesday of each month (open if these fall on a public holiday). Also closes early when sold out.
cultural_comfort_note: This is a traditional countryside soba house in a converted home where you remove your shoes and sit on tatami; little to no English is likely, so a few words of Japanese and patience will help a foreign student feel welcome.
caveats: Lunch only (11:30-15:00) and closes when the day's soba sells out, so arrive early. Cash only. Reservations are accepted on weekdays only; weekends can mean long waits (reported up to two hours during holidays). It is in the mountainous Bessho area south of the Saigawa, so a car is effectively required. Not suitable for celiac or vegan diners.

bio.background: Soba Miyakawa sits in the wooded southern foothills of Kanazawa, in Bessho-cho, an area locally known for its bamboo shoots. The shop occupies a repurposed traditional private house (minka): diners remove their shoes and settle onto tatami at tables, with shoji screens framing views of the garden, and the menu hand-written on folding fans. An adjacent farm stand sells same-day-harvested vegetables and wild mountain plants (sansai).
bio.specialty: Self-milled, hand-cut juwari (100% buckwheat) soba made by the kihiki / namiko-uchi method from Fukui heirloom buckwheat, offered in two styles: a thick-cut from whole unhulled groats (genso-ba) and a fine-cut from hulled groats (maru-nuki). Signature plates include ten-mori soba with seasonal mountain-vegetable tempura and tsuke-kamo (duck dipping) soba. In spring, late April to mid-May, bamboo-shoot dishes are the seasonal draw. House-made soba-cha (buckwheat-tea) pudding and soba ice cream are popular finishes.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen avoids chemical seasonings, drawing the dipping sauce's umami from bonito and kelp, and aims for a balance in which neither the noodle nor the broth dominates.

anecdotes.text: The buckwheat is ground in-house on a stone mill and cut by hand into 100% buckwheat noodles, with two cuts available depending on whether whole or hulled groats are used.
The shop is so popular it is often fully booked, and during holiday periods waits of around two hours have been reported.
Spring bamboo-shoot dishes draw significant crowds from late April through mid-May, reflecting the area's reputation for bamboo shoots.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tempura (flour-battered, deep-fried) is on the menu, indicating wheat flour and a fryer are in use in the same kitchen as the soba.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dipping sauce is a traditional bonito-and-kelp dashi tsuyu, which uses soy sauce (typically wheat-containing); no gluten-free sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The tsuyu/broth is built on bonito (fish) and kelp dashi, so even plain soba is not vegan.

positives.text: The noodles are 100% buckwheat (juwari), so the noodle itself contains no wheat flour, though the surrounding kitchen and sauce are not gluten-free.

lead: 能加万菜 市場屋 近江町総本店
name_ja: 能加万菜 市場屋 近江町総本店
cuisine: 居酒屋・海鮮・寿司（近江町市場の地物海鮮を中心とした市場食事処）
neighborhood: 近江町市場（武蔵ヶ辻／十間町口すぐ）
gf_detail: This is a seafood-bowl, sushi and Kanazawa-oden market eatery where soy sauce, wheat-based seasonings (including local ishiru fermented-fish sauce) and dashi are used heavily across the menu. There is no documented gluten-free program, no dedicated preparation area, and no allergen handling information published. Sushi and kaisen-don are served with regular wheat-based soy sauce, and oden broth is not gluten-free. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: The entire concept is fresh seafood sourced directly from Omicho Market — seafood rice bowls (kaisen-don), nigiri sushi, crab dishes and fish-based Kanazawa oden. Dashi and fish-based seasonings (ishiru) are foundational. No vegan or vegetarian dishes are documented, and the kitchen is built around raw and cooked fish. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
geocode_note: GSI geocoded 石川県金沢市下堤町19-1 to [136.655502, 36.570988], within the central Kanazawa box. Matches the Omicho Market (Jukkencho exit) location.
seats_text: 78 seats; second floor available for groups up to 40.
hours_raw: 7:00–22:00 (L.O. food 21:30), open daily. Note: hours have varied around a 2025 renovation; older listings showed 7:00/8:00–15:00/16:00/18:00. Confirm directly before visiting.
closed_days: 年中無休 (open year-round)
cultural_comfort_note: Very approachable for a foreign student: it sits right inside the bustling Omicho Market, offers an English menu, and the casual point-and-order seafood-bowl format makes it easy even with little Japanese.
caveats: Hours appear to have changed around a 2025 renovation and differ between listings (some show 7:00–15:00/16:00, the current main listing shows 7:00–22:00); confirm the day's hours before visiting. No allergen, gluten-free, or vegan information is published — unsuitable for celiacs or vegans.

bio.background: Ichibaya is the fifth shop of the Kanazawa-based Noukabanzai (能加万菜) brand, which centers on Ishikawa's local ingredients and food culture. Set right at the Jukkencho entrance of Omicho Market, it serves fresh seafood bought directly from the market that morning, with breakfast service starting early so visitors can eat a seafood bowl from the start of the day.
bio.specialty: Signature dishes are the 特選近江町市場屋丼 (premium seafood bowl) and generously topped nigiri sushi, alongside other kaisen-don, a crab Kaga-temari bowl, and Kanazawa oden — all served in regional ceramics such as Kutani porcelain and Yamanaka lacquerware.
bio.philosophy: The shop aims to offer 'delicious food and good time' that becomes an enjoyable page in visitors' memories of Kanazawa, showcasing Ishikawa's ingredients, seasonings (such as ishiru) and craft tableware.

anecdotes.text: It is the fifth location of the Noukabanzai brand, positioned so guests can enjoy seafood bowls, sushi and Kanazawa oden together right inside the market.
The kitchen highlights Ishikawa craft tableware — Kutani porcelain and Yamanaka lacquerware — and traditional seasonings like ishiru fermented-fish sauce.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sushi and seafood bowls are served with standard wheat-based soy sauce, and local ishiru (fermented-fish) seasoning and dashi are used throughout the menu.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is built entirely around fresh fish and shellfish from Omicho Market, including raw seafood bowls, nigiri sushi, crab dishes and fish-based Kanazawa oden; no vegan items are documented.

positives.text: An English menu is available, which can help travelers identify ingredients.

cuisine: 寿司（おまかせ・江戸前／能登の食材）
neighborhood: 片町・木倉町（香林坊交差点近く）
gf_detail: This is a traditional Edomae-style sushi counter where nigiri is brushed with nikiri (wheat-based soy sauce) before serving, and cooked appetizers rely on dashi and soy-based seasonings. There is no gluten-free menu and no indication of allergen accommodation. Not safe for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: An omakase sushi restaurant built entirely around Noto seafood — sweet shrimp, nodoguro (blackthroat seaperch), white-fleshed fish, sea urchin, anago and similar. There are no vegan options; this is not a destination for plant-based diners.
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned [136.65155, 36.561558] for 石川県金沢市木倉町4-10, within the central Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 17 seats total: 9 counter seats and 8 tatami (zashiki) seats.
closed_days: 水曜日・日曜日・祝日（Wednesday, Sunday, and public holidays）
cultural_comfort_note: This is an intimate, reservation-only counter where the master serves a set omakase flow and mostly hosts regulars, so a foreign student should book by phone two months ahead and be comfortable with a quiet, Japanese-language, high-end atmosphere.
caveats: High-end omakase at roughly 20,000-29,999 yen per person. New guests can only reserve from two months in advance; last seating around 19:30. Not suitable for gluten-free or vegan diners.

bio.background: Otome Sushi opened in 1995 on a quiet back lane in Kanazawa's Kibura-machi, just off the lively Katamachi district near the Korinbo crossing. The master, Kazuhiko Tsurumi, was born in Kanazawa and began his apprenticeship at age sixteen, bringing roughly four decades of craft to his counter. The shop has grown into one of the city's most celebrated sushi houses, earning two Michelin stars in the 2021 Michelin Guide Hokuriku.
bio.specialty: An omakase course rooted in the seasonal seafood of the Noto Peninsula — sweet shrimp, nodoguro (blackthroat seaperch), white-fleshed fish, squid scored with fine knife work, sea urchin and anago — moving through cooked appetizers into a procession of nigiri. The chef brushes each piece with nikiri sauce before it is served.
bio.philosophy: Tsurumi is known for meticulous sourcing and understated, balanced seasoning, letting the quality of Noto ingredients lead while quietly focusing on his work rather than performing for guests. Small touches, such as hiding a layer of uni sauce beneath the sea urchin, reflect his attention to detail.

anecdotes.text: On arrival the master greets each guest with a simple choice — whether to start with appetizers (tsumami) or go straight to nigiri — setting the tone for a personal, conversational meal.
Writers consistently note the shop serves mostly local regulars rather than tourists and offers exceptional value, with prices well below comparable Tokyo counters.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Nigiri is brushed with nikiri (a soy-sauce-based glaze) before serving, and standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat — there is no indication of a gluten-free alternative.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire omakase is built around fish, shellfish, and seafood from the Noto Peninsula, with no plant-based course described.

cuisine: 味噌ラーメン・つけ麺 (miso ramen and tsukemen)
neighborhood: 金沢駅周辺（北鉄金沢駅）
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates within the Kanazawa box for 石川県金沢市堀川町6-3.
seats_text: Around 12-13 counter seats only; small, queue-prone shop.
closed_days: Mondays (some listings note irregular closures, so confirm before visiting)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, busy counter ramen shop where you order quickly and slurp; basic Japanese or pointing helps, and expect to line up at peak times.
gf_detail: This is a wheat-based ramen and tsukemen shop. Noodles are made from wheat, and miso/pork-bone broths typically contain wheat-derived soy and miso ingredients. Not safe for celiacs; no gluten-free options documented.
vegan_detail: A miso ramen specialist built on pork-bone (tonkotsu) broth with miso; no vegan options documented. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.

bio.background: Menya Taiga is a small, much-loved miso ramen specialist near Kanazawa Station (steps from Hokutetsu Kanazawa Station). It built a regional reputation as a Hokuriku miso ramen destination and grew into a tiny group with a flagship, a Takayanagi branch, and a tanmen-focused shop.
bio.specialty: Specially blended red miso (described in sources as Hokkaido red miso) simmered into an aromatic, rich yet clean broth. Signature bowls include the special miso ramen, red miso ramen, black miso ramen, and miso tsukemen, with seasonal limited bowls such as oyster or crab miso ramen.
bio.philosophy: A wellness-minded touch sets it apart: guests are served a complimentary vegetable juice before the meal, framed as helping moderate blood-sugar spikes. The miso broth is praised as mellow and balanced rather than oily, finished with aonori, fresh ginger, and yuzu for layered flavor.

anecdotes.text: Ranked at the top for Ishikawa Prefecture on a major ramen database and known to draw weekday lunch queues, with the shop sometimes opening early yet still unable to clear the line.
Recognized as a Tabelog Ramen WEST Top 100 selection for 2025.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are boiled in the same kitchen; no gluten-free preparation documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Miso-based broths at ramen shops typically contain wheat-derived miso and soy ingredients; no allergen-free claims documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork-bone based with pork toppings; no separate vegan preparation documented.

caveats: Sources disagree slightly on opening time (Tabelog lists 11:00; the local Kanazawa listing and other sources list 11:30) and on closed days (most list Mondays; Tabelog notes irregular closures). Owner/chef name not documented. Confirm exact hours before visiting.

lead: 海鮮市場料理 市の蔵
name_ja: 海鮮市場料理 市の蔵
name_en: Kaisen Ichiba Ryori Ichi no Kura
cuisine: 加賀料理・蟹料理・海鮮（治部煮など郷土料理あり）
gf_detail: This is a seafood and Kaga-cuisine restaurant built around fish, crab, tempura, grilled dishes, and rice bowls. Cooking relies heavily on Japanese soy sauce, miso, and dashi — all of which typically contain or are processed with wheat — so it cannot be considered celiac-safe. No gluten-free menu, gluten-free soy sauce, or dedicated preparation is documented, and signature dishes (jibuni stew, crab miso shell-grill, set meals) are wheat- and dashi-based. A celiac traveler should not rely on this restaurant.
vegan_detail: The entire concept is seafood-market cuisine: live crab, seasonal fish, sashimi, rice bowls, and duck jibuni. Dashi (bonito/fish stock) underlies most dishes. No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented and the kitchen is fundamentally fish- and meat-based. Not a viable option for vegans or vegetarians.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市青草町88番地 近江町いちば館2階
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市青草町88 returned [136.656036, 36.571659], within the Kanazawa box. Matches the Omicho Ichiba-kan building in central Kanazawa.
neighborhood: 近江町市場（いちば館2F）
seats_text: Approximately 100 seats: counter, sofa, and table seating plus private tatami rooms (4-16) and banquet spaces for groups up to about 38-40.
hours_raw: 11:00～22:00（L.O. 21:30）年中無休（元日のみ休みの記載あり）
closed_days: Open year-round (年中無休); some listings note closed January 1st only.
cultural_comfort_note: A busy, tourist-friendly market restaurant in the heart of Omicho where pointing at the picture menu and basic greetings will get you a warm welcome, though deep allergy questions may be hard to convey in English.
caveats: Heavily seafood- and dashi-based; unsuitable for vegans, vegetarians, and celiacs. Popular tourist spot, so it can be crowded at lunch.

bio.background: Ichi no Kura sits on the second floor of the Ichiba-kan building right inside Omicho Market, the centuries-old marketplace locals call 'Kanazawa's kitchen.' The restaurant draws directly on that bustling market energy, sourcing fish and crab from just downstairs and serving them in a warm, wood-filled dining room.
bio.origin: 近江町市場（いちば館2F）, central Kanazawa
bio.specialty: Seafood market cuisine and Kaga regional dishes: seasonal fish set meals and kaisen-don rice bowls at lunch, and at dinner traditional Kaga fare alongside crab served year-round. Signature plates include crab miso grilled in the shell (かに味噌甲羅焼き), crab salad, duck jibuni stew (鴨の治部煮), and squid-liver miso grill (いかわた味噌焼き).
bio.philosophy: To let visitors enjoy the lively spirit of Omicho Market over a meal — pairing Kanazawa sightseeing with fresh local seafood — and notably to offer crab dishes all year rather than only in season.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's defining feature is that you can enjoy crab dishes year-round, not just during winter crab season, thanks to its place inside the market.
Lunch focuses on seasonal fish in set meals and rice bowls, while evenings turn to traditional Kaga cuisine and dishes using local meat.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Cuisine is built on soy sauce, miso, and dashi (jibuni stew, grilled dishes, set meals), which in standard Japanese cooking contain or involve wheat; no gluten-free alternatives are documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: A seafood- and meat-centered kitchen using fish dashi throughout; no vegan or vegetarian preparation is mentioned.

lead: 能登牛 串焼き・炙り寿司 たくみ
name_ja: 能登牛 串焼き・炙り寿司 たくみ
cuisine: Noto beef aburi (seared) sushi and grilled beef skewers — standing-counter stall
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The seared nigiri and skewers are served with the shop's own soy-based sauce (Japanese soy sauce normally contains wheat), and rice/sushi is central. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated handling, and no allergen information published. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. This is a dedicated Noto beef stall — every menu item (seared beef nigiri, beef skewers, seared beef rolls) centers on A5-grade beef. There are no plant-based options.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市青草町88 近江町いちば館1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市青草町88 returned [136.656036, 36.571659], within the Kanazawa box. Matches the Omicho Ichiba-kan building in Omicho Market.
neighborhood: Omicho Market (Omicho Ichiba-kan 1F / Seseragi-dori), central Kanazawa
seats_text: No seats — standing-counter stall (tachigui). Order and eat freshly grilled items on the spot; takeout also available.
hours_raw: 10:00–16:00 (per Omicho Market official listing). Tabelog lists Fri–Sun 09:00–16:00, closed Mon–Thu. Effectively a weekend-focused operation; weekends can see lines and early sell-outs.
closed_days: Monday–Thursday (open mainly Friday–Sunday; sources differ slightly on exact days)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny standing stall in the bustling market where you point, pay cash, and eat on the spot — easy for a foreign student, though there is no English menu and staff likely speak limited English.
caveats: Hours and closed days vary by source (Tabelog: Fri–Sun 09:00–16:00; market listing: 10:00–16:00 closed Mon–Thu). Cash only. No seating — standing only. Popular dishes can sell out on busy weekends.

bio.background: A small standing-counter stall tucked into the Omicho Ichiba-kan building on the first floor of Kanazawa's lively Omicho Market, Takumi specializes in Noto beef — a prized local brand. Noto beef is raised in the nature and sea breeze of the Noto Peninsula and is so scarce (only around 930 head shipped a year, almost all consumed within Ishikawa) that locals call it a phantom brand.
bio.specialty: Seared Noto beef nigiri (aburi sushi) and hand-skewered, grilled Noto beef. The kitchen sources A5-grade-or-higher Noto beef, skewers each stick by hand, and grills to order. For the seared nigiri it uses Ishikawa Koshihikari rice, lightly torches the surface of the beef, and finishes it with the shop's own soy-based sauce. The lineup also includes seared tuna and seared gunkan rolls.
bio.philosophy: Making premium, hard-to-find Noto beef approachable — grilled fresh and eaten immediately, standing right there in the market, so visitors can taste a regional luxury without a formal restaurant.

anecdotes.text: The seared Noto beef nigiri (from around 700 yen) is described by visitors as filling the mouth with umami and sweetness, with a melting texture; the convenience of eating rare premium beef on the spot has made it especially popular with tourists.
The shop uses a golden cow as its signboard mascot and frames itself as supporting local Kanazawa and Ishikawa tourism.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Seared nigiri and skewers are finished with the shop's own soy-based sauce; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, so the sauce should be assumed to contain gluten.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Entirely a Noto beef stall — all items are beef-based and grilled on shared equipment; no plant-based options.

lead: フルーツむらはた 本店
name_ja: フルーツむらはた 本店
cuisine: フルーツパーラー・喫茶（パフェ）
neighborhood: 武蔵町（近江町市場すぐ・めいてつエムザ向かい）
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 石川県金沢市武蔵町2番12号, well within the central Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 49 seats (7 counter, 42 table); 1F fruit shop, 2F parlor
closed_days: No regular closing days listed (open daily)
cultural_comfort_note: A bustling, walk-in-only fruit parlor where you can simply point at the seasonal parfait you want, so a foreign student can enjoy it comfortably even with no Japanese, though English support is not guaranteed.
gf_detail: A traditional fruit parlor serving parfaits with cake, cookies, cereal/granola and wheat-based components; no gluten-free menu or celiac handling is documented, so it is not safe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Parfaits are built on dairy ice cream, cream and milk; although the fresh fruit is plant-based, no vegan or dairy-free options are documented.
caveats: Founding year (1914 / 大正3年) and the ~880yen fruit parfait price come from secondary food blogs; the official site confirms a "100+ year" heritage but does not state an exact year. Walk-in only (no reservations); can have queues at peak times.

bio.origin: Kanazawa, Musashi-cho (steps from Omicho Market, across from Meitetsu M'za)
bio.background: Murahata is a long-established greengrocer founded in 1914 (Taisho 3) that has grown into a fruit specialist with over a century of history. Its flagship in Musashi-cho keeps a fresh-fruit shop on the ground floor and a beloved fruit parlor upstairs.
bio.specialty: Seasonal fruit parfaits made with whatever fruit is at its peak — from the classic fruit parfait to honey-peach (suimitsuto), Miyazaki mango and matcha versions — plus a takeout 'Kanazawa Parfait.'
bio.philosophy: As a specialist fruit company, Murahata pursues the possibilities of fruit and aims to make fruit gifts an indispensable part of Japanese life, insisting that its quality cannot be spoken of without its hundred-year history.

anecdotes.text: The shop began in 1914 as a greengrocer and is now a century-old fruit specialist whose parfaits draw regular queues in central Kanazawa.
The classic fruit parfait is praised for the careful cut and quality of each piece of fruit at an approachable price, a hallmark of a dedicated fruit shop.

positives.text: Built around fresh seasonal fruit, so naturally fruit-forward, though parfaits include dairy and wheat-based items.

lead: 金沢町家発酵カフェ（四十萬谷本舗 本店）
name_ja: 四十萬谷本舗 本店（金沢町家発酵カフェ）
name_en: Shijimaya Honpo Honten (Kanazawa Machiya Hakko Cafe)
cuisine: Fermentation cafe (amazake, koji-based gelato and sweets) attached to a traditional Kanazawa fermented-food shop
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. The shop's core trade is fermented foods (kaburazushi/turnip sushi, koji pickles) and the cafe's signature items are amazake (sweet rice-koji drink), amazake soda, and koji-based gelato. Amazake and gelato can be naturally wheat-free, but there is no published allergen handling, no dedicated equipment information, and koji/fermented products often involve shared production with wheat-containing items. Not confirmed celiac-safe — a sensitive traveler must ask in person about each item.
vegan_detail: The cafe's headline offerings are plant-friendly by nature: amazake (made from rice koji), amazake soda in straight/shiso/yuzu/ginger, and koji-based gelato including amazake and amazake-yuzu-basil flavors, plus Kaga-vegetable gelato lines (e.g., Kaga roasted-tea). These are rice/fruit/vegetable based rather than dairy-forward, so there are vegan-friendly choices. However, the cafe does not advertise a certified vegan menu, some gelato may contain dairy, and the parent shop's main products (turnip and daikon sushi cured with fish) are not vegan. Confirm dairy/egg content of individual gelato flavors before ordering.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市弥生1丁目17-28
geocode_note: GSI address search returned [136.64856, 36.549435] for 石川県金沢市弥生1丁目17-28; within the Kanazawa box.
neighborhood: Yayoi (Teramachi / Nomachi area, south of the Sai River)
seats_text: Small cafe corner inside the historic main shop; roughly 10 cafe seats reported, overlooking a traditional courtyard garden.
hours_raw: Shop 9:00-19:00 (Sun/holidays 9:00-18:00); Cafe approx 9:00-17:00 (L.O. 16:30). Tourism listing notes shop 9:00-18:00, closed Sundays. Hours vary by source.
closed_days: Sources differ: one tourism listing states closed Sundays; a cafe listing states closed the first Sunday of each month. Confirm before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: This is a historic family fermented-food shop with a quiet cafe corner; staff likely speak limited English, so bring a translation app or a few Japanese phrases and point to the amazake or gelato you want.
caveats: The original pitch (麹カレー koji curry, dedicated vegan/veg menu) is not strongly documented — the café's verifiable offerings are amazake drinks, amazake soda, and koji/Kaga-vegetable gelato (sweets and drinks rather than a full meal). Hours and closed days differ between sources; confirm directly. The parent business is primarily a fermented-food retail shop (turnip/daikon sushi), so this is a snack-and-sweets cafe stop, not a sit-down restaurant.

bio.origin: Kanazawa (Yayoi district, near the Teramachi temple area south of the Sai River)
bio.background: Shijimaya Honpo was founded in 1875 (Meiji 8) and has spent roughly 150 years making Kanazawa's traditional fermented foods, most famously kaburazushi (turnip cured with yellowtail and rice koji) and daikon-zushi. The Yayoi main store occupies a Kanazawa machiya (townhouse) building that has kept its traditional appearance for about 140 years, in an area recognized for its preserved historic streetscape.
bio.specialty: In one renovated section of the historic shop the family opened a 'fermentation cafe,' turning the koji expertise behind their pickles into gentle, body-friendly sweets and drinks: amazake (sweet rice-koji drink), amazake soda in shiso, yuzu and ginger, and koji-based gelato including amazake, amazake-yuzu-basil and Kaga-vegetable flavors.
bio.philosophy: The company frames its work around valuing local tradition and contributing to healthy, enriched living through food and fermentation.

anecdotes.text: The cafe sits inside a Meiji-era machiya and looks onto a traditional inner courtyard garden, so visitors sip amazake or eat koji gelato surrounded by the old shop's architecture.
The shop also offers hands-on pickling experiences for kaburazushi and koji pickles, and the family even keeps historic Japanese swords that are explained on certain tour courses.

positives.text: Signature cafe items are rice-koji amazake, amazake soda and koji-based gelato, which are plant-friendly and naturally wheat-free options rather than dairy- or meat-forward dishes.

lead: 洋食屋 グリルニュー狸
name_ja: 洋食屋 グリルニュー狸
cuisine: 洋食 (Japanese-style Western comfort food) — yoshoku
neighborhood: 小立野 (Komatsuno), near the Ishikawa Prefectural Library, on the Kenrokuen / Hondamachi side of central Kanazawa
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned exact-address match for 石川県金沢市小立野三丁目27番12号; coordinates fall within the central Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 16 counter seats around an open kitchen
hours_raw: Lunch 11:00-14:30, Dinner 17:00-20:30 (L.O. 20:30). Closed Tuesdays and the 3rd Monday of each month.
closed_days: Tuesdays, plus the 3rd Monday of each month
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, much-loved local yoshoku counter run by three generations of one family; there is no indication of an English menu or English service, so a foreign student will be most comfortable with a Japanese-speaking friend or a translation app and some patience.
gf_detail: This is a classic yoshoku kitchen built on wheat — demi-glace, fried cutlets (panko), gratin and soy-seasoned fried rice all feature heavily, and the demi-glace base is simmered for about a week. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated equipment, and no documented allergen handling. Not safe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: A meat-and-seafood Showa-era Western diner: the signatures are tongue stew, hayashi rice with a week-simmered beef-tendon demi-glace, hamburger steak, salmon steak and chashu-and-shiitake fried rice. Sauces and dressings are house-made but built on meat stock; there are no documented vegan or vegetarian options.

bio.chef_name: 矢田 紀彦 (Norihiko Yada), founder; with son 佳弘 (Yoshihiro) and grandson 勇紀 (Yuki)
bio.background: Founded in 1967 (Showa 42) by Norihiko Yada, who had trained at a Kanazawa Western-style eatery called Tanuki Chaya and borrowed the kanji 狸 (tanuki) from his old employer as a tribute when he opened his own place. The restaurant later settled in its current Komatsuno location near the prefectural library, where it is run today by three generations of the Yada family working the same open kitchen together.
bio.specialty: Its most famous dish is the dark, soy-seasoned 'yakimeshi' fried rice — born from the idea of cooking in a 'tanuki color' (a deep brown) and made with chashu pork and dried shiitake. Also iconic are the hayashi rice over a demi-glace simmered roughly a week from beef tendon and aromatic vegetables and finished with a runny egg, the tongue stew (tan-shichu), hamburger steak, and an Omicho-Market salmon steak.
bio.philosophy: Everything from sauces to dressings is made in-house, with the family describing the use of unexpected hidden ingredients to give each dish depth and individuality. The third generation has spoken of wanting to faithfully protect the old-shop yoshoku flavors while gradually adding his own touch.

anecdotes.text: The name comes from the founder's former workplace, the Western eatery 'Tanuki Chaya'; Norihiko Yada took the tanuki (狸) character with him as a homage when he went independent in 1967.

Second-generation Yoshihiro has said the three generations all share the wish to make the shop better — 'we sometimes clash, but I respect my son's fresh ideas and am gradually handing things over' — while third-generation Yuki has said he wants to protect the old-shop flavors and eventually add his own character.
The signature dark fried rice is described locally as 'black yakimeshi,' and the tongue stew has fans who say they could eat its carrots every day.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is wheat-dominated (panko-fried cutlets, gratin, demi-glace, soy-seasoned fried rice) in a single small kitchen; no gluten-free provision is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature yakimeshi is explicitly a soy-sauce-seasoned fried rice (醤油ベース), and standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Core dishes are beef tongue stew, beef-tendon demi-glace, hamburger steak, salmon and chashu — the kitchen is meat- and fish-centered with no documented vegan separation.

caveats: A 16-seat counter-only family diner that fills with regulars; expect a wait at lunch and no English support. Not suitable for celiac or vegan diners. Hours and the rotating 3rd-Monday closure should be reconfirmed by phone (076-262-6658) before a special trip.

lead: サニーベルコーヒー 尾張町喫茶
name_ja: サニーベルコーヒー 尾張町喫茶
cuisine: 自家焙煎コーヒー・喫茶／パフェ・ティラミス
neighborhood: 尾張町（ひがし茶屋街と近江町市場の間）
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates within the central Kanazawa box for 尾張町2-11-2.
seats_text: 16 seats (8 counter, 2 tables of 4)
hours_raw: 10:00-17:00 (Fri from 09:00 per one source); Tabelog lists Fri 09:00-16:00, Tue/Wed/Sat/Sun 10:00-16:00; closed Mon & Thu
closed_days: 月曜・木曜 (Monday & Thursday)
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, design-forward café where you order coffee and dessert at the counter; a few words of Japanese or pointing at the pastry case will get you through comfortably.
vegan_detail: This is a specialty coffee and dessert café built around dairy-heavy items: café latte, panna cotta, tiramisu, and soft serve ice cream. No vegan menu or plant-milk options are documented. A black drip coffee or espresso would be the only incidental vegan order; ask about milk alternatives, as none are confirmed.
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. The signature desserts (tiramisu/panna cotta with biscuit, macarons) and the general café context mean wheat is present. No gluten-free handling or dedicated preparation is documented, so this is not celiac-safe; plain black coffee is naturally GF.
caveats: Hours vary slightly between sources (one article lists 10:00-17:00 with L.O. 17:00; Tabelog shows 16:00 close and an earlier 09:00 Friday open). Closed Mondays and Thursdays. Small 16-seat café; can fill up given its location between Higashi Chaya District and Omicho Market.

bio.background: This is the second location of Sunny Bell Coffee, a self-roasting specialty coffee shop regarded as one of Kanazawa's representative coffee houses, whose flagship sits in Kasaichi-machi. The Owaricho branch opened on April 17 (2024), tucked between the Higashi Chaya geisha district and Omicho Market.
bio.specialty: Self-roasted drip coffee and graceful latte art carry over from the original shop, but this branch leans into sweets: a tiramisu panna cotta with espresso-soaked biscuit and mascarpone-cream cheese, seasonal mini panna cotta parfaits (mango, blueberry), and house soft serve ice cream (with an affogato option).
bio.philosophy: Where the flagship is a coffee-first counter, the Owaricho location was conceived as a place to slow down: its stated concept is for guests to relax and savor sweets and drinks at leisure, and a dedicated pastry chef was brought on to broaden the dessert lineup.

anecdotes.text: The space is designed in a deliberately minimalist style with restrained, muted color tones, built around an espresso machine and a pastry showcase.
A summer-season tiramisu soft serve was introduced as a branch-exclusive twist on the shop's signature dessert.

cuisine: 自家焙煎スペシャルティコーヒースタンド（ハンドドリップ／ラテ・自家製スイーツ）
gf_detail: A specialty coffee stand, not a gluten-free destination. The food offerings are wheat-based pastries such as canelé and tiramisu, and no gluten-free menu or celiac-safe handling is documented. Black coffee and the bean selection are naturally gluten-free, but there is no dedicated GF preparation, so it is not suitable for strict celiacs beyond plain coffee.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan venue. The signature sweets (tiramisu, soft-serve, pudding, parfaits) are dairy- and egg-based, and milk-based lattes feature heavily. Black drip coffee and whole-bean purchases are plant-based, but no plant-milk option or vegan food is documented; a vegan visitor would need to ask and would largely be limited to black coffee.
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja 石川県金沢市笠市町5-8 via GSI; first feature coordinates within the Kanazawa box.
neighborhood: 笠市町（金沢駅東口と近江町市場の間、ひがし茶屋街方面）
seats_text: 5 seats (small counter-style coffee stand)
closed_days: 月曜・木曜（Monday and Thursday）
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny five-seat, cash-only roastery stand where a few words of Japanese and patience go a long way, though pointing at the bean lineup and ordering a drip coffee is easy enough for a foreign student.
caveats: The provided target metadata describes the 尾張町/ひがし茶屋街 area, which is the brand's second shop (Owari-machi Kissa, opened April 2024). The supplied Tabelog URL (17010661) corresponds to the original honten at 笠市町5-8, and this record reflects that honten. The two locations have slightly different hours and closed days.

bio.background: Sunny Bell Coffee is the flagship roastery of Masashi Takamatsu, an award-winning latte artist who has placed in multiple latte-art competitions. He opened the shop in 2013 and moved it to its current Kasaichi-machi premises in 2017, building a five-seat space where vintage roasting equipment is on full view to customers.
bio.specialty: House-roasted single-origin specialty coffee (typically 5-7 varieties on offer) brewed by meticulous hand pour, alongside signature sweets such as canelé and tiramisu. He roasts on a Fuji Royal R-105 semi-hot-air 5kg roaster, favoring light-to-medium roasts that highlight each bean's individual character.
bio.philosophy: Takamatsu emphasizes the individual characteristics of each bean, monitoring color and aroma closely to preserve fragrance and aims to deliver a special cup that is hard to reproduce at home.

anecdotes.text: The roasting aroma is said to spread across the whole surrounding area, drawing people to the tiny five-seat stand.
Cafes across Japan source Takamatsu's beans, and he opened a second shop, Owari-machi Kissa, in April 2024 between Higashi Chaya District and Omicho Market.

positives.text: Whole-bean purchases and plain black drip coffee are naturally free of gluten, dairy, and animal products.

cuisine: そば・手打ち蕎麦（粗挽き九割そば）／日本酒の品揃え豊富な地元密着の蕎麦店・居酒屋
gf_detail: This is a buckwheat soba shop, but the signature noodle is described as "九割そば" (nine-tenths buckwheat), meaning roughly 10% wheat flour binder is used — so the soba itself is NOT gluten-free. The dashi-based broths (soba tsuyu, oyakodon sauce) are made with kombu and bonito but typically also contain wheat-based soy sauce/mirin. There is no documented gluten-free menu, no dedicated preparation, and no allergy protocol on the official site or any fetched source. Not safe for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: A traditional soba and izakaya-style shop. Broths are built on bonito (katsuo) and kombu dashi, and signature dishes feature charcoal-grilled chicken and eggs (oyakodon, tenmori with fried shrimp). No documented vegan menu or plant-based options. Not suitable for vegans; a vegan would need to ask, and even plain soba is served with fish-based dashi tsuyu.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市二口町ハ21
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI from address 石川県金沢市二口町ハ21 (chō-level match, first feature). Within Kanazawa box. Note: the brief's area label said 二ツ寺町, but both Tabelog and the official site give the address as 二口町 (Futakuchi-machi), near Wakamiya Bridge toward the Kanazawa Station east-side area.
neighborhood: 二口町（若宮大橋近く／金沢駅東口・駅西方面）
seats_text: 53 seats total: counter 9, tables 16, tatami (zashiki) 28.
hours_raw: 平日 11:00-14:00 / 18:00-21:30 (L.O.21:00)。土日祝 11:00-14:30 / 17:30-21:30 (L.O.21:00)。お蕎麦が売り切れた場合、早めに閉店することがあります。定休日：月曜（祝日含む）および第4日曜。
closed_days: Mondays (including public holidays) and the 4th Sunday of the month; Tabelog also notes occasional 2nd-Sunday-evening closures. May close early if the day's soba sells out.
cultural_comfort_note: A welcoming local soba shop with tatami, table, and counter seating that suits solo diners and families, but there is no sign of English support, so a few basic Japanese phrases (or a translation app) will help you order comfortably.
caveats: Address is 二口町 (Futakuchi-machi), not the 二ツ寺町 label in the brief. The "九割そば" (90% buckwheat) noodle still contains wheat binder and the tsuyu uses fish dashi, so this place is not celiac-safe or vegan despite being a soba specialist. Hours vary slightly between fetched sources (official site lists L.O. 21:00 and close 21:30; Tabelog lists close 21:00); reservations are accepted but not taken for weekend lunch walk-in slots (11:30-14:30).

bio.background: Founded in 1996, this neighborhood soba shop sits near Wakamiya Bridge in the Futakuchi-machi district on the station-west side of Kanazawa. It is now run by the second-generation proprietor, who carries forward the traditions inherited from the founder while applying, in the shop's own words, 'old wisdom with new thinking.'
bio.specialty: The house specialty is coarsely-ground nine-tenths buckwheat soba (粗挽き九割そば) with a rich, rustic flavor and a distinctive texture, served with a dashi built primarily on bonito and kombu. The most famous dishes are the tenmori (soba with lightly fried shrimp and seasonal vegetables) and a premium oyakodon made with charcoal-grilled chicken and rich 'Ranoh' (蘭王) eggs from Oita Prefecture over a house dashi-based sauce.
bio.philosophy: The shop describes itself as 'a place to enjoy soba and sake together' (蕎麦と日本酒を楽しむ場), emphasizing painstaking effort and quality ingredients.

anecdotes.text: The proprietor is known for an exceptional sake program — the shop stocks well over 50 (and by some accounts more than 100) varieties of Japanese sake from across the country, including rare and seasonal bottles, with appetizers designed specifically as sake pairings.

Locals praise the coarse-ground, wind-flavored buckwheat noodles and the meticulous boiling technique that highlights the texture unique to roughly milled soba, alongside an unusually deep sake list for a soba shop.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The signature soba is '九割そば' (nine-tenths buckwheat), meaning it contains roughly 10% wheat flour as a binder — the noodles are not gluten-free, and a shared soba-boiling kitchen makes cross-contamination with wheat likely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes such as the oyakodon and soba tsuyu use a dashi/kaeshi base; traditional kaeshi is made with soy sauce and mirin, which normally contain wheat. No wheat-free seasoning is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are based on bonito (katsuo) and kombu fish/seaweed dashi, and signature dishes feature chicken, egg, and shrimp, so plant-based diners cannot avoid animal-derived stock.

cuisine: 純喫茶・喫茶店（昭和レトロな個人店、コーヒー・トースト・ランチセット）
neighborhood: 本町（金沢駅東口から徒歩約5分）／北鉄金沢駅
gf_detail: This is a classic Showa-era Japanese coffeehouse (junkissa) whose menu centers on toast, sandwiches and lunch sets built on bread, plus coffee. The core offerings are wheat-based and there is no indication of gluten-free preparation or any allergen accommodation. Not suitable for celiacs or strict gluten avoidance.
vegan_detail: A traditional kissaten focused on coffee, toast and lunch sets. Black coffee is plant-based, but most food items (toast with butter, sandwiches, lunch sets) typically involve dairy, egg or meat, and no vegan menu is documented. A vegan visitor would need to ask and would likely be limited to a black coffee or similar drink.
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned [136.650665, 36.575375] for 石川県金沢市本町二丁目18番32号; within the Kanazawa box.
seats_text: Small independent coffeehouse with counter seating on one side and a tucked-away sofa/booth area on the other; exact seat count not documented.
closed_days: 日曜・祝日（Sundays and national holidays）
cultural_comfort_note: A quiet, old-school neighborhood coffeehouse run by a welcoming "mama-san"; there is no English menu and little tourist traffic, so a few words of Japanese and a relaxed attitude go a long way.
caveats: Cash only, no reservations, and closed Sundays and national holidays. The food menu is bread- and coffee-centered with no documented gluten-free or vegan options, so it is best treated as a coffee/toast stop rather than a dietary-friendly destination.

bio.background: A long-running Showa-retro junkissa (pure coffeehouse) tucked into a residential pocket of Honcho, about a five-minute walk from Kanazawa Station behind the hotel district. It has been in operation for over 30 years; the Honcho shop is actually the second location, the original having stood near what is now Musashi's studio street. The building predates the cafe itself and was reinforced with sturdy pillars to keep it standing.
bio.philosophy: Preserves an increasingly rare junkissa culture: an unhurried, understated space for savoring a cup of coffee, the kind of place that would be nearly impossible to recreate today.
bio.specialty: A clean, refreshing blend coffee well suited to a quiet morning, alongside classic kissaten fare like toast and lunch sets, plus nostalgic touches such as a sprinkle-topped coffee float.

anecdotes.text: The ceiling holds decorative lead lamps and vintage lampshades once used in cabarets; they normally stay dark, but the welcoming mama-san will sometimes light them up for guests who show genuine interest.
Behind a striking black awning and tiled roof that can look almost forbidding from the street, the interior opens into a calm, wood-scented retreat with counter seating and a hidden sofa nook.

name_ja: カフェフレール（ギャラリー三田内）
cuisine: 喫茶店・カフェ（純喫茶 / コーヒー・ケーキ・カレー・ハヤシライス）
gf_detail: A Showa-era kissaten serving cakes, curry and hayashi rice — all wheat-based — with no documented gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or allergy accommodation. Not suitable for anyone with celiac disease. Coffee and plain hot drinks are the only items unlikely to contain gluten, but cross-contamination cannot be ruled out and staff allergy handling is undocumented.
vegan_detail: No vegan offering is documented. The menu centers on coffee/tea, cakes (fruit cake, granola cheesecake — contain dairy/egg) and light meals like curry and hayashi rice. A vegan could likely have black coffee or tea, but any solid food would need to be confirmed with the owner; nothing on the documented menu is verified plant-based.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市尾張町1丁目8-5
neighborhood: 尾張町（金沢蓄音器館・泉鏡花記念館近く、国登録有形文化財「旧三田商店」煉瓦建築内）
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市尾張町1丁目8-5; first feature coordinates [136.662933, 36.570934], within the Kanazawa box.
seats_text: Counter 6 seats plus table seating (about 6 more); a private room accommodates up to 8. Small intimate space, roughly a dozen seats.
hours_raw: 10:00～22:00 (L.O. 21:30)。ランチタイム割引 11:30～14:00。年に数回不定休。※併設のギャラリー三田の展示自体は10:00～18:00。
closed_days: 不定休（年に数回程度）
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed neighborhood kissaten where a friendly owner chats with regulars; you'll be warmly received with a few words of Japanese and a smile, though English menus are not guaranteed.

bio.chef_name: 髙 隆秀（タカ タカヒデ）
bio.background: The café sits inside Gallery Mita, the former Mita drapery shop (旧三田商店) — a reinforced-concrete building raised in 1930 (Showa 5) with scratch-tile walls and Art Deco detailing, designated a National Registered Tangible Cultural Property in 1997 and counted among the finest surviving modern buildings in Kanazawa. A tearoom first opened in the space around 1967 as 'Kissaten Luault,' closed for a time, and was revived in 2005 as Café Frère under the current owner, who carefully preserved the original interior.
bio.specialty: Blended coffee (a light, refreshing cup with gentle acidity and mild bitterness) alongside homemade sweets such as fruit cake and granola cheesecake, plus light meals like curry and hayashi rice; beer and whiskey are also served.
bio.philosophy: Preserve and share a piece of Showa romance — letting guests linger over a quiet cup beneath original early-Showa stained glass while honoring the heritage of the cultural-property building.

anecdotes.text: The retro stained glass dates to the building's 1930 construction: the former Mita drapery was a regional agent for Kansai Stained Glass Manufacturing, so original Art Deco geometric and plant-motif panels — made with natural stone powder — were preserved and reused when the tearoom opened. On sunny days the outside light pours beautifully through them.

The owner is known as conversational and knowledgeable; first-time visitors are encouraged to take the counter for an unhurried experience, and the room is steadily filled with local office workers and neighbors into the evening.

caveats: The dining function is the café Café Frère (カフェフレール) located inside the Gallery Mita building; the gallery exhibition space keeps shorter hours (10:00-18:00) while the café runs to 22:00 (LO 21:30). Café phone on Tabelog is 076-264-4115; gallery phone is 076-222-0056. No gluten-free or verified vegan items are documented; allergy handling is undocumented, so confirm directly for any dietary need.

cuisine: Kaga regional cuisine / kyodo-ryori (Kanazawa kaiseki) — jibu-ni, kabura-zushi, Kaga vegetables
neighborhood: Nagamachi (Nagamachi Samurai District / near Nishi Chaya, walkable from Korinbo)
address_ja: 石川県金沢市長町1-1-17 青木クッキングスクール1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市長町1-1-17; within Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 36 seats total, including small private tatami rooms (2-6 guests) and sunken-hearth seating.
hours_raw: Cafe 9:30-11:00, lunch 11:00-15:00, afternoon cafe 15:00-17:00 (Mon/Tue/Thu/Fri), dinner from 17:00 (kitchen close around 20:30 L.O., to ~21:30). Hours vary with reservation status. Closed Wednesdays.
closed_days: Wednesdays (or the following day if Wednesday is a public holiday)
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional, refined regional-cuisine restaurant where some Japanese helps; staff are welcoming but English support is not guaranteed, so calling ahead or asking your guide to reserve is wise.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature jibu-ni dredges duck/chicken in wheat flour and simmers it in a soy-sauce-based dashi, and the kaiseki courses rely on wheat-containing soy sauce; no gluten-free accommodation is documented. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The cuisine centers on duck/chicken jibu-ni, Sea of Japan fish, and bonito/seafood dashi; no vegan menu is documented. Vegetable-forward Kaga dishes exist but are not animal-free.
caveats: Tabelog currently lists this location as 掲載保留 (listing on hold), but the official site, the city tourism association, and Kanazawa coupon/passport sites confirm the restaurant is operating. A separate dinner-oriented venture (酒彩AO) appears at the same address, so confirm which service you are booking. Hours flex with reservations, so call ahead, especially for dinner.

bio.background: Etsuko Aoki opened the Nagamachi Cooking Salon (later Aoki Cooking School) in 1957 and spent more than 50 years researching and reviving Kanazawa's traditional home and regional cooking. In December 1992 she opened Shiki no Table on the ground floor of her cooking school in the Nagamachi samurai district so visitors could taste the dishes she had documented for decades.
bio.specialty: Kaga regional cuisine: the city's signature jibu-ni (duck or chicken dusted in wheat flour and simmered with sudare-fu, lily bulb and shiitake in a soy-mirin dashi), winter kabura-zushi (cured yellowtail and turnip), and dishes built around Kaga vegetables and Sea of Japan fish.
bio.philosophy: Her cooking emphasizes 'food and health,' using Hakusan water, local seasonal ingredients and hand preparation to keep authentic Kanazawa home cooking alive.

anecdotes.text: Aoki published a book of Kanazawa regional cuisine in 1982 and that year received the 3rd Kanazawa City Cultural Activity Award.
She served as culinary supervisor for the 2013 film 'A Tale of Samurai Cooking' (武士の献立), starring Aya Ueto, which is set among Kaga-domain kitchen samurai.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Signature jibu-ni coats the meat in wheat flour, so wheat is integral to the kitchen's core dish.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Regional kaiseki dishes are built on soy-sauce and mirin based dashi, which in standard Japanese cooking contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes rely on duck/chicken and Sea of Japan fish with seafood/bonito dashi; no vegan preparation is documented.

positives.text: Run by a veteran cuisine researcher and cooking-school principal, so the kitchen is knowledgeable about ingredients; reservations are recommended and allow diners to discuss needs in advance.

cuisine: Japanese sweets cafe (kanmidokoro) — zenzai, matcha, and wagashi served on Kanazawa lacquerware
neighborhood: Hirosaka / Korinbo (near Kanazawa 21st Century Museum, next to City Hall)
address_ja: 石川県金沢市広坂1-1-60 能作ビル4F
