geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja via GSI; coordinates fall within the Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 20 seats: 8 counter seats, two 3-person tables, one 4-person table, one 2-person table.
hours_raw: 11:00-18:00 (L.O. 17:30); official site notes 11:00-17:45. Closed Tuesday and Wednesday (irregular closures in August).
closed_days: Tuesday and Wednesday (irregular in August)
cultural_comfort_note: A calm traditional sweets cafe where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the picture menu will get you a bowl of zenzai or matcha; English support is limited but the relaxed teahouse setting is welcoming.
gf_detail: This is a traditional wagashi sweets cafe. Signature items center on shiratama (rice) and anko (red bean), but the celebrated 吉はし (Kichihashi) jo-namagashi and toast contain wheat, and there is no gluten-free menu or celiac-safe handling documented. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Many sweets here are plant-based by tradition (anko red-bean paste, shiratama rice dumplings, kuzu jelly, matcha), but the popular matcha cream zenzai is topped with matcha ice cream (dairy), and wagashi may contain egg. No vegan labeling exists, so a vegan diner would need to ask which items are dairy- and egg-free.

bio.background: Urushi no Mi is a kanmidokoro (Japanese sweets cafe) tucked onto the 4th floor of the flagship store of Nosaku, a Kanazawa lacquerware house founded in 1780 that specializes in Kanazawa lacquerware and Kaga maki-e gilding. The cafe has operated for over 20 years, with large windows overlooking Hirosaka Street and seasonal views of central Kanazawa, just steps from the 21st Century Museum and next to City Hall.
bio.specialty: The signature dish is the matcha cream zenzai, made with plump house-prepared Noto Dainagon red-bean paste and freshly boiled shiratama dumplings, crowned with light matcha ice cream. The cafe also serves jo-namagashi from the renowned Kichihashi confectioner alongside matcha — refined sweets normally reserved for formal tea ceremonies.
bio.philosophy: Designed with the elegant feel of a luxury boutique, the cafe lets guests experience Kanazawa's craft heritage directly: some menu items are served on Nosaku's own lacquerware so that the sweets and the traditional craft are enjoyed together.
roles: sweets cafe within the Nosaku lacquerware flagship store

anecdotes.text: The matcha cream zenzai pairs house-made Noto Dainagon bean paste and freshly boiled shiratama with a generous scoop of light matcha ice cream, and is widely cited as the standout order.
Some dishes are served on Nosaku's own lacquerware, letting visitors taste the sweets while touching the shop's traditional craft.

positives.text: Several traditional offerings are inherently plant-based, including house-made Noto Dainagon red-bean paste, shiratama rice dumplings, kuzu (arrowroot) jelly, and matcha.

caveats: No allergy, cross-contamination, or ingredient-handling information is published, so GF and vegan diners should confirm details in person. The matcha cream zenzai contains dairy (matcha ice cream); Kichihashi wagashi and toast contain wheat.

lead: インデアンカレー 本店
name_ja: インデアンカレー 本店
cuisine: Kanazawa curry / yoshoku (Western-style) curry house
neighborhood: Mikuchimachi, western-central Kanazawa
gf_detail: This is a classic Kanazawa-curry house. The curry is built on a dark roux that is wheat-flour based (Japanese curry roux always contains wheat), and dishes such as katsu curry and the signature 野菜玉子カレー involve breaded cutlets and sauces (tonkatsu sauce, ketchup) that typically contain wheat. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented. Not safe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: The food is meat- and egg-forward. The house curry roux is a meat-based Kanazawa-style sauce, the signature dish is a vegetable-and-egg omelet curry (野菜玉子カレー) topped with mayonnaise, and most plates come with cutlets. No vegan options are documented.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市三口町火353
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 石川県金沢市三口町火353, within the Kanazawa bounding box.
seats_text: 42 seats including 6 counter seats
hours_raw: 11:00-21:00 (Mon-Wed, Fri-Sun); closed Thursday
closed_days: Thursday
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, locally loved curry counter where you order at the table and eat with the distinctive Kanazawa fork-spoon; little to no English, so a foreign student should point at the picture menu and be ready to use cash or a Japanese mobile-pay app.
caveats: Cards are not accepted, though cash and several QR/mobile payments (PayPay, d-barai, Rakuten Pay, au PAY) work. The official website could not be confirmed (TLS certificate error on www.indian-curry.jp), so no official URL is listed.

bio.background: Indian Curry is one of the original names of the Kanazawa-curry world. The brand was born in 1964 and at its peak ran 48 directly operated and franchised shops across the three Hokuriku prefectures before the company pulled back from direct operation in the early Heisei years, leaving only a handful of franchise outlets. This Mikuchimachi shop marked the brand's revival as its flagship 'honten' (main store), reopening on December 3 after roughly two decades away from direct operation. The relaunch was driven by a former customer, local businessman Tai Hitoshi, who teamed up with Nomura Kenji, the founder's brother who still oversees the curry-roux production.
bio.specialty: The signature plate is the 野菜玉子カレー (vegetable-and-egg curry): a stir-fried vegetable omelet set over the dark house roux and finished with tonkatsu sauce, ketchup and mayonnaise, medium size around 630 yen. It is served in true Kanazawa-curry style — curry and a bed of shredded cabbage on a stainless-steel plate, eaten with the pointed fork-tipped 'split spoon.' Cutlet (katsu) curry and curry spaghetti round out the menu.

anecdotes.text: The flagship revived the historic brand after about 20 years, reopening December 3 in Mikuchimachi, led by a former customer who partnered with the founder's brother who still makes the curry roux.
Store manager Kitagawa Eiji said they hoped to open several more locations within the prefecture and eventually expand nationwide.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Kanazawa-style curry roux is wheat-flour based and breaded cutlets are fried in shared oil; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The house roux is a meat-based Kanazawa curry sauce and the signature dish includes egg and mayonnaise, so vegan separation is not offered.

lead: 金沢おでん 地酒地魚 新近江町商店
name_ja: 金沢おでん 地酒地魚 新近江町商店
name_en: Kanazawa Oden Jizake Jizakana Shin-Omicho Shoten
cuisine: 金沢おでん・地魚・海鮮（居酒屋）
neighborhood: 近江町市場（近江町いちば館2階・武蔵ヶ辻）
address_ja: 石川県金沢市青草町88 近江町いちば館2F
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [136.656036, 36.571659] for 石川県金沢市青草町88; within Kanazawa box.
seats_text: Small space (~10 tsubo) with roughly 17-18 seats: counter seats for solo diners plus table seating for up to ~14; sources vary slightly (Tabelog lists 17, HotPepper lists 18).
hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Fri, Sat, Sun, holidays: 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:30) and 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30). Closed Wednesday and Thursday. Irregular closures may also occur. (Note: the Omicho market page lists different daytime-focused hours; Tabelog and HotPepper agree on the 11:00-15:00 / 17:00-21:00 schedule used here.)
closed_days: Wednesday and Thursday (plus occasional irregular closures)
cultural_comfort_note: Very easy for a foreign student: it sits inside Omicho market, uses touchscreen ordering at every seat with prices shown, and English menus are available, so you can order seafood bowls and oden without speaking Japanese.
gf_detail: Not celiac-safe and no gluten-free menu is documented. Kanazawa oden is built on a fish/dashi broth simmered with wheat-containing fish cakes (練り物) and seasoned with regular (wheat-based) soy sauce, and the kitchen also serves sushi, tempura-style and grilled rice-bowl dishes, so cross-contact is unavoidable. Celiac travelers should avoid; others with mild sensitivity should ask staff directly.
vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian offering. The menu is centered on fresh seafood (sashimi, nodoguro and seafood rice bowls, sushi), Noto beef, and Kanazawa oden made with fish-based dashi and seafood/meat ingredients. No plant-based dish is documented; vegans cannot eat here in a meaningful way.

bio.background: A compact market eatery on the second floor of the Omicho Ichiba building, right inside Kanazawa's famous Omicho fresh-food market near Musashigatsuji. The shop brings the market's catch straight upstairs, pairing it with the city's beloved oden in one casual spot built for both locals on a lunch break and visitors exploring the market.
bio.specialty: Kanazawa oden served alongside market-fresh seafood: sashimi and seafood rice bowls (including a nodoguro/throat-perch don), sushi, and Noto beef dishes such as a grilled Noto-beef rice bowl, with rice from Noto Koshihikari and local sake to match.
bio.philosophy: Freshness and local sourcing first: fish bought direct from the surrounding market, Noto-grown rice, and regional sake and ingredients combined so visitors can taste several Kanazawa specialties in one small space.

anecdotes.text: Every seat has a touchscreen ordering system that displays prices, so solo diners and tourists can order easily and know the cost up front.
The shop opened inside the Omicho Ichiba building as a place combining fresh seafood and oden, noted by a local Kanazawa info site when it launched.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Kanazawa oden is simmered as a shared pot with wheat-based fish cakes and other items, so broth and ingredients are not gluten-free and share cooking surfaces with other dishes.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Regional dishes (sushi, sashimi, oden, rice bowls) are served with standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat; no gluten-free tamari is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is built around seafood, fish dashi, and Noto beef; oden broth is fish-based, so even vegetable items would share fish/meat broth and surfaces.

positives.text: Touchscreen ordering at every seat shows item names and prices, helping diners review what they are ordering, and English menus are available.

caveats: Two sources give slightly different seat counts (17 vs 18) and the Omicho market page lists different operating hours than Tabelog/HotPepper; the Tabelog/HotPepper schedule (lunch 11:00-15:00, dinner 17:00-21:00, closed Wed/Thu) is used as it is corroborated by two sources. No allergy, gluten-free, or vegan information is published, so dietary notes are inferred from the documented seafood/oden/beef menu rather than stated policy.

cuisine: 寿司・海鮮丼 (Sushi & seafood rice bowls)
neighborhood: 近江町市場（武蔵ヶ辻・北鉄金沢駅）
address_ja: 石川県金沢市上近江町33 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded to 上近江町33 via GSI address search; coordinates [136.656235, 36.570789] fall within the central Kanazawa box (Omicho Market area).
seats_text: 10 counter seats only
hours_raw: 11:00–15:00 (closed Tuesdays). Note: some listings show 10:00–16:00 with irregular days off.
closed_days: Tuesday
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, casual 10-seat counter inside the bustling Omicho Market where pointing at the seafood-bowl photos and a friendly konnichiwa will get you far; bring cash, as cards are not accepted.
gf_detail: This is a traditional sushi and seafood-bowl counter where soy sauce (which normally contains wheat) is used throughout, and sushi rice is seasoned with vinegar that may also contain wheat-derived ingredients. There is no documented gluten-free menu, no dedicated preparation area, and no indication of celiac-safe practices. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: The entire concept is fresh fish and shellfish delivered daily from the family's fishing operation on Noto Island — seafood rice bowls, sushi, sea urchin, salmon roe, crab and nodoguro. There are no documented vegan dishes; this is not a destination for plant-based diners.

bio.background: 鮨 えのめ is a small family-run sushi counter tucked inside Kanazawa's lively Omicho Market. Its defining feature is a direct line to the sea: the owner's family are fishermen (網元, amimoto — heads of a fishing operation) at Enome harbor on Noto Island, and the catch is delivered fresh each morning straight from that family operation, supplemented by direct buying at Kanazawa Port.
bio.origin: Noto Island (能登島), Ishikawa — Enome harbor
bio.specialty: Its signature is a seafood rice bowl (海鮮丼) heaped with eleven kinds of seasonal fish and shellfish, alongside sushi featuring snow crab, sea urchin (uni), salmon roe (ikura) and grilled nodoguro (blackthroat seabream). The rice is grown in the owner's home region of Noto.
bio.philosophy: Direct sourcing from the family's own Enome fishing harbor and Kanazawa Port, served casually so visitors can enjoy top-grade local seafood amid the open, energetic atmosphere of the market.

anecdotes.text: The fish is brought in each morning directly from the owner's family, who work as fishermen (網元) at Enome harbor on Noto Island, and even the sushi rice comes from the owner's Noto hometown.
The signature seafood bowl piles eleven kinds of seasonal fish and shellfish into a single dish, sourced directly from Enome harbor.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a traditional sushi and seafood-bowl counter, soy sauce is used throughout for sushi and donburi; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. No gluten-free soy sauce or wheat-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is entirely seafood-focused, with all dishes built around fish, shellfish, uni and roe, so cross-contact with animal products is unavoidable.

caveats: Two listings give slightly different hours and phone numbers: Tabelog and the Kanazawa tourism site show 11:00–15:00, closed Tuesdays, phone 090-7276-0875; the Omicho Market site shows 10:00–16:00 with irregular days off and phone 090-5825-7167. Hours/closures may vary seasonally — confirm before visiting. Reservations are not taken (walk-in only) and the venue is cash-only.

cuisine: Kissaten (retro café) — homemade baked curry, light meals, and drip coffee
gf_detail: Not gluten-free in any way. The menu centers on wheat-heavy dishes — baked curry (roux), sandwiches, fried udon, spaghetti — and there is no documented gluten-free preparation, dedicated equipment, or allergen handling. Not safe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: No vegan offerings documented. The food is classic Japanese-Western café fare (curry with meat, egg sandwiches, jako/dried-fish fried rice, café au lait) and coffee is the staple. No plant-based menu or substitutions are noted.
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市下堤町37 returned [136.655502, 36.570988], within the central Kanazawa box near Omicho Market / Musashigatsuji.
neighborhood: Omicho Market (Emza entrance) / Shimotsutsumi-machi, Musashigatsuji
seats_text: 17 seats; tucked up a hidden staircase between a butcher and a rice-ball shop near the Emza entrance of Omicho Market
hours_raw: 08:00-16:30 (closes around 16:30); closed Sundays and public holidays
closed_days: Sundays and public holidays
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, smoke-friendly old-school café full of market regulars and run by its longtime owner — wonderful atmosphere but no English and no allergy accommodation, so a foreign student is best off going with a guide or a Japanese-speaking friend.
caveats: Cash only. Smoking is permitted indoors, so the small 17-seat room can be smoky. Clientele is mostly market workers and locals; no English menu or service is documented. Not suitable for gluten-free or vegan diners.

bio.background: Aka is a retro kissaten hidden up a small staircase between a butcher and a rice-ball shop near the Emza entrance of Omicho Market. The owner, Kaoru Fukue, opened it here roughly four decades ago — and has now run it for some 41 years — despite having no prior ties to the market; he was simply drawn to the room's unusual trapezoidal shape. Though it sits in one of Kanazawa's busiest tourist areas, most of the regulars are people who work in the market.
bio.specialty: Drip coffee is the heart of the place, alongside its much-loved homemade baked curry (yaki-curry). Over the years the kitchen has added light meals like jako (dried-fish) fried rice, sandwiches and spaghetti.
bio.philosophy: The menu grew organically out of listening to customers. As Fukue puts it, the shop originally served little more than fried udon and sandwiches, but expanded over time in response to regulars saying what they wanted to eat.

anecdotes.text: Fukue says he opened in Omicho with no connection to the area, won over simply by the room's distinctive trapezoidal shape about 40 years ago.
On how the menu grew: "Originally we mostly did fried udon and sandwiches. But as we kept answering 'I want to eat that,' it just kept growing."

lead: ラーメン みなと屋
name_ja: ラーメン みなと屋
cuisine: ラーメン（鶏・魚・昆布・野菜を煮込んだ天然素材由来のクリアスープの中華そば。醤油・塩・味噌、加賀酒粕ラーメン、治部煮ラーメンなど地元色のある一杯）
gf_detail: A wheat-noodle ramen shop whose broth is built on chicken, fish, and kombu with shoyu/miso seasonings, so wheat-based noodles and soy sauce are central. There is no documented gluten-free menu, no dedicated fryer, and no stated allergen handling. Not suitable for celiacs or anyone needing strict gluten avoidance.
vegan_detail: The menu lists vegetable ramen (野菜ラーメン) variations, but the house broth is made from chicken and fish (plus kombu and vegetables), so the standard soups are not vegan and the vegetable ramen is not confirmed to use a plant-only stock. No vegan-specific preparation is documented; a strict vegan would need to ask, and the kitchen is fundamentally a meat/fish dashi operation.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市青草町88 近江町いちば館B1
geocode_note: GSI geocoded to 石川県金沢市青草町88 at [136.656036, 36.571659], within the central Kanazawa box (Omicho Market). Matches the listed address.
neighborhood: 武蔵ヶ辻・近江町市場（近江町いちば館 B1）
seats_text: 総席数12席。近江町いちば館B1のフードコートも利用可。
hours_raw: 11:00～19:00（公式・ホットペッパー）。ぐるなび等では11:00～16:00の記載もあり、ラストオーダー・短縮の可能性あり。不定休。
closed_days: 不定休（irregular holidays; market closures may apply）
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 12-seat counter ramen stand inside the Omicho Market food hall where a smile, a point at the menu, and a few words of Japanese will get you served comfortably; bring cash as cards are not accepted.
caveats: Reported hours conflict between sources (11:00-19:00 vs 11:00-16:00); confirm same-day, especially for evening visits. Cash only. Note: this is the broth-focused 中華そば shop みなと屋 — the のどぐろ「近江町ラーメン」is a separate nearby shop and is not part of this restaurant's documented menu.

bio.background: A small ramen counter tucked into the basement of the Omicho Ichiba-kan in the heart of Kanazawa's Omicho Market, the city's centuries-old fresh-food bazaar. It serves old-school chuka soba (Chinese-style noodle soup) to market shoppers and visitors.
bio.specialty: Clear, broth-driven chuka soba in shoyu, shio, and miso, alongside local-flavored bowls such as Kaga sake-lees ramen (加賀酒粕ラーメン吟醸) and a jibuni-style ramen (治部煮ラーメン) that nods to Kanazawa's signature simmered dish.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen builds a clear soup from natural ingredients — chicken, fish, kombu, and vegetables simmered gently just below a boil — and describes its approach as using ingredients with controlled additives so guests can taste the natural umami.

anecdotes.text: The shop frames its bowls around a clear, natural-ingredient broth of chicken, fish, kombu, and vegetables simmered at a careful low temperature.
Local-color menu items include Kaga sake-lees ramen and a jibuni ramen referencing Kanazawa's famous jibuni stew.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles and shoyu/miso seasonings are central to every documented bowl; broth uses chicken, fish, and kombu. No gluten-free option is mentioned.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Shoyu and miso chuka soba are core menu items, indicating wheat-containing soy sauce/miso in the seasoning base.

vegan_cross_contact.text: House broth is made from chicken and fish; even vegetable ramen variations are not confirmed to use a plant-only stock.

cuisine: 金沢おでん・どて焼き・串焼き（市場内の立ち飲み酒場）
neighborhood: 近江町市場（武蔵ヶ辻）
gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. The signature 金沢どて焼き is simmered in Kaga miso (wheat-fermented) and Ono soy sauce (wheat-based), and the menu centers on skewers, fried items (ふぐのからあげ, 塩からあげ — wheat-flour batter) and market dishes. Beer is also served. No gluten-free accommodation is documented and the kitchen is wheat-heavy.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The menu is built around grilled skewers, doteni (simmered offal/motsu), fried pufferfish and chicken, and market seafood. No vegan options are documented.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市上近江町38 近江町市場内
geocode_note: Geocoded from 石川県金沢市上近江町38 via GSI; first feature coordinates [136.656357, 36.57132], within the Kanazawa box.
seats_text: Standing-bar (立ち飲み) style with a counter; no fixed seat count documented. Small market stall — patrons may wait when busy.
hours_raw: 9:00～19:00（不定休）. Note: a separate listing shows 8:30-20:00 daily; hours are best confirmed before visiting.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular holidays); may sell out early
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny standing counter inside the bustling market where a few words of Japanese and a point at the skewers go a long way; it is cash-only and casual, so it is welcoming if you are comfortable ordering simply and standing among locals.
caveats: Hours differ between sources (9:00-19:00 vs 8:30-20:00); both indicate daily operation with irregular closures. The shop is a recently opened (March 2025) market standing bar and may sell out of items; confirm operation before visiting.

bio.background: 近江町どてや is a small standing-bar (立ち飲み) tucked into a passageway of Kanazawa's Omicho Market, the city's centuries-old central food market. It opened in March 2025 as an easy place to stop in mid-shopping for a skewer and a drink, where local shoppers and visitors mingle through the morning, midday and evening bustle of the market.
bio.specialty: The signature dish is 金沢どて焼き (Kanazawa doteyaki) — skewers slowly simmered each day in 108-year-old Kaga miso and the mild, sweet Ono soy sauce of the region (2 skewers for 500 yen). The menu also features ふぐのからあげ (fried pufferfish) and のと塩からあげ (Noto salt fried chicken), paired with local sake, draft beer and highballs.
bio.philosophy: Carrying on the traditional flavors of Ishikawa: the doteyaki is prepared and simmered in-house every day by hand, leaning on ingredients sourced right from the surrounding market so each bite tastes of Kanazawa.

anecdotes.text: The doteyaki is made with a 108-year-old Kaga miso and mellow, sweet Ono soy sauce, slowly simmered by hand each day to bring out a deep, aromatic flavor that the shop describes as distinctly Kanazawa.
Sitting right by the market passage, it is the kind of counter you drift into at the end of a market stroll for a light drink of local sake while shoppers and tourists cross paths around you.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen fries pufferfish and chicken (wheat-flour batter) and grills skewers in a tiny shared counter space, so cross-contact with wheat is effectively unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature doteyaki is simmered in Kaga miso and Ono soy sauce, both of which are wheat-fermented and not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes center on meat skewers, doteni (simmered offal) and seafood; even non-meat items share the same simmering and frying station, so no dish can be considered free of animal products.

cuisine: Seafood / kaisendon (seafood rice bowls), with Kanazawa oden and local sake
neighborhood: Omicho Market (Musashigatsuji)
address_ja: 石川県金沢市下松原町49
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI from 石川県金沢市下松原町49; coordinates fall within the Kanazawa box and match the Omicho Market location.
seats_text: Roughly 80-90 seats including counter seating for solo diners and tables/座敷 for groups; a comparatively large market eatery (official market listing states 全90席; an editorial guide states 80席).
hours_raw: Sun & weekdays 9:00-17:00; Sat & day before holidays 9:00-20:00 (editorial source). Note: the official Omicho Market listing states 7:00-17:00, with Sat/day-before-holiday until 20:00. Open hour varies between sources.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures, no fixed weekly day off)
cultural_comfort_note: A busy, tourist-friendly seafood-bowl spot inside Omicho Market where pointing at the picture menu and a few words of Japanese will get you by, but bring cash since cards are not accepted.
gf_detail: This is a seafood-bowl and Kanazawa-oden restaurant where wheat-based soy sauce, dashi, and other seasonings are central; nothing in the sources indicates gluten-free accommodation or awareness, so it is not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: The restaurant is built around raw fish, seafood bowls, and dashi-based dishes. The official Omicho Market listing notes that vegetarian-friendly options are available, but there is no documented dedicated vegan menu and fish/dashi are pervasive, so vegans should ask carefully and expect very limited choices.
caveats: Hours and seat count differ slightly between the official Omicho Market listing and editorial guides (open hour 7:00 vs 9:00; seats 90 vs 80). No dedicated Tabelog page was located. The restaurant opened around 2022 (about two years before a May 2024 article).

bio.background: 海鮮つるや is a seafood-bowl restaurant inside Kanazawa's Omicho Market, the centuries-old food market locals call 'Kanazawa's kitchen.' A relative newcomer, it opened around 2022 and quickly became a popular stop for fresh kaisendon. It is sized deliberately large for the market so guests can be seated quickly: '極力待たせないように、と80席のキャパシティで客を迎えている' (the place welcomes guests with 80 seats so as to keep waiting to a minimum).
bio.specialty: Seafood rice bowls are the draw, from the lavish 特上海鮮丼 piled with nodoguro (blackthroat seaperch), tuna, white shrimp, salmon roe, octopus, sea bream, sweet shrimp, squid, salmon, crab claw and steamed shrimp, to the value おまかせ市場丼. Signature single-topping bowls include the のど黒丼 (nodoguro bowl), いくら丼 (salmon roe bowl) and かんぱち丼 (amberjack bowl). Beyond bowls, the kitchen leans into local Kanazawa flavors with 金沢おでん simmered in a well-developed bonito broth, regional sake from local breweries, and northern delicacies such as Noto sea mozuku and Toyama firefly squid in oil pickle.
bio.philosophy: A commitment to Kanazawa's local flavors paired with quick, comfortable seating that suits everyone from solo diners at the counter to larger groups.

anecdotes.text: Find it by heading straight in from the Emza-side entrance of Omicho Market and turning right at Tadamura Suisan; it has been popular with many diners since opening about two years before a May 2024 feature.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a kaisendon and Kanazawa-oden restaurant, dishes rely on standard (wheat-based) soy sauce and bonito dashi; no gluten-free soy sauce or accommodation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu centers on raw fish and seafood prepared in a shared kitchen; vegans should expect heavy cross-contact with fish and dashi.

positives.text: The official Omicho Market listing notes that vegetarian-friendly options are available and the restaurant is child-friendly, with counter seats welcoming solo diners.

lead: あまつぼ じもの亭
name_ja: あまつぼ じもの亭
cuisine: 海鮮丼・金沢おでん（市場内の海鮮・郷土料理）
neighborhood: 近江町市場（上近江町／北鉄金沢駅）
gf_detail: This is a seafood-rice-bowl and Kanazawa-oden eatery built around soy sauce, dashi, and shared simmering broth. Sashimi bowls are served with wheat-based soy sauce, and oden uses a kombu/bonito broth seasoned with sake and salt but served alongside wheat-containing items and dipping sauces. There is no indication of gluten-free preparation or precautions, so it is not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: The menu centers on fresh fish (sweet shrimp, yellowtail, sea bream, crab, salmon roe, white shrimp, firefly squid) and oden simmered in a bonito-based dashi. No vegan or vegetarian dishes are documented; this is a seafood and local-cuisine destination, not suitable for vegans.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市上近江町27-1 近江町市場内
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市上近江町27 returned [136.656784, 36.570999], within the central Kanazawa box at the 近江町市場 location.
seats_text: Counter seats, table seats, and a spacious semi-private room in the back; described as having ample seating. Exact seat count not published.
hours_raw: 8:00-16:00 (last order 15:30), closed Wednesdays. Note: some listings cite an earlier 7:00 open and sake is advertised from 8:00.
closed_days: Wednesdays
cultural_comfort_note: A friendly, casual market eatery where pointing at the photo menu and a smile will get you a great seafood bowl, though staff mainly speak Japanese so a few basic phrases help.
caveats: Hours sources conflict slightly: the shop's own opening article lists 8:00-16:00 (LO 15:30, closed Wed), while some market listings show 7:00 open. Confirm before an early-morning visit. Seat count is not officially published.

bio.background: When the long-loved Jimonotei seafood-bowl shop inside Omicho Market closed at the end of 2024 after years in business, it didn't simply disappear. Amatsubo, a respected Kanazawa oden house and a member of the city's Kanazawa Oden 50-Year Veterans Association, stepped in to take over the spot and reopened it in early February 2025 under the name Amatsubo Jimonotei, blending the old market institution with their own oden heritage.
bio.origin: Kanazawa, inside Omicho Market (上近江町)
bio.specialty: The headline dish is the Kanazawa-don (around 2,980 yen), a seafood bowl piled with eleven kinds of fish bought that morning in the market just outside the door, including sweet shrimp, baigai whelk, yellowtail, sea bream, crab, and salmon roe. Alongside it the shop serves Kanazawa oden, with a set of fifteen carefully chosen ingredients simmered in a kombu-and-bonito broth seasoned only with sake and salt, plus market small plates like fried white shrimp and marinated firefly squid.
bio.philosophy: A market food stop where you can sit from early morning, order oden and a one-plate dish, and enjoy them with a glass of celebrated local sake while the market bustles around you.

anecdotes.text: The oden broth follows a traditional recipe built on kombu and bonito and seasoned with nothing but sake and salt, a nod to Amatsubo's place in Kanazawa's Oden 50-Year Veterans Association.
Because it opens early, you can pair Kanazawa oden and small plates with morning sake from celebrated breweries such as the Noguchi Naohiko Sake Institute, Kuruma Brewery, and Kazuma Brewery.

roles: market food stop with local sake

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sashimi seafood bowls are served with standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All featured dishes are seafood-based and oden shares a bonito-dashi simmering broth, so there is no separation from animal ingredients.

lead: 金沢茶屋 割烹つづみ
name_ja: 金沢茶屋 割烹つづみ
cuisine: 加賀料理・割烹（治部煮・うなぎ・天然ふぐなど郷土色のある会席・割烹料理）
neighborhood: 本町（金沢駅東口から徒歩約5分）
address_ja: 石川県金沢市本町2-17-21 金沢茶屋 1F・2F
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [136.650238, 36.576942] for 石川県金沢市本町2-17-21; within the central Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 30席。個室あり（事前予約制、2名〜30名以上対応）。
closed_days: 年中無休（無休。施設都合で臨時休業の場合あり）
cultural_comfort_note: This is a refined, ryokan-style kappo with formal multi-course Kaga cuisine and dinner courses around 10,000+ yen, so a foreign student would likely feel most comfortable visiting with a guide or for a special lunch rather than dropping in casually.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The cuisine centers on soy-sauce-based Kaga dishes, and the signature jibuni is explicitly made by dusting duck with wheat flour; dashi, soy sauce, and wheat are pervasive. No gluten-free menu or allergy protocol is documented, so this is not safe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The menu is built on seafood (sashimi, fugu, swordfish), eel, wagyu, duck (jibuni), and fish/meat dashi. No vegan or vegetarian course is advertised; any plant dishes would still use bonito/kombu-meat-based stocks.
caveats: Tabelog lists the page as 掲載保留 (listing on hold), but the official site (news updated May 2026) and Ishikawa tourism listings confirm the restaurant is actively operating as part of the 料理旅館 金沢茶屋 (Kagaya group). Detailed allergy-handling information was not found and should be confirmed by phone.

bio.origin: Kanazawa (Honmachi, near the station east exit)
bio.background: Kappo Tsuzumi is the dining heart of Kanazawa Chaya, a culinary ryokan in the Kagaya hospitality group, set a short walk from Kanazawa Station. It serves Kaga cuisine — the refined castle-town cooking of the old Kaga domain — in a space that blends traditional Kanazawa wa aesthetics with quiet comfort.
bio.specialty: The signature is jibuni, Kanazawa's hallmark dish: duck (and seasonal ingredients) lightly dusted in wheat flour and simmered, which gives a soft, silky texture and a gentle, rounded flavor. Seasonal courses also feature high-quality domestic eel grilled Kaga-style, natural fugu in winter and spring, sashimi, and wagyu, all built around carefully selected seasonal Kanazawa fish and vegetables.
bio.philosophy: The house follows Kagaya-style hospitality summed up in five principles — flavor, sincerity of welcome, comfort, traditional craftsmanship, and approachability — aiming to pair a refined setting with seasonal gastronomy.
roles: dining restaurant within a culinary ryokan

anecdotes.text: The jibuni here is described as made the traditional way using only wheat flour to coat the duck, producing the dish's characteristically soft texture and mild, gentle taste.
The restaurant belongs to the Kagaya group and presents itself as offering Kagaya-style 'taste and hospitality,' carrying the welcome of its hot-spring heritage into a station-side Kanazawa setting.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Signature jibuni is coated in wheat flour and the cuisine is soy-sauce and dashi based; wheat and gluten are pervasive across the menu.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Kaga cuisine here relies on standard Japanese soy sauce (wheat-containing) and soy-based seasonings; no wheat-free tamari is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes center on seafood, eel, fugu, duck, and wagyu with fish/meat-based dashi, so plant items would share animal-based stocks and prep.

lead: 健康食工房たかの きらめき
cuisine: 自然食・マクロビオティック・ヴィーガン（穀菜食）
gf_detail: Not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen. The cooking is grain-and-vegetable based and uses soy sauce (typically wheat-containing) and likely other wheat-derived seasonings, so it is not celiac-safe by default. The restaurant states gluten-related information can be provided if you inquire in advance before your visit, so contact them ahead and confirm — do not assume dishes are GF.
vegan_detail: Fully plant-based (100% vegan). No animal products at all — no meat, fish, eggs, or dairy — and no white sugar or chemical additives. The kitchen also avoids "gokun" (pungent alliums such as garlic and onion), so the cooking is suitable for vegans and aligns with macrobiotic and shojin-style eating. Everything on the menu can be eaten by vegans.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市三口新町3-11-22
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates within the Kanazawa box; matches 三口新町 location southeast of Katamachi toward Saigawa Ohdori.
neighborhood: 三口新町（片町から犀川大通り南東、西金沢方面）
seats_text: 15 seats total, plus a raised tatami space on the first floor that can seat about five adults.
hours_raw: 11:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:00). Operating hours may vary depending on demand.
closed_days: Sundays (usually open on public holidays, including holidays that fall on a Sunday). Occasional temporary closures.
cultural_comfort_note: A small, calm, owner-run natural-food spot that explicitly welcomes vegan and vegetarian diners; a little basic Japanese helps, but the all-plant-based set menus make ordering straightforward for a foreign student.

bio.background: A quiet, owner-run natural-food restaurant in Kanazawa's Mikuchi-shinmachi neighborhood, established in 2000 and run for roughly a quarter-century. It set out to be a 'gentle, quiet, and comforting place' serving wholesome food in central Kanazawa.
bio.specialty: Plant-based set meals built around vegetables and grains — the Healthy Set (around 1,500 yen), a Premium Set (around 2,500 yen), smaller Mini sets, a children's plate, and take-out bento. Dishes use no white sugar, no animal products (no meat, fish, eggs, or dairy), and no chemical additives.
bio.philosophy: Cooking that preserves the 'natural strength of the ingredients' — food is prepared fresh and not pre-plated, with no microwave or other 'unnatural' heating, relying on fermentation and careful preparation. The kitchen also avoids pungent alliums (garlic and onion), reflecting a macrobiotic and shojin-influenced approach, and uses purified water throughout.

anecdotes.text: Open since 2000, the place markets itself on roughly 27 years of natural, additive-free cooking and welcomes vegan, vegetarian, macrobiotic and shojin diners, including international visitors.
Dishes are deliberately not pre-plated and never microwaved; the kitchen states it cooks to preserve the natural strength of vegetables and grains.

caveats: Cash only, no Sundays. Gluten-sensitive diners should phone ahead (076-263-7730) since soy sauce and other wheat-based seasonings are used. Hours can flex with demand, so calling before a special trip is wise.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The kitchen uses soy sauce and other natural seasonings; standard Japanese soy sauce typically contains wheat, so gluten-free cannot be assumed. Gluten-related information is offered if you inquire in advance.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Entirely plant-based kitchen with no meat, fish, eggs, or dairy used at all, which minimizes risk of animal-product cross-contact.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The restaurant invites diners to ask about gluten and dietary needs ahead of their visit and notes it accommodates religious and cultural dietary needs within practical limits.

positives.text: 100% plant-based: no animal products, no white sugar, no chemical additives, and no garlic or onion — a safe, predictable choice for vegans and many macrobiotic/shojin diners.
Food is cooked fresh to order rather than pre-plated, and prepared with purified water.

lead: 近江町市場 杉本水産 近江町店
name_ja: 近江町市場 杉本水産 近江町店
cuisine: うなぎ・どじょうの蒲焼き（炭火焼き）／鮮魚店併設の老舗カウンター
neighborhood: 近江町市場（青草町・近江町いちば館内）
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature unagi and dojou kabayaki are grilled and repeatedly basted with a tare (glaze) made from local Oono soy sauce and malt syrup — soy sauce here is wheat-based, so the core product contains gluten. No gluten-free preparation or accommodation is documented, and there is no dedicated fryer/equipment for allergen separation. Celiac travelers should avoid the kabayaki.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. This is a fish-shop counter whose entire offering is animal-based: charcoal-grilled freshwater eel (unagi) and loach (dojou), plus fresh-fish prepared deli items and, in winter, local snow crab (Kano-gani). There are no documented plant-based dishes.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市青草町88 近江町いちば館内
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja (青草町88) via GSI; result within Kanazawa box and consistent with Ohmicho Market location.
seats_text: A fresh-fish stall with a small charcoal-grill counter inside the Ohmicho Ichiba-kan building; seat count not documented (primarily a takeaway/standing market counter).
hours_raw: 9:00～16:00（売り切れ次第終了）、水曜は9:00～13:00。定休日：日曜・祝日。（市場サイトでは9:00～17:30の表記もあり）
closed_days: 日曜・祝日（水曜は午後休、午前のみ営業）。売り切れ次第終了。
cultural_comfort_note: A bustling fish-market stall where you point and order at the counter; very little English, so it is easiest if you come with a guide or a Japanese-speaking friend, though pointing and gestures work fine for grabbing a skewer.
caveats: Hours vary slightly by source (Tabelog lists 9:00-16:00 with Wednesday afternoons closed; the market site lists 9:00-17:30). The shop closes early once items sell out. Payment is QR-code based per Tabelog; confirm cash acceptance on arrival as listings are inconsistent.

bio.background: Sugimoto Suisan is a fresh-fish shop in Kanazawa's Ohmicho Market with more than 80 years and four generations of history. The founder's great-grandfather started in the market, and the father and grandfather worked the same stalls before the current manager, Mr. Sugimoto, took over. He once dreamed of becoming a pilot or truck driver, but was drawn into the family trade after watching his relatives' passion for market life.
bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled freshwater eel (unagi) and loach (dojou) kabayaki are the signature. The Kanazawa-style unagi is split down the back and grilled Kansai-style; the small dojou kabayaki skewers are a local specialty sold year-round (around 150 yen each). In winter the shop also sells local Kano snow crab.
bio.philosophy: The work begins at the company factory around 3 a.m. each morning, where eel and loach are split, skewered, and grilled over charcoal — repeatedly basted with a house tare made from local Oono soy sauce and malt syrup, with each piece adjusted for the fish and the weather. Mr. Sugimoto says what he values most is hearing customers tell him directly that the food he made is delicious, and he is working to extend eel-eating beyond the peak summer season and to improve conditions for the early-rising market crew.

anecdotes.text: During Doyou no Ushi no Hi (the midsummer eel day), the factory turns out more than 1,500 kabayaki skewers.

The kabayaki is grilled in three traditional stages — saki (splitting), kushi-uchi (skewering), and yaki (charcoal grilling) — with the eel basted in tare made from local Oono soy sauce and malt syrup between grillings.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The kabayaki tare (basting glaze) is made with local Oono soy sauce and malt syrup, and the eel is repeatedly coated with it during grilling — standard soy sauce is wheat-based, so the signature product contains gluten.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The shop is entirely a fish and shellfish operation (eel, loach, fresh seafood, winter crab); no plant-based options are offered and all grilling equipment is used for fish.

lead: 金沢近江町市場 みやむら
name_ja: 金沢近江町市場 みやむら
cuisine: うなぎ・どじょう蒲焼き／のどぐろ・蟹などの海産物専門店 (charcoal-grilled eel and loach, plus local seafood)
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core products are kabayaki — eel and loach grilled with a soy-sauce-and-mirin-based tare that contains wheat. No gluten-free preparation, dedicated equipment, or accommodation is documented. Not celiac-safe.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. This is a specialist eel/loach and seafood shop — every signature item is animal-based (grilled eel, loach, crab, shrimp). There are no documented vegan or vegetarian options.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市青草町88 近江町いちば館 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市青草町88 returned [136.656036, 36.571659], within the Kanazawa box. Matches the Omicho Market location.
seats_text: Small market stall/counter operation with individual seating; no private rooms and not set up for group reservations. Exact seat count not documented — primarily a takeaway and souvenir shop within Omicho Market.
closed_days: Sundays and public holidays (open daily in November and December, with Sunday hours 9:00-14:00 in that period)
cultural_comfort_note: This is a busy local market stall where you point and order grilled eel or loach to take away; bring a guide or translation app, as little English is expected and it is not a sit-down restaurant experience.
caveats: Primarily a market shop and souvenir counter rather than a sit-down restaurant; hours vary slightly between sources (closes 17:00 or 17:30) and the shop keeps special November-December hours. The detailed owner/founding story comes from a business-support profile of the "かばやき屋"/みやむら loach-kabayaki business and should be treated as background.

bio.background: Miyamura grew out of one man's devotion to a single dish. Owner Yamauchi Hiroto had loved the loach kabayaki (dojou no kabayaki) of an old Kanazawa shop in the Kanaiishi district since childhood — a shop that had grilled loach for more than fifty years before its aging owner finally closed it. When it shut, Yamauchi tracked the retired master down and begged to taste the dish one more time. The old man wouldn't reopen, but he agreed to teach. He passed on his filleting and skewering technique, his charcoal-grilling method, his original iron skewers and grill, and the closely guarded secret tare. Yamauchi started making it as a hobby for friends, and their encouragement pushed him to open his own shop at the former location.
bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled freshwater eel (unagi kabayaki), white-grilled eel (shirayaki), eel rolls (unagi-maki), and the signature grilled loach (dojou no kabayaki) — fired each morning when the charcoal is hottest. The stall also sells local seafood: Kaga and Toyama Bay zuwai crab, hair crab, and local shrimp (amaebi, shiroebi, gasuebi).
bio.philosophy: Everything is made in-house from live fish to sauce — domestic wild loach and live eel, natural salt and purified water, real charcoal, and quality tare ingredients. Yamauchi puts taste and repeat customers above cheap pricing, and labels the origin of his fish on the packaging.

anecdotes.text: When the original kabayaki master refused to reopen his shuttered shop, Yamauchi persuaded him to teach instead, inheriting the old iron skewers, grill, and secret tare that had been used for over fifty years.

A wholesale order from within Omicho Market kick-started rapid growth; within about eight years of going independent the shop was selling on the order of 250,000 skewers a year, becoming the prefecture's top seller of loach kabayaki.
Yamauchi developed his own vacuum-sealing method (patent pending) that extended shelf life from a few days to about two months, letting the kabayaki travel nationwide as a Kanazawa souvenir.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All signature items are kabayaki glazed with a soy-and-mirin tare, which contains wheat; there is no documented gluten-free preparation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The kabayaki tare is a soy-sauce-based glaze made in-house; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The shop deals exclusively in eel, loach, crab, and shrimp grilled on shared charcoal — no vegan items and pervasive animal-product contact.

cuisine: Dojo kabayaki (charcoal-glazed grilled loach) — Kanazawa regional specialty
neighborhood: Yokoyama-cho (near the Asano River and Higashi Chaya district)
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市横山町18-10; within Kanazawa box.
seats_text: Primarily a takeout-only specialty shop (お持帰り専門); no documented dine-in seating.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures); closed during the New Year period
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, traditional family-run takeout counter where little or no English is spoken — easiest to visit with a local guide or someone who can order in Japanese.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The single product is loach glazed in a soy-sauce-based tare (soy sauce, sugar, mirin, sake), and Japanese soy sauce and mirin typically contain wheat. There is no gluten-free preparation, so this is not safe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The shop exclusively sells grilled loach (dojo, a freshwater fish), so the entire menu is animal-based with no plant-based options.
caveats: Hours (9:00-19:00) and cash-only status are from listing data; closures are irregular so confirm before visiting. Seating count is unconfirmed — it operates essentially as a takeout specialist. No official website found.

bio.background: A third-generation family business in Kanazawa's Yokoyama-cho neighborhood, near the Asano River and the Higashi Chaya geisha district. The current proprietor, Asada Hiroko, carries on a shop her parents built; she is described as one of the last keepers of a vanishing craft, as Kanazawa once had dozens of dedicated dojo kabayaki specialists and now only a handful remain.
bio.specialty: Dojo kabayaki — freshwater loach, butterflied open along the back, skewered one to one-and-a-half fish per stick, and grilled until the surface turns crisp while the flesh stays tender, then finished in a long-standing sweet-savory tare of premium soy sauce, sugar, mirin and sake. It is Kanazawa's classic summer stamina food, often given as a gift for recovery from illness or after childbirth.
bio.philosophy: The shop pointedly rejects being labeled cheap B-grade gourmet, insisting on careful ingredient selection and technique worthy of a formal restaurant. The family uses larger farmed loach (avoiding wild fish over pesticide and supply concerns) and aims for a tare that is refined rather than cloyingly sweet.

anecdotes.text: The shop tells the dish's origin story: Christians exiled from Nagasaki's Urakami were imprisoned on Utatsuyama in Kanazawa during the Meiji era and caught loach to grill with soy sauce as nourishment — and the loach is split along the back rather than the belly to avoid evoking seppuku, considered inauspicious in this castle town.
The owner's mother, who is said to have lived to 100, believed that loach are a source of vitality.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature tare is built on premium soy sauce, sugar, mirin and sake; Japanese soy sauce and mirin typically contain wheat, so the glaze is a gluten source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The shop sells only grilled loach (a freshwater fish); there are no plant-based items, so it is unsuitable for vegans.

cuisine: 加賀料理・郷土料理（居酒屋割烹）／近海鮮魚、治部煮、のどぐろ塩焼き
neighborhood: 片町（香林坊・犀川近く）
address_ja: 石川県金沢市片町2-2-25 銀河会館1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市片町2-2-25 returned [136.652054, 36.56049], within the central Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 22 seats total (6 counter, 16 table); private use for groups of roughly 15-22.
hours_raw: 17:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:30), closed Mondays
closed_days: Mondays
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional Kaga-cuisine izakaya-kappo where a friendly greeting goes a long way; menus may be Japanese-only, so a little Japanese or a translation app will make ordering smoother.
gf_detail: This is a seafood- and dashi-heavy Kaga cuisine izakaya. Signature dishes like jibuni and grilled fish rely on soy sauce and dashi (typically wheat-containing soy and bonito stock), and tempura uses wheat batter. No gluten-free menu or handling is documented, so it is not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: The kitchen is built around fresh local seafood (sashimi, nodoguro, crab) and meat (Noto beef), with dashi-based seasoning throughout. No vegan or vegetarian dishes are documented. Kaga vegetable items exist but are not prepared vegan (dashi/soy). Not a vegan destination.

bio.background: Genzaemon Katamachi-ten is the Katamachi branch of the Genzaemon izakaya-kappo, set in the lively Katamachi entertainment district near Korinbo and the Saigawa river in central Kanazawa. The intimate space is decorated with Kaga Yuzen, the local kimono-dyeing tradition, giving a calm, refined feel that mirrors the regional cuisine.
bio.specialty: Near-shore seafood and Kaga regional dishes: salt-grilled nodoguro (blackthroat seaperch), sashimi platters, duck jibuni (the classic Kanazawa simmered dish), squid kurozukuri, and seasonal specialties like rock oysters, kobako crab and zuwai crab. Around 50 kinds of local sake are stocked alongside multi-course kaiseki options.

anecdotes.text: The dining room is finished with Kaga Yuzen textile motifs so guests can feel Kanazawa's craft culture while eating the region's seafood and Kaga vegetables.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Core dishes such as jibuni and grilled fish are seasoned with soy sauce and dashi, which normally contain wheat; no gluten-free soy sauce is documented.

caveats: Jibuni (治部煮) appears on partner/menu listings (e.g., Gurunavi, duck jibuni ~980 yen) though it is not prominently listed on every page. Reservations are accepted but not required. Multi-branch operation (also has a 本店), so not a single independent shop.

neighborhood: 駅西新町（金沢駅近く）
cuisine: Sushi and local seafood kaiseki / izakaya; signature thick-cut Aichi eel (unagi)
address_ja: 石川県金沢市駅西新町3-13-17
geocode_note: GSI geocode of full address; within Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 101 seats total: 11 counter, 20 table, 22 raised platform/zashiki, 40 tatami, plus private rooms (one up to 8).
hours_raw: 11:00–14:30 (L.O. 14:00) / 17:30–21:30 (L.O. 21:00), daily; irregular closures (不定休あり)
closed_days: No fixed closing day; irregular closures (不定休あり).
cultural_comfort_note: A polished, spacious banquet-style sushi and seafood restaurant aimed at family gatherings and formal occasions; a foreign student would be welcome but would do best coming with a Japanese-speaking guide or a clear plan, as it is not a casual drop-in spot.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. This is a sushi and seafood restaurant whose signature eel is glazed in a wheat-based soy tare, and dashi/soy seasonings run through the menu. No gluten-free accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The menu is built around fresh local fish, sushi, sashimi and grilled eel, all using fish-based dashi and seafood. No vegan options are documented.
caveats: Despite the target hint, this is not a small individual eel takeout shop. It is a large 101-seat sushi and seafood banquet restaurant produced by the 金沢まいもん寿司 (Kanazawa Maimon Sushi) group. Thick Aichi eel (unagi) is a documented signature/seasonal specialty, including a Doyo-no-Ushi whole-grilled-eel takeout sold from the parking lot, but eel is one feature among sushi, sashimi and seasonal seafood, not the sole focus.

bio.background: 魚匠庵 is a sister restaurant produced by the Kanazawa Maimon Sushi group, set near Kanazawa Station in Eki-nishi-shinmachi. It is a spacious, calm-toned restaurant built for gatherings, with counter, table, raised zashiki and tatami rooms designed for everything from family celebrations and first-meal (お食い初め) and coming-of-age rites to memorial dinners and banquets.
bio.specialty: The kitchen centers on fresh local fish landed at Kanazawa and Nanao ports, served as sushi, sashimi, grilled and steamed dishes, with seasonal stars like winter buri (yellowtail) shabu-shabu and Kaga crab. A signature draw is its thick-cut Aichi-prefecture eel, grilled in a continuously replenished house tare for an exceptionally meaty cut with a fluffy interior and rich, melting fat.
bio.philosophy: To convey the full deliciousness of just-caught local fish, served in a tranquil space offering an out-of-the-ordinary sense of relaxation.

anecdotes.text: For Doyo no Ushi (the midsummer eel day, around July 26), the restaurant grills its large whole eel over charcoal in a demonstration sale from its own parking lot, offering a master's whole-eel kabayaki as a limited takeout.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature eel is glazed in a continuously replenished house soy-based tare, which in Japan is virtually always made with wheat-containing soy sauce; sushi and grilled dishes likewise rely on standard soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is built around fresh fish, sushi and seafood with fish-based dashi throughout, so any non-seafood dish would carry a high risk of seafood and dashi cross-contact.

cuisine: 海鮮・地魚 大衆居酒屋（刺身・舟盛・郷土料理）
neighborhood: 金沢駅西口・広岡
gf_detail: A seafood-focused izakaya built around sashimi, fried local dishes, and grilled fish — soy sauce, tempura/karaage batter, and dashi are pervasive, and no gluten-free accommodation is documented. Not safe for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: The menu centers on fresh fish from Kanazawa Port (boat-style sashimi platters, grilled nodoguro, fish-and-lotus-root fritters). No vegan or vegetarian options are documented; dashi and seafood run through nearly everything.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市広岡1-5-23 金沢第一ビル 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市広岡1-5-23 returned [136.647781, 36.58062], within the central Kanazawa box near the station west exit.
seats_text: Around 48-50 seats (table seating, 4 seats x 12 tables); sources vary slightly between 48 and 50.
closed_days: No fixed regular closing day documented (irregular/unspecified); open daily per official site.
cultural_comfort_note: A lively, smoking-permitted local izakaya near Kanazawa Station with no documented English menu — comfortable for a foreign student who knows some Japanese or can point at dishes, but expect a Japanese-language setting.
caveats: Hours and seat count vary slightly between sources; one listing reported the place closed on Sundays while the official site lists Sunday hours (16:30-22:00) — confirm before a Sunday visit. There is also a related branch (魚八 はなれ); this record is the main 大衆酒場魚八.

bio.background: A casual seafood izakaya a five-minute walk from the west exit of Kanazawa Station, built around fish delivered directly from Kanazawa Port.
bio.specialty: Boat-style sashimi platters (舟盛), grilled and seared nodoguro (rosy seabass), and Kaga-style dishes such as Kaga lotus root and megisu fritters (加賀れんこんとめぎすの挟み揚げ).
bio.philosophy: The shop describes itself around reliable, affordable pricing that welcomes everyday patrons — an unpretentious neighborhood drinking spot rather than a formal restaurant.

anecdotes.text: The signature 舟盛 (boat platter) showcases the day's catch from Kanazawa Port, and nodoguro is highlighted for its rich fat, deep umami, and elegant, non-fishy taste.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Fried Kaga lotus root and megisu fritters share the kitchen; no dedicated gluten-free preparation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sashimi and seared fish are served with soy sauce, which in Japan is typically wheat-based; no gluten-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is seafood-centric (sashimi, grilled fish, dashi-based dishes), so vegan cross-contact risk is high and no plant-only preparation is documented.

name_ja: 大衆酒場 魚八 はなれ
cuisine: Kanazawa izakaya — fresh seafood, Kanazawa oden (kombu-dashi), robata grill and sushi-style dishes
neighborhood: Horikawa-cho, near Kanazawa Station East Exit
geocode_note: GSI address search returned [136.651733, 36.579224] for 石川県金沢市堀川町5番7号; within the Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 48 seats — counter seating overlooking the fish case, oden pot and robata grill, plus second-floor private-room-style table seating.
hours_raw: Mon-Fri 18:00-00:30; Sat 18:00-01:00; Sun closed
closed_days: Sundays
cultural_comfort_note: A lively, affordable local izakaya where second-floor tables use touch-panel ordering, so a few words of Japanese and a willingness to point at the menu will carry you through comfortably.
gf_detail: This is a soy-sauce and dashi-heavy izakaya — oden simmered in dashi, fried items (tempura/fry), and sushi-style dishes all involve wheat-containing soy sauce or batter. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented, so it is not safe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: The menu is built around fresh fish, sashimi, oysters, nodoguro and seafood, and the oden and dishes are made with fish/kombu dashi. No vegan options are documented; even vegetable items rely on dashi or animal-based seasoning.
caveats: Seats (48) exceed a typical mom-and-pop size and the venue is the second branch of the Uohachi group, so it is categorized OMNI rather than MOM_AND_POP. Opened October 15, 2024.

bio.background: Uohachi Hanare opened on October 15, 2024 in Horikawa-cho, a five-minute walk from Kanazawa Station's east exit. It is the second outpost of the popular Taishu Sakaba Uohachi, whose original location sits two minutes from the station's west (port) side. The shop leans into Kanazawa's harbor, with fresh fish delivered from Kanazawa Port displayed in a counter-side case.
bio.specialty: Fresh seafood and Kanazawa oden. Signature plates include the five-piece Kanazawa oden assortment (金沢おでん盛り合わせ5種, ~1,098 yen) simmered in a light-yet-deep dashi, nodoguro and other local fish, sashimi platters, the 'toro-taku nokke' sushi (トロたくのっけ寿司), and gando fry with house tartar-ponzu (がんどフライ 特性タルタルポン酢).
bio.philosophy: A casual, high-value neighborhood drinking spot — small plates start at 299 yen — built on daily fresh fish and the theatre of an open counter where guests watch the fish case, oden pot and robata grill at work.

anecdotes.text: A standout is the Kanazawa oden assortment, described as 'oden infused with dashi that tastes light yet deeply flavorful' (出汁がしみしみのおでんは、あっさりながら深みのある味わい).
Appetizers begin at 299 yen, earning it a reputation as an exceptional-value (コスパ抜群) station-front izakaya.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu includes fried items (fries, tempura-style dishes) and is a general izakaya kitchen; no dedicated gluten-free preparation is documented, so cross-contact with wheat is likely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sushi, sashimi and oden are served/seasoned with standard Japanese soy sauce and dashi, which contain wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Oden and dishes use fish/kombu dashi and the kitchen centers on seafood; no vegan preparation is documented.

cuisine: 加賀料理・懐石（料亭旅館）
gf_detail: This is a traditional Kaga-cuisine kaiseki ryotei where multi-course menus lean heavily on dashi, soy-based seasonings, and wheat-containing components typical of Japanese fine dining. No gluten-free program, dedicated preparation, or celiac accommodation is documented. Not suitable for strict gluten avoidance; a serious celiac would need to discuss needs in advance and accept high uncertainty.
vegan_detail: The kaiseki courses center on seasonal mountain and sea ingredients (sashimi, grilled fish, simmered seafood) and dashi, so the standard menus are not vegan or vegetarian. As an upscale ryotei serving private rooms by reservation, a plant-based course might be arranged if requested well ahead, but no vegan offering is documented. Vegans should inquire directly before booking.
geocode_note: GSI address search returned an exact match for 石川県金沢市寺町一丁目8番50号; coordinates fall within the Kanazawa bounding box.
neighborhood: 寺町・野町
seats_text: Private rooms accommodating roughly 1-20 diners, with a main hall for larger groups; seating is by private room rather than a fixed counter/table count.
closed_days: 無休（年中無休／予約制）
cultural_comfort_note: This is a formal, reservation-only historic ryotei with kaiseki courses starting around 12,000 yen at lunch and far higher at dinner, so a foreign student would be most comfortable visiting with a Japanese-speaking guide or host who can handle booking and etiquette.

bio.background: 金茶寮は昭和8年（1933年）、加賀百万石・前田家の元家老であった横山男爵の別邸を譲り受けて開業した料亭旅館。約1,000坪の庭園のなかに、明治期に建てられた本館と数寄屋風の離れが点在する。城下町金沢を代表する料理旅館として歴史を重ねてきた。
bio.origin: Kanazawa, Ishikawa (寺町), on the site of a former Maeda-clan retainer's villa
bio.specialty: 加賀の郷土料理に京都の懐石の手法を取り入れた会席料理。山海の幸をふんだんに用いた多皿構成のコースを、庭園を望む座敷・離れで供する。
bio.philosophy: 創業以来『一客一亭』のおもてなしを旨とし、客をもてなす料亭旅館としての文化の継承と発信を使命に掲げる。
roles: Kanazawa guest house for Kaga cuisine

anecdotes.text: 皇族や、吉田茂・佐藤栄作といった歴代首相をもてなしてきた由緒ある料亭として知られる。
2016年に『ミシュランガイド富山・石川（金沢）2016 特別版』で一つ星を獲得した。
京都の懐石料理の手法に地元の豊富な山海の幸を取り入れた料理を、約1,000坪の庭園と数寄屋造りの建物のなかで提供している。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a Kaga-cuisine kaiseki ryotei, menus rely on dashi and soy-based seasonings; no allergen or gluten information is published, so wheat-containing soy sauce should be assumed present.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Courses are built around sashimi, grilled fish and simmered seafood alongside dashi, so animal ingredients and cross-contact are pervasive on the standard menus.

positives.text: Meals are served in private rooms by reservation, so dietary requests can in principle be discussed directly with the kitchen when booking ahead.

caveats: History details (founding year 1933, Yokoyama baron villa origin, '一客一亭' philosophy, notable guests, and the 2016 Michelin one-star) are confirmed via the Rakuten Travel guide and search summary of the official site; the official /about/ page rendered only partial text. No dietary, allergen, vegan, or gluten-free information is published anywhere for this venue. Dinner kaiseki starts around 25,000 yen and lunch around 12,000 yen.

cuisine: 手打ちそば（越前そば）／ Hand-made Echizen-style soba
neighborhood: 野町・にし茶屋街（寺町方面、金沢市中央部）
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned [136.647369, 36.556923] for 石川県金沢市野町2-24-2; within the Kanazawa box.
seats_text: Small machiya townhouse: counter seating on the ground floor and a tatami room on the second floor; exact seat count not documented.
hours_raw: 11:00–17:00 (L.O. 16:00); closes early when the day's soba sells out. Closed Wednesdays and the 2nd and 4th Thursdays.
closed_days: Wednesdays, plus the 2nd and 4th Thursday of each month (also closes when soba sells out)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny owner-run soba counter in a traditional machiya where ordering is simple by pointing at the soba dish, but little to no English is documented, so a few Japanese words help.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free or celiac-safe. Echizen soba noodles are typically a buckwheat-wheat blend, and the cold soba is served with a wheat-based soy/dashi tsuyu poured over grated daikon; the recommended dish also adds aburaage (fried tofu) prepared with soy. No dedicated gluten-free preparation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan destination. The signature oroshi soba is dressed with a dashi-based tsuyu (typically containing bonito/kombu), so it is not reliably vegan. A plain soba could in theory be eaten without the fish-based broth, but no documented vegan preparation exists, so vegans must ask in advance and may find options limited.
caveats: The Tabelog page lists the shop under the current name そば処 はしもと with the former name (旧店名) 手打ち蕎麦 はなれ. Seat count and exact payment methods are not clearly documented; reservation is reportedly accepted but not required. Hours can end early on sold-out days.

bio.background: The owner hails from Fukui Prefecture and brings authentic Echizen-style soba to Kanazawa's historic Nishi Chaya-gai geisha district. The shop sits in a traditional machiya townhouse, with an elegant ground-floor counter and a second-floor tatami room. It originally opened under the name 手打ち蕎麦 はなれ (Teuchi Soba Hanare) and now operates as そば処 はしもと.
bio.specialty: Echizen oroshi soba. The signature dish is おろしそば (oroshi soba), where diners combine grated spicy daikon radish and dashi to coat 100% Fukui buckwheat noodles; the chef's recommendation is 油揚げ入りおろしそば (oroshi soba topped with crispy aburaage sourced from the long-established Fukui maker Taniguchiya / 谷口屋).
bio.philosophy: Hand-making the soba fresh each morning starting at 5 AM using Fukui-sourced buckwheat flour, bringing the rare Echizen soba style to Kanazawa.

anecdotes.text: The owner begins hand-making the day's soba at 5 AM, and the shop closes once the soba sells out.
Uses 100% Fukui Prefecture buckwheat flour and sources its aburaage directly from Taniguchiya, a famous long-established fried-tofu maker in Fukui.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Cold oroshi soba is served with a soy/dashi tsuyu, which in Japan is normally made with wheat-containing soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The standard tsuyu is dashi-based and the recommended dish adds aburaage; no fish-free or animal-free preparation is documented.

name_ja: 玄米菜食 明制（てんてん・野町）
cuisine: 玄米菜食・マクロビオティック（完全菜食の和食・ケーキ）／ Brown-rice macrobiotic plant-based Japanese cuisine
neighborhood: 野町（金沢市中央部の南西寄り）
gf_detail: No gluten-free claim is documented. This is a brown-rice macrobiotic vegan kitchen, not a celiac-safe operation. Documented dishes include a soba (buckwheat) galette made with soy milk and soy-based meat alternatives, and Japanese plant-based cooking that typically relies on wheat-containing soy sauce. There is no mention of gluten-free soy sauce, a dedicated fryer, or cross-contamination controls. A celiac traveler should not assume safety here.
vegan_detail: Fully vegan. The kitchen uses no meat, fish, eggs, dairy, chemical seasonings, or additives, centering on local pesticide-free vegetables, brown rice, and traditional seasonings. Vegetable-forward dishes (steamed and roasted vegetables, soy-meat curry, soy-milk buckwheat galette) and vegan cakes are served. Macrobiotic in approach, so the whole menu is plant-based by default.
geocode_note: GSI address search returned [136.650894, 36.552162] for 石川県金沢市野町3-27-19; within the Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 18 seats
hours_raw: 予約制《金・土・日》11:30～お客様に合わせて（その他の曜日はご希望に応じてご予約承ります）
closed_days: 月～木（Monday–Thursday; other days by advance reservation only）
cultural_comfort_note: This is a tiny, reservation-only home-style macrobiotic kitchen run on a personal schedule, so a foreign student should book ahead and expect to manage in Japanese, but the plant-based welcome is warm.
caveats: Reservation-only and runs on an irregular, owner-set schedule (regular service Fri/Sat/Sun from 11:30, other days only by request). Cash only. Hours_week shows an approximate lunch window since closing time is "to suit the guest" rather than fixed. Not gluten-free or celiac-safe. The venue also hosts cooking classes and kimono-dressing lessons alongside the dining.

bio.background: A small, intimate brown-rice macrobiotic kitchen in Kanazawa's Nomachi district, serving entirely plant-based Japanese cooking by reservation. Beyond meals, the owner shares the same lifestyle through cooking classes and traditional kimono-dressing lessons held at the same place.
bio.specialty: Macrobiotic vegan Japanese plates built around local pesticide-free vegetables and traditional seasonings: steamed vegetables (with carrot cut into heart shapes and tofu standing in for egg), roasted carrots described as surprisingly sweet with concentrated umami, a soy-based 'meat' curry seasoned with spices that wins over non-vegetarians, and a thick, chewy buckwheat galette made with soy milk. Vegan cakes round out the menu.
bio.philosophy: Cooking with no meat, fish, eggs, dairy, chemical seasonings, or additives — letting local organic vegetables and traditional condiments carry the meal so that, in the spirit one visitor described, both stomach and heart feel at ease.

anecdotes.text: A visitor wrote that even the bold, impactful soy-meat curry satisfied non-vegetarians dining alongside vegetarians, and that the food left both the stomach and the heart feeling relieved and at ease.
Guests may bring their own alcohol for a 300-yen corkage, lending the small room a relaxed, social feel.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free or celiac protocols are documented; the kitchen is macrobiotic vegan, not allergen-controlled for wheat.
A signature dish is a buckwheat (soba) galette made with soy milk; buckwheat galettes are commonly blended with wheat flour, and no wheat-free claim is made.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Traditional Japanese seasonings are used; no gluten-free soy sauce is mentioned, so assume standard wheat-containing shoyu.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire kitchen is animal-product-free (no meat, fish, eggs, or dairy), so vegan cross-contact with animal ingredients is not a concern.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Service is fully reservation-based and tailored to the guest, which suggests dietary requests can be discussed when booking, though no specific allergy-handling protocol is documented.

positives.text: Completely vegan kitchen using local pesticide-free vegetables, brown rice, and no chemical seasonings or additives — an easy, reliable choice for vegans and vegetarians.

lead: 加賀麸司 宮田・鈴庵
name_ja: 加賀麸司 宮田・鈴庵
cuisine: 加賀麩料理・郷土料理（生麩・治部煮などの伝統金沢料理）
neighborhood: ひがし茶屋街エリア（東山）
gf_detail: This restaurant's entire cuisine is built on 麩 (fu), which is wheat gluten — the signature product is essentially concentrated wheat protein. Courses are fu-centered (生麩 sashimi, fried fu, fu steak, fu desserts), and the famous local dish 治部煮 is thickened and seasoned with wheat-based ingredients. This is one of the least gluten-free-safe restaurants imaginable for a celiac diner; it should be avoided entirely by anyone who must exclude gluten.
vegan_detail: Much of the fu cuisine is plant-based in origin (wheat gluten), and several preparations are vegetarian-leaning, but the menu is not vegan: courses include 治部煮 (jibuni, which uses chicken/duck and dashi) and egg dishes, and dashi-based seasoning is used throughout. No documented vegan course. A vegan diner would need to inquire in advance about which fu dishes can be served without animal products; this is not confirmed possible.
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned exact address match (石川県金沢市東山三丁目16番8号); coordinates within central Kanazawa box.
seats_text: Seat count not published. Small traditional restaurant with tatami/zashiki seating and a private room (個室) seating around 2; full non-smoking.
hours_raw: 11:30-15:00（火・木・金・土・日・月）、水曜定休。予約制。※公式サイトでは9:00-16:00の記載もあり、来店前に予約時間の確認を推奨。
closed_days: 水曜日（Wednesday）。公式サイトでは月曜も休みと記載される場合あり。
cultural_comfort_note: This is a quiet, reservation-only traditional fu-cuisine restaurant where little to no English is likely; book ahead (ideally with help) and be ready to dine in a calm, formal atmosphere.
caveats: 完全予約制。麩（小麦グルテン）が全料理の中心のため、グルテンフリーの方には全く不向き。営業時間・定休日は出典により差があるため予約時に要確認。

bio.background: Suzuan is the restaurant arm of Kaga Fuzukasa Miyata, a long-established Kanazawa fu (wheat gluten) maker founded in 1875 (Meiji 8). Set in a quiet residential pocket within walking distance of the Higashi Chaya District, the dining room has a traditional wooden interior, a view onto a courtyard garden, and distinctive lapis-lazuli-toned walls.
bio.specialty: Multi-course fu cuisine that shows off the versatility of 麩 — raw fu (生麩) served sashimi-style, glazed and fried fu, fu 'steak', vinegared and simmered preparations, and fu-based sweets finished with black honey, alongside the Kaga classic 治部煮 (jibuni). Three set courses are offered (roughly 3,850-6,050 yen).
bio.philosophy: To carry forward the heritage of Kaga fu — a craft refined in the Edo period by Funaki Den'nai, a chef to the ruling Maeda clan, who perfected fu that would not fall apart when simmered (the 'sudare-fu' used in jibuni) — while also demonstrating that fu can pair with Western ingredients and modern techniques.

anecdotes.text: Kaga fu traces to 1725, when the Maeda-clan chef Funaki Den'nai-kanehaya perfected simmer-proof fu and created the 'sudare-fu' still used in the local dish jibuni.

A reviewer's 'Course Three' ran to eight items, from raw-fu sashimi through fu steak and fried fu to rice with chestnut and millet and a fu dessert with black honey, illustrating how the kitchen builds an entire meal around a single ingredient.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The restaurant's core ingredient, fu, is wheat gluten; virtually every dish on the fu courses is wheat-based, so the entire menu is unsafe for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Traditional Kaga preparations such as jibuni rely on Japanese soy sauce and broth, which standardly contain wheat; no wheat-free seasoning is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Courses include jibuni (chicken/duck and dashi) and egg dishes, and dashi-based seasoning is used across the menu, so plant-only fu dishes are not guaranteed and would need to be requested in advance.

cuisine: 野菜料理・薬膳・おばんざい
neighborhood: 長町武家屋敷エリア（長町、香林坊近く）
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature dish "百薬御膳" features Kaga-fu (a wheat-gluten product) in its dessert, and the kitchen uses miso, sauces and likely soy sauce throughout. There is no dedicated gluten-free menu, no dedicated fryer, and no documented celiac-safe handling. This is not a safe choice for celiac travelers.
vegan_detail: Vegetable-forward and health-oriented, but not vegan. The popular set's herb "hamburger" combines tofu with chicken, and other menu items include chicken and natto/avocado dishes; fish/dashi presence elsewhere is not documented. Some plant-based vegetable side dishes exist, but full vegan meals are not advertised — vegans should call ahead (076-255-1893) to ask what can be prepared without animal products.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市長町2丁目3-23-2
geocode_note: GSI geocode at chō/banchi level (長町2丁目3-23); within Kanazawa box.
seats_text: About 22-24 table seats (small intimate space, ~60 sqm).
hours_raw: Lunch 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:30); some morning service reported 7:30-10:00 and occasional dinner on weekends/Mondays/holidays. Closed Tuesdays (open if a holiday); irregular closures occur — call to confirm.
closed_days: 火曜日（祝日の場合は営業）、不定休あり
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny owner-run spot where the friendly husband-and-wife team are happy to chat about ingredients, but service is in Japanese, so a few basic phrases (and pointing at the set menu) will make a foreign student feel welcome.
caveats: Cash only. Hours are irregular with occasional unscheduled closures and limited evening service — best to call ahead (076-255-1893) the same day. Not suitable for celiac diners (Kaga-fu/wheat used) and not vegan (signature dish contains chicken).

bio.background: A husband-and-wife team who previously ran an ethnic-cuisine restaurant in Uchinada before relocating to Kanazawa, where they reimagined their cooking around traditional Japanese medicinal herbs. They opened 百薬キッチン in January 2010 in a hidden-gem space reached through a narrow passage beside a residential building in the Nagamachi samurai-house district.
bio.specialty: The signature lunch '百薬御膳' (Hyakuyaku Gozen) built around a medicinal-herb tofu-and-chicken hamburger with herb sauce, served with 10+ organic vegetables, brown rice from contract farmers, miso soup, a mugwort/Kaga-fu sweet from the old confectioner Miyata, and a daily herbal tea. Other dishes include avocado-natto donburi, herb-fried chicken, and mugwort-fu ginger carpaccio; evenings feature playful 'medicinal junk food' like herb tacos and bagna cauda.
bio.philosophy: Built on the idea that 'everything we eat becomes medicine' (食べるものすべてが薬になる) — extremely healthful food that is still everyday-delicious. Medicinal herbs (loquat, goji, mugwort, etc.) are sourced from Shirakane specialists and organic vegetables from local and Miyagi farmers.

anecdotes.text: Co-owner Megumi says they set out to make food that is 'extremely healthful yet enjoyable to eat normally,' noting the medicinal-herb tofu hamburger is especially popular with children.

The dessert mugwort sweet is made with Kaga-fu from the traditional Kanazawa confectioner Miyata, and the open kitchen with glass jars of herbs invites guests to chat with the owners about ingredients.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Kaga-fu (a wheat-gluten product) is used in the signature set's dessert, and wheat/gluten ingredients are present in the kitchen, so cross-contact is likely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Miso soup and house sauces/dressings are standard parts of the menu; conventional (wheat-containing) soy sauce/miso use is typical and no gluten-free substitution is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature herb hamburger blends tofu with chicken, and chicken/egg/dairy-based items appear across the menu, so plant-only dishes share a kitchen with animal products.

positives.text: Strong emphasis on organic local vegetables, brown rice and medicinal herbs, with owners openly discussing ingredient sourcing — a good fit for plant-forward eaters who can ask about specific dishes.

name_ja: グリーンノート（光明食養研究所）
cuisine: Macrobiotic / brown-rice natural-food cafe attached to an organic grocery (玄米・マクロビオティック・食養)
neighborhood: 瓢箪町 (Hyotanmachi), near Omicho Market and Hashiba-cho, central Kanazawa
gf_detail: This is a macrobiotic / brown-rice (玄米) place, so rice-based dishes are naturally a good base, but documented menu items include brown-rice nut BREAD (玄米木の実パンセット) which contains wheat, and macrobiotic cooking commonly uses miso, shoyu, and tamari that often contain wheat. No dedicated gluten-free program or cross-contamination controls are documented. A celiac traveler should ask directly about each item.
