vegan_detail: A macrobiotic / 食養 (food-as-medicine) cafe whose documented offerings — brown-rice fermented foods, brown-rice nut bread set, natural sweets, health teas — are plant-based in character. Macrobiotic cuisine is typically vegan, but the menu is not formally certified vegan and items are not individually documented, so confirm specific dishes (especially sweets) when ordering.
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市瓢箪町7-28 returned [136.658096, 36.577755], inside the Kanazawa box.
seats_text: A small irori (sunken-hearth) brown-rice cafe ("奄美") attached to the food-education shop; seat count not documented but very small.
hours_raw: Hours not formally published. Described as essentially open with no fixed closing day (基本的に無休), but the proprietor frequently runs outside events/food-education sessions, so advance reservation is recommended before visiting to eat.
closed_days: No fixed regular closing day documented; opening is irregular and depends on the owner's schedule/events.
cultural_comfort_note: This is a tiny owner-run shop and food-education space rather than a regular restaurant, so it helps to call ahead in Japanese (or have a Japanese-speaking friend reserve) and be ready for a personal, conversation-heavy visit.
caveats: Primarily a natural-food shop and 食養 (macrobiotic food-education) institute with a small attached brown-rice cafe; it operates on an irregular, by-appointment basis rather than fixed restaurant hours. Phone listed as 090-4686-4124. No Tabelog page found; treat as a niche destination and reserve before going to eat.

bio.background: Green Note is a natural-food shop and food-education space in Kanazawa's Hyotanmachi, a short walk from Omicho Market. It also goes by 光明食養研究所 (Komyo Shokuyo Kenkyusho), framing itself as a research and teaching practice around 食養 (shokuyo, food as medicine) and macrobiotics as much as a place to buy and eat.
bio.philosophy: The shop's motto is 『台所は家庭の薬局、健体康心』 — roughly, 'the kitchen is the family pharmacy; a healthy body, a peaceful heart.' It centers brown rice, fermented foods and additive-free macrobiotic eating as everyday medicine.
bio.specialty: An attached brown-rice cafe with a traditional irori sunken hearth serves macrobiotic plates and snacks: the food-nutrition basic set (食養基本セット) and the brown-rice nut bread set (玄米木の実パンセット), plus brown-rice fermented foods, natural sweets and health teas. It also stocks Osawa Japan and other additive-free macrobiotic products.

anecdotes.text: The cafe is built around an irori (sunken hearth) and is named '奄美' (Amami), serving brown-rice fermented foods, natural sweets and health teas alongside the shop's macrobiotic groceries.
Affordable tasting-style sets such as the brown-rice nut bread set and a food-nutrition basic set are offered, along with samples of enzyme/fermented foods.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Macrobiotic cooking here relies on miso/shoyu/tamari seasonings, which commonly contain wheat; no wheat-free guarantee is documented.

positives.text: Menu centers on brown rice and additive-free macrobiotic, largely plant-based foods, which is a friendly base for vegan diners.

cuisine: 和カフェ・甘味 (Japanese sweets cafe / wagashi tea house)
gf_detail: The cafe's own site promotes "low-calorie menus with minimal allergens and many gluten-free options," reflecting its health-focused fermented-adzuki concept. However this is a wagashi cafe and several signature items contain wheat (fresh dorayaki plate, certain accompaniments), so it is not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen and no celiac cross-contamination protocol is documented. A coeliac should ask staff which specific items (e.g. parfaits, anmitsu without wheat components) are safe and be aware cross-contact is possible.
vegan_detail: The menu is built around adzuki beans, kinako, matcha and Kaga hojicha, but parfaits and many sweets include dairy (vanilla ice cream, whipped cream) and the cafe makes no vegan claim. Some plant-based components (fermented adzuki, mochi/shiratama dumplings, agar-based anmitsu jelly, black tea/roasted tea) exist, so a vegan could likely assemble a limited option by asking staff to omit dairy, but nothing is marketed as vegan.
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 石川県金沢市尾張町二丁目11番29号, within the Kanazawa box.
neighborhood: 尾張町 (Owaricho), between Omicho Market and the Higashi Chaya District
seats_text: 14 seats across a two-story Kanazawa machiya: white-wood counter seating overlooking Owaricho street on the ground floor and tatami rooms upstairs.
hours_raw: 10:30-18:00 (last order 17:30). Closed Wednesdays and the 1st and 3rd Tuesdays per the cafe's own site; the Tabelog listing shows closed Mondays and Wednesdays. Closures may vary, so confirm before visiting.
closed_days: Wednesdays, and the 1st/3rd Tuesdays (per official site); Tabelog lists Monday and Wednesday closed. Irregular Tuesday/Monday closures vary by source.
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed townhouse sweets cafe used to tourists from the nearby tea district; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the picture menu will carry you through comfortably.
caveats: Two sources disagree on weekly closed days (official site: Wed + 1st/3rd Tue; Tabelog: Mon + Wed). The "many gluten-free options" line comes from the cafe's marketing copy and is not a verified celiac-safe protocol.

bio.background: Cafe Kan is the townhouse cafe of Koshiyama Kanseido, a long-established Kanazawa wagashi (Japanese confectionery) house. The cafe moved from Musashi-machi to a renovated Edo-period machiya in Owaricho, reopening on 10 February 2023 with a fuller menu, placing it on the walking route between Omicho Market and the Higashi tea district.
bio.specialty: Signature items center on the Kaga hojicha (roasted barley tea) and kinako parfait, the matcha and pistachio parfait developed by the proprietor, fermented-adzuki cream anmitsu, and a seasonal fresh dorayaki plate. Guests can choose standard or fermented adzuki for their parfaits.
bio.philosophy: Themed 'delicious and healthy,' the cafe runs an in-house 'AZUKI de HAKKO' (fermented-legume) research effort, fermenting adzuki and other beans with rice koji to draw out natural sweetness with roughly half the usual sugar while keeping nutritional density.

anecdotes.text: The matcha and pistachio parfait is described as a creation refined through the seventh-generation owner's own experimentation.
On relocating in February 2023, the shop set up an 'AZUKI de HAKKO Research Institute' to develop fermented-bean sweets under a delicious-and-healthy concept.

positives.text: The cafe's own site states it offers low-calorie menus with minimal allergens and many gluten-free options, reflecting its fermented-adzuki health concept.

cuisine: 加賀料理・日本料理（治部煮、かぶら寿し、瓢弁当などの郷土料理）
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. This is a traditional Kaga cuisine restaurant built around soy sauce, dashi, and wheat-based seasonings; signature dishes such as jibu-ni and kabura-zushi rely on wheat-containing ingredients. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented. The menu centers on seafood (e.g., botan-ebi, kombu-jime), jibu-ni (typically duck or chicken), and fish-based dashi. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
geocode_note: GSI geocoded to 石川県金沢市兼六町1番; coordinates fall within the central Kanazawa box (Kenrokuen Garden area).
neighborhood: 兼六園内・瓢池畔（兼六町）
seats_text: Approximately 64 seats total: about 32 table seats in the main building (本館) and about 32 tatami seats in the water pavilion (水亭).
hours_raw: 9:30-16:30 (extended during cherry blossom season and Kenrokuen garden illumination events). Seasonal variation noted; the garden tea-house listing also cites 8:00-17:00 in winter and 7:00-18:00 in summer for garden access.
closed_days: Wednesdays (occasionally moved to Tuesday or Thursday)
cultural_comfort_note: A historic, formal Kaga-cuisine tea house inside Kenrokuen Garden where some etiquette and Japanese ability help; a foreign student would be most comfortable visiting with a guide or Japanese-speaking companion, especially for a full meal reservation.
caveats: Located inside Kenrokuen Garden, so garden admission may apply to reach it. Full meals are best reserved in advance; casual matcha and bento are available as walk-in. Hours shift during cherry blossom and illumination seasons.

bio.background: Miyoshian was founded in 1875 (Meiji 8), the year after Kenrokuen Garden first opened to the public, and stands on the shore of Hyoike Pond, often called the birthplace of the garden. Its name reflects its three original structures: a main building (本館) that also manages the adjacent tea room, a water pavilion (水亭) set over Hyoike Pond, and a villa built above Suiryaku Waterfall.
bio.specialty: Kaga regional cuisine including jibu-ni and winter-only house-made kabura-zushi, seasonal bento boxes, and matcha tea served with seasonal wagashi. Diners eat by the pond with views of the changing seasons and the sound of Suiryaku Waterfall.
bio.philosophy: The house frames the experience around the garden ideals of 蒼古 (ancient verdure) and 幽邃 (profound, tranquil depth), emphasizing quiet enjoyment of food and tea within refined natural surroundings.

anecdotes.text: Since its founding it has hosted members of the imperial family and many notable figures.
The venue also hosts tea-style weddings (茶婚式) and receptions overlooking the garden.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Traditional Kaga dishes such as jibu-ni and kabura-zushi rely on soy sauce and other wheat-containing seasonings; no wheat-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is built on seafood, meat-based jibu-ni, and fish dashi, with no vegan options documented.

lead: インドめし ティルパティ
name_ja: インドめし ティルパティ
cuisine: インド料理
neighborhood: 藤江北（県庁・駅西エリア）
gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The kitchen is built around naan baked in a tandoor oven (wheat flour), and the vegetarian curries use soy meat dumplings; Indian curries here are also commonly thickened/seasoned with wheat-containing ingredients. Rice (Koshihikari) is available as an alternative to naan, but there is no documented gluten-free protocol, no dedicated fryer or prep area mentioned, and cross-contamination from a flour-heavy kitchen is likely. A celiac diner should not assume safety; rice-based dishes would need to be confirmed individually with staff.
vegan_detail: Vegetarian-friendly rather than strictly vegan. There is a vegetable curry (with soy-meat dumplings) and a vegetable set aimed at vegetarians, plus a choice of naan or rice. However, dairy is heavily used in Indian cooking here (naan typically contains milk/yogurt/butter; kulfi dessert and likely paneer/cream-based curries are present), so dishes are not confirmed dairy-free or egg-free. A vegan diner can find plant-based mains but must ask specifically about ghee, butter, milk, yogurt and cream in each dish.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市藤江北4丁目490番地
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 金沢市藤江北4丁目; within the Kanazawa bounding box.
closed_days: 水曜日
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, family-friendly neighborhood curry house where a friendly greeting in Japanese goes a long way; the chef is Indian and the menu is approachable, but staff communication is mainly in Japanese so basic phrases help.

bio.background: Tirupati opened on 27 July 2000, timed with the relocation of the Ishikawa Prefectural Government to the nearby Fujie/駅西 district, and has run continuously in the same Fujie-kita spot for around 25 years. Head chef Mohan Kumar brings roughly 40 years of cooking experience and blends the shop's own spice mixes by hand.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen leans toward lighter, health-conscious Indian cooking: Himalayan rock salt and Koshihikari rice are used, and the chef deliberately cuts back on the oil typical of Indian curry while keeping authentic flavor. The name 'Tirupati' is taken from a famous Indian temple, chosen with the wish that the restaurant be a place where many people gather and happiness and abundance circulate.
bio.specialty: Curries with naan baked in a traditional tandoor oven, vegetable curry with soy-meat dumplings, chicken kofta, biryani and tandoori chicken; six levels of spice heat from mild to extra-hot, plus a kulfi (cardamom and saffron ice cream) dessert. Set meals include a vegetable set and a dessert lunch.

anecdotes.text: The shop opened the same year the prefectural government moved nearby and has served the 駅西 area's families and office workers ever since, with lunchtimes drawing local families.
Chef Mohan Kumar says: 塩はヒマラヤ岩塩、お米はコシヒカリを使用しています。通常のインドカレーは、油を多く使いますが、なるべく使わないようにヘルシーに仕上げています。
The restaurant has run an online shop for about 20 years selling its curries, naan, tandoori chicken, biryani, original spice blends, Himalayan salt and chai packs.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen centers on a tandoor oven baking wheat-flour naan, so airborne flour and shared surfaces make cross-contact likely; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The vegetarian vegetable curry uses soy-meat dumplings and the menu relies heavily on dairy (naan, kulfi, likely cream/paneer dishes); no vegan-specific separation is documented, so confirm ghee/butter/milk per dish.

positives.text: Offers a dedicated vegetable curry and vegetable set aimed at vegetarians, plus a choice of Koshihikari rice instead of naan.
Uses Himalayan rock salt and reduces oil for a lighter, more health-conscious style of Indian cooking.

caveats: Hours and closed day (Wednesday) confirmed via Tabelog; the official site does not list hours. Vegetarian options are documented but dairy/egg/gluten details were not specified per dish and should be confirmed in person. Located in west Kanazawa (藤江北, near the prefectural government / 駅西), not the central tourist core.

cuisine: ほうじ茶専門茶房・和カフェ（丸八製茶場直営、季節の和菓子）
neighborhood: 東山（ひがし茶屋街）
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市東山1-26-13 returned [136.667831, 36.572357], within the central Kanazawa box.
seats_text: Seat count not published; intimate seating inside a renovated machiya townhouse. Walk-ins write their name on a list and waits of 30 minutes to an hour are common at peak times.
hours_raw: 12:00-17:00 (L.O. 16:30); closed Mondays and Tuesdays
closed_days: 月曜・火曜（Monday, Tuesday）
cultural_comfort_note: A calm, tourist-friendly tea house in the Higashi Chaya District where you write your name on a waiting list and choose a hojicha after smelling the leaves; a few words of Japanese help, but the simple tea-and-sweets format makes it easy to enjoy.
gf_detail: This is a roasted-tea (hojicha) tea house; the tea itself is naturally gluten-free, but the food offering is seasonal wagashi (traditional Japanese sweets) which commonly contain wheat flour or are dusted with it, and there is no documented gluten-free handling. Celiac travelers should ask about the specific sweet of the day; treat as not celiac-safe by default.
vegan_detail: Hojicha is vegan, and many Japanese wagashi are plant-based (bean paste, rice, sugar), but the seasonal confections rotate and some may contain egg or dairy. No vegan labeling is documented, so vegan guests should confirm the day's sweet.
caveats: Seat count and payment methods are not published; popular spot with frequent waits. Hours can change—the shop recommends phoning ahead.

bio.background: Issho is a hojicha specialty tea house in the heart of Kanazawa's historic Higashi Chaya District, opened and directly operated by Maruhachi Seicha, a Kaga roasted-tea (kaga boucha) producer founded in 1863 in Kaga City, Ishikawa. The shop occupies a renovated traditional machiya townhouse that blends a tea service area, a rotating monthly gallery, and an upstairs coworking space.
bio.specialty: Hojicha (roasted green tea) paired with seasonal wagashi. Guests are first served a water-brewed cup of the prized 'kensho kaga boucha' (a roasted tea made from first-harvest stems), then choose from a small selection of hojicha after smelling the leaves; the food menu is limited to roasted tea and seasonal sweets.
bio.philosophy: The shop's concept is 'kaoru, ajiwau, tsunagaru' — to be fragrant, flavorful, and connected — framing the tea house as a place to work, think, and take a breather over Japanese tea, where new ideas and conversation arise.

anecdotes.text: On arrival, customers are welcomed with a complimentary cup of water-brewed kensho kaga boucha, and pick their hojicha by smelling the different tea leaves before ordering.
The menu deliberately consists only of hojicha and seasonal Japanese sweets, reflecting the shop's focus on showcasing roasted tea.

positives.text: Hojicha roasted tea, the shop's core offering, is naturally gluten-free and vegan.

cuisine: そば粉のガレット＆コーヒー（フレンチ・ブルターニュ風ガレット、町家・蔵リノベカフェ）
neighborhood: 東山・観音町（ひがし茶屋街周辺）
gf_detail: Galettes are made from buckwheat (soba) flour, which is naturally wheat-free, and that is the core of the menu. However, this is NOT a dedicated gluten-free kitchen: there is no documented allergen protocol, no stated dedicated equipment, and savory galettes are built with cheese, cured meats and sauces whose ingredients are not published. Celiacs should not assume safety — confirm the batter is 100 percent soba (no wheat blend) and ask about cross-contamination directly before relying on it.
vegan_detail: The menu centers on meat, seafood, cheese and butter (Noto pork bacon, halibut, duck, four-cheese, etc.), so it is not a vegetarian or vegan destination. No vegan options are documented. Sweet galettes and coffee may offer something workable, but you would need to ask in person; assume no reliable plant-based main.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市観音町1丁目6-7
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [136.666428, 36.572075] for 石川県金沢市観音町1-6-7; within Kanazawa box.
hours_raw: 営業 11:00-17:00（ガレットの提供は11:00-14:00、以降はカフェ・デザート）。定休日：月曜日。
closed_days: 月曜日
cultural_comfort_note: A stylish, welcoming café near the Higashi Chaya district where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will get you a long way; staff are used to tourists but English may be limited.
caveats: One social media post lists closed days as both Monday and Tuesday, but the official site and Tabelog list Monday only — verify before a Tuesday visit. Galette service ends around 14:00; afternoons are café/dessert only. Gluten-free travelers should not assume celiac safety despite the soba base.

bio.background: YAE opened on August 22, 2022 in Kanazawa's Higashiyama / Kannon-cho area, just around the corner from the famous Higashi Chaya geisha district. The shop occupies a traditional machiya townhouse with an attached storehouse (kura) that has been strikingly renovated — glass-fronted contemporary design set against exposed old wooden beams — so the space itself is a draw, blending the city's history with a modern café feel.
bio.origin: 金沢市東山・観音町（ひがし茶屋街周辺）
bio.specialty: Buckwheat (soba) galettes in the French Breton style. The signature is the Noto pork house-made bacon galette complète; seasonal savory galettes feature Hokuriku ingredients such as halibut with mushrooms or roasted duck with raspberry sauce, alongside dessert galettes and coffee in the afternoon.
bio.philosophy: The concept is 'a full course meal experienced on a single plate of galette' (1皿のガレットで味わうコース料理) — using Hokuriku and seasonal produce layered onto each crepe.

anecdotes.text: The buckwheat is a heritage (zairai) variety grown in Echizen City, Fukui Prefecture, chosen for its rich aroma; lightly roasted, it is described as drawing out a deep, subtle umami.
The café fills a renovated storehouse and townhouse, prized as much for its photogenic interior as for the food.

positives.text: Galettes are made with buckwheat (soba) flour from a heritage Echizen variety, a grain that is naturally free of wheat.

cuisine: ビストロ／フレンチ（フランスの田舎料理・ベルギービール）
neighborhood: 香林坊
address_ja: 石川県金沢市香林坊2-12-34 ニュー香林坊ビル206
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja via GSI; coordinates fall within the central Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 16 seats
hours_raw: Dinner 18:00-22:30 (L.O. ~21:00). Lunch on Saturdays/Sundays 12:00-14:00; weekday lunch by reservation made the previous day. Thursdays dinner only (no lunch). Closed Wednesdays (official site notes Tuesday closures around year-end).
closed_days: Wednesdays (Thursday is dinner only). Weekday lunch requires advance reservation.
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 16-seat back-alley bistro run as a personal labor of love, so it helps to have a few words of Japanese or a translation app, and calling ahead (ideally the day before) is appreciated, especially for weekday lunch.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. The menu centers on French country cooking (choucroute with sausages, cassoulet, breaded pork, brownies, petit fours) and beer, all of which typically contain wheat. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented. This is a meat-forward French bistro (pork, sausages, cassoulet). Lunch courses do include a vegetable appetizer, but a vegan would need to call ahead to ask whether anything can be prepared without animal products; nothing specific is promised.

bio.background: Le Mars is a small, hidden French bistro tucked into a back alley of Kanazawa's Korinbo district. It is an independent, personally run establishment that celebrated its 20th anniversary in April, having opened around 2005. The dining room is intimate with an antique, distinctly European feel and warm lighting that locals describe as nostalgic and inviting.
bio.specialty: French country home cooking such as choucroute and cassoulet, paired with French wines and a selection of Belgian beers. A documented lunch featured a vegetable appetizer, a pork main with a crispy aromatic exterior and tender interior, a chocolate brownie, and coffee with petit fours.
bio.philosophy: Bringing rustic regional French food and an unhurried European atmosphere to a quiet Kanazawa side street.

anecdotes.text: The bistro marked its 20th anniversary in April, a milestone for a tiny owner-run spot in a Korinbo back alley.
Visitors describe stepping inside as feeling completely transported to Europe, with a warm, nostalgic interior.

positives.text: Reservations (ideally the previous day) are encouraged and can be made by phone or via Facebook/Instagram direct message, which gives diners a chance to discuss requests in advance.

caveats: No allergy, gluten-free, or vegan information is published by the restaurant. Categorization as a meat-forward French country bistro means celiac and vegan diners should not assume safe options. Hours are somewhat irregular (weekday lunch by prior reservation only, Thursday dinner only, closed Wednesdays); confirm before visiting.

lead: 欧風料理酒場 カミイ
name_ja: 欧風料理酒場 カミイ
cuisine: European-style bistro / wine sakaba (洋食・ビストロ)
neighborhood: 片町 (Katamachi)
address_ja: 石川県金沢市片町2丁目29-2 2F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to the building at Katamachi 2-29-2; within the Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 8 counter seats only (kitchen-facing). Note: some listings describe it as a roughly 6-seat counter; sources give 8.
hours_raw: 18:00–00:00 (L.O. 23:30), weekdays, Saturdays and holidays. Closed Sundays (open if a Monday is a holiday).
closed_days: Sundays (may open if the following Monday is a public holiday)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny chef-run counter bistro where the owner cooks and serves solo in Japanese; warm and approachable, but with only a handful of seats and a blackboard-only menu, comfort comes from basic Japanese and a willingness to chat with the chef.
gf_detail: No gluten-free program. This is a European-style bistro serving homemade pizza, pork ribs and wine-pairing dishes; wheat, soy and standard seasonings are pervasive and there is no documented allergen handling. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The menu is built around fresh seafood from Omicho Market, Noto pork spare ribs and other meat/fish dishes; no vegan menu is documented. Vegetable dishes appear on the daily blackboard but are not prepared as vegan.

bio.background: Chef-owner Yasunori Kamii honed his craft over roughly 18 years at the long-established beer hall Pilsen and at bistros around Kanazawa before opening his own counter restaurant in Katamachi in October 2017.
bio.specialty: Ingredient-driven European cooking from a daily blackboard menu, with signatures including Noto pork spare ribs and homemade pizza, plus a wine list curated entirely by the chef's own palate.
bio.philosophy: Kamii shops Omicho Market each day and builds the menu around 'what he finds exceptional,' favoring seasonal seafood and local vegetables over a fixed card, served across an intimate counter where he cooks and hosts single-handedly.

anecdotes.text: The shop is a hideaway reached by a narrow staircase to the second floor, with counter seating facing the kitchen so guests interact directly with the chef.
Kamii spent about 18 years at the long-standing beer hall Pilsen before striking out on his own in 2017.

caveats: Seat count varies slightly between listings (commonly 8, occasionally described as ~6). No official website found; reservations available via third-party listings. Smoking is permitted, which may affect sensitive diners.

cuisine: 加賀料理・郷土料理・おでん懐石（和空間）
neighborhood: 本町（金沢駅東口徒歩約5分）
gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. This is a Kanazawa oden kaiseki restaurant where dishes are simmered in dashi-based broths and seasoned with soy sauce, which in Japan almost always contains wheat. There is no documentation of gluten-free accommodation, dedicated equipment, or wheat-free options. A celiac traveler should not consider this restaurant safe.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. The cuisine centers on Kanazawa oden and Kaga regional kaiseki built around bonito/kombu dashi, seafood (including items such as hotaruika/firefly squid and seasonal fish), and animal-based ingredients. No vegan or vegetarian course is documented. A plant-based diner could at best ask, but the menu is fundamentally fish- and dashi-based.
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to 石川県金沢市本町二丁目2番3号; coordinates fall within the central Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 32 seats total (10 counter seats and 22 in private rooms)
hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-14:00 (L.O. 13:30); Dinner 17:30-22:00 (L.O. 21:30). Closed Sundays (or the following day if Sunday is a public holiday).
closed_days: Sundays (and the following day when a Sunday falls on a public holiday)
cultural_comfort_note: This is a refined reservation-based kaiseki restaurant where some Japanese and a clear sense of dining etiquette help; a foreign student should book ahead and be ready for a higher-end, formal experience rather than a casual drop-in.
caveats: Dinner is a high-end kaiseki experience (course pricing in the roughly ¥10,000-15,000 range); reservations are required. Lunch is more accessible. Not appropriate for gluten-free or vegan diners.

bio.background: Kanazawa Kagami opened in 2019 as the oden-kaiseki sister restaurant of Amatsubo, a long-established Kaga regional-cuisine and izakaya group with more than half a century of history in the city. It occupies a renovated roughly hundred-year-old machiya (traditional Kanazawa townhouse) a short walk from Kanazawa Station, where aged wooden beams and traditional architecture are paired with a calm, modern interior.
bio.specialty: The signature is 'oden kaiseki' — Kanazawa oden reimagined and served course-style, plate by plate, with different broths matched to individual ingredients. The flagship Kenroku course showcases seasonal local produce sourced from nearby markets such as Omicho, alongside seasonal seafood, and an extensive list of Ishikawa sake (including Kuroobi, Tengu Mai, and Gorin).
bio.philosophy: The restaurant works from the idea of 食の温故知新 — drawing on tradition while evolving it — so that guests can taste both the heritage and the modern evolution of Kanazawa oden in a single meal.

anecdotes.text: The dining room is set inside a restored century-old Kanazawa townhouse, keeping its old timber framing while creating a quiet space where each oden course arrives one dish at a time.
Reviewers note dishes such as firefly squid and seasonal fish prepared with delicate technique, reflecting the kaiseki framing of what is normally a casual oden tradition.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Oden is cooked by simmering ingredients together in a shared dashi broth, so even naturally gluten-free items absorb the wheat-containing soy seasoning and share a common pot — high cross-contamination risk for celiac diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The cuisine is built on Kanazawa oden and Kaga kaiseki seasoning, which relies on soy sauce; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat and no wheat-free alternative is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are based on bonito/kombu dashi and feature seafood throughout the oden and kaiseki courses, so plant-only items would still be cooked in fish-based broth.

cuisine: French bistro / wine bar (古民家ビストロ・ワインバー)
neighborhood: 東山（ひがし茶屋街）
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 石川県金沢市東山1-23-6; within Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 18 seats, including a striking 8.3-meter single-slab African bubinga counter
hours_raw: 18:00-22:00 (last seating around 20:30); closed Wednesdays
closed_days: Wednesday
cultural_comfort_note: This is an intimate, reservation-only owner-chef bistro where some Japanese and advance booking will help; it is a quiet adult dining spot rather than a casual drop-in.
gf_detail: A French bistro built around dishes like veal cutlet (breaded/fried), pâté de campagne, and pasta. These are wheat-heavy and there is no documented gluten-free menu or handling, so it is not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: A meat- and fish-forward French bistro (signature veal cutlet, pâté de campagne); no vegan menu or vegan handling is documented.
caveats: Reservations are required, and same-day cancellations are charged the full course price; payment appears to be cash-only (electronic money and QR-code payment not accepted).

bio.background: Ryomon is a machiya (traditional townhouse) bistro tucked one street behind the main thoroughfare of Kanazawa's Higashi Chaya geisha district, where it blends seamlessly into the historic teahouse streetscape. Its latticed entrance offers glimpses of guests nursing wine glasses along a striking 8.3-meter single-slab African bubinga counter, lit in a moody, intimate glow.
bio.specialty: Owner-chef Yamada Hisato is known for a reserved, artisan temperament and meticulous craftsmanship. The signature veal cutlet (仔牛のカツレツ) is cooked to a tender, fine-grained doneness and served with only salt to let the veal's natural sweetness come through; pâté de campagne, pasta, and seasonal fish and vegetable dishes round out the menu, paired with a serious wine selection.
bio.philosophy: Restraint and technical precision over heavy saucing, with portion sizes thoughtfully adjusted to the size of the party.

anecdotes.text: The chef is described as a taciturn craftsman whose precise work and refined sensibility earn consistent trust, adjusting servings so couples and solo diners alike eat comfortably.
The veal cutlet is served with salt alone, no sauce, so the pure sweetness of the veal is intensified rather than masked.

cuisine: Okonomiyaki / yakisoba / takoyaki / dagashi (Showa-retro teppan diner)
neighborhood: Nagamachi
geocode_note: GSI address search returned the address point for 石川県金沢市長町三丁目11番6号; within the Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 21 seats total (about 9-10 on the first floor, 12 upstairs for larger groups)
hours_raw: Mon/Tue/Thu 11:00-14:00 & 16:00-19:00; Fri 11:00-14:00 & 16:00-21:00; Sat 11:00-21:00; closed Wed & Sun (plus occasional irregular closures).
closed_days: Wednesday and Sunday (plus occasional irregular closures)
cultural_comfort_note: A warm, chatty husband-and-wife place where you order verbally and pay after; the friendly proprietress (originally from Tokyo) puts first-timers at ease, but basic Japanese will make the experience much smoother.
gf_detail: Wheat is central to the entire menu — okonomiyaki batter, yakisoba noodles, takoyaki, and taiyaki are all wheat-flour based and finished with wheat-based sauces. This is not a celiac-safe environment and there is no documented gluten-free handling.
vegan_detail: A teppan diner built around egg-batter okonomiyaki, seafood toppings (e.g., Noto oysters, octopus takoyaki), beef-tendon stew, and dashi/sauce — no documented vegan options. Not suitable for strict plant-based diners.
caveats: Hours are irregular with occasional unannounced closures; the menu is wheat- and seafood-heavy, so it does not suit gluten-free or vegan diners. Cash plus QR payments (PayPay, au PAY) only — no credit cards or e-money.

bio.background: Kado no Mise occupies the spot of 'Shima' (島), a beloved okonomiyaki shop that ran for roughly 50 years and served as an after-school hangout for neighborhood kids in the 1960s-70s before closing for over a decade following its elderly owner's passing. Owner Kakura used to come here as a schoolboy, buying simple okonomiyaki for around 15 yen and cooking it himself on the griddle. After a 2016 alumni reunion at the nearby elementary school, talk turned to the shuttered shop; when the building came up for sale, Kakura bought it and reopened it as Kado no Mise, recreating Shima's recipes with help from the original owner's daughter and her suppliers.
bio.specialty: Yakisoba served the old-school way in a donburi bowl eaten with a fork (inherited from Shima), griddle okonomiyaki (from about 220 yen, with a seasonal Noto-oyster version), takoyaki, taiyaki, beef-tendon stew, and a wall of nostalgic dagashi (penny candy). In summer, dojo kabayaki appears, using loach from Kakura's own eel farming in Nanao.
bio.philosophy: Kakura, a former company head who once trained at a German hotel school and dreamed of opening a bakery, runs the place as 'doraku' (a labor of love / hobby) rather than a serious business, and has declined offers to expand so the shop stays small and manageable. The name 'corner shop' happens to echo the German 'An der Ecke' (at the corner) he once imagined for that bakery.

anecdotes.text: A wall holds a neighborhood map where regulars past and present pin where they used to live, turning the shop into a living memory board of the old merchant quarter.

Despite classmates' enthusiasm at the reunion that inspired the revival, Kakura jokes that 'nobody comes' — yet the toys, manga, picture books and penny candy still draw solo daytime drinkers and weekend dads with their kids.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki batter, yakisoba noodles, takoyaki and taiyaki are all wheat-flour based and prepared on a shared teppan griddle, so cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are finished with okonomiyaki/yakisoba sauce, which is typically wheat-based; no gluten-free sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Egg batter, seafood (Noto oysters, octopus), dashi and beef-tendon stew are core to the menu and share the same griddle, so plant-based dishes cannot be reliably separated.

cuisine: 大衆割烹・海鮮居酒屋（のどぐろ、刺身盛り、金沢おでん、地酒）
gf_detail: A seafood izakaya built around soy-sauce and dashi-heavy Japanese cooking. Signature dishes like sashimi, seared nodoguro and grilled nodoguro sushi rely on soy sauce (wheat), and the Kanazawa oden is simmered in ishiru (fish sauce) and includes wheat gluten (fu). There is no documented gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or allergen handling. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: This is a fish- and seafood-centered kappo/izakaya — nodoguro, sashimi platters, smoked mackerel, and oden simmered in fish sauce (ishiru) are the core of the menu. Dashi and fish stock run through the dishes. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented; not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市片町2-24-2 returned [136.651382, 36.56004], within the Kanazawa box.
neighborhood: 片町（Katamachi）
seats_text: 108 seats total, including counter, table, sunken kotatsu, and private rooms (open kitchen counter on the ground floor).
hours_raw: 17:00-23:30 (L.O. 23:00), open daily including holidays
closed_days: 無休（年中無休）／ none listed
cultural_comfort_note: A lively, popular local izakaya where a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu work fine, though staff may speak limited English and the room can be bustling.

bio.background: A casual yet refined kappo-style seafood izakaya in Kanazawa's Katamachi entertainment district, set in a restored traditional wooden building with gunjo (indigo) walls — a historically prestigious Kanazawa color — and decorative candy molds. The ground floor has an open kitchen with counter seating so guests can watch the chefs work, while upper floors offer private tatami rooms.
bio.specialty: Nodoguro (rosy seabass), called 'the king of white fish' for its rich, melting fat: served as seared sashimi, grilled, and as seared nodoguro sushi rolls. Also known for abundant 'bucket' sashimi platters of seafood landed that morning, and Kanazawa oden simmered in Noto ishiru (fish sauce), with more than 15 local Ishikawa sake varieties.
bio.philosophy: The house motto is '大衆の値、割烹の味' — people's prices with kappo-quality cooking — built on locally sourced seafood and regional ingredients from the sea and mountains of Hokuriku.

anecdotes.text: The Kanazawa oden is simmered in ishiru, a traditional fermented fish sauce from the Noto Peninsula, and served with local ingredients including daikon, wheat gluten (fu), and white shrimp dumplings.
The restored building features indigo (gunjo) walls, a color historically reserved for prestigious use in Kanazawa, with antique candy molds as decor.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Soy-sauce- and dashi-based seafood cooking throughout; sashimi and grilled dishes are served with soy sauce, and oden is simmered in fish sauce — high gluten exposure with no documented gluten-free handling.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce (wheat-containing) is used for sashimi and grilled dishes; no gluten-free tamari mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is built on fish, seafood, and fish-based dashi/ishiru stock; no vegan or vegetarian dishes documented.

caveats: Operated by 株式会社 花組 (a restaurant company), so not an independent mom-and-pop; large 108-seat venue. No documented dietary accommodations — confirm directly by phone (076-225-8724) for any allergy needs.

lead: 川端鮮魚店 片町店
name_ja: 川端鮮魚店 片町店
cuisine: Seafood izakaya (fishmonger-run) — sashimi, shichirin charcoal-grilled fresh fish and shellfish, local Ishikawa/Noto catch, buri-shabu, local sake
neighborhood: Katamachi
address_ja: 石川県金沢市片町2-2-20 木谷ビル 2F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to 片町2-2-20; within Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 66 seats total (counter, tables, and tatami seating)
hours_raw: Mon-Thu 17:00-23:00 (food L.O. 22:00); Fri 17:00-23:30 (food L.O. 22:30, drink L.O. 23:00); Sat & day-before-holiday 17:00-00:00 (food L.O. 23:00, drink L.O. 23:30); Sun closed (open if day before a holiday)
closed_days: Sundays (but open if the Sunday falls on the day before a public holiday)
cultural_comfort_note: A lively, locally packed seafood izakaya where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the fish display go a long way; little to no English is expected, so a translation app helps.
gf_detail: This is a wheat- and soy-sauce-heavy seafood izakaya with no documented gluten-free accommodation. Sashimi and grilled fish are served with regular soy sauce (which contains wheat), and tempura/fried items and izakaya sauces are common. Not safe for celiacs without bringing your own tamari and confirming each dish; no dedicated GF handling is documented.
vegan_detail: A seafood-focused izakaya built around fresh fish, shellfish, sashimi and charcoal grilling; dashi (bonito/fish stock) is pervasive. There is no documented vegan menu or vegan-friendly preparation. Not a suitable destination for vegans.
caveats: Cash only — no cards, e-money, or QR payments accepted. Hours from one search snippet (17:00-21:00) conflict with the official listings (17:00-23:00 weekdays); two independent sources (Tabelog and HotPepper) agree on the later closing times used here.

bio.background: Run by the Kawabata family, who hail from Nanao in the Noto region. The Katamachi flagship opened in 2009 in Kanazawa's main entertainment district, with a later branch added in Kikuracho around the time of the Hokuriku Shinkansen's arrival. Rather than a polished seafood restaurant, it deliberately adopts a working fishmonger aesthetic — fishing gear, flags and sake-brewery aprons decorate the room, staff dress in fishmonger uniforms, and display cases and tanks of live fish and shellfish greet you at the entrance.
bio.specialty: Five-piece sashimi platters of regional catch, shichirin (tabletop charcoal) grilling of fresh fish and shellfish, buri-shabu (yellowtail hot pot), and seasonal items such as natural Noto rock oysters in summer; paired with local sake.
bio.philosophy: Bring the freshness and value of a fish market straight to the table — sourcing whole tuna and catch delivered fresh from Ishikawa's fishing ports, and letting diners grill it themselves over charcoal.

anecdotes.text: The shop styles itself as a fishmonger rather than a restaurant: the interior is hung with fishing equipment, flags and sake-brewery aprons, staff wear fishmonger garb, and live tanks and display cases of the day's catch sit at the entrance.
The owners come from Nanao in Noto, and the Katamachi original opened in 2009; fish is procured fresh-caught directly from Ishikawa Prefecture's fishing ports.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sashimi and grilled seafood are served with standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat; no wheat-free tamari is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is centered on fish and shellfish with fish-based dashi; no vegan-safe preparations are documented.

name_ja: 大松水産 近江町旬彩
cuisine: 海鮮立ち食い・立ち飲み（刺身・炙りのどぐろ・浜焼き）/ Seafood standing bar
gf_detail: This is a seafood standing bar where sashimi and grilled fish are served with wheat-based soy sauce (shoyu), and sets come with sides that are not gluten-controlled. There is no gluten-free menu and no allergen accommodation. While raw sashimi itself contains no grain, the soy sauce for dipping contains wheat and there is no celiac-safe protocol, so this is not safe for those with celiac disease. Travelers avoiding gluten could in theory eat plain sashimi without soy sauce, but should ask.
vegan_detail: An entirely seafood-focused standing bar run by a fish wholesaler. The menu is sashimi, oysters, sea urchin, scallops, grilled nodoguro and other shellfish. There are no vegan or vegetarian dishes; this is not a destination for plant-based diners.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市上近江町21-1
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to chōme-level point for 上近江町21, within the central Kanazawa bounding box. Located inside Omicho Market.
seats_text: Standing-style (tachigui/tachinomi) counter inside the fish shop; no fixed seat count documented.
hours_raw: 9:00-15:30 (L.O. 15:00); closed Wednesdays (closures can be irregular)
closed_days: Wednesday (irregular closures possible)
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, no-frills standing seafood bar inside a busy market staffed by older local women with little English — friendly and easy if you point at what you want and pay with cash or PayPay, but a foreign student with no Japanese may feel more at ease bringing a guide or going with a confident friend.
caveats: The eat-in/standing-bar operation is run by the long-established fish retailer 大松水産 and trades under the name 近江町旬彩 (formerly 大松水産近江町旬彩焼); the Tabelog listing uses 大松水産. Hours and the Wednesday closure can shift; confirm before a special trip. The retail fish shop itself keeps slightly different hours (8:00-17:00).
neighborhood: 近江町市場（上近江町）/ Omicho Market, near Musashigatsuji & Hokutetsu Kanazawa Station

bio.background: Daimatsu Suisan is a fish merchant that has worked the stalls of Omicho Market for around 138 years, dealing in everything from luxury Kano crab, kobako crab, winter yellowtail and sweet shrimp to everyday sea bream and flounder. Within their corner of the market they run a casual standing eat-in counter, known as Omicho Shunsai, where the same fresh fish they sell can be eaten on the spot.
bio.specialty: Reasonably priced fresh sashimi and aburi (lightly seared) nodoguro, plus oysters, sea urchin, scallops and other shellfish served standing-style. The well-known sembero set pairs a draft beer with three kinds of sashimi and a small side for 1,000 yen.
bio.philosophy: Letting visitors enjoy a working fishmonger's catch simply and cheaply, on their feet, in the middle of the market.

anecdotes.text: The counter opens at 9am, making it one of the market's spots for 'morning drinking' (asanomi) over fresh fish.
Service is handled by two older women working the counter, adding to its homey, local feel.
The seared nodoguro is praised for its fat that gently melts and pairs well with a drink.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sashimi and shellfish are served to be eaten with standard soy sauce, which is wheat-based; no gluten-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: An all-seafood standing bar (sashimi, oysters, urchin, scallops, seared nodoguro) with no vegan or vegetarian dishes documented.

positives.text: Items are simple, single-ingredient seafood (raw sashimi, oysters, scallops, urchin) where a gluten-avoidant diner could choose to skip the soy sauce, though no allergen protocol exists.

cuisine: Sake shop with kakuuchi (standing drink) - local Ishikawa, Fukui & Toyama sake
neighborhood: Omicho Market (Kami-Omicho), near Musashigatsuji / Meitetsu M'za
gf_detail: This is a sake shop centered on Japanese sake (a brewed rice product) with a kakuuchi standing-drink counter rather than a kitchen. Sake itself is generally wheat-free, but the shop is not a food establishment with gluten-free provisioning, has no allergen handling, and any accompanying snacks (otsumami) are not controlled for gluten. Not suitable as a celiac-safe destination.
vegan_detail: A liquor retailer / standing-drink sake bar, not a food destination. There is no vegan menu; any nibbles served alongside drinks are incidental and unverified. Not relevant as a vegan dining choice, though the sake itself is plant-based.
address_ja: 石川県金沢市上近江町30-1
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市上近江町30-1 returned [136.656784, 36.570999], within the Kanazawa box; matches Omicho Market location.
seats_text: No fixed dining seating; it is a retail sake shop with a small standing-drink (kakuuchi) counter inside Omicho Market.
hours_raw: Omicho Market listing: 9:00-16:00, closed Sundays, holidays and Wednesdays (subject to change). Other listings give weekday 8:00-18:00 and Sun/holiday 9:00-17:00; hours vary by source.
closed_days: Wednesdays and Sundays/holidays (varies; confirm before visiting)
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional neighborhood sake shop inside the bustling market where the staff speak Japanese; a foreign student comfortable pointing and using basic Japanese can enjoy a casual standing drink, but little English is likely.
caveats: Hours and closed days differ noticeably between sources, so confirm directly before visiting. Kakuuchi (standing drink) is the shop's known draw but was not explicitly described on the pages reviewed; primary business is sake retail.

bio.background: Sake no Ozawa has stood inside Omicho Market - long called the kitchen of Kanazawa - for more than a hundred years, and it is the only liquor merchant within the market. Generations of local cooks, fishmongers and residents have relied on it, giving rise to the local saying that when it comes to regional sake, you think of Sake no Ozawa.
bio.specialty: Roughly 500 varieties of sake centered on Ishikawa jizake (local sake), rounded out with selections from neighbouring Fukui and Toyama, plus seasonal regional recommendations. A small standing-drink (kakuuchi) counter lets locals sample a pour on the spot.
bio.philosophy: The shop describes itself as having been born and raised within the market, growing through the support of its community and remaining devoted to serving its customers.
roles: the only liquor shop in Omicho Market

anecdotes.text: The shop is the sole liquor merchant in Omicho Market and has operated there for over a century, earning the reputation that for local sake, it's Sake no Ozawa.
It stocks about 500 varieties, primarily Ishikawa local sake alongside Fukui and Toyama, with seasonal recommendations.

cuisine: ホルモン・焼肉 (Hormon / yakiniku — grilled beef and offal)
neighborhood: 野町・長町（片町方面、北鉄野町駅／香林坊エリア南）
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to 長町2-3-15; within the central Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 14 seats — 6 at the counter plus two small raised tatami areas seating about 4 each.
hours_raw: 18:00–00:00 (Tuesday closed)
closed_days: 火曜日 (Tuesdays)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, smoky old-school grill run by an elderly couple where some basic Japanese (or a translation app) and an easygoing attitude will help a foreign student feel welcome.
gf_detail: A traditional yakiniku/hormon house where grilled meats and offal are served with wheat-based soy and tare marinades; there is no documented gluten-free handling and the kitchen is not celiac-safe.
vegan_detail: The menu is built almost entirely around beef, pork, offal (hormon), and horse-meat sashimi, with no documented vegan or vegetarian dishes.
caveats: Hours and closing day are drawn from listing aggregators and may change; confirm by phone before visiting. No official website was found.

bio.background: Okahisa is a long-running neighborhood grill tucked into the streets between Nomachi and Nagamachi, south of the Korinbo/Katamachi area. It is run by an older married couple, and the small, well-worn room carries a retro Showa-era feel — the kind of smoky, lived-in yakiniku house where regulars keep coming back.
bio.specialty: Carefully curated grilled meats and offal (hormon): beef heart, beef tongue, beef ribs, liver, pork cheek and pork soft-bone (nankotsu), plus horse-meat sashimi and yukhoe.

anecdotes.text: The couple, said to be in their sixties, run the place themselves at a counter-centered room of about 14 seats, drawing a loyal crowd of regulars who come for good meat at an approachable price.
Diners describe it simply as the go-to spot when you are craving good meat, with the dishes praised as uniformly delicious.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Yakiniku/hormon grill where meats are coated in wheat-based soy and tare sauces and cooked on shared grills; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard wheat-containing soy sauce and tare marinades are typical for this style of grill; no wheat-free tare is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Effectively an all-meat and offal menu cooked on shared grills, with no vegan items documented.

lead: 焼肉ホルモンくろまる
name_ja: 焼肉ホルモンくろまる
cuisine: ホルモン・焼肉（grilled offal and yakiniku）
neighborhood: 金沢駅近く（堀川町・七ツ屋）
gf_detail: This is a charcoal/grill yakiniku and offal house whose flavor centers on house-made tare (marinade/dipping sauce), which in Japanese grill houses is almost always soy-sauce-based and therefore contains wheat. Marinated meats, shared grills, and sauces make this unsuitable for celiacs; no gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or allergen handling is documented. Treat as not celiac-safe.
vegan_detail: A meat-focused restaurant specializing in beef and pork offal (horumon) and grilled meat. The only notably plant-based item documented is the complimentary mound of cabbage (お通しの山盛りキャベツ), but everything is cooked around and with meat on shared grills. There is no vegan menu and the concept is fundamentally non-vegan.
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI using Tabelog-listed address 石川県金沢市堀川町5-7 (official site lists 堀川町5-9, same block); result [136.651733, 36.579224] falls within the Kanazawa box.
seats_text: 52 seats per the official site (counter for solo diners, plus tables and tatami; banquet space for up to ~45). A listing aggregator shows 34 seats; the official figure is used here.
hours_raw: Mon-Sat, holidays & day-before-holiday: 18:00-24:00 (Fri/Sat L.O. 23:30). Sunday: 16:30-22:00 (L.O. 21:30).
closed_days: 不定休（irregular closures）
cultural_comfort_note: A lively, locally-loved grill-it-yourself horumon shop with counter seats for solo diners, but the menu and signage are in Japanese and no English support is documented, so some basic Japanese or a translation app will help a foreign student a lot.
caveats: Tabelog and the official site list slightly different street numbers (堀川町5-7 vs 5-9) and seat counts (34 vs 52); the official site values are treated as authoritative. All seats are documented as smoking-permitted. Strict cancellation policy: 100% charge if cancelled after 10:00 on the reservation day.

bio.background: A back-alley grill house tucked into the streets just a few minutes' walk from Kanazawa Station's east exit, Kuromaru bills itself as a 'meishten in an alleyway' (路地裏に佇む名店) built around fresh, carefully sourced offal and grilled meat sold at friendly prices.
bio.origin: Kanazawa (堀川町, near Kanazawa / Hokutetsu-Kanazawa / Nanatsuya stations)
bio.specialty: House specialties center on offal: the 'white mix' and 'red mix' pork plates (トントロ, ほっぺ, ガツ) at around 748 yen, and コプチャン (beef small intestine, ~825 yen) described as プリプリで濃厚な甘み — springy with a rich sweetness. Domestic-beef horumon is given thorough prep so it carries no off-smell, finished with the house's secret tare.
bio.philosophy: Fresh, well-prepped offal and select cuts at accessible prices, with house-made sauces as the signature.

anecdotes.text: The complimentary starter is a heaping mound of cabbage (お通しの山盛りキャベツ) that's a hit even with women diners, and the mix-horumon plates are the house's calling card.
Described as a name-brand spot hidden in an alley a 3-minute walk from Kanazawa Station's east exit.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Yakiniku/horumon served on shared grills with soy-based house tare; no gluten-free accommodation or allergen handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The restaurant's signature is its house-made tare (秘伝の特製ダレ), which in Japanese grill houses is virtually always soy-sauce-based and thus contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: An all-meat grill concept; the only plant item noted is a complimentary cabbage starter, and all cooking happens on grills shared with offal and meat.

lead: 立ち飲み餃子 大笑
name_ja: 立ち飲み餃子 大笑
cuisine: Gyoza standing bar / casual Chinese izakaya (餃子・立ち飲み)
neighborhood: Honmachi, near Kanazawa Station / Hokutetsu-Kanazawa Station
geocode_note: GSI address search returned a single feature for 石川県金沢市本町二丁目３番４号 at [136.650162, 36.573814]; within the Kanazawa box.
seats_text: Standing bar (立ち飲み) — patrons stand; no fixed seat count published. Small-scale venue suited to solo drop-ins.
hours_raw: 17:00–24:00 (Mon–Thu, Sun); 17:00–01:00 (Fri, Sat, and days before holidays). Open year-round (年中無休). Per official group site.
closed_days: None — open year-round (年中無休)
cultural_comfort_note: A warm, casual standing bar where a friendly greeting and pointing at the gyoza menu will carry you; little to no English, so a few Japanese words go a long way.
gf_detail: The whole concept is gyoza (wheat-flour dumpling wrappers) plus Chinese small plates and Chinese-style oden, almost certainly cooked with wheat-based soy sauce. There is no gluten-free menu and the kitchen is built around wheat — not safe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: A meat-and-drink standing bar: gyoza, stamina gyoza, Chinese oden and izakaya snacks are the draw, typically pork/meat-filled and made with soy/meat-based seasonings. No vegan menu is documented; not a fit for vegans.
caveats: Standing-only venue with no published seat count; QR-code payment is accepted (cash also fine), reservations are not taken. Hours come from the operator's official site and may shift on holidays.

bio.background: A friendly standing-bar gyoza joint a short walk from Kanazawa Station, in the Honmachi neighborhood. It belongs to a homegrown Kanazawa company, Tachinomiya Co., which began in 2009 with a skewer bar called Kushi Sakaba Daisho; the founder fell in love with Osaka's standing-bar culture during an apprenticeship there and brought that easygoing, shoulder-to-shoulder spirit back home. The gyoza shop is one of several 'Daisho' standing bars the group runs around the city.
bio.origin: Kanazawa (Honmachi), part of the locally founded Tachinomiya / Daisho family of standing bars
bio.specialty: Hand-made gyoza is the headliner, with several varieties on offer (including a house Daisho gyoza, coriander/pakuchi gyoza and a stamina gyoza), alongside soup dumplings, simple izakaya snacks and a Chinese-style oden. Reviewers praise the dumplings for a crisp exterior and juicy filling.
bio.philosophy: The parent company sums up its outlook as keeping smiles alive on both sides of the noren — a town where everyone can smile equally — and sees standing-bar culture as a way to connect customers, producers, the region and staff. The shop's own catchphrase plays on this: 'gyoza and beer are Japanese culture.'

anecdotes.text: The venue is described as an at-home standing-bar setting where hand-made dumplings and small plates come paired with the owner's warmth and hospitality — welcoming even for a quick solo drink and a plate of gyoza.
Within the group's lineup, the gyoza shop is cast as the one embodying 'the charm of a father's human warmth.'

gf_cross_contamination.text: A gyoza-focused kitchen working constantly with wheat-flour wrappers; flour and wheat-based sauces are pervasive, so cross-contamination is effectively unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No specific information published; as a Chinese-style izakaya, standard wheat-containing Japanese/Chinese soy sauce should be assumed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Meat-centric menu (meat-filled gyoza, stamina gyoza, Chinese oden); no vegan options documented and shared cooking surfaces make plant-only dining impractical.

cuisine: 餃子・町中華
neighborhood: 野町・神田（金沢中心部南、片町・香林坊から南へ）
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市神田1-2-20 returned [136.63887, 36.560726], within the Kanazawa box.
seats_text: Counter seating only; a compact family-run shop. Exact seat count not published.
hours_raw: 17:00-23:30 (L.O. 23:00), Monday-Saturday; closed Sundays and year-end/New Year holidays.
closed_days: Sundays; year-end/New Year holidays
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, homey family-run counter shop in a residential neighborhood — friendly but Japanese-only is likely, so a few words of Japanese (or a translation app) will make ordering smoother for a foreign student.
gf_detail: This is a gyoza and town-Chinese izakaya: gyoza wrappers are wheat, and dishes lean on soy sauce and dashi. There is no gluten-free menu or documented accommodation, so it is not safe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: The menu centers on meat/vegetable gyoza, cream croquettes, beef-tendon stew, sashimi, and Kanazawa oden — all involving meat, seafood, and animal- or fish-based dashi. No documented vegan options.
caveats: Address is in 神田1-2-20 (near 野町), slightly south of the central 片町/香林坊 core. Seat count not published. Cards/e-money/QR accepted per booking sites.

bio.background: One of Kanazawa's beloved 'numbered gyoza' shops, 第六ギョーザの店 sits quietly in a residential pocket near Nomachi and is run as a snug, counter-only family operation — the proprietor working alongside his daughter and her husband. The numbered gyoza shops are part of a local Kanazawa tradition said to trace back to a postwar gyoza street stall.
bio.specialty: Pan-fried gyoza — both the regular slightly-small, crisp-bottomed dumplings and a refreshing shiso (perilla) variety — alongside cream croquettes, beef-tendon stew, and affordable Kanazawa oden.

anecdotes.text: A solitary, compact shop tucked into a residential neighborhood, run by the proprietor with his daughter and her husband; the younger man handles the wrapping and cooking, and the shiso gyoza in particular won over visitors with its fresh aroma.
A long-time regular noted the place keeps its warm, at-home hospitality even after 15 years.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Gyoza wrappers are wheat-based and prepared in a shared kitchen; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes and dipping rely on standard soy sauce (wheat-containing) and dashi typical of town-Chinese/izakaya cooking.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is built around meat, seafood, and animal/fish dashi; no vegan preparation or separation is documented.

cuisine: ホルモン焼肉（焼肉・もつ）/ Hormone (offal) yakiniku grill
neighborhood: 片町・野町（北鉄野町駅から徒歩約12分、片町・香林坊から南）
gf_detail: This is a grilled-offal and yakiniku restaurant. Yakiniku dipping sauces (tare) and marinades are soy-sauce based and contain wheat, and braised/seasoned offal dishes use the same. There is no documented gluten-free menu, no dedicated fryer, and grilling is done on a shared gas grill. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: The menu is built around grilled beef offal (hormone), wagyu cuts, pork, and fresh seafood. There are no documented vegan or vegetarian dishes; even side items would be cooked on shared meat surfaces. Not a vegan destination.
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 石川県金沢市片町2-21-20 returned [136.650558, 36.559402], inside the Kanazawa box.
seats_text: About 20 seats: roughly 5-6 counter seats plus a small tatami/zashiki area (koagari) seating 2-10. Cozy, home-like room.
hours_raw: 月-土・祝: 19:00-翌05:00（フードL.O. 04:00、ドリンクL.O. 04:30）／日曜定休。(Hot Pepper lists 18:00 open; Tabelog lists 19:00.)
closed_days: 日曜（Sundays）
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, late-night neighborhood offal-grill run by locals with a Showa-retro feel; staff likely speak little English, so basic Japanese and a relaxed attitude help, and offal/meat dishes mean it is not suited to vegan or strictly gluten-free diners.
caveats: Opens only in the evening and runs deep into the night (until 5am), closed Sundays. Opening time differs between listings (18:00 vs 19:00) — confirm by phone. No documented allergy, vegan, or gluten-free accommodation.

bio.background: A long-running, family/individually operated hormone (offal) yakiniku spot in Kanazawa's Katamachi entertainment district, just south of Korinbo and a short walk from Hokutetsu Nomachi station. It has been grilling in the neighborhood for over 20 years, with a cozy, home-like Showa-retro room of a few counter seats and a small tatami corner.
bio.specialty: Beef offal (hormone) and wagyu cuts grilled at the table, with offal cut by hand to order so it arrives at its freshest. Dishes mentioned include wagyu harami (skirt), pork belly, and braised tongue (tan nikomi); fresh Kanazawa seafood is also served alongside the meat.
bio.philosophy: The shop emphasizes that how the offal is cut affects its quality, so each order is hand-cut fresh on the spot to maximize flavor and texture.

anecdotes.text: Described as an established yakiniku shop carrying on for more than 20 years in Katamachi, where offal is hand-cut after you order for exceptional freshness.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All meat and offal is grilled on a shared tabletop gas grill, and yakiniku tare/marinades are soy-sauce based (wheat-containing). No gluten-free menu is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Yakiniku dipping sauces and braised offal seasonings are soy-sauce based and contain wheat; no wheat-free sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is meat-, offal-, and seafood-focused with grilling on shared surfaces; no separate vegan preparation is documented.

cuisine: ホルモン・焼肉 (hormone/offal yakiniku)
neighborhood: 片町 (Katamachi)
geocode_note: Geocoded from address 石川県金沢市片町1-9-17; coordinates within central Kanazawa box. Note: target brief listed the area as 野町, but the confirmed address is 片町 (Katamachi), the central nightlife district just north of Nomachi, roughly 13 min walk from Hokutetsu Nomachi Station.
seats_text: 15 seats total (7 counter seats plus a small raised tatami area)
closed_days: Sundays
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 15-seat late-night counter grill where almost no English is spoken, so some Japanese (or a translation app) and comfort with ordering offal yakiniku will help a foreign student feel at ease.
gf_detail: This is a grilled-meat and offal joint where dishes are seasoned with wheat-based soy/tare and served alongside dipping sauces, so it is not safe for celiacs. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: The entire concept is hormone (offal) and beef yakiniku; there are no documented plant-based meals beyond incidental sides like kimchi. Not a viable option for vegans.

bio.background: A small, locally loved hormone (offal) and yakiniku counter tucked into Kanazawa's Katamachi nightlife district, with just 15 seats including a row of counter stools and a small raised tatami nook. It is the kind of intimate, owner-run grill that draws regulars for late-night eating and drinking.
bio.specialty: Charcoal-style grilled offal and beef cuts: documented menu items include salted upper tongue (上タン塩), wagyu ribeye, outside skirt (ハラミ系), and side dishes such as kimchi natto.

caveats: Target brief listed the area as 野町, but the confirmed address is 片町1-9-17 (Katamachi). Tabelog rating is low with very few reviews, but the listing is active with no 閉店/掲載保留 flag. No official website found. Payment methods (cards vs cash) are not documented, so cash_only is left false but unconfirmed. under30 budget is not listed anywhere, marked unsure.
